My favorite might be the T-55 Tyro, but the other 2 are really fun as well. just know, the Heli has a crazy learning 
curve and is very hard to fly at the start. Otherwise, the community is mostly super friendly and looking for a good time, PvP is more common nowadays, and PvE is a blast. the only issues are the standard VR lag (rare freezing, ever so occasionally choppy, etc.), and the game isn't getting majorly updated, so don't expect any huge updates or lost of new content. We might be lucky enough to get another DLC, but that isn't likely. Over all, this is one of my favorite games to play, even with not having too much time on it. This game is amazing. At first i struggled a lot with simple things like locking onto targets and even just figuring out the conditions to fire various weapons, and i always got shot down by enemy planes which made most of my gameplay an excercise in preppeing the plane for take-off and practising pre-flight checklists more than i was actually in the air. Just around the 9 hour mark though something just clicked and i started actively dodging missiles, reliably taking down enemy planes and clearing the skies for ground strikes, and generally staying in the air for much longer. A lot of times now i actually run out of ammo before i get shot down and have to head back to re-arm. And let me tell you, that feeling when it suddenly clicks, when you dodge your first missile or take down your first plane, its absolutely intoxicating. You feel such incredible mastery like you finally cracked the code on a skill you were struggling with, and it fills you with such a childish joy for the game and makes you want to keep pushing and challenge yourself with other maps and scenarios. 

I almost gave up around the 6 hour mark feeling like i just didnt understand how to defend myself, but the feeling of finally mastering it enough to do real damage and achieve decent survivability was worth every crash and every eaten missile.

This is a game that very clearly doesn’t care about mass appeal, and I respect it for that even if it comes at a cost. SOVL Fantasy Warfare feels closer to an experimental passion project than a traditionally finished product. It strips fantasy combat down to its bare essentials and puts all the emphasis on difficulty, spacing, and player discipline. Combat is slow, weighty, and extremely punishing. Mistakes are costly, and there’s very little room for improvisation. You either learn enemy behavior and timing, or you fail. When it works, it feels earned, but the learning curve is steep and can feel hostile rather than challenging at times. Progression is also very limited, which makes every encounter feel samey after a while. There aren’t many systems to engage with beyond combat itself, and the lack of meaningful variety can make the experience feel repetitive, especially during longer sessions. I also found the feedback during fights to be inconsistent sometimes it’s not clear why you took damage or what you could have done differently. The presentation is extremely minimal, almost to a fault. The visuals, animations, and UI feel unfinished, and while that might be intentional, it doesn’t always work in the game’s favor. The lack of clear tutorials or explanations adds to the mystery, but it also makes the game unnecessarily confusing, especially early on. Performance and stability can be uneven as well. Nothing game-breaking for me, but enough roughness to remind you that this isn’t a polished experience. Combined with the difficulty, it can make frustration build faster than it probably should.

solid table top battle game. It really scratches the war hammer old world itch. Really enjoy this game. Scratches the war strategy board game itch that we have. 
It has a simplistic gameplay approach and once you understand the core 'mechanics'/rules of the game, every battle becomes a dance of millimetres to get the best positioning. Every mistake is punished and those hail mary plays can come off with some luck. 

However, with every game that includes RNG - there are some sour moments. 
Supposedly the dice are completely random but based off of my decent amount of gameplay I can attest otherwise. I get lucky at times, no doubt. However, the AI consistently rolls higher than me, whilst my saving throws are consistently terrible. 
For example, when recently completing an empire playthrough with an imperial dreadnought, my 'tank' consistently rolled 1s on its saving throw of 2+ throughout the entire run. Either I am incredibly unlucky or something isn't right. I would love to say I was just unlucky this run, however, I am constantly finding scenarios where my rolls should mathematically favour me but instead I end up losing 2 knights to flanked slingers...

I might be paranoid about the dice rolling but seeing some other reviews I am confident that the AI has some advantages, which feels cheap. 

Otherwise, I really enjoy this game and will continue to play it (with moderate breaks to control my frustrations) and I will even add some DLC to support the devs and spice up the gameplay. 
Would love to see more units added, more maps and of course more Acts so I can have my OP war band smash through endless armies! These things will surely add many many more hours of gameplay to my future.

I cannot say enough nice things about this game. It's available for free on PC, Android, and iOs. As far as I can tell, all versions contain the same content. The base game is free, with DLC factions being reasonably priced. Even without owning the DLC, I can still battle against it. This gives me a sort of "test drive" against these units. I can already tell which I am interested in buying and which I am not.

The game itself does a wonderful job of breaking everything down. Any skill I don't understand I can mouse over (or tap on mobile) to see what it does. The army builder does a great job of letting you know whether or not your army is illegal (as in over capacity or over the points limit). You can see all dice rolls and all associated modifiers regarding them.

The mini-campaign slowly unlocks extra units as you play. This gives a neat incentive to try all the factions out. I just wish we were allowed more than 1 "save file". The rogue-lite boosts to units and gear and branching paths helps lend a sense of purpose to the game,. not just random playing.

Battles can take as little as 10 minutes. Perfect while on the toilet or my lunchbreak.

I'm a big fan of Return To Castle Wolfenstein, and it's my childhood game.

RealRTCW is a huge mod, and a lot of effort was put into it; however, it's not a definitive way to play RTCW. It's not that great tbh, and there's a lot of wasted potential.

[b]The new enemies don't add much to the game.[/b] The first one is the dog, which is great in concept but not much so in execution, as it's not nearly as dangerous, fast, or [i]annoying[/i] as it should be. The priest appears in a few places and, well, he's just there shooting lightning, I guess. Robodog with venom looks silly.

[b]The AI is not much improved compared to the base game.[/b] In fact, I think it's kinda downgraded. It's way easier, actually. What I noticed is that when AI doesn't have an available ranged attack (like loopers) AND the player is in an unreachable spot, the AI attempts to hide behind obstacles. I also noticed that the soldiers don't kick back grenades anymore, which was a fun feature of the base game. Overall, soldiers are not very aggressive.

[b]The new weapons are great but unbalanced.[/b]
 You have three 9mm SMGs now and one of them is clearly superior to the others, which kinda also a feature of the base game, altho there are only two 9mm SMGs.

 The revolver is not nearly as powerful as a revolver should be; it's pretty underwhelming.

 Kar98 is only useful with a scope and for very long distances.

 The shotgun is TOO POWERFUL [spoiler]I've never thought I'd ever have to say anything like that ever lol[/spoiler].

 The gas nade is bugged and therefore not very usable.

 Lots of wasted weapon slots: the luger and silenced luger are two separate weapons, and so are the colt and akimbo colts. Pistols don't have an alt-fire like in the base game to switch them into other mode/variant.

 Overall, there are too many weapons that are not worth using. Super weapons like the tesla gun would've benefitted from new particle effects. Separate fire rates are unnecessary for automatic rifles; just give them the maximum one, and that's it. I actually have a script to switch between fire rates instead of using separate buttons for firing, and it's much more convenient. The knife and sniper rifles could also use the same button for throw/scoping in. There's really no need for three buttons.

[b]The gun play is pretty much the same.[/b] I mean, there's an option for bullet drop/realistic ballistics, but it doesn't work for me for or at least I didn't notice the difference. There are changes as I described in the section about the AI, but they're not all that substantial. I wouldn't really call it "Overhauled gunplay".

[b]Some achievements require playing Dark Army Uprising and ET Singleplayer, but neither of these mods is in the spirit of RTCW.[/b] There was clearly a lot of work put into those, but they're not very good tbh. ET Singleplayer is practically a different game[spoiler], a call of duty game[/spoiler]. Dark Army Uprising is all over the place, the cutscenes are annoying, and the crypt is too big. Its only good idea is the flamethrower proto soldier and how it ambushes the player, that was actually amazing.

[b]This mod is definitely worth playing.[/b] There are a lot of good things I can say about it, such as great weapon sounds and animations, as well as modern features such as wide screen support. Some areas were expanded like the way you can get a shotgun in the first village is neat. The elevator sequence in the rocket launch mission is innovative.

To conclude, I want to say that I hope this mod will reach the greatness it deserves.

[h2]Review:[/h2] ✅

RealRTCW is a Labor of love from the developers Wolfmap, RTCWCoop Team, and Dark Matter Productions, with an extremely well done design of the original game Return to castle Wolfenstein with better textures, Models, Weapons, & sound design, making this a game BEGGING to be played by long time fans, There is also DLC content like the "Supporter pack" "German voice pack" & "Agency weapon pack" which gives you more customized weapons for the main Protangonists Arsenal, the German voice pack is for those wanting to feel like your facing a real German Nazi spoken in there language, you can't miss a Mod like this, this is a MUST PLAY if you've already played the original game, It's that good. And with continued support from the developers, there's also a mode in the game called "Survival" a brand new mode which pits the player against waves of on coming enemies, you pick up power ups like Quad damage & Haste which increase speed.

[*] Misc Improvements and Fixes

You definitely want to go and check out RealRTCW!

Amazingly deep strategy game. Every turn has important information to engage with and every map can be unique from the amazing planet gen engine. Must play.

It's by far the best 4X game I've ever played. The game is indeed a bit difficult to start playing, but it's not as hard as people say. I recommend watching the DasTactic tutorial and some let's play videos, and you'll be ready to play for the first time.  

To play, you don't need to delve too deeply into the mechanics the first time and play on easy mode. As you get the hang of it, you'll learn about other mechanics, and the game will truly open up to all its complexity.
And that's where the game shines. It has several interconnected mechanics that form a complete game. It has that "just one more turn" effect that hooks you and you don't even notice the time passing.

I recommend the game even at full price and am looking forward to the new DLC which promises to improve the game even further.

This game is what most 4x want to be-- a game that actually makes you feel like you're running some kind of empire; managing your staff, planning research and production, all while fighting wars and scooping up minor regimes.

The world generation options are incredible and the game has extremely high replay value because each run feels quite different.

The research system is exactly what I want in other games-- it splits research into distinct pathways and groups, and you choose the allocation of your bureaucracy across them, without having to fiddle with 'research this tech or that', the research paths work on their own.

The combat is good, pretty easy to understand, and gives you a lot of options.

The supply system is a bit hard to understand at first, but once you realize how to get trucks and trains going it's mostly a matter of checking up on your infrastructure from time to time as you build out instead of being tedious.

My only complaints about the game come down to UX decisions, and the lack of polish. Well and the fact that this is basically a WWII ground combat engine being stretched pretty thin for the Sci Fi setting. 

Some might find the leader management tedious but I find it to be the missing link for most 4x games.

TL;DR

THE GOOD

- You feel like you are running an actual empire, not just playing a game
- Combat and logistics are excellent
- The world feels quite logical and alive
- Tons of replay value

THE BAD

- UX is terrible, and confusing, and not explained well
- Multiplayer uses an extremely antiquated system instead of host/client
- Kinda ugly in some respects
- Not all text was translated into english correctly

MAYBE GOOD/MAYBE BAD

- Games can be LONG on the normal map size+

------------------------

This game ported to a more modern engine with a proper UX design and networking multiplayer would be nirvana for me personally.

Overall, I recommend it just because it brings so many good ideas to the genre, and that outweighs some of the negatives of the engine.

I'm not exactly sure how to praise an addiction. This is not a game, it's a safe space for freaks like me to feel alive. 
I think about policy changes when I wake up and optimal troop movement before sleep takes me. 
I think about how immersive the planet generation is, the struggle to paint the map and engulf everything in your colors. 
The AI is possibly the best I ever encountered. It knows how to flank, it ruins your carefully laid plans. It takes advantage of carelessness. I love it.
It's a story generator. Every time you click 'start new game' your get another epic tale. 
It may appear intimidating (I avoided it for so long because of it) but once you learn a few systems, everything else falls into place. Its difficulty comes mainly from the learning curve, not gameplay.

Now, the UI looks like it's stuck in the 90's, the portraits are hilarious and it is missing some QOL features like having a 'select next unit' button, but nothing can detract from the complete joy Shadow Empire brings me.

PS: What I wouldn't give to have friends that are as deeply interested as me for these kinds of games...
extremely complex and I've had to restart a few times but I'm having fun with the learning process, definitely seems like the type of game I could sink lots of hours into.

This game....

Is the anti-version of the idea of "one more turn".  I have never spent more hours on the same turn, wondering if I have made the best possible choice with every decision/turn that proceeds forward of that moment.  Do you play a 100 turns in and afterwards ask yourself would I now do these 100 turns over again knowing what I know now?  As uncommon as that might play, this is the kind of game that will embellish that end.

This game has a bunch of other gamish mechanic's that you may have enjoyed.  Did you like Civ 3?  Did you like CK 2?  Did you like vicky 1 & 2?  How about Pride of Nations?  This game without any measure of imitation nabbed some of the best mechainics out these other titles and created its own lore.  

The dev should be supported when comparables fail to compare.  If the game gets better if Naval units get added, great, if it ruins it, then no.  This engine is interesting and I hope the dev continues development of content.vn

I just won my first game on my 3rd or 4th try. I'm not saying that to get applauded, I played it on easy settings seasonned by embarrassing saves scumming. I say that because I just bought the game last week and, for me, to win a game this long (126 rounds - at least 60 hours) in less than a week means that the game is excellent.
In fact, when I saw it on a surprisong good call by the youtube algorithm, I was at the beginning of a week long vacation where gaming all day was already the plan. 
But enough about me, what about the game.
First, it's this heroin-like addiction of just one more turn. The more your Regime grows and your troops become ever so badass, the more you crave to press that damned "end your turn" button.
You have exploration, war, tons of techs and units, I mean... you can ride in the desert with your bikers wielding lasr rifles! There are cards with various powers, diplomacy, city building, BOMBERS, logistic and so many other cool things. It's neverending.
Are there things that could be better?
For sure, mostly QOL stuff, AI being a bit stupid sometime (as all games), an incredibly steep learning curve which means it is highly satisfying when you finally get it, but it's all minor.
For me, this game is the closest I've been to the fun I had as a kid, playing with plastic toy soldiers in the backyard.

As a decades-long 4X nut, I absolutely can't get enough of this game. The strategic depth is unlike anything ever made. I've heard many players claim this is the best 4X game ever made - and they are absolutely right! From resource management, to population control, to combat logistics - this one has it all and does it best. Steep learning curve, but once you get a handle on it, this one will get a handle on you. If you're a 4X fan and don't have Shadow Empire in your library, your library is incomplete.

I did not know that this game included or was going to include Gen AI art when I bought it. If I did, I would have skipped it. Gen AI LLMs are trained on stolen art without giving compensation or credit to the original artists, which is indefensible when included in a commercial product.

If this doesn't bother you, the game itself is easily the most complex and deep strategy games I have ever played. $40 is a bit steep (considering the dev saved a lot on artist fees), but this thing has a ton of value if you can snatch it for 50% off.

I have a lot of other good things to say about my experience with this title, but I'm hungry now so bye.
This is a great game. Every game is different. It is very challenging.

Are you kidding me? How else to LARP distant future post-apocalyptic societal reset planetary conquest?
I SAID LARP not like actually being in the 4th or 5th reset... Whiskey Tango Foxtrot is this timeline?

IF WE CAN GET OVER THE CLUNKY HIGH LEARNING CURVE UI, WE'RE GOOD.

Well at the bare beginning you can 'design' your planet by rolling its features. You can as easily make a lush jungle moon hydroid as you could make a barren rock. Or finding a middle ground; a desert steppe planet with scarce resources you have to fight everyone over. There are loads of mutators and no planet is ever the same.
The map is an hexagonal grid and movement is turn-based AP system. Each hex is approx 200km wide and map size = planet size. You get to roll for this and more such as atmosphere and biosphere. After generating a planet you get to populate it and promptly apocalypse them.
Now we create our post-apocalyptic warlord regime, pick your leading ideologies:
democracy - meritocracy - autocracy
commerce - government - enforcement
heart - mind - fist

If you do not pick the right ones, you WILL endure the pain of managing and keeping those scores stable within your rule, all while fighting your political parties who will inevitably try to concentrate power through demands and leader hoarding so as to bring 'stability' or ideology stagnation.

You also get to wage WAR, which is the main focus of this game. War for planetary conquest. Hurrah.
Depending on which 'ministries' or 'directorates' you've founded your government gains different capabilities through these organizations, such as Internal Affairs, Economic Council, Military Research, Model Design and a load of others. All of these and more have one but one purpose only and that is to sustain the war machine YEAAAAAAAHH DAKKA DAKKA DAKKA, we humans are space orcs after all...

Let's say you've founded the Model Design council: It will allow you to discover, research and design new units. It's such a simple, brilliant mechanic, but it is one of the things that make this game stand out. Making your own units enables you to specialize each model or make them great generalists or whatever. There are tons of technologies that open up different armaments and loadouts. You can also make a model as cheap or expensive as you'd like. You can design counters to other units.
So, for example: Lets design a new light tank
First we pick a caliber (25mm - 40mm - 60mm) 
Then it's armor's thickness (5mm - 25mm - 50 mm)
Finally it's engine size and type (light, med, heavy, double heavy, etc / diesel, electric, nuclear, etc)
We have to make sure the engine can carry it's weight or you'll get an AP movement penalty.
Then our Model Council Director gets to actually develop it, throughout the following turns they will make a series of RNG rolls to determine the vehicle stats. This process is affected by their skills (technician, inventor, improvisation) and might result in an excellent model or a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ one.
You can economize and make a 25mm gun, 5mm armor, light diesel engine tank that cost 45 metal and 30 industrial points and nail the rolls and get a super effective anti-infantry light tank. Your director could also fumble the rolls of a 60mm gun, 50mm armor, heavy diesel engine and end up with a useless -50% AP movement metal hunk of junk that is 2.5x times the effort and resources to manufacture.
The important thing is to keep ahead of the tech curve, since your enemies will also do this and try to develop counters for your units, so it's a constant R&D battle over who's the top dog carrying a big stick.

Then we have logistics. Oh the logistics in this game... I recommend anyone new playing this gem to PLEASE PLEASE start out with midcore logistics initial mutator. Or you will NOT like the game.
Hardcore (standard) logistics is at least a couple more minutes each turn to manage traffic signs, which is in itself a complex and in-depth mechanic. The purpose of logistics in this game is to transport resources, personnel and possibly units along your logistics network. You can build roads, upgrade them, truck stations and also railroads. If you can't supply an hex, you can't field violence ergo no sovereign rights over the land; you will eventually lose it. So we carry stuff around in trucks for our troops, industry and people. You can also cut off the enemy's supply too.

[spoiler]SECRET SERVICE COUNCIL TIP: You can shadily kill your own leaders if they step out of line.[/spoiler]

These are just examples of how things usually work in this game. It is one of those games you have to literally read a 500 page manual. I did and I don't regret it a single bit. It is also one of those games that evolves slowly but deliberately, since it's been X years since release and how many DLCs does it have? Just a single one with another one in the oven. The graphics are late 90s overtime worker sneakily playing MS farming simulator / SimTower on his boss' work comp which had 1280 resolution (a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ for the era). You can mod it and the forums are full of lovely and colorful characters and it's the definition of small but passionate group of friendly fans. I think they all ride motorcycles too lmao

All in all 9.5/10 will nostalgia autism minmax design airplanes again and again forever
If this game was made in 1997 it would be considered one of the best ever made.
Would rather play this than HOI4

*sigh* Another day another pointless ideological war fought against indie devs who barely have enough time as is for development.

Everyone is entitled to their opinions but keep the review of a game strictly to the merits of the game. Shadow Empire was a unique and innovative game with lots of depth before it used generative AI in assets, and it will be afterwards. Graphics has almost no bearing on a strategy game anyways. If you want flashy human-made graphics go pay $100 for a PDX title and all of its DLCs.

But if you're only making a review on this many years old game because the dev went against the current luddite trend of hating everything that is AI: grow up. His blog covers his reasoning and its pretty solid. Some of you have never worked on a project or this scale and it shows.

First Impressions after 45 minutes (there are more below):

1) There is a 3 hour fan-made youtube tutorial that I am fairly certain I have to drag myself through just to get the gameplay basics down.

2) The UI is super overwhelmingly cluttered.  Hence the need for the tutorial before even feeling ready to play.

3) BUT, I did try opening the game and was immediately blown away by how UNREADABLE the fonts are!

4) There are discussions in the forums about how bad the fonts are, and the dev actually made the comment that people should just use a 4K monitor.  Are you OUT OF YOUR MIND???  I'm playing on a laptop, so this comment made me want to refund the game, just out of sheer contempt for the dev.  But instead, I tried following the suggestions here: https://forums.matrixgames.com/viewtopic.php?p=4662098#p4662098 which helped a little.  Still the dev really needs to look into making the game work for all displays using scalable fonts since most people don't use 4K monitors.  This docks the game's reasonable price down to about $8 in my book.  What a pile of sh....

And I haven't even started playing the game yet.  If you can't read the fonts what's the point?  I'll try going forward, but just keep in mind, if you aren't playing on a 4K monitor, you will want to think twice before buying this game.


-----------------------

Impression after 90 minutes:

The fonts and lack of a UI scaling slider is inconvenient, but the GFX options panel when selected at 1650x1050 (or whatever it was I picked) and with 7/8 checkbox turned on, is - at least for me, acceptable.  It would be nice to have more control, especially for a game that is SO heavy with UI elements, but I think this is a start.  I'm still tempted to experiment with the fonts to try to get things a little better.

Also as the dev said below, I did not quite quote him correctly.  This whole "fonts are too small" subject, I mean... really?  Nobody's got time to fiddle with that stuff when they can be playing games instead.  Just make it stupid simple and nobody needs to ask anymore.  But this is how things are now.

The dev is responsive, and seems to be genuinely wanting to improve things.

Its about time I reviewed this game!
As a fan of 4x; Stellaris and Hearts of Iron 4 as examples, this game scratches both itches at one time! I love this game, building from nothing, guiding my country as it has ups and downs! The game is versatile and stupidly repeatable! Don't like your current game, make a completely new one from scratch! Or you can even load the last planet to get a different build or different starting location. THE REPLAY-ABILITY IS UNMATCHED!

The graphics are stupidly simple and sometimes just comical when it comes to what 3D there is, but it makes up for ALL of it with how deep and invested the gameplay mechanics is! If you want the best mechanics of HOI IV and Stellaris packed into one, this is your game!

And don't get me started on the complex yet common-sense nature of the logistics. This game slaps you in the face only once, and that's when you first start playing it, trying to learn it is a challenge worth making! It doesn't hold your hand, but as you learn the system in a slow buildup of your nation, mistakes are made yet not ridiculously punishing when you start out, its fun to actually learn everything for yourself! Explore your menus.

I'll be honest, this is very much a spreadsheet simulator, but one that makes you feel ridiculously engaged, and you can focus on what you wanna focus on and not get beat by a stick by the game! (unless you are literally being beat over the head by a stick by the game! Which can happen, just not immediately)

the game only really starts to get challenging if you just don't learn things by around turn 45 or 50 in any game, which can take time to get to if you are pouring over the many mechanics (as you should be.)

All-in-all: 8/10 solid!
Graphics: 2/10 (But no one bought this game for this, nor was it made for it.)
Gameplay: 8-9/10!
UI: 7/10 Solid (spreadsheet sim, but the UI is easy to navigate once you explore as you should. All the info is laid out before you what you need)
Mechanics: SOLID 9/10 Would LOVE to see a sequel that improves on everything else, and perhaps the mechanics a bit more. However, with the Oceania expansion, it gets confusing and uncertain, I often just ignore it unless I can get a planet where the oceans are more important to my gameplay.

For those of us that want to conquer the world and feel like we actually were important in the creation and building of that world, this game is a SOLID MUST HAVE!

For those without patience to learn or pour over spreadsheets, trust me the game is worth it for the more strategic combat gameplay and management, you may still learn about the other mechanics in the mean time! Otherwise its just not for you :(
a turn-based 4X wargame at it's best from a great developer that's still be developed and improved and added to still to this day from the power house of VR Designs
Extremely good game, and the AI use isn't that bad. What matters is that it's an extremely good 4x game with deep mechanics you won't find anywhere else.

One of the best wargames I have ever played. The logistic system makes it unique and realistic: you can't just spam units everywhere and send them to the opposite side of the world. Without the backcountry's line of supply they are shortly dead.
The thoughful map/world generation gives very good replayability.
The learning curve is steep. It took me 70 hours to get familiar with the concepts of the game, but still after 100 hrs there are a lot of things to figure out.

One thing I would definitely improve: more variety to the music soundtracks could further help with the immersion to the game. Other than this, the game is a masterpiece!

As someone who has played rts, grand strategy and 4x game my whole life I have to say no other games even comes close to what this game has to offer. It scratches that itch of managing a nation through endless struggles and problems to solve with endless replay ability. The game has a fairly large learning curve so I recommend watching youtube tutorials and playthroughs. Also Google, google, google! Curious about something google it. Learning the game is half the fun and coming to an understanding of a new mechanic and being able to implement it to better your empire is very satisfying.
 
Overall I highly recommend this game for any fan of the genre.

This is a good game, if you're willing to work for it. I had this game sitting in my library for years, I booted it up a few times but couldn't really make heads or tails of it all. Until I decided to make a very conscious effort to learn how it all works. And then it turned out to be a great game.

I broke out the manual and online guides and meticulously dove into all the topics this game covers. This is not a wargame, not a standard 4X, not a grand strategy game. It's a little bit of everything. So that's why the game feels disjointed to a lot of gamers. It's a couple of things at once and only by closely examining the systems and figuring out how it connects can you get enjoyment out of the game. Sure, you can assign a leader. Sure, you can stack units and surround an enemy and mix and match until you get good odds. But understanding why this leader is good or why that attack will succeed will massively increase your enjoyment of the game. It makes you able to consciously play the game and make good choices.

And then it all opens up. Shadow Empire is a game of adaptability, of making the best of each new game. While you can choose what kind of planet you want to play on, starting conditions differ wildly. Not rerolling at sticking with a game and trying to make it work is where the real fun lies. Shadow Empire is like a toolbox. If you know what each individual tool does, you can pretty much fix everything. And the game requires you to learn every tool in the box. Not every problem is a nail and you need to understand more than just how the hammer works.

The fact that it is so complicated is the game's biggest strength, and its biggest problem. You need to learn it. The first 20 hours are not for leisurely gaming, they are for learning the basic concepts. They are for restarting so you can apply information you just learned. They are for reading parts of the manual so it will all start to make sense. They are for studying outside of the game for aother 20 hours on offline information. After those all those hours, you can start managing a nation in a way that no other game will have you do. Every choice matters. Every statistic has a tangible function (except for wittiness of leaders). Every report contains information you may at some point need. And because you took the time to learn it and understand its application, you will have a lot of fun strategizing and pondering your next move.

I don't know if the developer reads these reviews. But my hat's off to you. I think it is a masterpiece of brilliant complexity. I'm blown away by what it all does compared to other 4X and grand strategy games. Thanks for the fun so far. This will surely be a game that will stick with me for many years to come.

Honestly the best strategy game I've ever played. Everything going on has deep and relatively realistic mechanics. It's intimidating at first, but it only takes an hour or 2 to roleplay and see what happens. Maybe one day I'll know exactly how the mechanics work, but until then they do a good job mechanicing without my help.
I'm too much of a noob to comment on balancing or late game, but so far I'm absolutely loving it

Is it janky as hell? Yes

Does the UI need a rework or possibly an exorcism? Also yes.

Is is amazingly satisfying to play, presenting a complex and immersive challenge not quite like any other strategy game I've ever played? Heck yes.

Do I recommend it? Yes.
It takes a special kind of freak to like this game. But good news. I am that special kind of freak
Amazingly deep strategy game. Every turn has important information to engage with and every map can be unique from the amazing planet gen engine. Must play.

Very atmospheric to start with,
1000 layers of complexity

I dont care about AI art, it looks good and works.

Great game

Very difficult to learn and even more difficult to master. But if you invest a great deal of time, it's a very rewarding and enjoyable grand strategy experience.

At first, it feels like things happen for no reason like leaders rebelling, cities revolting, military units weak or dying, buildings outputting no resources. The game does a poor job of explaining what the causes are, but everything happens for a reason.

Leaders have their own tendencies toward ambition, corruption, heroism, and are part of factions with faction goals. Cities are affected by their governor, nearby enemies, population, public vs private sectors, rainfall, water pH, distance to the sun, atmosphere, planet characteristics, etc. Military units have design stats (driven by the design leader and research), and depend on complex logistics. AI cheats, but the player also has tools that AI does not.

The quantity of information is nearly overwhelming, but the massive pdf guide does explain most things. If you invest the time, few other strategy games can scratch the same itch.

It's early days for a game as large as this, but it has utterly grabbed me. I'll leave this initial review to counter some others, I might change it along the way when zeros appear after my play-time. 

An endevour like this just existing is fascinating to me and begs to be tried even just to see the simulation aspects. More importantly, I am constantly having fun and being on the edge of my seat and of control of my nation. The game is built up in a way that I'm organically learning. Every problem invites you to look around in the endless information for what you need. Often I discover new mechanics this way. The manual is great, and, believe it or not, very tight for its complexity. 

On UI, some people find it horrid, I find it wonderful, though not perfect. It took a while to understand how things work, but it has a very profesional, almost cockpit-like workflow. There's mostly multiple ways to find the same thing and I almost always find it quickly. I don't understand why BP isn't up there in the top bar with FP and PP and little stuff like this, but truth be told, it's no work to find it anyway. Once you know. You know. 

The UI looks great to me. Don't underestimate the immersive power of fonts. I miss when games dared to actually design UI instead of copy pasting mobile layouts. 

In summary, this game surprises me. I thought it'd be above my mental paygrade. I thought it'd be a bit on the dry side. I never thought I'd be sucked in like this. My initial impression is this is the most satisfying wargame system i've played. Beside that it's the first large scale 4x that has actually absorbed me. The only downside is that a lot of the rest of my library might be collecting dust for a bit.

Of all the 4x games I've played, this one feels the closest to actually managing a nation. The leader mechanics, the private/public economy, the logistics, it all makes everything feel so grounded. Definitely a learning cliff to get started (read the manual and watch some videos) but it's been well worth it

It's by far the best 4X game I've ever played. The game is indeed a bit difficult to start playing, but it's not as hard as people say. I recommend watching the DasTactic tutorial and some let's play videos, and you'll be ready to play for the first time.  

To play, you don't need to delve too deeply into the mechanics the first time and play on easy mode. As you get the hang of it, you'll learn about other mechanics, and the game will truly open up to all its complexity.
And that's where the game shines. It has several interconnected mechanics that form a complete game. It has that "just one more turn" effect that hooks you and you don't even notice the time passing.

I recommend the game even at full price and am looking forward to the new DLC which promises to improve the game even further.

This game is what most 4x want to be-- a game that actually makes you feel like you're running some kind of empire; managing your staff, planning research and production, all while fighting wars and scooping up minor regimes.

The world generation options are incredible and the game has extremely high replay value because each run feels quite different.

The research system is exactly what I want in other games-- it splits research into distinct pathways and groups, and you choose the allocation of your bureaucracy across them, without having to fiddle with 'research this tech or that', the research paths work on their own.

The combat is good, pretty easy to understand, and gives you a lot of options.

The supply system is a bit hard to understand at first, but once you realize how to get trucks and trains going it's mostly a matter of checking up on your infrastructure from time to time as you build out instead of being tedious.

My only complaints about the game come down to UX decisions, and the lack of polish. Well and the fact that this is basically a WWII ground combat engine being stretched pretty thin for the Sci Fi setting. 

Some might find the leader management tedious but I find it to be the missing link for most 4x games.

TL;DR

THE GOOD

- You feel like you are running an actual empire, not just playing a game
- Combat and logistics are excellent
- The world feels quite logical and alive
- Tons of replay value

THE BAD

- UX is terrible, and confusing, and not explained well
- Multiplayer uses an extremely antiquated system instead of host/client
- Kinda ugly in some respects
- Not all text was translated into english correctly

MAYBE GOOD/MAYBE BAD

- Games can be LONG on the normal map size+

------------------------

Shadow Empires is an amazingly complicated game. However, once you get into the flow of things, it becomes an excellent strategy experience, thanks to its incredible depth, multilayered management systems, and interconnected subsystems that all interact seamlessly with one another.

Somewhat recently they added AI generated content for images such as leader portraits and event screens with the option to turn it off (partially), but its still immensely disappointing to see it used regardless of any excuse that majority of players come up with. Still it is a fantastic game and it would be wrong of me to post a negative review simply because of that but it is something worth noting. Hopefully at some point in time with enough support the dev may be able to hire a few talented artists that could either re-imagine the original artwork style or come up with something new entirely.

I'm not exactly sure how to praise an addiction. This is not a game, it's a safe space for freaks like me to feel alive. 
I think about policy changes when I wake up and optimal troop movement before sleep takes me. 
I think about how immersive the planet generation is, the struggle to paint the map and engulf everything in your colors. 
The AI is possibly the best I ever encountered. It knows how to flank, it ruins your carefully laid plans. It takes advantage of carelessness. I love it.
It's a story generator. Every time you click 'start new game' your get another epic tale. 
It may appear intimidating (I avoided it for so long because of it) but once you learn a few systems, everything else falls into place. Its difficulty comes mainly from the learning curve, not gameplay.

Now, the UI looks like it's stuck in the 90's, the portraits are hilarious and it is missing some QOL features like having a 'select next unit' button, but nothing can detract from the complete joy Shadow Empire brings me.

PS: What I wouldn't give to have friends that are as deeply interested as me for these kinds of games...

I just won my first game on my 3rd or 4th try. I'm not saying that to get applauded, I played it on easy settings seasonned by embarrassing saves scumming. I say that because I just bought the game last week and, for me, to win a game this long (126 rounds - at least 60 hours) in less than a week means that the game is excellent.
In fact, when I saw it on a surprisong good call by the youtube algorithm, I was at the beginning of a week long vacation where gaming all day was already the plan. 
But enough about me, what about the game.
First, it's this heroin-like addiction of just one more turn. The more your Regime grows and your troops become ever so badass, the more you crave to press that damned "end your turn" button.
You have exploration, war, tons of techs and units, I mean... you can ride in the desert with your bikers wielding lasr rifles! There are cards with various powers, diplomacy, city building, BOMBERS, logistic and so many other cool things. It's neverending.
Are there things that could be better?
For sure, mostly QOL stuff, AI being a bit stupid sometime (as all games), an incredibly steep learning curve which means it is highly satisfying when you finally get it, but it's all minor.

Are you kidding me? How else to LARP distant future post-apocalyptic societal reset planetary conquest?
I SAID LARP not like actually being in the 4th or 5th reset... Whiskey Tango Foxtrot is this timeline?

IF WE CAN GET OVER THE CLUNKY HIGH LEARNING CURVE UI, WE'RE GOOD.

Well at the bare beginning you can 'design' your planet by rolling its features. You can as easily make a lush jungle moon hydroid as you could make a barren rock. Or finding a middle ground; a desert steppe planet with scarce resources you have to fight everyone over. There are loads of mutators and no planet is ever the same.
The map is an hexagonal grid and movement is turn-based AP system. Each hex is approx 200km wide and map size = planet size. You get to roll for this and more such as atmosphere and biosphere. After generating a planet you get to populate it and promptly apocalypse them.
Now we create our post-apocalyptic warlord regime, pick your leading ideologies:
democracy - meritocracy - autocracy
commerce - government - enforcement
heart - mind - fist

If you do not pick the right ones, you WILL endure the pain of managing and keeping those scores stable within your rule, all while fighting your political parties who will inevitably try to concentrate power through demands and leader hoarding so as to bring 'stability' or ideology stagnation.

You also get to wage WAR, which is the main focus of this game. War for planetary conquest. Hurrah.
Depending on which 'ministries' or 'directorates' you've founded your government gains different capabilities through these organizations, such as Internal Affairs, Economic Council, Military Research, Model Design and a load of others. All of these and more have one but one purpose only and that is to sustain the war machine YEAAAAAAAHH DAKKA DAKKA DAKKA, we humans are space orcs after all...

Let's say you've founded the Model Design council: It will allow you to discover, research and design new units. It's such a simple, brilliant mechanic, but it is one of the things that make this game stand out. Making your own units enables you to specialize each model or make them great generalists or whatever. There are tons of technologies that open up different armaments and loadouts. You can also make a model as cheap or expensive as you'd like. You can design counters to other units.
So, for example: Lets design a new light tank
First we pick a caliber (25mm - 40mm - 60mm) 
Then it's armor's thickness (5mm - 25mm - 50 mm)
Finally it's engine size and type (light, med, heavy, double heavy, etc / diesel, electric, nuclear, etc)
We have to make sure the engine can carry it's weight or you'll get an AP movement penalty.
Then our Model Council Director gets to actually develop it, throughout the following turns they will make a series of RNG rolls to determine the vehicle stats. This process is affected by their skills (technician, inventor, improvisation) and might result in an excellent model or a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ one.
You can economize and make a 25mm gun, 5mm armor, light diesel engine tank that cost 45 metal and 30 industrial points and nail the rolls and get a super effective anti-infantry light tank. Your director could also fumble the rolls of a 60mm gun, 50mm armor, heavy diesel engine and end up with a useless -50% AP movement metal hunk of junk that is 2.5x times the effort and resources to manufacture.
The important thing is to keep ahead of the tech curve, since your enemies will also do this and try to develop counters for your units, so it's a constant R&D battle over who's the top dog carrying a big stick.

Then we have logistics. Oh the logistics in this game... I recommend anyone new playing this gem to PLEASE PLEASE start out with midcore logistics initial mutator. Or you will NOT like the game.
Hardcore (standard) logistics is at least a couple more minutes each turn to manage traffic signs, which is in itself a complex and in-depth mechanic. The purpose of logistics in this game is to transport resources, personnel and possibly units along your logistics network. You can build roads, upgrade them, truck stations and also railroads. If you can't supply an hex, you can't field violence ergo no sovereign rights over the land; you will eventually lose it. So we carry stuff around in trucks for our troops, industry and people. You can also cut off the enemy's supply too.

[spoiler]SECRET SERVICE COUNCIL TIP: You can shadily kill your own leaders if they step out of line.[/spoiler]

These are just examples of how things usually work in this game. It is one of those games you have to literally read a 500 page manual. I did and I don't regret it a single bit. It is also one of those games that evolves slowly but deliberately, since it's been X years since release and how many DLCs does it have? Just a single one with another one in the oven. The graphics are late 90s overtime worker sneakily playing MS farming simulator / SimTower on his boss' work comp which had 1280 resolution (a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ for the era). You can mod it and the forums are full of lovely and colorful characters and it's the definition of small but passionate group of friendly fans. I think they all ride motorcycles too lmao

All in all 9.5/10 will nostalgia autism minmax design airplanes again and again forever

Everyone is entitled to their opinions but keep the review of a game strictly to the merits of the game. Shadow Empire was a unique and innovative game with lots of depth before it used generative AI in assets, and it will be afterwards. Graphics has almost no bearing on a strategy game anyways. If you want flashy human-made graphics go pay $100 for a PDX title and all of its DLCs.

But if you're only making a review on this many years old game because the dev went against the current luddite trend of hating everything that is AI: grow up. His blog covers his reasoning and its pretty solid. Some of you have never worked on a project or this scale and it shows.

First Impressions after 45 minutes (there are more below):

1) There is a 3 hour fan-made youtube tutorial that I am fairly certain I have to drag myself through just to get the gameplay basics down.

2) The UI is super overwhelmingly cluttered.  Hence the need for the tutorial before even feeling ready to play.

3) BUT, I did try opening the game and was immediately blown away by how UNREADABLE the fonts are!

4) There are discussions in the forums about how bad the fonts are, and the dev actually made the comment that people should just use a 4K monitor.  Are you OUT OF YOUR MIND???  I'm playing on a laptop, so this comment made me want to refund the game, just out of sheer contempt for the dev.  But instead, I tried following the suggestions here: https://forums.matrixgames.com/viewtopic.php?p=4662098#p4662098 which helped a little.  Still the dev really needs to look into making the game work for all displays using scalable fonts since most people don't use 4K monitors.  This docks the game's reasonable price down to about $8 in my book.  What a pile of sh....

And I haven't even started playing the game yet.  If you can't read the fonts what's the point?  I'll try going forward, but just keep in mind, if you aren't playing on a 4K monitor, you will want to think twice before buying this game.


-----------------------

Impression after 90 minutes:

The fonts and lack of a UI scaling slider is inconvenient, but the GFX options panel when selected at 1650x1050 (or whatever it was I picked) and with 7/8 checkbox turned on, is - at least for me, acceptable.  It would be nice to have more control, especially for a game that is SO heavy with UI elements, but I think this is a start.  I'm still tempted to experiment with the fonts to try to get things a little better.

Also as the dev said below, I did not quite quote him correctly.  This whole "fonts are too small" subject, I mean... really?  Nobody's got time to fiddle with that stuff when they can be playing games instead.  Just make it stupid simple and nobody needs to ask anymore.  But this is how things are now.

The dev is responsive, and seems to be genuinely wanting to improve things.

Its about time I reviewed this game!
As a fan of 4x; Stellaris and Hearts of Iron 4 as examples, this game scratches both itches at one time! I love this game, building from nothing, guiding my country as it has ups and downs! The game is versatile and stupidly repeatable! Don't like your current game, make a completely new one from scratch! Or you can even load the last planet to get a different build or different starting location. THE REPLAY-ABILITY IS UNMATCHED!

The graphics are stupidly simple and sometimes just comical when it comes to what 3D there is, but it makes up for ALL of it with how deep and invested the gameplay mechanics is! If you want the best mechanics of HOI IV and Stellaris packed into one, this is your game!

And don't get me started on the complex yet common-sense nature of the logistics. This game slaps you in the face only once, and that's when you first start playing it, trying to learn it is a challenge worth making! It doesn't hold your hand, but as you learn the system in a slow buildup of your nation, mistakes are made yet not ridiculously punishing when you start out, its fun to actually learn everything for yourself! Explore your menus.

I'll be honest, this is very much a spreadsheet simulator, but one that makes you feel ridiculously engaged, and you can focus on what you wanna focus on and not get beat by a stick by the game! (unless you are literally being beat over the head by a stick by the game! Which can happen, just not immediately)

the game only really starts to get challenging if you just don't learn things by around turn 45 or 50 in any game, which can take time to get to if you are pouring over the many mechanics (as you should be.)

All-in-all: 8/10 solid!
Graphics: 2/10 (But no one bought this game for this, nor was it made for it.)
Gameplay: 8-9/10!
UI: 7/10 Solid (spreadsheet sim, but the UI is easy to navigate once you explore as you should. All the info is laid out before you what you need)
Mechanics: SOLID 9/10 Would LOVE to see a sequel that improves on everything else, and perhaps the mechanics a bit more. However, with the Oceania expansion, it gets confusing and uncertain, I often just ignore it unless I can get a planet where the oceans are more important to my gameplay.

For those of us that want to conquer the world and feel like we actually were important in the creation and building of that world, this game is a SOLID MUST HAVE!

For those without patience to learn or pour over spreadsheets, trust me the game is worth it for the more strategic combat gameplay and management, you may still learn about the other mechanics in the mean time! Otherwise its just not for you :(
a turn-based 4X wargame at it's best from a great developer that's still be developed and improved and added to still to this day from the power house of VR Designs
Extremely good game, and the AI use isn't that bad. What matters is that it's an extremely good 4x game with deep mechanics you won't find anywhere else.

One of the best wargames I have ever played. The logistic system makes it unique and realistic: you can't just spam units everywhere and send them to the opposite side of the world. Without the backcountry's line of supply they are shortly dead.
The thoughful map/world generation gives very good replayability.
The learning curve is steep. It took me 70 hours to get familiar with the concepts of the game, but still after 100 hrs there are a lot of things to figure out.

One thing I would definitely improve: more variety to the music soundtracks could further help with the immersion to the game. Other than this, the game is a masterpiece!

As someone who has played rts, grand strategy and 4x game my whole life I have to say no other games even comes close to what this game has to offer. It scratches that itch of managing a nation through endless struggles and problems to solve with endless replay ability. The game has a fairly large learning curve so I recommend watching youtube tutorials and playthroughs. Also Google, google, google! Curious about something google it. Learning the game is half the fun and coming to an understanding of a new mechanic and being able to implement it to better your empire is very satisfying.
 
Overall I highly recommend this game for any fan of the genre.

This is a good game, if you're willing to work for it. I had this game sitting in my library for years, I booted it up a few times but couldn't really make heads or tails of it all. Until I decided to make a very conscious effort to learn how it all works. And then it turned out to be a great game.

I broke out the manual and online guides and meticulously dove into all the topics this game covers. This is not a wargame, not a standard 4X, not a grand strategy game. It's a little bit of everything. So that's why the game feels disjointed to a lot of gamers. It's a couple of things at once and only by closely examining the systems and figuring out how it connects can you get enjoyment out of the game. Sure, you can assign a leader. Sure, you can stack units and surround an enemy and mix and match until you get good odds. But understanding why this leader is good or why that attack will succeed will massively increase your enjoyment of the game. It makes you able to consciously play the game and make good choices.

And then it all opens up. Shadow Empire is a game of adaptability, of making the best of each new game. While you can choose what kind of planet you want to play on, starting conditions differ wildly. Not rerolling at sticking with a game and trying to make it work is where the real fun lies. Shadow Empire is like a toolbox. If you know what each individual tool does, you can pretty much fix everything. And the game requires you to learn every tool in the box. Not every problem is a nail and you need to understand more than just how the hammer works.

The fact that it is so complicated is the game's biggest strength, and its biggest problem. You need to learn it. The first 20 hours are not for leisurely gaming, they are for learning the basic concepts. They are for restarting so you can apply information you just learned. They are for reading parts of the manual so it will all start to make sense. They are for studying outside of the game for aother 20 hours on offline information. After those all those hours, you can start managing a nation in a way that no other game will have you do. Every choice matters. Every statistic has a tangible function (except for wittiness of leaders). Every report contains information you may at some point need. And because you took the time to learn it and understand its application, you will have a lot of fun strategizing and pondering your next move.

I don't know if the developer reads these reviews. But my hat's off to you. I think it is a masterpiece of brilliant complexity. I'm blown away by what it all does compared to other 4X and grand strategy games. Thanks for the fun so far. This will surely be a game that will stick with me for many years to come.

Honestly the best strategy game I've ever played. Everything going on has deep and relatively realistic mechanics. Is is amazingly satisfying to play, presenting a complex and immersive challenge not quite like any other strategy game I've ever played? Heck yes.

Do I recommend it? Yes.

To simplify this review, in it's current state (Version 0.49) Back of Space is not worth the 10$ price tag. It lacks several features that its predecessor (OldTv) had.

I am a huge fan of the OLDTV franchise (if you can even call it that). I still stand by the fact that my best purchase ever was COLDTV (2$ DLC for OLDTV). When I heard of the release of Back of Space, I had what can only be described as a minor heart attack. 

OLDTV is free, Back of Space is 10$ and has less features than OLDTV, so I reccomend at least trying out the predecessor before getting this one. 

I have faith in the developers, but if you haven't played OLDTV and this is your first experience with this "franchise", play OLDTV first, you won't regret it.

At the time of writing, I'd rate Back of Space Version 0.49 around a 7/10, as long as the game gets consistent updates, I'm sure that it will soon overgrow its predecessor. 

Note: I noticed that the latest update added the settings menu, yet upon opening the game, there is no visible settings menu for me, so unless my computer is not loading something correctly, or the UI is messed up, then I don't exactly know what to do about the latest update. Bug or not, I still commend the devs for being active with the community and I look forward to further development, thank you.

I bought this game a few months ago, and immediately refunded it when I realized this project seemed to have been abandoned with radio silence from the devs about future updates. It was pretty disappointing because I absolutely loved OLDTV. 
Once I found that the game is being updated again, I can feel good about buying it again. I still don't think its worth $10 in it's current state, but I have faith in 3RD BOOK that they can finish this game.

I liked OldTV and ColdTV, so this game feels very much as an extension of that, with a bit more elaboration on the style. The background of each region changes and the music has more variety than the previous titles. 
There's also a story to it unlike the previous version's enigmatic approach: you're playing a guy trying to back the whole internet using the same method the previous games' kid clicked through the channels. At the same time, your life is crumbling around you, with you girlfriend leaving you after the first mission and your need for drones to copy the internet constantly siphoning your funds away. How the protagonist deals with that is down to you and it gives different upgrades and modifiers depending on how you go through the story. Overall, I think it's a big improvement compared to the impersonal feel of the previous games.
On the part of the personality of the dev, I can't say I've interacted enough to say anything. Also, I don't think the review field is here to review the dev, but review to the game, and I think it has great potential.

Already beat the prerelease like 9 times on most of my free times. Totally recommend it and vastly prefer to play on discovery mode, however the game sometimes doesn't provide any clue at all on what to input if you haven't played juice mode, so here are some codes I discovered on my own:

TKV: The Karman Virus, an unaccessible region
TSM: The Screaming Machine, another unaccessible region.
TOR: Alternative command to SELL.
SYS: Unlocks System setup on discovery mode.
SET: Opens settings.
BOS: Another way of getting in the Backofspace server. (prerelease)

and some tips:
Alpha1 = Ctrl + 1
Alpha2 = Ctrl + 2
Alpha"a" = Ctrl + "a", presumably

Would love to have a training mode on the top right btw, and more tips throughout the game for discovery mode enjoyers like me.

Briliant! Especially panoramic mod is a gamechanger!

Forge your own story, be whoever you want, do whatever you want (but be ready for reaction of the world and consequences)! Game will describe and draw all your actions and react to them.

This game is especially good with Saphire subscriptoin and local qwen-image-2512(lightning).
+ my own text in "img_prompt_story_turn.txt" to unleash full potential of qwen by precise scene description.
 Consistent character images depicting all his actions gives a lot of immersion in case of using own comfyui api. Otherwise even on Saphire subsctiption image generation does not depicts the same character on next scene, losing type of appearance and giving only vague image of the theme of current turn.

About the game in general - it gives a lot of immersion, but sometimes inconsistencies in history/chat/player location/abilitiy checks happens.

Good parts of the game:
Even the avatar redrown automatically to reflect the state and mostly  correctly but not every time (it will be good to find the prompt responsible for generation avatar and for amending character descripton to improve it and reflect current body, equipment and armor state)

Rich scenario generator with AI aid.
Map, places, characters, items, quests
Music, TTS narrating in different voices.
And many more QOL things.

Especially "Panoramic mode".
It is a great step further, even slow and with image collage problems. Defenitely something new which potentially may solve many problems with inconsestency just remembering items and spatial state "in image". 



Cons:
The game is a legacy project with a lot of old restrictions and problems and a lot of things which may be improved. 

Generation of Items in places is.. weird. E.g. I start the game in my room and it creates: Old scetchbook, Monitor (just monitor only) and curtains! In room full of useful items (bed, cabinets, wardrobes, tools and other stuff). Such item generation is good for "Munchkin" game: kick the door, kill the monster, take loot, repeat. But for simulation of the realistic world such item generation is useless. Sometimes it become useful when Item suggested to be added from promt - it is good, but not every time.

NPCs are passive.
Context size restriction (even for own api) which leads to inconsistences.

Maybe it will be good to add emergency button to review the whole context with history and fix errors in states of character, npcs, places, items? Or, at least, add the autocheck after the turn, which ensures whether the main character still in the current location and whether main character still possesses/wears weapon/clothes/armor, because it often was not reflected even on sapfire, deepseek, hermes 3 405b.

Map have two possible improvements: With addition of time system it will be better to count elapsed time instead of range restriction.
2. The map is chaotic and sometimes illogical in terms of spatial placement.
The situation may be mproved with coordinates system and generation of full terrain with ability to travel through it not only from place to place but in wildeness too. (The idea from Scaldsong can provide the inspiration in this part of the game)



So, overall, the game definitely worth it and may bring a lot of joy, especially with improvements of consistency, world simulation and other features which are awailable now with better AI and much longer context.
Thanks to the author for creating and continuously improving the game!

Wow! This game is amazing! The responses that it generates pull you deeper and deeper into the story. I hope that video generation is added instead of just still pictures. It would make it even more immersive!

Ok , I realised today that I've not posted a review for this one, and after seeing my playtime i think its well over due... its good. 

Seriously though between the base game and editing the prompt files you can make almost any world to play in. The game style as like a AI run DnD session. Yes its AI run so AI can be AI and do stupid things and you sometimes have to kick it back on track and theres limited memory for the game story, meaning that the AI will forget things eventually, but you can use third party LLMs or subscribe to a faster in game service that provide significantly more memory, but i think its worth it. I have payed for MMOs monthly and put less time into them. Whilst playing this game I also learnt alot about AI prompting to edit the files so Im now constantly tweaking and testing new things with it. Also the developer is constantly updating and listens to feed back.
Very nice that you can anything you want. Hopefully in the future we will be able to get a boost to quality and be able to control a character instead of it being text based.

Generation of Items in places is.. weird. E.g. I start the game in my room and it creates: Old scetchbook, Monitor (just monitor only) and curtains! In room full of useful items (bed, cabinets, wardrobes, tools and other stuff). Such item generation is good for "Munchkin" game: kick the door, kill the monster, take loot, repeat. But for simulation of the realistic world such item generation is useless. Sometimes it become useful when Item suggested to be added from promt - it is good, but not every time.

NPCs are passive.
Context size restriction (even for own api) which leads to inconsistences.

Maybe it will be good to add emergency button to review the whole context with history and fix errors in states of character, npcs, places, items? Or, at least, add the autocheck after the turn, which ensures whether the main character still in the current location and whether main character still possesses/wears weapon/clothes/armor, because it often was not reflected even on sapfire, deepseek, hermes 3 405b.

Map have two possible improvements: With addition of time system it will be better to count elapsed time instead of range restriction.
2. The map is chaotic and sometimes illogical in terms of spatial placement.
The situation may be mproved with coordinates system and generation of full terrain with ability to travel through it not only from place to place but in wildeness too. (The idea from Scaldsong can provide the inspiration in this part of the game)



So, overall, the game definitely worth it and may bring a lot of joy, especially with improvements of consistency, world simulation and other features which are awailable now with better AI and much longer context.
Thanks to the author for creating and continuously improving the game!

Very very fun if you're willing to work with it and lose immersion from time to time. I've spent time building a character and it can be very fun early game, but sometimes you need to remind it of things. This can be done through RP, so it isn't that much of an issue. Highly reccommend playing with mods as they greatly improve some things: Reactive Realms especially.

you wanna feel like a kid and make your own fantasy world RPG without limits?
I explored dungeons, created worlds, played as a superheroes, conquered warhammer as Frieza, played a post apocalyptic sci fi rpg. whatever the hell you want to do, if you have enough imagination you can do anything with this game. its amazing and constantly updating. 
One game to rule them all. A very fun game!

This game is honestly the peak of what AI should be like in the gaming space. Not in some random game that no one wants it in, not in the dev process of every single game on the market, but in these curated experiences that, well before you go into, are clearly utilizing AI. If you can get past the 'Ew, AI' and really get into the game, it's incredibly enjoyable no mater how you play. I do recommend it if you like DnD type games, but want to play solo.

Strengths

-Technically quite interesting - and gives an indication of how an LLM might one day integrate with games.  
-It provides some fun playing a text based RPG - with the randomness of an LLM.  I found that fun and look forwards to where the technology will lead.
- There are periodic updates and improvements.  

Bear in mind:

- Context length is an issue - and one would need a subscription to get the full experience 
- As with anything LLM driven the AI does some wonky stuff

After fiddling around with I have not used it much (I didn't want a subscription), but I was quite happy with the experience and will be keeping an eye on the updates.

Very solid fun game, the AI is very creative and does a good job at driving the story forward. It does sometimes mess up or go some strange routes but every little thing about it is customizable and you can edit what has happened, currently the best AI dungeon game on steam and just gets better with every update. try it out and create your own wacky story's/characters the possibilities are endless!

Wow, this is about as good as I imagined it to be! You can have any adventure in any way you want! I started a Fallout like game with a ghoul-like character, found a scavanger buddy and it ended up with me draining his radiation power for myself. Infinite possibilities, even with the free AI model! Support this now!
a very good AI driven text adventure game. just try it. no need to hate AI at all

Wow! This game is amazing! The responses that it generates pull you deeper and deeper into the story. I hope that video generation is added instead of just still pictures. It would make it even more immersive!
Gamifying AI is probably the only good thing that'll come from the tech and this is a pretty good effort at it, though I personally don't prefer the included non-subscription LLM and image gen.

Ok , I realised today that I've not posted a review for this one, and after seeing my playtime i think its well over due... its good. 

Seriously though between the base game and editing the prompt files you can make almost any world to play in. The game style as like a AI run DnD session. Yes its AI run so AI can be AI and do stupid things and you sometimes have to kick it back on track and theres limited memory for the game story, meaning that the AI will forget things eventually, but you can use third party LLMs or subscribe to a faster in game service that provide significantly more memory, but i think its worth it. I have payed for MMOs monthly and put less time into them. Whilst playing this game I also learnt alot about AI prompting to edit the files so Im now constantly tweaking and testing new things with it. Also the developer is constantly updating and listens to feed back.

Its a pretty good program. it has its strengths and weaknesses. If you like roleplay and to choose your own adventure, this is for you. 

Strengths:
- It will continue to provide roleplay and story for as long as you want. 
-You can play any kind of scenario you want. Ive played everything from farmers in the apocalypse, to babysitter who uncovers a coven witch conspiracy, to political space operas, to gaslamp fantasy with magic and giant mechs. 
- the freedom is outstanding, if you can think it, the ai will roll with it. cast a spell thats niche and never heard of? sure! fly a spaceship even though youre just a farmer, its basically a tractor right?
- the dev is constantly adding new features

Weaknesses
- The Roguelite image AI will make a lot of bodyhorror and fused limbs pictures even with extensive negative prompts. 
- The storyteller isnt a mind reader; if youre tryng to pull off some plan that takes more than one prompt, its best to tell the ai your intentions or intended outcome ooc right at the start. 
- context length is an issue if youre running your own SLM, or LLM. some input prompts take 5500 context, even at 8k context length it only leaves 2500 for output. if youre running less, good luck.
- the ai will hallucinate and forget things a lot. the way the program talks to the ai isnt consistent and no contextshift can be applied. further, history is tied to individual npc's, if the program loses an npc and makes a new one, the history is lost.

totally creepy. the world is dark, bleak, hopeless, violent. but i made a friend. an injured tech-priest that i manged to help. we are trying to locate a dead navigator, to get hold of his implants. okay, whom am i kidding; we are running for our lives because some derelict security drone is after us. 
the game is fascinating, i have great freedom in what i try to do. the AI simulates the world around me according to my actions and the skill checks of course. 
dark, gritty and mean. then i look at the game description here and see vibrant, bright colors and im what the **** .. ? maybe my description of the world should not have been "warhammer 40k".  in my version of the game are corpse eating entities, damaged data relays, gangers, violent thugs - you name it. 
so far im freaked out, but in a good way. the amount of freedom i have is creepy.

Awesome idea
i think dice rolling should be done by the player and not automatically
also rtl text support would be great since the engine knows to answer in hebrew for example, only it's backwards.
and image generation should be optional, it makes the game flow very slow in times.

This game is like a fun little sneak peak into what might be coming to gaming from AI in the future. It's very rough, but it also works surprisingly well sometimes. It appears to be actively developed (for now anyways) and I think it's absolutely worth the price even in its current state. This is a game for tinkerers who like to mess around with game systems or even game files (the game features support for modding, which basically amounts to messing with the prompts that get sent to the AI). It's the type of thing that can get you excited for what the future holds. 

I purchased this game awhile ago, and it was basically unplayable then. This game is now in what I consider a playable state, I've been able to run a little (albiet oftentimes janky) playthrough, something that I wouldn't have been able to tolerate last time I tried the game out. It's come a long way, and I hope it continues to show us what the current state of AI is capable of in this new unique type of open-form gaming. 

Perfect for storytellers, people with vivid imaginations, and day dreamers. This game can feel like that weird dream you had last night.

Also, as a last thing, the music is pretty good! 

Remember to check out the workshop page for mods if you buy (enable in settings). I recommend subscribing to and enabling "Lodactio's mod" OR the popular "Reactivity mod" with Vivid V2 images enabled if you choose to buy the $20 a month Sapphire subscription (as of December 2025).

haven't tried using a physical AI model or any .ggufs, but Im really impressed by how fast and exceptional the free cloud model is. Images generate in less than two seconds, the quality is great, the AI is very accurate and fluid. theres some funny moments here and there too which I like. compared to AI dungeon, it feels more immersive. you get actually play your own generated story as a game. i would consider the price for the speed of the model that you get, it feels faster than AI dungeon. theres also alot of support and discussions in the community hub so that was also super reassuring. really solid overall and fun to play.
Very nice that you can anything you want. Hopefully in the future we will be able to get a boost to quality and be able to control a character instead of it being text based.

Before writing the review below, I realized there are several ways to improve the experience. Many of the most annoying elements can be turned off, and if you’re into roleplay, you can even god-mod via the console. How you present information to the AI model is crucial to getting the behaviors you want. The experience is enjoyable enough that it’s worth refining—263 hours in, that much is clear. Because of that, I don’t want my original review to stand without an addendum. Everything below reflects the out-of-the-box experience; if you want something different, be prepared to roll up your sleeves and tweak it.

The more you put into it, the more you get out of it
Gets better each day
