- It will continue to provide roleplay and story for as long as you want. 
-You can play any kind of scenario you want. Ive played everything from farmers in the apocalypse, to babysitter who uncovers a coven witch conspiracy, to political space operas, to gaslamp fantasy with magic and giant mechs. 
- the freedom is outstanding, if you can think it, the ai will roll with it. cast a spell thats niche and never heard of? sure! fly a spaceship even though youre just a farmer, its basically a tractor right?
- the dev is constantly adding new features

Weaknesses
- The Roguelite image AI will make a lot of bodyhorror and fused limbs pictures even with extensive negative prompts. 
- The storyteller isnt a mind reader; if youre tryng to pull off some plan that takes more than one prompt, its best to tell the ai your intentions or intended outcome ooc right at the start. 
- context length is an issue if youre running your own SLM, or LLM. some input prompts take 5500 context, even at 8k context length it only leaves 2500 for output. if youre running less, good luck.
- the ai will hallucinate and forget things a lot. the way the program talks to the ai isnt consistent and no contextshift can be applied. further, history is tied to individual npc's, if the program loses an npc and makes a new one, the history is lost.

totally creepy. the world is dark, bleak, hopeless, violent. but i made a friend. an injured tech-priest that i manged to help. we are trying to locate a dead navigator, to get hold of his implants. okay, whom am i kidding; we are running for our lives because some derelict security drone is after us. 
the game is fascinating, i have great freedom in what i try to do. the AI simulates the world around me according to my actions and the skill checks of course. 
dark, gritty and mean. then i look at the game description here and see vibrant, bright colors and im what the **** .. ? maybe my description of the world should not have been "warhammer 40k".  in my version of the game are corpse eating entities, damaged data relays, gangers, violent thugs - you name it. 
so far im freaked out, but in a good way. the amount of freedom i have is creepy.

This game is like a fun little sneak peak into what might be coming to gaming from AI in the future. It's very rough, but it also works surprisingly well sometimes. It appears to be actively developed (for now anyways) and I think it's absolutely worth the price even in its current state. This is a game for tinkerers who like to mess around with game systems or even game files (the game features support for modding, which basically amounts to messing with the prompts that get sent to the AI). It's the type of thing that can get you excited for what the future holds. 

I purchased this game awhile ago, and it was basically unplayable then. This game is now in what I consider a playable state, I've been able to run a little (albiet oftentimes janky) playthrough, something that I wouldn't have been able to tolerate last time I tried the game out. It's come a long way, and I hope it continues to show us what the current state of AI is capable of in this new unique type of open-form gaming. 

Perfect for storytellers, people with vivid imaginations, and day dreamers. This game can feel like that weird dream you had last night.

Also, as a last thing, the music is pretty good! 

Remember to check out the workshop page for mods if you buy (enable in settings). I recommend subscribing to and enabling "Lodactio's mod" OR the popular "Reactivity mod" with Vivid V2 images enabled if you choose to buy the $20 a month Sapphire subscription (as of December 2025).

Because of that, I don’t want my original review to stand without an addendum.   The only real issues I have run in to are not with the game itself, but the limitations of AI technology, such as AI having no long term memory to speak of. Briliant! Especially panoramic mod is a gamechanger!

Forge your own story, be whoever you want, do whatever you want (but be ready for reaction of the world and consequences)! Game will describe and draw all your actions and react to them.

This game is especially good with Saphire subscriptoin and local qwen-image-2512(lightning).
+ my own text in "img_prompt_story_turn.txt" to unleash full potential of qwen by precise scene description.
 Consistent character images depicting all his actions gives a lot of immersion in case of using own comfyui api. Otherwise even on Saphire subsctiption image generation does not depicts the same character on next scene, losing type of appearance and giving only vague image of the theme of current turn.

About the game in general - it gives a lot of immersion, but sometimes inconsistencies in history/chat/player location/abilitiy checks happens.

Good parts of the game:
Even the avatar redrown automatically to reflect the state and mostly  correctly but not every time (it will be good to find the prompt responsible for generation avatar and for amending character descripton to improve it and reflect current body, equipment and armor state)

Rich scenario generator with AI aid.
Map, places, characters, items, quests
Music, TTS narrating in different voices.
And many more QOL things.

Especially "Panoramic mode".
It is a great step further, even slow and with image collage problems. Defenitely something new which potentially may solve many problems with inconsestency just remembering items and spatial state "in image". 



Cons:
The game is a legacy project with a lot of old restrictions and problems and a lot of things which may be improved. 

Generation of Items in places is.. weird. E.g. I start the game in my room and it creates: Old scetchbook, Monitor (just monitor only) and curtains! In room full of useful items (bed, cabinets, wardrobes, tools and other stuff). Such item generation is good for "Munchkin" game: kick the door, kill the monster, take loot, repeat. But for simulation of the realistic world such item generation is useless. Sometimes it become useful when Item suggested to be added from promt - it is good, but not every time.

NPCs are passive.
Context size restriction (even for own api) which leads to inconsistences.

Maybe it will be good to add emergency button to review the whole context with history and fix errors in states of character, npcs, places, items? Or, at least, add the autocheck after the turn, which ensures whether the main character still in the current location and whether main character still possesses/wears weapon/clothes/armor, because it often was not reflected even on sapfire, deepseek, hermes 3 405b.

Map have two possible improvements: With addition of time system it will be better to count elapsed time instead of range restriction.
2. The map is chaotic and sometimes illogical in terms of spatial placement.
The situation may be mproved with coordinates system and generation of full terrain with ability to travel through it not only from place to place but in wildeness too. (The idea from Scaldsong can provide the inspiration in this part of the game)



So, overall, the game definitely worth it and may bring a lot of joy, especially with improvements of consistency, world simulation and other features which are awailable now with better AI and much longer context.
Thanks to the author for creating and continuously improving the game!

I do not post reviews on games, but when I read through latest bunch of negative reviews I decided I will contribute to this discourse. 

The reviews that complain that Sui Generis is not coming out, should be thrown to the bin immediately. Sui Generis is the the game that is ultimate goal of the devs they want to create. This game is a dungeon crawler test ground for that game, therefore the reviews are here for THIS game and not for you being upset that the dev did not create the sequel yet. 

Yes this game is in early access and it is unfinished. 
Does it mean the devs abandoned it? No they have been here for those 10+ years working on this passion project.
Does it mean there is not enough content to play? Again no, you will be able to get at least 30 hours of content, but the game works with such interesting concepts of physics that you might want to even play more of it.

Okay so is it worth the money? That is seriously up to you. If I want to have objective analysis of the games I played in the past I look at how much of that game I have played vs how much I spent into it. This game costs 14 bucks so even if I was to play only 14 hours out of this game. I would still find it worth the money. Then again like I said it is subjective. You should decide for yourself. 

That being said they do deserve all support because they are committed on this game and are making it better over time.

So many aspects of this game could be so much better — and actually enjoyable — if the developers gave just a little more of a damn.

The inventory management is ass. Straight up the worst I’ve ever seen. In Arena mode, if you don’t organize your stuff early on, you’re basically ♥♥♥♥♥♥ for the rest of the run. And the whole Inept → Novice tier item design? Not intuitive at all. You spend more time trying to understand if something is trash or usable than actually playing.

They could also have added some kind of combat lock-on system to reduce the sheer amount of mouse dragging. This is the only game that has made my wrist genuinely hurt. Not even osu! did that to me back when I played it like a maniac. Don’t get me wrong — the combat learning curve is pure satisfaction. It feels amazing when it clicks. But once you’ve learned the system? It stops being challenging and starts being physically uncomfortable and mechanically tedious — yet still mandatory if you want to stay consistent in fights.

Now the main campaign… Jesus Christ.

It’s uninteresting at its core. You get zero direction at the start. No meaningful clues, no sense of where you should be heading. So what happens? You run in circles through the same damn starter areas. And by the time you finally understand what you’re supposed to do, you’re already bored out of your mind from revisiting the same places over and over.

Better level design would have fixed SO much. I’d be way happier if the game just gave me a rough sense of direction and let me push forward, dying and learning along the way. That would’ve been fun. Instead, it’s confusion first, boredom second.

And the environments all look the same. It’s just one big medieval concrete maze. Who thought that would be a good idea without a minimap? This becomes an even bigger problem when the game basically forces you to keep your eyes glued open, afraid to blink because you might miss a key item or important loot. But good luck with that — because you’ll be checking a thousand useless junk items and getting mentally exhausted in the process.

Give items clearer visual categories. Add marks. Colors. Symbols. ANYTHING. How the hell am I supposed to know what’s important at the start of this ♥♥♥♥? I’m not asking for a glowing yellow brick road — just basic visual clarity.

And please, for the love of sanity, add a system where we can mark explored paths. Let me draw lines on the floor, scratch the wall, drop a sign — something — so I know “yeah, I’ve already been through this useless dead-end hell corridor.” That’s not handholding. That’s basic respect for the player’s time.

Some of the level design decisions feel so bad I just want to say: please don’t reproduce. Don’t have kids. Use condoms.

Now, to be fair —

Arena mode is fire. Straight up hours and hours of guaranteed fun. That mode alone carries the game hard. But even there, the inventory management is still a crime against humanity, and I want to have a very serious conversation with whoever designed it.

Overall:
8/10 right now.
Easy 10/10 in the future if they improve UI, inventory, and level readability.

Because beneath all the suffering… there’s a masterpiece trying to breathe — or a sadistic ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, or a bad developer… or maybe all of the above.

Exanima is one of those games that I still think back on and return to since I first played. There is no other game like it. If you've ever seen on of those Hema fights where guys in heavy armor wrestle and wail on each other with axes and swords, that's what the combat in exanima feels like. Combine that with a truly old school dungeon crawl and incredible gear progression and that is this game in a nutshell. It's clunky and frustrating, especially in the beginning, and inventory management certainly could use improvement, but Exanima is one of those games that feels like the developers are making a game personally tailored for me. I higly recommend it for people who like slower, mechanically challenging dungeon crawls, as well as those looking to play something very different from anything else that has been made before.

Despite it's eternal development and YEARS between updates, I still feel Exanima is worth the money for what the game offers. I return annually just to play through the story mode. There's a lot of nuance in the mechanics of this game. While it seems like just goofy physics similar to once you really grasp the movement, the controls are tight. The learning curve throws many people off, and to that I say; do the arena challenges in their completion first before playing the rest of the game. The main story has hours and hours of exploration, challenge, interesting lore and great secrets/puzzles that greatly reward paying attention to your environment. Everything is placed intentionally. If something looks like it could have something under it, it probably does. You can walk out of level 1 verrry well equipped, and basically be a knight walking out of level 3. 

Armor layering is a thing. But there is such a thing as too much. To simplify it, anything that looks covered by a piece of armor IS covered. If you have a sleeveless chain vest, the stats of the vest will NOT be covering your arms.   Armor effectiveness goes from the outside in. If outer armor makes you impervious to slashing damage but is weak to impacts/crushing damage, then a chain vest is useless since it only really helps with slash. A cushioned gambeson however greatly reduces impacts. So if your outer armor is hit with an impact, the damage is reduced by your outer armor, then stops at your gambeson, leaving you untouched or lightly bruised. 

The game holds the value it sells itself at for what it currently offers. Don't set expectations for it to ever be truly finished, updates are just bonuses to the very unique and obsessively crafted experience that it already can provide with the current content.

It hurts me to see the people review bombing this game.

The only real issue is that this game is probably not going to ever leave the EA state. Why? Because "[i]the devs are simply milkin' y'all guys lul[/i]"? Heck no. The development team just wanted to bite off more that they can chew. Now I see them as doing their best to get along with the community and the sponsors/publishers, but they have a very large technical debt they aren't able to solve. 

The same thing happened to GSC and their Stalker long time ago, when they had to "release at least something", with "something" being a really raw and barely playable product. You can't just make such a massive and unique game without trapping yourself in a never-ending development loop.

Does this mean the game is bad? No. It is fun to play, it is a unique experience, it looks good. The lore and the pretty-much-low-fantasy setting of the game are interesting. And it has some content to enjoy too.

The combat isn't "clunky", as people say. It [b]is not[/b] hard to get into (if you like it), and it kinda feels like a real H.E.M.A. fight. Sometimes it looks weird, but you don't notice it if [b]you[/b] are the one playing the game.

And I like the health system, it is really immersive without being too much realistic/casual.

The game also has some other cool mechanics you don't see in other games really often. For example, I was surprised to discover that you don't have to fight the "zombies" in the early game, as they usually attack you only if you stare at them or get in their way (they are like some psychos in a mental asylum, not your usual "zombies").

As the conclusion, I would say it is a good game to play and an interensting experience to gain. Just keep in mind that it is a "tech demo" of the game that may never come out.

If you like how it looks and what it sounds like, and you don't doubt too much, you may buy it for sure.

The game is fun if you like a challenge. It has a uniques system for combat and movement with a steep learning curve. Once you get to know the feel, it is very rewarding. There is a "RPG" element but its not very in depth. You get to learn new "Skill" by using your weapon and armor but it only applies to your next character. Also the spells.. I havent got the chance to use it very much, its not that usefull and/or intuitive. I've only got one. The map design is very nice but very confusing without a map, you might run around in circle a lot and when you find a key, good luck finding what it unlocks. 

The arena part was the best part for me, you get to manage your own team and gear, suit them and level them up for different types of combat. I got confused at first, I though I had to have multiple combatants to fight in duo/trio... but it has to be an hired guy, an NPC. All YOUR characters are controlled by you, thats why you need a mercenary.

Keep in mind that it has not been updated for more than 2 years now and its been out since 2015, some things might be rough around the edges but, keep pushing, keep learning the mechanics and you will make it worth your while, at a low price point I think it is recommendable, even if its only for the arena part.

Does it mean there is not enough content to play? Again no, you will be able to get at least 30 hours of content, but the game works with such interesting concepts of physics that you might want to even play more of it.

Okay so is it worth the money? That is seriously up to you. If I want to have objective analysis of the games I played in the past I look at how much of that game I have played vs how much I spent into it. This game costs 14 bucks so even if I was to play only 14 hours out of this game. I would still find it worth the money. Then again like I said it is subjective. You should decide for yourself. 

That being said they do deserve all support because they are committed on this game and are making it better over time.

I'm glad I haven't been waiting 10 years for it but it's very fun for what's there. 

You can't beat the main story yet but the combat is fun and what story that is there is compelling. Dark, moody, mysterious atmosphere with great music, sounds, and graphics. For only $15 it's worth it

Game itself is amazing. Be sure to check the Discord to play the beta, which feels much more rounded and is actively updated the devs also explains clearly why development is structured that way.

I would easily recommend this to others, and I genuinely believe this game will only improve with time. Its engine alone is worth paying attention to; it’s impressive what the team has already achieved and what they’re aiming for.

I’ve spent a lot of time with this game, including the updated beta (0.9.3), and it has been consistently impressive. The engine and design choices create an immersive, intuitive, and rewarding experience I haven’t found anywhere else.

Recently, there’s been a spike in negative reviews from a small but very vocal part of the community that’s unhappy with the game’s direction mainly its slow, deliberate development cycle. Their frustration is valid for them, but it doesn’t reflect the broader reality of what it takes to build an entire engine while developing a game like Exanima on top of it. The longer gap between Steam updates isn’t due to inactivity but rather the continuous compounding work that the team is deciding to do.

For me and for everyone I’ve recommended the game to it’s been absolutely worth playing. It’s unique, genuinely something special, and I believe the game itself is the kind of challenge people feel compelled to take on. Every time you learn something new, it pulls you in even further.

I enjoy this game quite a lot. My only issues are the slow progress developers are making (I do understand that this is quite an ambitious project, even factoring in the small team working on it), the lack of publicity this project, and its "soon" to come sequel, Sui Generis, have is quite an issue. I've had a lot of fun both in the Arena mode, the story, and in the practice mode, but this game is seriously slept on.

Make no mistake, this game is HARD, but, once you get the hang of it even a little it becomes a lot more satisfying actually winning more difficult fights. Its a time sink, there's no denying that it took me a few hours to get through the first few levels and I still missed things.

I love it so far, and am genuinely excited and looking forward to the future of this game and Sui Generis.

Devs, seriously, update the store page, "Around six to nine months, give or take three months"... its been ten years. Tell people about the game, put up some more gameplay so people can see it on the store and not instantly go "Ah ♥♥♥♥ this game is dead af". You're leaving money on the table by neglecting your main/only source of revenue and players for this game. It deserves that at the VERY least.


Players: Give it a shot. Its $15 USD, its worth the price and its not breaking the bank. Show some support for a team busting their ass to make a game "Sui Generis" (Means like no other).

The scope is grand and incredible. I am genuinely rooting for Bare Mettle to pull this off. I know its going to take quite some time. I struggle with patience... especially when I'm as passionate about something as I am this game.


Takeaways: Game good, development slow, plans awesome, hopes high, enjoyment good. 

Its a great dungeon-diving, high skill ceiling, adventure game, with surprisingly fun (albeit at times frustrating) physics implemented. The puzzles can be complex or quite simple, being able to interact with almost every single prop you see makes for some interesting possibilities, from holding a lever down with a bench to creating a bridge with planks, and more. Its hard to get the hang of, and hard to put down.

So many aspects of this game could be so much better — and actually enjoyable — if the developers gave just a little more of a damn.

The inventory management is ass. Straight up the worst I’ve ever seen. In Arena mode, if you don’t organize your stuff early on, you’re basically ♥♥♥♥♥♥ for the rest of the run. And the whole Inept → Novice tier item design? Not intuitive at all. You spend more time trying to understand if something is trash or usable than actually playing.

They could also have added some kind of combat lock-on system to reduce the sheer amount of mouse dragging. This is the only game that has made my wrist genuinely hurt. Not even osu! did that to me back when I played it like a maniac. Don’t get me wrong — the combat learning curve is pure satisfaction. It feels amazing when it clicks. But once you’ve learned the system? It stops being challenging and starts being physically uncomfortable and mechanically tedious — yet still mandatory if you want to stay consistent in fights.

Now the main campaign… Jesus Christ.

It’s uninteresting at its core. You get zero direction at the start. No meaningful clues, no sense of where you should be heading. So what happens? You run in circles through the same damn starter areas. And by the time you finally understand what you’re supposed to do, you’re already bored out of your mind from revisiting the same places over and over.

Better level design would have fixed SO much. I’d be way happier if the game just gave me a rough sense of direction and let me push forward, dying and learning along the way. That would’ve been fun. Instead, it’s confusion first, boredom second.

And the environments all look the same. It’s just one big medieval concrete maze. Who thought that would be a good idea without a minimap? This becomes an even bigger problem when the game basically forces you to keep your eyes glued open, afraid to blink because you might miss a key item or important loot. But good luck with that — because you’ll be checking a thousand useless junk items and getting mentally exhausted in the process.

Give items clearer visual categories. Add marks. Colors. Symbols. ANYTHING. How the hell am I supposed to know what’s important at the start of this ♥♥♥♥? I’m not asking for a glowing yellow brick road — just basic visual clarity.

And please, for the love of sanity, add a system where we can mark explored paths. Let me draw lines on the floor, scratch the wall, drop a sign — something — so I know “yeah, I’ve already been through this useless dead-end hell corridor.” That’s not handholding. That’s basic respect for the player’s time.

Some of the level design decisions feel so bad I just want to say: please don’t reproduce. Don’t have kids. Use condoms.

Now, to be fair —

Arena mode is fire. Straight up hours and hours of guaranteed fun. That mode alone carries the game hard. But even there, the inventory management is still a crime against humanity, and I want to have a very serious conversation with whoever designed it.

Overall:
8/10 right now.
Easy 10/10 in the future if they improve UI, inventory, and level readability.

Because beneath all the suffering… there’s a masterpiece trying to breathe — or a sadistic ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, or a bad developer… or maybe all of the above.

The whole game takes some getting used to, but if you're willing to power through the learning curve of unique combat and movement the game is so satisfying. Every encounter is dangerous in all the right ways. You really are just some dude, not the chosen one so there are enemies way stronger than you, but there is a way to beat them. Work smarter not harder is the way of the game. The lack of saves is pretty rough though, I fell down a hole towards the end of the available game and I lost probably half of my progress. I still think that Exanima is great, but tough.

Exanima is one of those games that I still think back on and return to since I first played. There is no other game like it. If you've ever seen on of those Hema fights where guys in heavy armor wrestle and wail on each other with axes and swords, that's what the combat in exanima feels like. Combine that with a truly old school dungeon crawl and incredible gear progression and that is this game in a nutshell. It's clunky and frustrating, especially in the beginning, and inventory management certainly could use improvement, but Exanima is one of those games that feels like the developers are making a game personally tailored for me. I higly recommend it for people who like slower, mechanically challenging dungeon crawls, as well as those looking to play something very different from anything else that has been made before.

This is one of my all time favorite games and I have a criminally low amount of hours given towards it considering how great it is.

When I 1st purchased it many years ago it was incredibly impressive for its physics based combat and dark atmosphere.  The game has only grown in features and content and I highly recommend this for anyone who has an interest in an old school fantasy vibe with top tier physics.

Prepare to fumble your way through the darkness at first, but with patience and practice learn how to parry like a true knight and fight your way through a spiraling dungeon of peak intensity and atmosphere.

What a wonderful game, thank you devs I've been in love since day 1 <3
Fun physics and concept but stressfull

well worth the 15 bucks. the recent people crying in the reviews have wayyyy to much time on their hands and way too high of expectations for a 15 dollar game all the while having 100s of hrs on it lol get real.

this has some real potential im really glad to see the devs are still working on this wonderful project its been the most fun ive had with a game in a minute
its a great physics based medieval combat game with good graphics. Very enjoyable especially arena mode

Although I am only 9hrs into the game, I am up to level 3 and I have also spent some time in Arena. I have found the game to be great, I love that the combat is physics based and movement of the weapon and footwork matters. 
I do however absolutely hate that the camera often gets me killed because the wasd keys follow in line of where your reticle is for your mouse, which is horrible when you are fighting and moving your mouse to fight. I think having an auto lock on or something like that would seriously help gameplay. 
Currently I am recommending it, but beware of death by camera angle.

The combat is super fun and rewarding. The lore is intriguing and mysterious. Each level makes you feel lost yet the atmosphere pulls you into every room. For a $15 game, this game is very well worth it and it's improved a lot over the years. 

There are a lot of negative reviews about the devs not releasing a completely different game (Exanima is supposedly a test game to experiment that game's dungeon system), but if you take a second to even play this one, whatever they're doing is quite ambitious already. I hope they finish Exanima and find their well deserved success.
good game. physics are cool. wish had more content
controls are overly complex for zero reason, and the progression is even more confusing edit: besides this, the game is really good but very punishing. hopefully it isnt stuck in early access forever
it is a fun i like the realistic feel to the game makes it allot more challenging. Great game for the dungeon crawler fans

have played this game since ive been in highschool, im 30 and its getting closer and closer. Ive played much of this game and have enjoyed this game a lot over the years. This games ambition is insane and if every other development team took their jobs as serious as these guys did we would have a revolution in gaming. now I know its been forever in development but its aspirations have just been so high, so id rather that than a less than revolutionary game.

this game has been one that i have always come back to because of the talented team at bare mettle. i started playing over 8 years now and have seen quite a few changes (all for the better) and have also told many people this is my all time favorite game.  The physics and attention to detail always get me to come back to this game. the devs post monthly diaries that keep me in touch with the game and reminds me of how much care they have for this title. this game is more than worth the purchase if you are on the fence about it.

Just started, but I had to come here and write a review. The game is awesome. Most fresh and original combat in a game I've seen in a long time. Movement and swinging your weapon takes a second to get the hand of, but it is well worth the time. The way you interact with your environment is also very unique. My favorite part is everything in the game is weighty and deliberate. I did have to remap my controls instantly though the base controls weren't it for me.

Like I said just started will update if my opinion changes
Amazing and hoping it gets finished, also hoping for Sui Generis!
Game is super hard but fun to learn. Each kill you get is super rewarding and searching for better gear with Derrin is always a good time. Funny ragdoll type physics as well.

Game:  Good, hard.

exanima is in development for a decade and still doesn't have an ending for the "main story". that made me a little disappointed and killed my will to reach the final floors... but still, the game is very enjoyable,
it has a very realistic combat system. you can even use real stances and techniques from real life in it. 
the arena mode gives the game a little bit of replayability and it can take several hours to reach the final levels of the dungeon in the main story.

There is a lot to like about this game.  First, the price is right!!  Also it is a game where you are the general manager of your favorite team.  This simulation uses fictitious players and team names.  Not a problem for me.  You are responsible for developing your stadium and selecting minor league teams.

The biggest responsibility is to decide who to keep, who to let go and how much to pay everyone.  You have a limited amount of money so bad decisions will cost you money.

A great game for a great price!

Hell Division is a gritty third-person action game developed by Quacky Games and published by Whale Rock Games that centers its entire experience around the fantasy of piloting a high-tech combat drone in a dystopian, corporate-controlled future. Rather than casting the player as a traditional soldier or hero, the game places you inside a machine—an autonomous weapon designed to operate where humans cannot—immediately setting a colder, more mechanical tone that aligns well with its cyberpunk influences. From the outset, Hell Division positions itself as a focused combat experience, prioritizing tension, positioning, and survival over cinematic storytelling or spectacle.

The setting frames a near-future world dominated by ruthless megacorporations locked in a shadow war for technological supremacy. You are deployed as a single advanced drone unit sent into hostile zones to disrupt enemy operations and eliminate rival machines. The narrative is intentionally minimal, serving more as contextual flavor than a driving force, but it succeeds in establishing a bleak atmosphere where corporations treat warfare as a calculated business expense. This stripped-back storytelling places emphasis on the player’s role as a disposable asset, reinforcing the idea that you are just one unit in a much larger, uncaring system.

Gameplay revolves around third-person drone combat across a series of contained mission areas. You maneuver freely through urban environments, industrial complexes, and neon-lit cyberpunk zones while engaging waves of enemy drones that vary in size, behavior, and threat level. Combat demands constant movement; hovering in one place for too long is often a death sentence. Dodging incoming fire, managing distance, and prioritizing targets are critical, as enemy swarms can overwhelm you quickly if approached recklessly. The game leans more toward tactical survival than pure arcade shooting, rewarding cautious positioning and situational awareness.

Enemy encounters escalate steadily as missions progress, introducing tougher units and denser formations that test both reflexes and patience. Some adversaries pressure you with aggressive pursuit, while others attack from range, forcing you to adapt on the fly. This layered enemy design gives combat a sense of tension, as engagements often feel like controlled chaos rather than straightforward shootouts. Success usually comes from reading the battlefield and exploiting brief openings rather than overpowering enemies outright.

Visually, Hell Division commits fully to its cyberpunk aesthetic. Neon highlights, dark cityscapes, and metallic industrial structures dominate the environments, creating a mood that feels hostile and oppressive. While the game is clearly built on a modest budget, its art direction compensates by maintaining a consistent visual identity. The environments may not be highly detailed, but they effectively communicate the world’s tone: cold, corporate, and mechanically brutal. Lighting plays a key role, often guiding the player’s eye while adding atmosphere to otherwise stark locations.

Sound design supports this mood with mechanical hums, weapon fire, and electronic effects that reinforce the feeling of piloting a machine rather than inhabiting a human body. Audio cues are functional, helping players track threats during intense encounters, though the overall soundscape is more utilitarian than cinematic. Music, when present, underscores tension rather than driving emotion, aligning with the game’s restrained approach to presentation.

One of the more divisive aspects of Hell Division is its control and interface design. Piloting a drone feels intentionally weighty and sometimes awkward, which can add to immersion but may also frustrate players expecting fluid, arcade-style responsiveness. The learning curve is noticeable, particularly early on, as players acclimate to movement, aiming, and spatial awareness in three-dimensional combat spaces. Once mastered, the controls can feel deliberate and precise, but reaching that point requires patience and tolerance for initial friction.

Progression in Hell Division is straightforward, focusing on completing missions rather than extensive customization or RPG-style upgrades. This simplicity keeps the experience focused but also limits long-term variety. Without deep progression systems or branching paths, replayability depends largely on enjoyment of the core combat loop. For some players, this focus is a strength, stripping away distractions and letting the gameplay stand on its own. For others, it may feel like missed potential in a genre that often thrives on customization and growth.

Ultimately, Hell Division is a game defined by its concept and atmosphere more than its polish. It delivers a distinct take on third-person combat by placing players inside a drone rather than a human avatar, emphasizing isolation, vulnerability, and mechanical precision. Its cyberpunk world and tactical combat can be genuinely engaging for players willing to invest time into mastering its systems, even if its rough edges and limited scope hold it back from broader appeal. For those drawn to niche action experiences, dystopian sci-fi settings, and methodical combat over spectacle, Hell Division offers a tense and memorable, if imperfect, journey into corporate warfare from the cold perspective of a machine.

Rating: 7/10

A cool and tough tactical shooter. Drone control is unusual, but very authentic — you feel like a real operator. The atmosphere of the gloomy future is perfectly conveyed, the sound and graphics are excellent. The disadvantage is not for those who are looking for a simple arcade game. High complexity and the need to think through every maneuver. All in all, a very worthwhile thing for your money!

Hell Division is a gritty third-person action game developed by Quacky Games and published by Whale Rock Games that centers its entire experience around the fantasy of piloting a high-tech combat drone in a dystopian, corporate-controlled future. Rather than casting the player as a traditional soldier or hero, the game places you inside a machine—an autonomous weapon designed to operate where humans cannot—immediately setting a colder, more mechanical tone that aligns well with its cyberpunk influences. From the outset, Hell Division positions itself as a focused combat experience, prioritizing tension, positioning, and survival over cinematic storytelling or spectacle.

The setting frames a near-future world dominated by ruthless megacorporations locked in a shadow war for technological supremacy. You are deployed as a single advanced drone unit sent into hostile zones to disrupt enemy operations and eliminate rival machines. The narrative is intentionally minimal, serving more as contextual flavor than a driving force, but it succeeds in establishing a bleak atmosphere where corporations treat warfare as a calculated business expense. This stripped-back storytelling places emphasis on the player’s role as a disposable asset, reinforcing the idea that you are just one unit in a much larger, uncaring system.

Gameplay revolves around third-person drone combat across a series of contained mission areas. You maneuver freely through urban environments, industrial complexes, and neon-lit cyberpunk zones while engaging waves of enemy drones that vary in size, behavior, and threat level. Combat demands constant movement; hovering in one place for too long is often a death sentence. Dodging incoming fire, managing distance, and prioritizing targets are critical, as enemy swarms can overwhelm you quickly if approached recklessly. The game leans more toward tactical survival than pure arcade shooting, rewarding cautious positioning and situational awareness.

Enemy encounters escalate steadily as missions progress, introducing tougher units and denser formations that test both reflexes and patience. Some adversaries pressure you with aggressive pursuit, while others attack from range, forcing you to adapt on the fly. This layered enemy design gives combat a sense of tension, as engagements often feel like controlled chaos rather than straightforward shootouts. Success usually comes from reading the battlefield and exploiting brief openings rather than overpowering enemies outright.

Visually, Hell Division commits fully to its cyberpunk aesthetic. Neon highlights, dark cityscapes, and metallic industrial structures dominate the environments, creating a mood that feels hostile and oppressive. While the game is clearly built on a modest budget, its art direction compensates by maintaining a consistent visual identity. The environments may not be highly detailed, but they effectively communicate the world’s tone: cold, corporate, and mechanically brutal. Lighting plays a key role, often guiding the player’s eye while adding atmosphere to otherwise stark locations.

Sound design supports this mood with mechanical hums, weapon fire, and electronic effects that reinforce the feeling of piloting a machine rather than inhabiting a human body. Audio cues are functional, helping players track threats during intense encounters, though the overall soundscape is more utilitarian than cinematic. Music, when present, underscores tension rather than driving emotion, aligning with the game’s restrained approach to presentation.

One of the more divisive aspects of Hell Division is its control and interface design. Piloting a drone feels intentionally weighty and sometimes awkward, which can add to immersion but may also frustrate players expecting fluid, arcade-style responsiveness. The learning curve is noticeable, particularly early on, as players acclimate to movement, aiming, and spatial awareness in three-dimensional combat spaces. Once mastered, the controls can feel deliberate and precise, but reaching that point requires patience and tolerance for initial friction.

Without deep progression systems or branching paths, replayability depends largely on enjoyment of the core combat loop. For some players, this focus is a strength, stripping away distractions and letting the gameplay stand on its own. For others, it may feel like missed potential in a genre that often thrives on customization and growth.

Combat demands constant movement; hovering in one place for too long is often a death sentence. Dodging incoming fire, managing distance, and prioritizing targets are critical, as enemy swarms can overwhelm you quickly if approached recklessly. The game leans more toward tactical survival than pure arcade shooting, rewarding cautious positioning and situational awareness.

Enemy encounters escalate steadily as missions progress, introducing tougher units and denser formations that test both reflexes and patience. Some adversaries pressure you with aggressive pursuit, while others attack from range, forcing you to adapt on the fly. This layered enemy design gives combat a sense of tension, as engagements often feel like controlled chaos rather than straightforward shootouts. Success usually comes from reading the battlefield and exploiting brief openings rather than overpowering enemies outright.

Visually, Hell Division commits fully to its cyberpunk aesthetic. Neon highlights, dark cityscapes, and metallic industrial structures dominate the environments, creating a mood that feels hostile and oppressive. While the game is clearly built on a modest budget, its art direction compensates by maintaining a consistent visual identity. The environments may not be highly detailed, but they effectively communicate the world’s tone: cold, corporate, and mechanically brutal. Lighting plays a key role, often guiding the player’s eye while adding atmosphere to otherwise stark locations.

Sound design supports this mood with mechanical hums, weapon fire, and electronic effects that reinforce the feeling of piloting a machine rather than inhabiting a human body. Audio cues are functional, helping players track threats during intense encounters, though the overall soundscape is more utilitarian than cinematic. Music, when present, underscores tension rather than driving emotion, aligning with the game’s restrained approach to presentation.

One of the more divisive aspects of Hell Division is its control and interface design. Piloting a drone feels intentionally weighty and sometimes awkward, which can add to immersion but may also frustrate players expecting fluid, arcade-style responsiveness. The learning curve is noticeable, particularly early on, as players acclimate to movement, aiming, and spatial awareness in three-dimensional combat spaces. Once mastered, the controls can feel deliberate and precise, but reaching that point requires patience and tolerance for initial friction.

Progression in Hell Division is straightforward, focusing on completing missions rather than extensive customization or RPG-style upgrades. This simplicity keeps the experience focused but also limits long-term variety. Without deep progression systems or branching paths, replayability depends largely on enjoyment of the core combat loop. For some players, this focus is a strength, stripping away distractions and letting the gameplay stand on its own. For others, it may feel like missed potential in a genre that often thrives on customization and growth.

Ultimately, Hell Division is a game defined by its concept and atmosphere more than its polish. It delivers a distinct take on third-person combat by placing players inside a drone rather than a human avatar, emphasizing isolation, vulnerability, and mechanical precision. Its cyberpunk world and tactical combat can be genuinely engaging for players willing to invest time into mastering its systems, even if its rough edges and limited scope hold it back from broader appeal. For those drawn to niche action experiences, dystopian sci-fi settings, and methodical combat over spectacle, Hell Division offers a tense and memorable, if imperfect, journey into corporate warfare from the cold perspective of a machine.

Final Upgrade, developed and published by TaosX, is a sprawling automation and space-strategy sandbox that imagines the galaxy not as a battlefield or a frontier of exploration, but as a massive industrial canvas waiting to be systematized. It takes the core appeal of factory-building games—the joy of turning raw matter into complex, self-sustaining production webs—and expands it outward until production chains span star systems, planets, and entire fleets of player-designed ships. Instead of embodying a conquering empire or a wandering explorer, the player becomes an architect of interconnected processes, building a mechanized civilization whose strength comes not from firepower, but from logistics, efficiency, and relentless technological momentum. It is a game that asks players to think like an engineer rather than a commander, designing not just individual machines, but the invisible flow between them.

The early game introduces this vision through modest beginnings. A handful of vessels mine asteroid fields, basic crafting modules produce simple components, and the player learns how to automate fundamental resource loops—metal refining, energy distribution, storage allocation, and material transport. What differentiates Final Upgrade from genre peers is how quickly even these early systems reveal hidden layers of complexity. Ships are not static vessels purchased from a menu—they are modular constructions whose internal layout, propulsion arrangement, cargo space, and computing capacity directly influence their performance. A transport freighter with a poorly optimized interior may bottleneck an entire supply chain, and a miner lacking structural support may operate inefficiently. This design philosophy—where every detail matters—establishes a satisfying sense of authorship over the entire industrial ecosystem.

As the player expands into new star systems, the scale increases dramatically. Individual planets become specialized industrial centers, contributing unique resources or manufacturing capabilities. Trade routes must be automated and safeguarded. Energy production, once a simple concern, becomes a galaxy-wide balancing act. Supply shortages in one sector cascade into distant manufacturing delays, forcing players to monitor and redesign logistics networks rather than merely placing more machines. The game thrives on these emergent problems—never scripted, always systemic—giving progression a natural, self-generated rhythm. Instead of waiting for a quest or objective, players feel pushed onward by the consequences of their own decisions, and victory comes from taming the chaos they created.

The technological progression system reinforces this expanding scope. Research is not just a checklist of upgrades—it unlocks new building blocks for creative engineering, new transportation models, new hull designs, new automation tools, and ultimately, the ability to construct staggering megastructures that reshape entire star systems. Mastery comes not from grinding resources, but from learning how to integrate these technologies into existing infrastructure without destabilizing what already works. Late-game empires become dizzyingly complex organisms—ships ferrying advanced components between orbital foundries, refineries feeding terraforming operations, computational stations optimizing traffic, and automated fleets maintaining the entire industrial lattice with minimal player oversight. Watching the galaxy run itself becomes one of Final Upgrade’s most rewarding endgame experiences.

Visually, the game embraces clarity over spectacle. Its interface-driven presentation favors crisp icons, readable galaxy maps, and clean ship schematics, ensuring that even enormous networks remain understandable at a glance. Planets, starfields, and nebulae provide atmosphere, but never overshadow the informational backbone that drives decision-making. The soundscape reinforces this functional beauty—soft ambient hums, mechanical pulses, and understated musical themes that support long sessions without fatigue. It’s a game built for focus rather than adrenaline, and its aesthetic choices reflect that purpose.

However, the game’s ambition also creates barriers. The learning curve can feel imposing, especially for players unfamiliar with automation or logistical simulations. Tutorials help, but they cannot fully prepare players for the complexity that unfolds hours later, when intersystem production chains require troubleshooting and strategic redesign. The interface, while powerful, demands patience and experimentation. Some players may crave stronger guidance, clearer goals, or narrative framing to contextualize their actions. Performance may dip in massive late-game networks, and occasional rough edges remind players that Final Upgrade is the product of a small, evolving indie effort rather than a polished corporate release. But for players who enjoy wrestling with complexity, these challenges become part of the appeal rather than deterrents.

Final Upgrade ultimately succeeds because it respects the intelligence and curiosity of its audience. It does not simplify or shrink its systems to broaden its reach—it trusts players to rise to the challenge, to experiment, fail, redesign, and gradually transform a handful of miners into a galaxy-spanning industrial civilization. It offers the fantasy of engineering mastery rather than conquest, and derives excitement not from explosions or scripted drama, but from witnessing a perfectly optimized supply chain operate across light-years. Few games capture the thrill of creation on such a grand, systemic scale.

For fans of automation sandboxes, complex management simulations, modular ship design, and open-ended strategy, Final Upgrade offers a deep, demanding, and immensely satisfying experience—one that rewards long-term thinking, analytical problem-solving, and a fascination with interconnected systems. It asks for time, patience, and commitment, but it gives players a galaxy that becomes unmistakably theirs—built not through brute force, but through elegant efficiency.

Final Upgrade, developed and published by TaosX, is a sprawling automation and space-strategy sandbox that imagines the galaxy not as a battlefield or a frontier of exploration, but as a massive industrial canvas waiting to be systematized. It takes the core appeal of factory-building games—the joy of turning raw matter into complex, self-sustaining production webs—and expands it outward until production chains span star systems, planets, and entire fleets of player-designed ships. Instead of embodying a conquering empire or a wandering explorer, the player becomes an architect of interconnected processes, building a mechanized civilization whose strength comes not from firepower, but from logistics, efficiency, and relentless technological momentum. It is a game that asks players to think like an engineer rather than a commander, designing not just individual machines, but the invisible flow between them.

The early game introduces this vision through modest beginnings. A handful of vessels mine asteroid fields, basic crafting modules produce simple components, and the player learns how to automate fundamental resource loops—metal refining, energy distribution, storage allocation, and material transport. What differentiates Final Upgrade from genre peers is how quickly even these early systems reveal hidden layers of complexity. Ships are not static vessels purchased from a menu—they are modular constructions whose internal layout, propulsion arrangement, cargo space, and computing capacity directly influence their performance. A transport freighter with a poorly optimized interior may bottleneck an entire supply chain, and a miner lacking structural support may operate inefficiently. This design philosophy—where every detail matters—establishes a satisfying sense of authorship over the entire industrial ecosystem.

As the player expands into new star systems, the scale increases dramatically. Individual planets become specialized industrial centers, contributing unique resources or manufacturing capabilities. Trade routes must be automated and safeguarded. Energy production, once a simple concern, becomes a galaxy-wide balancing act. Supply shortages in one sector cascade into distant manufacturing delays, forcing players to monitor and redesign logistics networks rather than merely placing more machines. The game thrives on these emergent problems—never scripted, always systemic—giving progression a natural, self-generated rhythm. Instead of waiting for a quest or objective, players feel pushed onward by the consequences of their own decisions, and victory comes from taming the chaos they created.

The technological progression system reinforces this expanding scope. Research is not just a checklist of upgrades—it unlocks new building blocks for creative engineering, new transportation models, new hull designs, new automation tools, and ultimately, the ability to construct staggering megastructures that reshape entire star systems. Mastery comes not from grinding resources, but from learning how to integrate these technologies into existing infrastructure without destabilizing what already works. Late-game empires become dizzyingly complex organisms—ships ferrying advanced components between orbital foundries, refineries feeding terraforming operations, computational stations optimizing traffic, and automated fleets maintaining the entire industrial lattice with minimal player oversight. Watching the galaxy run itself becomes one of Final Upgrade’s most rewarding endgame experiences.

Visually, the game embraces clarity over spectacle. Its interface-driven presentation favors crisp icons, readable galaxy maps, and clean ship schematics, ensuring that even enormous networks remain understandable at a glance. Planets, starfields, and nebulae provide atmosphere, but never overshadow the informational backbone that drives decision-making. The soundscape reinforces this functional beauty—soft ambient hums, mechanical pulses, and understated musical themes that support long sessions without fatigue. It’s a game built for focus rather than adrenaline, and its aesthetic choices reflect that purpose.

However, the game’s ambition also creates barriers. The learning curve can feel imposing, especially for players unfamiliar with automation or logistical simulations. Tutorials help, but they cannot fully prepare players for the complexity that unfolds hours later, when intersystem production chains require troubleshooting and strategic redesign. The interface, while powerful, demands patience and experimentation. Some players may crave stronger guidance, clearer goals, or narrative framing to contextualize their actions. Performance may dip in massive late-game networks, and occasional rough edges remind players that Final Upgrade is the product of a small, evolving indie effort rather than a polished corporate release. But for players who enjoy wrestling with complexity, these challenges become part of the appeal rather than deterrents.

Final Upgrade ultimately succeeds because it respects the intelligence and curiosity of its audience. It does not simplify or shrink its systems to broaden its reach—it trusts players to rise to the challenge, to experiment, fail, redesign, and gradually transform a handful of miners into a galaxy-spanning industrial civilization. It offers the fantasy of engineering mastery rather than conquest, and derives excitement not from explosions or scripted drama, but from witnessing a perfectly optimized supply chain operate across light-years. Few games capture the thrill of creation on such a grand, systemic scale.

For fans of automation sandboxes, complex management simulations, modular ship design, and open-ended strategy, Final Upgrade offers a deep, demanding, and immensely satisfying experience—one that rewards long-term thinking, analytical problem-solving, and a fascination with interconnected systems. It asks for time, patience, and commitment, but it gives players a galaxy that becomes unmistakably theirs—built not through brute force, but through elegant efficiency.

It offers the fantasy of engineering mastery rather than conquest, and derives excitement not from explosions or scripted drama, but from witnessing a perfectly optimized supply chain operate across light-years. Few games capture the thrill of creation on such a grand, systemic scale.

For fans of automation sandboxes, complex management simulations, modular ship design, and open-ended strategy, Final Upgrade offers a deep, demanding, and immensely satisfying experience—one that rewards long-term thinking, analytical problem-solving, and a fascination with interconnected systems. It asks for time, patience, and commitment, but it gives players a galaxy that becomes unmistakably theirs—built not through brute force, but through elegant efficiency.

Final Upgrade, developed and published by TaosX, is a sprawling automation and space-strategy sandbox that imagines the galaxy not as a battlefield or a frontier of exploration, but as a massive industrial canvas waiting to be systematized. It takes the core appeal of factory-building games—the joy of turning raw matter into complex, self-sustaining production webs—and expands it outward until production chains span star systems, planets, and entire fleets of player-designed ships. Instead of embodying a conquering empire or a wandering explorer, the player becomes an architect of interconnected processes, building a mechanized civilization whose strength comes not from firepower, but from logistics, efficiency, and relentless technological momentum. It is a game that asks players to think like an engineer rather than a commander, designing not just individual machines, but the invisible flow between them.

The early game introduces this vision through modest beginnings. A handful of vessels mine asteroid fields, basic crafting modules produce simple components, and the player learns how to automate fundamental resource loops—metal refining, energy distribution, storage allocation, and material transport. What differentiates Final Upgrade from genre peers is how quickly even these early systems reveal hidden layers of complexity. Ships are not static vessels purchased from a menu—they are modular constructions whose internal layout, propulsion arrangement, cargo space, and computing capacity directly influence their performance. A transport freighter with a poorly optimized interior may bottleneck an entire supply chain, and a miner lacking structural support may operate inefficiently. This design philosophy—where every detail matters—establishes a satisfying sense of authorship over the entire industrial ecosystem.

As the player expands into new star systems, the scale increases dramatically. Individual planets become specialized industrial centers, contributing unique resources or manufacturing capabilities. Trade routes must be automated and safeguarded. Energy production, once a simple concern, becomes a galaxy-wide balancing act. Supply shortages in one sector cascade into distant manufacturing delays, forcing players to monitor and redesign logistics networks rather than merely placing more machines. The game thrives on these emergent problems—never scripted, always systemic—giving progression a natural, self-generated rhythm. Instead of waiting for a quest or objective, players feel pushed onward by the consequences of their own decisions, and victory comes from taming the chaos they created.

The technological progression system reinforces this expanding scope. Research is not just a checklist of upgrades—it unlocks new building blocks for creative engineering, new transportation models, new hull designs, new automation tools, and ultimately, the ability to construct staggering megastructures that reshape entire star systems. Mastery comes not from grinding resources, but from learning how to integrate these technologies into existing infrastructure without destabilizing what already works. Late-game empires become dizzyingly complex organisms—ships ferrying advanced components between orbital foundries, refineries feeding terraforming operations, computational stations optimizing traffic, and automated fleets maintaining the entire industrial lattice with minimal player oversight. Watching the galaxy run itself becomes one of Final Upgrade’s most rewarding endgame experiences.

Visually, the game embraces clarity over spectacle. Its interface-driven presentation favors crisp icons, readable galaxy maps, and clean ship schematics, ensuring that even enormous networks remain understandable at a glance. Planets, starfields, and nebulae provide atmosphere, but never overshadow the informational backbone that drives decision-making. The soundscape reinforces this functional beauty—soft ambient hums, mechanical pulses, and understated musical themes that support long sessions without fatigue. It’s a game built for focus rather than adrenaline, and its aesthetic choices reflect that purpose.

However, the game’s ambition also creates barriers. The learning curve can feel imposing, especially for players unfamiliar with automation or logistical simulations. Tutorials help, but they cannot fully prepare players for the complexity that unfolds hours later, when intersystem production chains require troubleshooting and strategic redesign. The interface, while powerful, demands patience and experimentation. Some players may crave stronger guidance, clearer goals, or narrative framing to contextualize their actions. Performance may dip in massive late-game networks, and occasional rough edges remind players that Final Upgrade is the product of a small, evolving indie effort rather than a polished corporate release. But for players who enjoy wrestling with complexity, these challenges become part of the appeal rather than deterrents.

Disclosure up front:  This is my first Gal Civ game.  A lot of the negative reviews I've seen seem to be centered on the experience returning/veteran players are having with this game.   The consensus seems to generally follow that GC4 is not enough of an advancement on GC3 to justify paying for it if you already have previous entries in the series.  I'd like to acknowledge the existence of this consensus opinion and concede that it is not mutually exclusive with anything I've written here.

So, with that caveat, I will no longer bury the lede:  I have immensely enjoyed my time with this game.  As an avowed fan of the turn based 4X genre, Gal Civ presents a deep, satisfying mesh of interlocking systems that will largely be familiar to genre vets:  you start small, you claim planets (our sci fi re skin of cities) the planets get you more stuff so you can more easily build fleets to get more resources etc

Some of the more unique mechanics to this particular 4x iteration took me a game to wrap my head around.
 Anecdotal Example:  building mining starbases early and often is crucial for securing an income of strategic resources.  I built absolutely none of these until fairly late in the game, instead leveraging my civilization's advanced science to trade of technologies I'd researched to other civilizations in exchange for the resources, and since I was on an easy difficulty for my first game (with a lot of civilizations on the map) this actually worked out.  I've included the full anecdote because I think it also exemplifies some of the fun ways systems can overlap in this game!

My main critique is the battle layer.  I feel like the 4X genre has progressed past the simple days of old where you just make the two armies smash against each other until one is gone.  So many other entries in this genre have found ways to make the battle layer more interesting. For Example: 

Proxy studios games like Zephon or 40k Gladius have heavy combat focus and do not separate units fighting into another layer and keep one unit per tile, so that the movement layer is also simultaneously a tactical positioning layer.  This can get very VERY convoluted in the late game when there are tons of units out, but it is generally engaging.

But a LOOOOOOOOOT of games have a separate tactical layer for battles, like Endless Legends, Humankind, and at least one other game in my library at this very moment have similar but distinct ways of transitioning the player into a tactical positioning layer when a battle starts to add more depth to combat strategy than just "make fleet have big number smash into other fleet"

And with a game with so much depth to its mechanics nearly everywhere else I find myself disappointed by the simplicity of the combat system, especially given how much emphasis is put on the ability to design your own ships.  What's the point of designing my ships if it just means that when I choose to watch the generated battle video, the attack animations look slightly different?

Overall though, definitely recommend for players new to the series.  Vets should seek out reviews by returning players to view the aformentioned negative-leaning consensus from that group

[b]Galactic Civilizations IV: Supernova Edition[/b] is the expanded and definitive version of [i]Galactic Civilizations IV[/i], released on October 19, 2023. Developed and published by [i]Stardock Entertainment[/i], it aims to refine the base game and introduce substantial new mechanics, content, and AI-driven features.

One of [b]Galactic Civilizations IV: Supernova Edition[/b]’s most notable additions is [i]AlienGPT[/i] — an AI-powered tool that uses generative AI to create civilizations, lore, dialogue, quests, and even custom graphics based on player prompts. This is a distinctive feature in the genre, aimed at boosting customization and emergent narrative play.

Multiplayer (co-op and competitive) and a more integrated modding system allow players to share factions, ship designs, and custom content, broadening longevity and community engagement. 

[b]Galactic Civilizations IV: Supernova Edition[/b] also emphasizes improved onboarding with enhanced tutorials and UI/context guidance to help new players approach the complexity of 4X strategy more comfortably. [b]Galactic Civilizations IV: Supernova Edition[/b] is a substantial and ambitious upgrade over its base release. Worth purchasing for dedicated 4X players and series fans; a good value for its depth and features, with the caveat of complexity and pacing.

I haven’t finished my first campaign yet, but I wanted to leave an early review.

I’m a big fan of space strategy games — Stellaris, Endless Space 2, and others — and I probably have well over a thousand hours across the genre.

So how is Galactic Civilizations IV?

Honestly: much better than I expected. The reviews seem somewhat mixed, but having played a lot of space strategy games, I can say with confidence that this game is genuinely fun. It doesn’t try to reinvent the genre or do anything wild, but it does enough things well — and differently enough — to absolutely be worth trying.

(For clarity: I played the base game without DLC, since the sale ended before I could pick them up, but I do plan to try them later.)

What really stands out to me is how open the map feels. Many space games rely on lanes or fixed paths, which can be fun and practical, but often make space feel smaller or more constrained. Gal Civ IV goes in a different direction. Instead of following predefined routes, you’re operating in open space — sometimes across multiple theatres — where you freely explore, colonise, fight, and build stations. It genuinely feels more space-like, and I’ve missed that.

The game doesn’t excel at everything — or even at many things — but I actually think that’s fine. It does most things well, and that adds up to a very enjoyable experience. There are areas that could be smoother or more polished, but nothing felt fundamentally broken or frustrating.

I also really enjoyed the frequent in-game events. They add flavour and help the universe feel more alive, rather than just being a static map you optimise.

My biggest complaint is the UI. There’s a lot of double-clicking, accidental clicking, and small interactions that can get tiring over time. It’s not terrible, but it’s clearly the weakest part of the experience, and I hope it continues to be refined.

Overall, I can genuinely recommend giving this game a try. It’s far better than its reputation might suggest, and if you enjoy space strategy games, there’s a very real chance this ends up becoming one of your favourite entries in the genre. It may not look flashy at first glance, but it’s a solid, thoughtful, and genuinely fun space strategy game that deserves more attention.

But it's hard to get into that system because it's very much unlike the base game. Still, I don't regret buying it, and I recommend it for everyone who enjoys the base game.

⭐ Source of Madness — A Positive Review
Source of Madness is a mesmerizing and deeply atmospheric roguelite that stands out through its surreal art direction, unpredictable encounters, and bold use of procedural generation. It’s a game that thrives on chaos in the best possible way, offering an experience that feels fresh, unsettling, and strangely beautiful every time you play.

A World That Feels Truly Alien
The game’s Lovecraft-inspired landscapes are haunting and hypnotic. Every biome feels like a living nightmare stitched together from impossible geometry and writhing organic shapes. It’s the kind of world that pulls you in with equal parts fascination and dread.

Procedural Monsters That Never Repeat
One of the game’s biggest strengths is its AI-driven creature generation. Enemies aren’t just reskinned variants — they’re bizarre, unpredictable abominations that move and attack in ways you don’t expect. This keeps every run tense and exciting, because you never quite know what you’re about to face.

Magic-Based Combat That Rewards Experimentation
Instead of traditional weapons, you wield a variety of spells and abilities that encourage creative play. The combat feels chaotic at first, but once you get into the rhythm, it becomes a satisfying dance of dodging, positioning, and unleashing devastating magical combos.

A Distinctive Visual Identity
The hand-drawn, painterly art style gives the game a unique personality. It’s grotesque, dreamlike, and unforgettable — a perfect match for the cosmic horror themes.

Verdict
Source of Madness is a bold, imaginative roguelite that embraces the weird and delivers something genuinely original. If you enjoy atmospheric worlds, experimental design, and games that constantly surprise you, this one is absolutely worth your time.

I really didn't expect this game to look and play like this. Basically, you fight big monsters with ragdoll physics that clumsily crawl or fall towards you while hopelessly trying to attack you. And actually I don't dislike it. It makes them look even more grotesque and disgusting. But in terms of gameplay, that's pretty chaotic. In fact the most viable strategy is to constantly walk away while shooting, and hope you don't somehow get hit.

There are some concrete issues with the gameplay though. The jumping is on a face button while the aiming is on right stick, which is a bit inconvenient. And if you don't have a range weapon it's really difficult to play safely, because it's impossible to know how or when the enemy is able to hit you - but most of the time it isn't anyway lol, unless he has projectiles. In fact I find it weird that the devs wanted to make a Rogue-like out of this "thing". The genre usually requires a lot of precision to justify the perma-death. But after all why not make real games out of experimental projects like this, and the progression system is actually pretty dense and well crafted, with various character classes etc.

Also I really like the graphics style, very grim and colorless but still beautiful, especially in the city that serves as a hub, where various lifeless characters provide some bits of lore. In fact both the graphics and the ragdoll enemies remind me of the game Rain World, also very chaotic in its own way. Anyway, I recommend this game but don't expect an usual skill-based Rogue-like. It's mostly curiosity that will drive you through the game.

I like this game, it has probably one of the most satisfying sound designs I have ever heard... seriously, I'm in love with the menu and vendor sounds!

The combat isn't really that satisfying, which is in my opinion the biggest shortcoming of this game.
Suffice to say though, it's fun to play!

However I'd advise getting this when it's on sale, since $19.99 is a bit more on the expensive side when it comes to the amount of content and playability there is.

[h1]Strange, Beautiful, and a Somewhat Broken[/h1]


I’m someone who loves Lovecraftian horror, aesthetics, and stories that revolve around cosmic dread; that was the main reason I was drawn to this title.
If there’s “AI slop” there can also be “AI kino” and in my opinion, this game falls into the latter category. Still, I completely understand why people who see “AI” involved might be turned off. After finishing it 100%, here are my thoughts on the positives and negatives.


[h2]Positives[/h2]


[olist]
	[*][b]Combat:[/b] The combat can feel clunky at first, but as you progress, it starts to make sense. Once you understand the system, you can fully exploit your spells and items for maximum effect.
	[*][b]Surreal art and atmosphere:[/b] The art direction is outstanding. It creates an alien, dreamlike feeling... at times it feels like being trapped in a surreal acid trip. The world feels strange and mysterious, even a little unsettling, which perfectly matches the theme. The sense of dread fades a bit later on, but the early atmosphere made me want to keep exploring and learn more about the world.
	[*][b]Music: [/b]The soundtrack enhances that surreal tone beautifully and complements the visuals.
	[*][b]Normal difficulty:[/b] The normal mode feels well balanced. Acts 1 and 2 are fairly easy, but by Act 3 the challenge ramps up nicely, especially during the boss fight.
	[*][b]Enemy variety:[/b] Early on, enemies look and behave quite differently, with various limbs, tentacles, and attack styles. It keeps things interesting, though this variety starts to fade the longer you play.
	[*][b]Spells and items:[/b] There’s a great selection of spells and items, and you can even tweak drop rates if you prefer fewer of them. Some spells are clearly stronger than others, but experimenting is genuinely fun. As you progress, you unlock more options through the skill tree system.
	[*][b]Lore:[/b] The story and world are mysterious and intriguing, leaving a lot to the imagination... though there are aspects of this that I’ll touch on in the negatives.
[/olist]


[h2]Negatives[/h2]


[olist]
	[*][b]Controls: [/b]The controls are inconsistent and clunky at first. Once you adapt, it’s mostly fine, but the learning curve can be frustrating.
	[*][b]Nightmare and Insanity difficulties: [/b]These settings mostly just turn enemies into damage sponges that hit harder. They don’t add real challenge, so I recommend sticking with normal mode.
	[*][b]Inconsistent enemy melee attacks: [/b]Sometimes enemies make contact but deal no damage, which can break immersion.
	[*][b]Weird ragdoll behavior: [/b]Enemies occasionally move erratically, launching themselves several meters into the air or floating overhead... unintentionally funny, but odd.
	[*][b]Boss variety: [/b]There are very few bosses, which is disappointing because the ones that do exist are genuinely cool.
	[*][b]Curses:[/b] Curses don’t feel punishing enough to meaningfully affect gameplay.
	[*][b]Empty hub: [/b]The tower feels like it should hold secrets or NPCs, but it’s mostly empty aside from a few lore snippets from cultists. It gives the illusion of depth without much payoff.
	[*][b]Lore (again):[/b] While mystery is core to Lovecraftian storytelling, the game sometimes leans too heavily into vagueness. The world could use more fleshing out, and the Chapter 3 ending feels unsatisfying. Even after completing it, I expected something new to unlock in the castle... but nothing did. Like if the game got abandoned (probably is the case)
[/olist]


[h2]Final Thoughts[/h2]


Despite feeling a somewhat incomplete and having areas that could be expanded, this game still delivers a great atmosphere, distinctive art style, and enjoyable gameplay once you adjust to its quirks. It captures that unsettling Lovecraftian tone in a way that few games manage.


[h3]Final Score: 7.5 / 10[/h3]
