[h1]Strange, Beautiful, and a Somewhat Broken[/h1]


I’m someone who loves Lovecraftian horror, aesthetics, and stories that revolve around cosmic dread; that was the main reason I was drawn to this title.
If there’s “AI slop” there can also be “AI kino” and in my opinion, this game falls into the latter category. Still, I completely understand why people who see “AI” involved might be turned off. After finishing it 100%, here are my thoughts on the positives and negatives.


[h2]Positives[/h2]


[olist]
	[*][b]Combat:[/b] The combat can feel clunky at first, but as you progress, it starts to make sense. Once you understand the system, you can fully exploit your spells and items for maximum effect.
	[*][b]Surreal art and atmosphere:[/b] The art direction is outstanding. It creates an alien, dreamlike feeling... at times it feels like being trapped in a surreal acid trip. The world feels strange and mysterious, even a little unsettling, which perfectly matches the theme. The sense of dread fades a bit later on, but the early atmosphere made me want to keep exploring and learn more about the world.
	[*][b]Music: [/b]The soundtrack enhances that surreal tone beautifully and complements the visuals.
	[*][b]Normal difficulty:[/b] The normal mode feels well balanced. Acts 1 and 2 are fairly easy, but by Act 3 the challenge ramps up nicely, especially during the boss fight.
	[*][b]Enemy variety:[/b] Early on, enemies look and behave quite differently, with various limbs, tentacles, and attack styles. It keeps things interesting, though this variety starts to fade the longer you play.
	[*][b]Spells and items:[/b] There’s a great selection of spells and items, and you can even tweak drop rates if you prefer fewer of them. Some spells are clearly stronger than others, but experimenting is genuinely fun. As you progress, you unlock more options through the skill tree system.
	[*][b]Lore:[/b] The story and world are mysterious and intriguing, leaving a lot to the imagination... though there are aspects of this that I’ll touch on in the negatives.
[/olist]


[h2]Negatives[/h2]


[olist]
	[*][b]Controls: [/b]The controls are inconsistent and clunky at first. Once you adapt, it’s mostly fine, but the learning curve can be frustrating.
	[*][b]Nightmare and Insanity difficulties: [/b]These settings mostly just turn enemies into damage sponges that hit harder. They don’t add real challenge, so I recommend sticking with normal mode.
	[*][b]Inconsistent enemy melee attacks: [/b]Sometimes enemies make contact but deal no damage, which can break immersion.
	[*][b]Weird ragdoll behavior: [/b]Enemies occasionally move erratically, launching themselves several meters into the air or floating overhead... unintentionally funny, but odd.
	[*][b]Boss variety: [/b]There are very few bosses, which is disappointing because the ones that do exist are genuinely cool.
	[*][b]Curses:[/b] Curses don’t feel punishing enough to meaningfully affect gameplay.
	[*][b]Empty hub: [/b]The tower feels like it should hold secrets or NPCs, but it’s mostly empty aside from a few lore snippets from cultists. It gives the illusion of depth without much payoff.
	[*][b]Lore (again):[/b] While mystery is core to Lovecraftian storytelling, the game sometimes leans too heavily into vagueness. The world could use more fleshing out, and the Chapter 3 ending feels unsatisfying. Even after completing it, I expected something new to unlock in the castle... but nothing did. Like if the game got abandoned (probably is the case)
[/olist]


[h2]Final Thoughts[/h2]


Despite feeling a somewhat incomplete and having areas that could be expanded, this game still delivers a great atmosphere, distinctive art style, and enjoyable gameplay once you adjust to its quirks. It captures that unsettling Lovecraftian tone in a way that few games manage.


[h3]Final Score: 7.5 / 10[/h3]

Если вам интересна боёвка в духе Баротравмы, но на суше и с турбированным персонажем — эта игра про неё. Вайб от графония аналогичный — безумный коллаж жипегов. Неплохое звуковое оформление, хоть и у монстров звуки все +- одинаковые.

Алсо рекомендую как можно быстрее идти на сложность выше нормальной, потому что там включаются противники поинтереснее, и вроде быыыы новые сегменты для генерации карты.

Разработчику бы порекомендовал пройтись ещё раз по первому биому и улучшить текстуры, потому что на фоне других биомов он выглядит более стрёмно. В принципе видно, как с каждым новым биомом игра становится лучше визуально. Тажке было бы круто увидеть больше интересностей в башне знаний и чтобы там что-то происходило по мере прохождения. Плюс при интенсивности боёвки будто бы камеру раза в полтора отдалить надо.

it's a 6.5/10 for me.

⭐ Source of Madness — A Positive Review
Source of Madness is a mesmerizing and deeply atmospheric roguelite that stands out through its surreal art direction, unpredictable encounters, and bold use of procedural generation. It’s a game that thrives on chaos in the best possible way, offering an experience that feels fresh, unsettling, and strangely beautiful every time you play.

A World That Feels Truly Alien
The game’s Lovecraft-inspired landscapes are haunting and hypnotic. Every biome feels like a living nightmare stitched together from impossible geometry and writhing organic shapes. It’s the kind of world that pulls you in with equal parts fascination and dread.

Procedural Monsters That Never Repeat
One of the game’s biggest strengths is its AI-driven creature generation. Enemies aren’t just reskinned variants — they’re bizarre, unpredictable abominations that move and attack in ways you don’t expect. This keeps every run tense and exciting, because you never quite know what you’re about to face.

Magic-Based Combat That Rewards Experimentation
Instead of traditional weapons, you wield a variety of spells and abilities that encourage creative play. The combat feels chaotic at first, but once you get into the rhythm, it becomes a satisfying dance of dodging, positioning, and unleashing devastating magical combos.

A Distinctive Visual Identity
The hand-drawn, painterly art style gives the game a unique personality. It’s grotesque, dreamlike, and unforgettable — a perfect match for the cosmic horror themes.

Verdict
Source of Madness is a bold, imaginative roguelite that embraces the weird and delivers something genuinely original. If you enjoy atmospheric worlds, experimental design, and games that constantly surprise you, this one is absolutely worth your time.

I really didn't expect this game to look and play like this. Basically, you fight big monsters with ragdoll physics that clumsily crawl or fall towards you while hopelessly trying to attack you. And actually I don't dislike it. It makes them look even more grotesque and disgusting. But in terms of gameplay, that's pretty chaotic. In fact the most viable strategy is to constantly walk away while shooting, and hope you don't somehow get hit.

There are some concrete issues with the gameplay though. The jumping is on a face button while the aiming is on right stick, which is a bit inconvenient. And if you don't have a range weapon it's really difficult to play safely, because it's impossible to know how or when the enemy is able to hit you - but most of the time it isn't anyway lol, unless he has projectiles. In fact I find it weird that the devs wanted to make a Rogue-like out of this "thing". The genre usually requires a lot of precision to justify the perma-death. But after all why not make real games out of experimental projects like this, and the progression system is actually pretty dense and well crafted, with various character classes etc.

Also I really like the graphics style, very grim and colorless but still beautiful, especially in the city that serves as a hub, where various lifeless characters provide some bits of lore. In fact both the graphics and the ragdoll enemies remind me of the game Rain World, also very chaotic in its own way. Anyway, I recommend this game but don't expect an usual skill-based Rogue-like. It's mostly curiosity that will drive you through the game.

What i like: Realistic graphics, so far i have no lag, easy to learn, animals have unique perspectives and playstyles, a large selection of animals, and a nice growth system, just remember to actually grow into the next stage once at 1.0, the game can give some laughs.

What i dislike: Lion/croc/leo for carni and Rhino/hippo for herbi is meta, everything else doesnt get a chance, its fun a group of lions comes along and obliterates you. Dying over and over can be frustrating, but thankfully it doesnt always happen.

TIPS: as a new player, i suggest leopard or barb lion. birds cant fly until sub adult, you need to hit esc and click grow when it says growth 1.0. crocs can attack behind them with RMB and in front of them with LMB, attacking with RMB doesnt have an animation, though.

NOTES: im fairly new myself, and i got the hang of it quickly, if you decide to get the game, you will too, and you can always ask for help in global, however you might not get it, you miss all shots you dont take.

AIdventure is the best offering in the field of "AI driven text adventures." No monthly fees, a wide variety of AI models including KoboldAI and Chat-GPT, all your stories are private until you share them yourself, and best of all, the developers care! Updates frequently, answers questions in a helpful manner, and all in all just great people that deserve respect!

'Nuff said!

First off, I love the idea of this. I really do. It's a new take on the choose-your-own-ending books I used to read as a kid, but with the major difference being that it's a different experience each time. It is still new, so the AI interaction still needs some training, and I imagine there will be more ai options and stories over time. The AI seemed hell bent of making us to most of the story writing, which seems a little counter productive, you'd figure your prompts wouild encourage the next section of story, though it often seemed like the story only moved forward if we moved it forward. Again, that's likely just AI training and the more we play, the better it will respond. GREAT idea, turning into a great game, and I would recommend. (PS: An untrained AI writes some HILARIOUS stuff though... just sayin'!)

You not only have total freedom, meaning you can control what the AI does (for example, how many words can it say if you click "Generate"), but you also have 14-16 different AIs. I have to admit, the AI you choose can sometimes be too...annoying, but you can FULLY customize what the AI says.

Want to have a story-rich, deep and sophisticated world/RP, where every turn generated leaves you begging to know more? Want to have a full blown out one-hand experience to the point where you don't feel your body? YOU HAVE IT.

The newest experimental branch HEAVILY optimized the bot, it takes a few SECONDS for it to generate text. I love you Lya mwah

This is the perfect AI text game I was looking for.

Its basically AI Dungeon on your own PC without censorship with the character attributes and a 'TRY' prompt much like a dungeons and dragons table top style strength check. I became aware that one can download one of the GTP2 pre-trained models directly and just run the thing in a command window but its not very neat and you need to make use of various /commands. From what I understand this is basically a GUI wrapper with the same sort of functionality (at least now) with the addition of the previous mentioned TRY [attribute] text prompts ... which is really cool. You also generate a attribute sheet on a new character with the ability to randomize them all or manually input a value (the try system is based of percentile to your attributes value and it only comes into play when you do one of the challenges).

My system: i7-9700K factory OCed to 4.5ghtz, 32 GB of DDR5 ram, RTX 3060 12gb, installed SSD drive read/write of 559/524 with a random read/write of 368/297. The biggest one is a 25gb memory model that more or less maxes my ram and cpu but works well. The 16gb AI ones run without maxing my hardware so that is best for my system.

Another AI game I have and recomend is AI Rougelite on steam here which is a MUD-style game with AI image generation, items and skills/spells presented to be more of a dungeon crawler (also that runs off your GPU only). This one is more about stories and can run off your CPU and ram. I am VERY happy with each. This one is the PERFECT alternative to AI Dungeon with capabilities to perform a bit better then AI Dungeon's free Griffon model at the 25gb ones.

This one is new and in early access but it runs purely off GPT2 so it'll perform and run just like that. My hope is that the dev adds more custom bells and whistles to this as time goes on. Maybe expanding the TRY interaction system somehow. As it is now? You can run this out of the box in its current state just fine.

I give AIdventure a healthy 4.5 out of 5

It was already quite good for what I paid. But the new experimental branch has mad it 100 times better. It generates text much faster now that it splits the load between your CPU and GPU. It's a great alternative to AIDungeon and NovelAI at a good price and 100% guaranteed privacy since it's your own machine doing the work.

Pros:
Automatically downloads relevant GPT models from the internet. Nice interface.
Better the ai dungeon even if its a bit buggy

AIDventure,

Does some things that A.I. Dungeon doesn't let you do. And that's pretty cool.
One of those things is the "Ask another generation". This allows you to request another suggestion made by the system without losing the former ones. This means that you can just compare them to eachother and edit and pick the one you like the most.

Besides that, the pricing is okay for what you get and for what's possible so far.
Yes, you can do the same things as with all those other big shot engines, but it will take time for your low life rig to create the output. Just be patient and play around with the settings until you find something you can work with.
It is a fun idea and if you are just looking for an easy way to setup an ai chat on your computer it is great at that. It is easy to use and quite configurable. Please read the requirements though.

It's well worth buying as a budget bargain. A promising entry for anyone new to AI stories and adventuring. Developer has made a good start with this and has said that a tutorial and nicer interface is being worked on too. This would make this project a cheap and ideal beginner option that doesn't rely upon subscription services. I think over time this will make itself distinct as an option compared to vanilla KoboldAI and that will be a good thing for people to have increased options. Currently in Early Access, I look forward to hopefully updating this review with more good points on the features and abilities of this package in the future too.

Let me start this off by saying that I have known Lyaaaaa, been a mod for the discord and done the (admittingly improvable) German translations for a while now and I've used it for a long time before from itch, so I am probably a bit biased, but let me tell you that, for me personally, this has been my definitive alternative to Aidungeon and NovelAi if you don't want a subscription and cloud based solution.

AIDungeons practices are shady and NovelAI ist amazing but pricy. I'm not willing to spend 15 bucks a month for what it is.
AIdventure shifts the workload onto your computer, so there is no price to "rent" the software, allowing for a one-time purchase.
It has most features that I'd want for an AI-Story generator (and AI RPG if you prefer that, it can do both) that I want and with the lore book, most of what I've wanted is already in the game.
But that's not all, Lyaaaaa is extremely hard working, constantly adding new and unique features and fixing bugs and always has an open ear for users with suggestion or problems. For example the recent addition of the lorebook was originally an idea I pitched, because I felt it essential to make it generate more comprehensive stories, and a couple of months later Lyaaaaa had implemented a first draft for it.
And believe me when I tell you that there are even more awesome ideas floating around that will make this software even more unique.

The only other software comparable is KoboldAI and its various open source forks, and that's free and community driven. So why buy this and not just use a KoboldAI fork?
In my opinion with Lyaaaaa as a developer, who's constantly trying to improve performance and  the cool ideas floating and the ambition for the software to constantly grow and add more features, the money is well worth it for me and I can recommend this without a second thought.

Its basically AI Dungeon on your own PC without censorship with the character attributes and a 'TRY' prompt much like a dungeons and dragons table top style strength check. I became aware that one can download one of the GTP2 pre-trained models directly and just run the thing in a command window but its not very neat and you need to make use of various /commands. From what I understand this is basically a GUI wrapper with the same sort of functionality (at least now) with the addition of the previous mentioned TRY [attribute] text prompts ... which is really cool. You also generate a attribute sheet on a new character with the ability to randomize them all or manually input a value (the try system is based of percentile to your attributes value and it only comes into play when you do one of the challenges).

My system: i7-9700K factory OCed to 4.5ghtz, 32 GB of DDR5 ram, RTX 3060 12gb, installed SSD drive read/write of 559/524 with a random read/write of 368/297. The biggest one is a 25gb memory model that more or less maxes my ram and cpu but works well. The 16gb AI ones run without maxing my hardware so that is best for my system.

Another AI game I have and recomend is AI Rougelite on steam here which is a MUD-style game with AI image generation, items and skills/spells presented to be more of a dungeon crawler (also that runs off your GPU only). This one is more about stories and can run off your CPU and ram. I am VERY happy with each. This one is the PERFECT alternative to AI Dungeon with capabilities to perform a bit better then AI Dungeon's free Griffon model at the 25gb ones.

This one is new and in early access but it runs purely off GPT2 so it'll perform and run just like that. My hope is that the dev adds more custom bells and whistles to this as time goes on. Maybe expanding the TRY interaction system somehow. As it is now? You can run this out of the box in its current state just fine.

I give AIdventure a healthy 4.5 out of 5

It was already quite good for what I paid. But the new experimental branch has mad it 100 times better. It generates text much faster now that it splits the load between your CPU and GPU. It's a great alternative to AIDungeon and NovelAI at a good price and 100% guaranteed privacy since it's your own machine doing the work.

Pros:
Automatically downloads relevant GPT models from the internet. Nice interface.
Better the ai dungeon even if its a bit buggy

AIDventure,

Does some things that A.I. Dungeon doesn't let you do. And that's pretty cool.
One of those things is the "Ask another generation". This allows you to request another suggestion made by the system without losing the former ones. This means that you can just compare them to eachother and edit and pick the one you like the most.

Besides that, the pricing is okay for what you get and for what's possible so far.
Yes, you can do the same things as with all those other big shot engines, but it will take time for your low life rig to create the output. Just be patient and play around with the settings until you find something you can work with.
It is a fun idea and if you are just looking for an easy way to setup an ai chat on your computer it is great at that. It is easy to use and quite configurable. Please read the requirements though.

It's well worth buying as a budget bargain. A promising entry for anyone new to AI stories and adventuring. Developer has made a good start with this and has said that a tutorial and nicer interface is being worked on too. This would make this project a cheap and ideal beginner option that doesn't rely upon subscription services. I think over time this will make itself distinct as an option compared to vanilla KoboldAI and that will be a good thing for people to have increased options. Currently in Early Access, I look forward to hopefully updating this review with more good points on the features and abilities of this package in the future too.

Let me start this off by saying that I have known Lyaaaaa, been a mod for the discord and done the (admittingly improvable) German translations for a while now and I've used it for a long time before from itch, so I am probably a bit biased, but let me tell you that, for me personally, this has been my definitive alternative to Aidungeon and NovelAi if you don't want a subscription and cloud based solution.

AIDungeons practices are shady and NovelAI ist amazing but pricy. I'm not willing to spend 15 bucks a month for what it is.
AIdventure shifts the workload onto your computer, so there is no price to "rent" the software, allowing for a one-time purchase.
It has most features that I'd want for an AI-Story generator (and AI RPG if you prefer that, it can do both) that I want and with the lore book, most of what I've wanted is already in the game.
But that's not all, Lyaaaaa is extremely hard working, constantly adding new and unique features and fixing bugs and always has an open ear for users with suggestion or problems. For example the recent addition of the lorebook was originally an idea I pitched, because I felt it essential to make it generate more comprehensive stories, and a couple of months later Lyaaaaa had implemented a first draft for it.
And believe me when I tell you that there are even more awesome ideas floating around that will make this software even more unique.

The only other software comparable is KoboldAI and its various open source forks, and that's free and community driven. So why buy this and not just use a KoboldAI fork?
In my opinion with Lyaaaaa as a developer, who's constantly trying to improve performance and  the cool ideas floating and the ambition for the software to constantly grow and add more features, the money is well worth it for me and I can recommend this without a second thought.

If you're an AI Dungeon immigrate like me, you'll find AIdventure incredibly easy to get into and start making stories in no time at all. If not, the community hub has a great Guide that explains everything you need to get started.

AIdventure is the best offering in the field of "AI driven text adventures." No monthly fees, a wide variety of AI models including KoboldAI and Chat-GPT, all your stories are private until you share them yourself, and best of all, the developers care! Updates frequently, answers questions in a helpful manner, and all in all just great people that deserve respect!

'Nuff said!

Really awesome game! Really cool to just vibe to on 2nd monitor when playing the larger models because of the generation time. The only thing that would be nice is if it can finish the generation of text in a complete manner each time. Some models are better than that than others.

Amazing since it's a one time purchase unlike most AI text generation games.

I would really like to play this game, however I made the mistake of choosing an AMD GPU, which this game doesn't seem to support. : (

I can't really recommend it, mainly because I can't even play it due to my choice of GPU.. which honestly I regret even more now x.x

----- Update! -----

Leaving in the negative review from before just to show a contrast.

The Game Dev reached out to me and offered to help and they ended up figuring out a solution that now allows me to play this game. If anything, I recommend just because the dev themself seems to want to make this work and will work with their players to find solutions to problems with the game : )

I like it; custom stuff takes a lot to set up, but it is proving to be well worth the effort. Currently roaming around my own version of Gotham City, a little copy-paste work, a wiki here and there, toss it in the game lore book, and simmer!

There are some pre-generated adventures that are really cool, and you can modify those as well. Custom stuff can get as in-depth as the time you are willing to spend on creating the content. I have only had issues with one model so far; the other few I have used have been doing fine, and there is a good selection. All in all, this is turning out to be pretty cool.

Honestly, I think this game could have potential. The idea is there, but the execution definitely could do with some tweaking. Firstly, a tutorial would be great to help with better understanding the AI, character stats, etc. That being said, it is fun, and I think it would make a fun little edition to a D&D game if you wanted to try something different. So, overall, if I could give this game a neutral, I would. But, seeing as I can't, I will give it a positive as I think with some tweaking it could be a much better game.

First off, I love the idea of this. I really do. It's a new take on the choose-your-own-ending books I used to read as a kid, but with the major difference being that it's a different experience each time. It is still new, so the AI interaction still needs some training, and I imagine there will be more ai options and stories over time. The AI seemed hell bent of making us to most of the story writing, which seems a little counter productive, you'd figure your prompts wouild encourage the next section of story, though it often seemed like the story only moved forward if we moved it forward. Again, that's likely just AI training and the more we play, the better it will respond. GREAT idea, turning into a great game, and I would recommend. (PS: An untrained AI writes some HILARIOUS stuff though... just sayin'!)

You not only have total freedom, meaning you can control what the AI does (for example, how many words can it say if you click "Generate"), but you also have 14-16 different AIs. I have to admit, the AI you choose can sometimes be too...annoying, but you can FULLY customize what the AI says.

Want to have a story-rich, deep and sophisticated world/RP, where every turn generated leaves you begging to know more? Want to have a full blown out one-hand experience to the point where you don't feel your body? YOU HAVE IT.

The newest experimental branch HEAVILY optimized the bot, it takes a few SECONDS for it to generate text. I love you Lya mwah
Is it possible to add automatic Chinese translation, I would like to recommend it to my Chinese friends

World of Fate, developed and published by NIK’s Games, is an ambitious strategy sandbox that aims to merge grand-scale empire building with real-world geography. Rather than placing players in a fictional map or a procedurally generated world, the game’s defining feature is its use of real street and building data, allowing players to establish their empire in actual cities across the globe. This concept immediately sets World of Fate apart from conventional strategy titles, promising a sense of scale and personal connection that comes from conquering and managing locations that exist in reality. It is a bold idea, and one that shapes every aspect of the experience, for better and for worse.

Gameplay begins by selecting any real-world building as the foundation of your faction. From there, expansion is carried out by capturing nearby structures and assigning them roles within your growing domain. Buildings can become housing, production centers, storage facilities, or military outposts, forming the backbone of your economy and territorial control. As your holdings expand, you organize them into sectors, rename areas, and gradually transform scattered buildings into something resembling a coherent empire. This process feels deliberately methodical, emphasizing planning and long-term organization rather than rapid conquest or constant conflict.

Resource management and development sit at the core of progression. Captured buildings generate income and population, and growth depends on how effectively you balance expansion with infrastructure. There is a strong administrative flavor to the gameplay, as much of your time is spent managing sectors, assigning functions, and optimizing layouts. Military units and squads can be trained to defend territory or push outward, but in its current form, combat and conflict feel secondary to expansion and management. The strategic challenge lies more in maintaining a sustainable empire than in outmaneuvering enemies on a battlefield.

The pace of World of Fate is slow and deliberate, particularly in the early stages. Progress can feel incremental, as claiming buildings and watching numbers rise lacks the immediate feedback found in more action-oriented strategy games. This can be appealing to players who enjoy patient, systems-driven gameplay, but it may feel tedious to those expecting dramatic events or frequent turning points. Tutorials and onboarding are minimal, which means new players often need to experiment extensively to understand how systems interact. This learning curve reinforces the game’s sandbox nature but also contributes to a sense of opacity that can be discouraging.

Visually, the game prioritizes function over flair. Maps are clear and readable, but largely utilitarian, relying on icons, overlays, and interface elements rather than detailed environments. The real-world map data gives cities recognizable layouts, which is intriguing from a conceptual standpoint, but the presentation itself is sparse. The interface communicates information effectively once understood, yet it lacks the polish and clarity that could make complex systems feel more approachable. As a result, immersion comes more from the imagination and the novelty of real locations than from audiovisual presentation.

A major aspect of the game’s identity is its Early Access status, which strongly influences how it should be approached. Many of the most enticing features—such as deeper political systems, large-scale wars, alliances, advanced AI factions, and more dynamic multiplayer interaction—are either partially implemented or planned for the future. In its current state, the game feels more like a foundation than a complete strategic experience. The core loop of expansion and management is present, but depth and variety are limited, leading to repetition once the initial novelty wears off.

Community reception reflects this tension between potential and execution. Players intrigued by the concept often praise the originality of using real-world geography and the freedom to start anywhere on the planet. At the same time, criticism frequently centers on a lack of content density, unclear progression, technical issues, and concerns about long-term support. For some, the experience feels closer to a proof of concept than a fully realized game, making it difficult to recommend without reservations.

Ultimately, World of Fate is a game defined by its ambition. The idea of building and managing a global empire rooted in real locations is genuinely compelling and opens the door to strategic storytelling that few games attempt. However, the current execution struggles to fully capitalize on that promise. It is best suited for players who enjoy slow, administrative strategy sandboxes and are willing to tolerate rough edges in exchange for experimental ideas. For those seeking a polished, content-rich strategy game with immediate depth and strong presentation, World of Fate may feel incomplete. As a long-term project with significant unrealized potential, it remains intriguing, but its value depends heavily on how much patience a player has for systems still in development.

As your holdings expand, you organize them into sectors, rename areas, and gradually transform scattered buildings into something resembling a coherent empire. This process feels deliberately methodical, emphasizing planning and long-term organization rather than rapid conquest or constant conflict.

Resource management and development sit at the core of progression. Captured buildings generate income and population, and growth depends on how effectively you balance expansion with infrastructure. There is a strong administrative flavor to the gameplay, as much of your time is spent managing sectors, assigning functions, and optimizing layouts. Military units and squads can be trained to defend territory or push outward, but in its current form, combat and conflict feel secondary to expansion and management. The strategic challenge lies more in maintaining a sustainable empire than in outmaneuvering enemies on a battlefield.

The pace of World of Fate is slow and deliberate, particularly in the early stages. Progress can feel incremental, as claiming buildings and watching numbers rise lacks the immediate feedback found in more action-oriented strategy games. This can be appealing to players who enjoy patient, systems-driven gameplay, but it may feel tedious to those expecting dramatic events or frequent turning points. Tutorials and onboarding are minimal, which means new players often need to experiment extensively to understand how systems interact. This learning curve reinforces the game’s sandbox nature but also contributes to a sense of opacity that can be discouraging.

Visually, the game prioritizes function over flair. Maps are clear and readable, but largely utilitarian, relying on icons, overlays, and interface elements rather than detailed environments. The real-world map data gives cities recognizable layouts, which is intriguing from a conceptual standpoint, but the presentation itself is sparse. The interface communicates information effectively once understood, yet it lacks the polish and clarity that could make complex systems feel more approachable. As a result, immersion comes more from the imagination and the novelty of real locations than from audiovisual presentation.

A major aspect of the game’s identity is its Early Access status, which strongly influences how it should be approached. Many of the most enticing features—such as deeper political systems, large-scale wars, alliances, advanced AI factions, and more dynamic multiplayer interaction—are either partially implemented or planned for the future. In its current state, the game feels more like a foundation than a complete strategic experience. The core loop of expansion and management is present, but depth and variety are limited, leading to repetition once the initial novelty wears off.

Community reception reflects this tension between potential and execution. Players intrigued by the concept often praise the originality of using real-world geography and the freedom to start anywhere on the planet. At the same time, criticism frequently centers on a lack of content density, unclear progression, technical issues, and concerns about long-term support. For some, the experience feels closer to a proof of concept than a fully realized game, making it difficult to recommend without reservations.

Ultimately, World of Fate is a game defined by its ambition. The idea of building and managing a global empire rooted in real locations is genuinely compelling and opens the door to strategic storytelling that few games attempt. However, the current execution struggles to fully capitalize on that promise. It is best suited for players who enjoy slow, administrative strategy sandboxes and are willing to tolerate rough edges in exchange for experimental ideas. For those seeking a polished, content-rich strategy game with immediate depth and strong presentation, World of Fate may feel incomplete. As a long-term project with significant unrealized potential, it remains intriguing, but its value depends heavily on how much patience a player has for systems still in development.

Moons of Ardan has quietly abducted a large chunk of my weekend, and I regret nothing.
You’re in charge of a small civilisation of adorable Axonauts, spreading across multiple planets and moons, setting up production chains, research, and interplanetary logistics. No pressure, just casual galaxy management.

The real joy comes from juggling planets that all need different resources, then calmly shipping things across space like a very relaxed cosmic supply manager. It scratches that “everything must connect” itch in the most satisfying way possible.

It’s still in Early Access, but in my experience it’s been surprisingly stable and already feels like there’s plenty to get stuck into. If you enjoy space, city builders, and watching a tiny civilisation thrive without everything immediately exploding, this one is well worth boarding.

Warning: you may look up and realise several hours have passed. Time behaves strangely in space.

TL;DR: It's not a DIY base builder, but it is a game about conflicting narratives on commoning. 

I feel Common'hood is one of those games that will be forever polarizing. Part of it comes from the silly bugs like bad pathfinding and questionable game design decisions. However, even if that was fixed in the near future, it will still be a controversial title.

Contrary to people's expectations and the articles/reviews written about it thus far, this game is not supposed to be some base builder. Rather, you are supposed to figure out what you're supposed to do. This is implicit in the narrative, the lack of tutorials, and the NPCs you recruit. Everyone is unsure what to do next and that's the "meat" of the story, so to speak. There's no hand-holding or clear narrative/gameplay arc.

You're supposed to be fumbling about and trying to get things sorted out.

The entire game is oriented around the difficulty of building a commons. There's characters who will call you out for your productivism and the more you open up the warehouse, the more you cause the squatters within the place to be more susceptible to the police. Very few tasks are actually directly related to your overall progression; all of them have to do with the specific, individual needs of (prospective) members of your commons. You are all figuring out what it means to build a commons in spite of capitalism.

In other words, you're trying to organize [i]something[/i]. But you're not sure how and why you're doing it. This game wants you to interrogate yourself and your own goals.

The game is great, even if it is admittedly frustrating at times. If you jive with the game, you'll dig what it's trying to do. I describe it as a thinking exercise on what it means to build an anticapitalist commons, with the jank of [i]Pathologic[/i] or [i]S.T.A.L.K.E.R[/i]. It's a hard sell, but it's great for those who can handle what it demands from you.

A weird metaphor of life and the world?

Don't get me wrong, it's not that I didn't like the game. On the contrary, I've been a huge fan of Block'hood (Plethora's previous work) and I've been eagerly awaiting this new product. It was a delight to find similarities between these two very different games.
While Block'hood is a neighbourhood builder, Common'hood is a survival game: you start as a girl who lost everything but her father's books and workbench, go to live in the occupied factory where your parents used to work, meet other squatters and team-up with them to create an alternative form of social construct.
The main focuses of the game are automating production and optimising energy, building better shelter for you and your friends, fulfilling their personal quests and exploring the whole factory to find out the truth of the terrible accident in which your parents lost their life.
Mechanics are simple (build things to build more things to automate building things). Underlying themes are complex.
And this is where I felt the game unfortunately fell short.
As a privileged white girl who studied engineering and owns a bunch of books, you get to bring "new energy" to the disillusioned community of other young squatters, and all it takes is a bit of studying and some scrap metals to create huge robotic arms that will print out brand new homes for the whole neighbourhood. I'm an optimistic by nature, but the level of socialist positivism is to blatant it got annoying even for me.
Still, I understand what they were trying to do and, don't get me wrong, I endorse the underlying political agenda. I only felt it should have been presented with a little more care to nuances, being such an important theme.
I'm not going to lie it is very buggy and power intensive, but it is truly one of my favorite games. It is a story-driven survival game.

If you like building, crafting, and exploring, don't miss out on this underrated gem.
It has some minor hiccups. They're minor.
Building: It's incredible. The most free-form building mechanic I have seen. You can build structures by placing foundations, frames and skins. Or not. You can, for instance, frame in a window in a wall of a building.
Crafting: Find resources to make everything from parts for machinery to materials for construction. The machinery parts make sense, but on a scale that keeps the crafting mechanic fun. Motors require gears, wires, and scrap metal, for instance. Wires are made from copper scrap. Gears are made from metal scrap. 
Research: There's a research tree! Unlock new areas to add useful stations to your growing community, or to improve your own or their ability to do things faster.
Automation: Create something and then set it to build order. Your community friends will build it for you. Tell them to farm, make 4x4s or other parts, or even conduct research for you.
Story: There's an underlying story to it - a reason why your character is going through this and deciding to help others. It keeps you exploring, crafting, building all to make their lives, and yours, better.
This is a gem. It has room for improvement. Would love to see more freedom in rotation while placing machines or designing structures. Would like to see some tool tips on inventory items to indicate how those items are crafted or found. But these are minor. 
This game is enjoyable, and if you like building and crafting games, you really shouldn't miss out on this one.

I give this game a thumbs up but with several caveats that only the developers can eliminate.

Common Hood is an original and intriguing game. It has a captivating storyline that draws the player in. That is probably its greatest strength.

There is much to create and the creative elements are quite broad and engaging. The AI characters are unique and provide options for customization.

Gameplay is mostly linear. It is not open world as most would understand that concept. The player may roam the areas they have quested into, but other areas may not be accessed until certain qualifications have been satisfied. And it's not like attaining a level before access, it's finding a book or clearing debris that requires a certain tool etc. It reminds me of Myst but with fewer hints. Also there's a political take that is wasted on gamers. We don't play to wrestle with the real world.

The linear access venture wouldn't be bad except there are few clues or stated requirements to guide the player to the next step. So there is a lot of floundering around trying to figure out what to do next and much of it is completely non-productive. Players will likely spend a lot of time in Steam Discussion and Discord to advance.

So why thumbs up if it is that confusing? Because it needn't be. The developers should listen to players who overwhelmingly agree about the vagueness of the pathway and give a more directed course. It's fine to hunt awhile as long as you know what you are hunting for. And that goes for most aspects of the game. Give the players more insight into what they are seeking and why.

So, once the player has opened a new area, they understand a bit more. But there are few clues about what to do next. The player just knows that there is something else to know or acquire but not how to get there.

So give it a try and let the developers know how they can provide the player with a better game. I hope they'll listen because they could have a hit.

Although this game does have some bugs, I do appreciate the company's willingness to send quick patches and the problems are better. I have played many hours of Minecraft, and I think the building is fun. I even like making the machines and talking to the characters. It's a highly creative game, and I think it's worth a buy. As long as the company keeps making the effort at bug fixes, the game will be a winner in my opinion. My final thoughts is that it is a terrific game, and I have faith the developers will do what is needed to make some needed improvements. I will be patient with them.

I wish I could do more than the simple thumbs up or down. This is an alright game in total. To me, it has the perfect premise. But it's a buggy mess, and legitimately an unfinished game. This is an excellent prototype/proof of concept for a game that needs another year or two of development to realize.

The UI is bad, and takes a while to figure out how to navigate around the bugs. You get the hang of its idiosyncrasies before too long, but just because we learn how to avoid the bugs doesn't mean the UI isn't buggy crap. Bugs aside, there are many perplexing UI choices that any QA worker could have helped improve.

It's frustrating, because I want to give this game a whole-hearted recommendation for its content. But the product itself needs more development and QA.

I'm really rooting for it, but it seems the devs have moved on from the game. I hope that a modding community gets their hands on this, or another studio makes a spiritual successor.

Still worth a play if you're dealing with capitalist despair and are willing to navigate an unfinished game's minefield of bugs to envision a better future.

Honestly, so far I like this game quite a lot, there's a lot to like about it, but unfortunately there are quite a few problems with it as well.

Pro's.
1. the building system is very nice overall and you can make some pretty nice buildings out of it, same with the blueprint system, although there are some minor bugs that can mess with it at times but overall its great.

2. I love the community aspect, being able to befriend other characters to help your community is great and learning more about them is amazing as well, and most of the missions help a lot with learning about the game progression

3. The crafting system pretty nice and serves its purpose well, although I with there was some kind of wishlisting system so you could see the required materials without being at the crafting station.

4. the general gameplay at least to me is very fun to just chill too, while it may be repetitive that's not necessarily a bad thing, plenty of games are extremely repetitive, like stardew valley, animal crossing, etc... and do it well, as does this in my opinion.

5. The demo gives you tons of content to mess with alone and is probably the only reason I ended up picking this game up because it does a great job showing you the basics of the game to make sure this kind of game is even for you, and I really appreciate that.

Con's 
1. The game currently is in a pretty unpolished and glitchy state, with some areas like the elevator and concrete hallway at launch not working correctly although I believe they fixed those issues, as well AI not working correctly, like in every area after the first I have not been able to make farming work at all because the community members just run into the water tank for the whole day when they are farmers, unable to actually get any water out of it, which once again they seem to be working on fixing these problems, which I really appreciate them fixing some of the issues that can plague this game sometimes.

2. The sheer amount of crafting stations get pretty ridiculous, with me being where I assume is about halfway through the main game at the glasshouse, I have over 20 crafting stations with many of them being completely un-needed, like why are  things such as the band saw and chop saw completely separate from the table saw and not just upgrades of some kind to the table saw itself? same as with things like the bench grinder, drill press, lathe, etc... although they might have a very good reason for these choices, I dont understand them myself.

3. The fact that you just find blueprints and research books by themselves in rooms, potentially very important ones at that that are tied to companion quests, makes me constantly worried about whether or not I missed something important in the previous room, like how I still don't have the recycling research for Mark and i'm still not sure if I missed it or its in the next room and with how glitchy this game can be at times I worry if I am ever missing important objects due to a glitch, but I am fully willing to admit that this very well might just be a me problem and i'm just slow.

Overall, I really like this game and I think if these smaller issues like the glitchy-ness were to ever be resolved it would massively improve the experience and make it even better than it currently is, and I really hope they stick with this game and fix these issues. There may have been some positives and negatives that I have missed, but this game was a very pleasant surprise and I personally really hope this game gets the recognition it deserves for being this honestly unique and pleasant to play through, when everything works properly, and I really hope these devs can continue to make these unique games.

I have played every builder under the sun. They have a variety of options and sometimes limited interactivity with a story. This game has a bit of both. It is primarily a game that teaches building techniques with its new system which has the potential to grow and generate new ideas within it. Then a backend narrative that offers up secret characters, hidden areas and a robust development that may empty the tissue box. For those that have said the beginning is a bit slow, I think you need to get past that point like any game. Do we actually need to be handed every single thing? For each person, progress is a different animal. The ramp up to any end game content can be slow, I mean look at the old EQ or Wow vanilla days. We all complain how we miss it and then doing a quest or camping that mob for 10 days is too much for our Tik Tok brains now. This game does make you earn it, and learn it. (Rem those old Commodore tool days? Oh, thats right. nvm.) There is love in this game. More than I can say about the garbage content on some of the MMOs now a days. It is not modular repetition. The story is not copy past kill x things or harvest x things. It has heart. You feel that which the characters feel. I throw my gauntlet in the ring to complete this game. Now who out there is up for that?

Multiplayer right now is just sandbox mode with invited friends, so that is a bit of a bummer. However, single player is far more fleshed out and offers you an interesting story and lots to do. While the tutorial can be confusing at times, it's good enough that with just a little playing around you can figure it out. Building is done like nothing I've seen before, piece by piece you get a blueprint to work off of. Sometimes multiple blueprints combine to finish a large project. The big projects can seem daunting, but are always rewarding when completed. I really enjoy this aspect of the game, and the quest that go along with them.  

You will gain the trust of other people as you help them, and they can be ordered around to help you achieve your goals. This is a community that needs leadership, and you've been chosen! Put them to work building components, farming, salvaging materials, or researching something in the tech tree. Economy wise, there is really much game there. It's just a build it, and then sell it style. Then you can use the money to get the components you need for the bigger or newer project. The basic materials will keep trickling in, so you can build as many chairs / chest as you want, and sell them to get money for those rare materials you need. 

For not  being an EA release, it feels like it should of been. It is missing some pieces and parts, you might say. Some assembly is required after purchase. Ok, enough bad puns.... There are tech tree skills space holders in place, and like I mentioned, the multiplayer / cooperative game is only sandbox, and a huge let-down imo. I want to build a community together with my friends. Or be put in a cooperative situation where we must work together to achieve xyz goals. I hope we get some more cooperative elements added in the future, as that was my main draw to Common'Hood. 

To conclude, single player is worth it, if you like integral builders with the rare interesting story included, then this is for you. Don't buy it for the cooperative or MP play, as you will be disappointed.

The game still has some kinks that needs to be ironed out but once that gets done this is a perfect game for the people that kind of have nomadic ideas or feel like society as we know it today is too focused on things that doesn't matter.

Recommended with a caveat... The building system is super awesome and versatile but is also pretty finicky and requires alot of grinding and work... so if your the kind of person like me that enjoys harvesting one million sticks, making various different sized planks and boards, then smacking them together to your hearts content... then this game is a definite must.

The cast of characters you meet are also pretty cool in their individuality and each has their own wants and needs that need to be met in order to get the most out of the tasks you assign them (think rimworld style scheduling). There is also a neat-o story of the beginnings of complete post capitalism collapse.

But seriously, you come for the seriously awesome building system. The fact that the building blueprints you find are completely optional, and you can make your own from scratch, or just freehand every board, plank, and panel is just really freeing when compared to most build-centric games.

These complaints on you have to pack things if you run out of mats is nonsense. I was building the chair and now the floor platforms and if i run out of mats I just stop and go to the planar make more and then come back to put it. Whats the problem?

And hours to figure out the research? Its right there on the video on the help. Which keeps blinking annoyingly. What more does that tutorial need?

I hope people didnt need to watch a youtube vid on how to water the potatoes just coz it didnt give you a watercan and theres no well

I have been waiting for a long time for this game to come out. I really like the theme of the narrative and the wonderful soundtrack. While there are some issues to be resolved in regard to the UI, these are minor issues. The game can be a bit daunting at first with all the systems, but you can figure everything out at your own pace. Once I figured out the basics, I really enjoyed all the customization available. I recommend it, lots of potential to grow.
needs a bug patch to fix the day resource count screen thing where it shows everything you made that day i can't get past it so i'm stuck on day 6 technically

Hate that this game is getting so much negativity! I absolutely love the concept of being able to build everything from scratch and have a first person "community builder" type game. I know i haven't gotten into the real "meat" of the game yet, but so far it's been a blast!

I don't usually leave reviews, but I wanted to weigh in since my experience has been great. Yes it has some bugs, yes it has features I would have liked implemented differently, and yes it's missing some documentation, but honestly that's what I expected from a small development team who don't have a huge catalog. The team has been knocking out patch fixes and communicating within their Discord since they launched, thus far this has not been a drop it and abandon situation. 

I personally have enjoyed the challenge of needing to figure out what tool I need to find or what workshop I need to build next. I also played the Demo quite extensively before I bought it, so I knew what I was getting into. 

I do not regret my purchase, and with other 20+ hours sunk in I haven't run out of new spaces to explore or new things to build.

This game has a fantastic story and build to proceed process and game play.  The only disappointing part of the game is the inability to clean up and restore the factory and different areas.   Hopefully the story grows and develops to accommodate this ;)

I've really enjoyed the game so far, excited to see how the story progresses. Seems like there are a few bugs or odd little UI things that could be fixed at some point, but the architectural mechanics and environment are something I haven't seen before. I've been describing it to my friends as solarpunk minecraft. I know economy management is one of the main aspects (and I haven't progressed very far) but it would be cool if you could gradually shift to a gift economy or other mutual aid structures entirely instead of having dollars, but that's just a political nitpick given the focus on self sufficiency and community building. Tutorial could use some work for sure, but if you can manage without handholding I would absolutely recommend this game.

Very cool, little bugs - Frozzen while sleeping
Sleeping - "hold to skip"  does nothing at all
Sleep sleep sleep sleep sleep sleep

Will update the review as I play more, but so far it's a chill game... I really appreciate the feeling.  I've played a lot of building games, and this one is pretty unique.  Totally apart from anything else, there's a total lack of combat.
I do get that there's some remaining issues, and hopefully those will be patched.  But I also think some of the complexity in the interface is to allow builders to accomplish some pretty new and amazing things.
I'll be looking forward to seeing how the community grows around this game, as well as in it!

A few bugs like resources duplicating when cancelling a 'empty' blueprint after a reload, Ruby disappearing after a few days when finishing the bridge, and a 'ghost' block when rush building the chair for Alcott. Overall, not a bad game when these few bugs are fixed, has the potential to become one of the best 'colony management' games.

I’m not sure why everyone’s saying that it’s a bad game. I actually love it and think it’s great. Should they have released it in early access mode? Yes. Are there bugs? Yes. But if you look at the bigger picture, it’s a good game and pretty original. Obviously it still needs work to iron out the kinks but it doesn’t take away from the fact that it’s a decent game that has tons of potential.

I think the negative reviews are exaggerated. Yes, I do have to set my resolution every time I play, but it's not game breaking, I'm sure it's something they can fix. Only having an issue with missions that don't give enough details, leaving me clueless. I had to check a Youtube video on how to fix the crane (had no clue where the control panel was, now I do). In my opinion those are things that can be fixed easily and shouldn't be a reason to thumb a game down. 

Issues such as frequent crashes, would be a reason to thumb a game down, but the game runs fine (1440p, 100+ fps on a 6900 XT). The art and ways of constructing are original. Put a few farm plots at the start and that will make your first money. I started with 20 plots and have enough left to sell, to buy parts that I need. The game is relaxed, no stress, you can  take whatever time you need.

I only got 4 hours in, but did play the beta a year ago for +-10 hours. Hard to say how far they got with the game since, but at this point I don't feel it's fair to down vote the game for none game breaking issues, So far, I'm enjoying it.

I read that the game had mixed reviews. Yes there are a few glitches making things but I haven't found anything that requires more than 2 attempts to fix. I also read it requires sooo much clicking of the mouse. It doesn't, if you realise that you can click the end of the item that you are using it fills it in for you. Is the game worth $30, well that's up to you. If you want a challenge, probably not. I played the demo and it was ok but then the concept of this type of living is something is something I strive for in RL. Obviously, after the demo, I bought the full game and no regrets so far. Played about 10hrs including the demo.

While I initially said I would not recommend this game, I believe that view should be changed. The Common'hood game has been updated regularly and it seems as if the developers want to make this game better and are setting out patch updates when they become available.

Love this game!!!!  Game is still in early development, so expect the glitch, get stuck, lose stuff, or it disappears. No real written step by step, as this is so new. I followed a few YouTubers and got some great into. But still early so no endgame info yet. Hope to make my own. :)  If you love crafting, building, exploring, and not demanding perfect execution, then this is the game for you. Already a lot of regulars, as you will find in the official Discord channel (devs do show up there) and on the Steam discussion page. So looks like I am not the only one who is loving the game and accepting bugs for what they are. I loved it and shared it with my gf, who bought it for both of us. No multiplayer yet, but we just chat on Discord and share what we are doing, as we "play together". :) Have fun and enjoy this fun game!!

This isn't technically listed as Early Access or Beta.
It should be.

There's a lot of depth here and it's addicting, however it's pretty rough around the edges. If you play, you will find glaring bugs, a few items will disappear, things you are building will get stuck and you'll have to start them over, AI will get stuck in the wall, have messed up animation, and get stuck in quests... and you'll have a great time. I just lost a stack of fuses because I double-clicked on them and I haven't rage-quit yet.

Beautiful story, beautiful graphics, a VERY sophisticated world building system. I've played the game all the way through and I have to tell you the game is MUCH bigger than I expected. The parallel story lines are also really heartfelt. Sure, the game had a few bugs in the starting, but with every patch they're really solving all the problems. The dev team is looking at all the comments and resolving any issues real time. I've spent so much time in the sandbox itself just making different building and blueprints! I still haven't finished all the character arc's but I hope the game continues to build more places to explore in the future because I can see this expanding to include more characters in new areas easily!

Love the community aspect to the game "It takes a village!"

Why Buy?

* Potential - I like the concept and what is already included in the game
* Graphics are pleasing
* The level of detail is astounding, intimidating, yet gratifying 

However, there are quite a few bugs that will turn off a huge set of players. These bugs cause crashes and irritations that require some forgiveness and an active community to help get this to fruition.  I forgive you!

Hoping a workshop will be added in as that could make this one of those games you can't help but keep coming back.
Its a bit of a mind bender and some of the choices are not helpful with what happens next. But I enjoyed it

Really enjoyed this game, despite the bugs, but managed to complete it.
Going to play again to get the things i missed first time around.
I haven't played the final version yet, but had a lot of fun in the beta. Also had a lot of bugs , but most of them were fixed during the beta.
I love the story and game it created! Many hours well spent and easily one of my faves!

Played the demo for around 8 hours. Wasn't sure but thought I'd jump in and so glad I did. I haven't experienced any bugs or issues apart from me not reading things properly. Plenty to explore. What the machines can do or even what machine you need to build the part would be helpful but figuring it out is part of the fun right?
Great game has a few bugs but the devs are putting out patches to fix them.
The game is nice, it has a lot of ponential. I spect it to improve in the coming days.
Building needs a bit of refining because it glitches sometimes, but overall very enertaining.

I have not finished the story line yet, so I wasn't planning to review this early however in the or for the sake of justice I am going to cut and past from a forum discussion temporarily and come back and revise this when I finish the story line.  Text of Discussion follows: Was imagining my role in the future and it seems I must have to go around fixing all the machines if I install them. I would pay for a handyman DLC if there was another role for me DLC that comes alone with it. Not sure if anyone is planning on moving the game forward or not it really is a good game, challenging and thought provoking, tender and exciting, the graphics and idea is excellent. I want to say I suppose people just want to point and click and win without thought or a challenge or patience and understanding. I suppose Charlie and the Chocolate factory coin clicker or a Point and Click find it game would be more their style then. No offence intended it's just people have downed a good game and I have yet to figure out why. Hmmmmm..... thanks for the effort Dev team and all involved.
need multiplayer story.
Alright, some quests are not properly described

I read the reviews on this game where they are very mixed. I decided to buy the game and try it out for myself. The game is super cool, good story, incredibly many things that you can learn to make. Many places to explore. However, there are still some annoying little bugs in the game every now and then, so remember to save often.
Fun despite the bugs.

GREAT GAME! still has some small bugs and un-polished parts, but I'm sure in a cpl patches it'll be 100% - Cheap Entertainment - I solved it in 23.6 hrs my first try - I could do it in Half that now that I know - but I plan on playing forever pretty much - LOVE IT

There's something I like about this, I can't put my finger on it but it's got a lot of potential. weapon reload is atrocious but I guess that's why you get the little followers that auto shoot for you, which is cool. I like the x-com style base building mechanics, a bit shallow but could be made more interesting by collecting different resources. Maybe that's already in the game, heck, I've only played it for an hour and now I'm writing  a review about it.

Really if you're on the fence about it just push that buy button, (you know you want to) play it for a couple hours and if you don't like it then just get a refund.

nice music and aesthetics. basically a first person hotline miami. fun combat loop and has some tense moments with balanced difficulty making some encounters quite challenging but still enjoyable. one odd thing about the game is there is multiple endings and paths but it never actually lets you know clearly this so i played it rather linerally without realising there was multiple other levels. doesn't detract from the game as the gameplay is the standout not the rather forgettable plot.

Very stylish, gory and fast paced game. It maybe too hard and replayability is not working very good, but it has interesting story, though not a lot, and realy nice impact from weapons. If you like boomer shooters, this style and can handle a realy hard fights, than this trippy, gory and filled to the brim slowmo gun blazing fest is for you.
hotline miami look like but with FPS slow motion ,take a pill then everything is easy ;D

Everything it says it is and so much more. Was not expecting much depth to this game and that changed very quickly. The atmosphere and gameplay were great! I had so much fun, looking forward to more playthroughs! If you are on the fence about it, get over that fence and give it a go!

They really made Hotline Miami into a an immersive sim

It's actually really fun, if confusing, as it doesn't have the rails that HM has, but that is not something bad, you just have to be ready for it

It looked promising and, oh boy, did it deliver. The aggressive, fast-paced gameplay is reminiscent of Hotline Miami, except this is in 1st person. Will be replaying to see how the different endings play out.

World of Tanks is a free-to-play multiplayer tank combat MMO where players control armored vehicles from multiple nations, battle in team-based matches, and experience both tactical positioning and chaotic PvP firefights. (store.steampowered.com
)

Strengths:
✔️ Huge variety of tanks — light, medium, heavy, and tank destroyers, each with unique playstyles.
✔️ Tactical gameplay — positioning, angling, and teamwork are critical; matches are more strategic than run-and-gun shooters.
✔️ Historical detail — models and mechanics feel authentic, appealing to history buffs.
✔️ Regular updates — new tanks, maps, and events keep the game fresh.
✔️ Accessible free-to-play — easy to jump in and enjoy casual or competitive matches.

Weaknesses:
✖️ Grindy progression — unlocking high-tier tanks and equipment can take a lot of time or money.
✖️ Matchmaking imbalance — sometimes players face much stronger tanks or experienced opponents, which can be frustrating.
✖️ Slow-paced battles — matches can feel long, especially with heavy tank-heavy teams.
✖️ Pay-to-win temptation — premium tanks, ammo, and account boosts give advantages.
✖️ Steep learning curve for newcomers — understanding armor angling, shell types, and map strategy takes time.

Overall impression:
World of Tanks is a deep, strategic, and rewarding tank combat game. Ideal for players who enjoy teamwork, historical vehicles, and tactical battles, but casual players should be prepared for long matches and progression grind.
One tip: Once you spend, a better offer magically appears.

So, review time. IDK what all the drama is about, I won't pretend that I do. This is just a review of the game in its current state in January of 2026. It's (almost) everything I wanted in a VR Rhythm Game. Most of the games that people talk about using for exercise involve heavy arm use--but if you want to burn calories, you're best off using the bigger muscles: the legs (this is true for both men and women). This isn't DDR--it's all horizontal and vertical movement, there's no forward and back, but it will make you sweat. Plus, for the small library, there's a HUGE community contributed library of which I have barely even scratched the surface.

Constructive Criticisms:
(1) Using HTC Base Stations and Trackers for feet, the shoe calibration is wonky. It's almost mirrored, and I know that transformation matrices and quaternions can be hard, but this is just hilariously bad. So my quick workaround: strap the trackers to your ankles, compensate to the best of your ability by doing opposite of what it looks like you should do, then after "calibration" bend down and physically rotate the tracker around your ankle until the shoe is in the right spot. Devs, please fix this. 

(2) Game library is too small. "Okay but you said there's community content." Yes, I did, and there is, but of the tracks I sampled, most ONLY have a "Hard" mode with a rare "Normal" (which is also very hard for me), and "Expert" (that I don't even want to open for fear of shock). Now, I do hope to get to a point where I can play harder content, but bottom line is that there's not enough "Easy" content (that I've found) for people who are starting, who have a disability, or who are overweight. Devs, please add new content, I'll pay a fair fee for DLC to cover licensing costs--and Rebuff already knows how to tap the VRC scene for good artists.  

(3) Community Content that uses YT, the one community song I tried that connected to YT experienced a latency issue that was not-insignificant. The lag between the game and the song was more than half a beat. This is a tricky problem to solve, assuming it's not the fault of the community creator who provided the one mapping I tried. That having been said, I feel like there must be some way to fish out a fix or solution--but it just adds more fuel to the fire in Criticism (2), please add more content--I will happily pay for DLCs to cover song licensing and dance mapping. Maybe contract/hire some of your community creators if you need extra hands!  

Overall: If you have VR with FBT capacity and you want to sweat, this game is worth it. However, it also needs more content, and a little more fine-tuning: the last update was in June of last year, so someone needs to light a fire under the devs' butts, because the game is almost perfect.

So, review time. IDK what all the drama is about, I won't pretend that I do. This is just a review of the game in its current state in January of 2026. It's (almost) everything I wanted in a VR Rhythm Game. Most of the games that people talk about using for exercise involve heavy arm use--but if you want to burn calories, you're best off using the bigger muscles: the legs (this is true for both men and women). This isn't DDR--it's all horizontal and vertical movement, there's no forward and back, but it will make you sweat. Plus, for the small library, there's a HUGE community contributed library of which I have barely even scratched the surface.

Constructive Criticisms:
(1) Using HTC Base Stations and Trackers for feet, the shoe calibration is wonky. It's almost mirrored, and I know that transformation matrices and quaternions can be hard, but this is just hilariously bad. So my quick workaround: strap the trackers to your ankles, compensate to the best of your ability by doing opposite of what it looks like you should do, then after "calibration" bend down and physically rotate the tracker around your ankle until the shoe is in the right spot. Devs, please fix this. 

(2) Game library is too small. "Okay but you said there's community content." Yes, I did, and there is, but of the tracks I sampled, most ONLY have a "Hard" mode with a rare "Normal" (which is also very hard for me), and "Expert" (that I don't even want to open for fear of shock). Now, I do hope to get to a point where I can play harder content, but bottom line is that there's not enough "Easy" content (that I've found) for people who are starting, who have a disability, or who are overweight. Devs, please add new content, I'll pay a fair fee for DLC to cover licensing costs--and Rebuff already knows how to tap the VRC scene for good artists.  

(3) Community Content that uses YT, the one community song I tried that connected to YT experienced a latency issue that was not-insignificant. The lag between the game and the song was more than half a beat. This is a tricky problem to solve, assuming it's not the fault of the community creator who provided the one mapping I tried. That having been said, I feel like there must be some way to fish out a fix or solution--but it just adds more fuel to the fire in Criticism (2), please add more content--I will happily pay for DLCs to cover song licensing and dance mapping. Maybe contract/hire some of your community creators if you need extra hands!  

Overall: If you have VR with FBT capacity and you want to sweat, this game is worth it. However, it also needs more content, and a little more fine-tuning: the last update was in June of last year, so someone needs to light a fire under the devs' butts, because the game is almost perfect.

A fun puzzle game with easy achievements. All three achievements can be obtained in 10-15 minutes.
We interact with energy modules with special weapons, drag a couple of cubes and memorize the sequence to get past the lasers.
I liked the game, but it's too short. I hope more content will be added to the release.

made me slow down and actually think, not just push buttons. Some puzzles had me staring at the room for a good minute before it clicked, and that moment felt great. The gravity gun is fun to mess with, and the game doesn’t rush you, which I appreciated. Quiet, focused, and oddly satisfying to get through.
Well, it's a very visually beautiful puzzle.
It's difficult, but it will work
