Siralim Ultimate is the most intellectually stimulating games I've ever played. It's a delightful puzzle to figure out a new team that you can build, sometimes spending hours thinking through each nuance, and then making it, testing it, and refining it for days (or weeks), all while unlocking more and more content that adds more potential nuance or entirely new possibilities to explore. If you're the sort that likes free-form puzzles where no single solution is the one-and-all, where there are thousands of possible combinations of mechanics to use and dozens to hundreds to even thousands of ways to implement them - sometimes where a single perk difference, one spell, or one creature in the party changes everything - you will become engrossed in this game.

Honestly thee best game I ever played in my life, it's a game with refined polish and love over the years and the 4th game in the series.

Ultimate is the perfected entry from all 3 in the series and is receving a final update as 3.0 some time soonish? Regardless its the game that encourages creativity and offers different post game goals like collecting every single monster to even ranking up in guild by hunting down big super bosses. The game has alt of strong things, but the joy of this game's combat is perfecting each encounter on the 5th realm intensity with newer stratiges as you boast your roster and make your own fun or broken comps thanks to fusion, artifact traits you can fully customize your characters futher, and the many unique spells that can be modified as well for alot of different stratiges and tactics. 

The only thing you'll say to yourself is: I need more Brimstone!

That is a good thing- because it incentives and sparks your desires of building new teams or to go as far and as deep as you can in the game.

I'm almost to 600th floor and will see if can make it to the 1001th floor.

amazing game with it on sale definatly.

Possibly my favorite RPG from a mechanics standpoint. I have another 100+ hours on mobile. Extreme mechanical depth with absurd ability combinations, akin to Path of Exile for creature collectors. Tons of completionist-oriented content, an awesome update years after development concluded, and generally just a fantastic game. You won't play this game for it's lukewarm story, characters, or even general vibe, but it transcends the need for any of these things by excelling in its primary strength--deep progression mechanics--while achieving a satisfactory grade in all other areas. Buy it immediately if you're a numbers pervert who zerks off to ability procs.
This dev found a way to weaponize autism by giving us ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ unlimited choices. i love it

pokemon + balatro + binding of isaac = ultimate autistic obsession

for real though, if you like any of those games you'll most likely love this game. it features procedurally-generated dungeons with a 6v6 pve pokemon-style combat system that has a ton of depth and basically an infinite number of very satisfying builds. here's a breakdown of what I like and don't like:

gameplay - 9/10
this game really doesn't have very many guardrails when it comes to making a build that's op as ♥♥♥♥ (in fact, you're kind of encouraged to make a really strong build), but that gives it a lot of depth and makes it pretty fun. also, no matter how strong the build, there's almost always a hard counter that leaves you like "never in my 9000 hours". if you're ever at a point where you feel like the game is too easy, the difficulty setting goes from 1-10 and there's a bunch of challenges you can enable for better rewards. 

replayability - 11/10
there is a ridiculous amount of replayability in this game. there are over 30 classes and ~1400 unique creatures, with the ability to fuse creatures to combine their traits in your builds. there's also artifacts and spell gems, which have their own huge customization systems. there are likely millions of possible builds you can go for. additionally, there is a very extensive endgame, going all the way up to realm depth 999. the majority of the game's content (i.e. unique creatures and such) is actually found during the endgame, as when I finished the main storyline I only had ~300 creatures discovered.

* a quick side note about achievements, absolutely positively do [b]NOT[/b] buy this game if you are hoping for an easy 100% (unless you are a masochist). The 300 achievements you see on Steam are only a small fraction of the ~5300 achievements ingame, which are linked to steam though achievements like "get x% of achievement points". out of the thousands of people that have thousands of hours in this game, there's likely only a handful of people in the world who have a true 100%.

story - 4/10
as much as i [i]want[/i] to like the story, it is rather bland for most of the game and has a lot of filler. it's also almost exclusively told through dialogue, with no real cutscenes in the entire game. the story is cohesive enough to follow along, but don't be expecting an indie story masterpiece like undertale or deltarune.

configurability - 10/10
there's a ton of stuff in this game to mess around with, even when excluding all the build customizability. there are tons of settings, including a builtin turbo mode option to make grinding quicker. you can also customize your castle to make it look pretty much however you want, with tons of floors, walls, decorations, expansions, NPCs/utility stations, etc.

tutorial/progression - 5/10
the game's introduction to the mechanics is pretty understandable. however, if you skip the dialogue on accident (as did I), you can't easily get back that information. the menus are pretty easy to understand though, so even if you don't get the explanation, you'll likely figure it out anyway.

the gameplay loop is extremely repetitive, with the pretty much infinite realm-based progression system being used as the basis of the main story's progression. however, even though it's repetitive, it's a pretty satisfying gameplay loop (imo) and usually doesn't get too boring since you're constantly getting new mechanics and things to add to your build such as perk points, projects, creatures, etc. I really would have liked to see at least something dynamic about the realms aside from just the textures, layout (which is only unique because of procedural generation and doesn't mix up the algorithm much), and creatures.

overall - 7/10
i think this game is totally worth the cost. it has its flaws, but makes up for a lot of it by giving you tons of content to play around with. you will definitely get way more playtime out of it than any similarly-priced game.

*** Some early game spoilers to explain some game mechanics. ***

So far only at Floor 11, but will update as I get deeper, if anything changes up significantly.

It was when I got Fusion at Floor 9, that this game became significantly better, and was ultimately when I reached my selling point.

So part of the selling point of this game is that it's basically like Pokemon, except it has all your units out, along with the enemy. You're trying to get better units, but at level 9 is when you can fuse two units together, to get an averaging of their stats, as well as both of the special traits that each unit has. Having the right 2 traits together can make for a really powerful unit.
In addition, the NPC that offers this, can level up your units. This was critical for me. This was the make it or break it NPC for me. Because a dreadful thought I had leading up to this, was that I would have to level up each unit from Level 1. I was playing the game in an agonized state before floor 9, because I didn't want to do this.
Luckily the Level Up ability he offers will level them up to your highest level for some of your resources that are generally pretty plentiful if you really clear out the maps when you do them.

I'll continue to see what more projects bring to the table, as well as what future floors bring to me. But it's now that I can finally relax and enjoy this game. There's supposed to be like 400 some odd floors, so there's plenty of gameplay to this game, and plenty of units to unlock and farm.

Siralim Ultimate is the most intellectually stimulating games I've ever played. It's a delightful puzzle to figure out a new team that you can build, sometimes spending hours thinking through each nuance, and then making it, testing it, and refining it for days (or weeks), all while unlocking more and more content that adds more potential nuance or entirely new possibilities to explore. If you're the sort that likes free-form puzzles where no single solution is the one-and-all, where there are thousands of possible combinations of mechanics to use and dozens to hundreds to even thousands of ways to implement them - sometimes where a single perk difference, one spell, or one creature in the party changes everything - you will become engrossed in this game.

All the negative reviews I've read speak the truth: grindy gameplay, simplistic writing, inane characters, ugly and confusing UI/UX, repetitive dungeons, repetitive music, boring quests, shallow world-building, and almost too many different systems to balance and tweak your team composition. Yet I still enjoy this game. 

I really enjoy games that allow you to craft over-powered teams and character builds, and with enough patience this game provides tons of options. But that is its greatest flaw: the enormous amount of time required to unlock all of these systems to craft your ultimate team. 
I understand the reasoning that instant access to all the game's systems at the beginning would overwhelm the vast majority of people (myself included), but the current design cycle is: Steamroll enemies and bosses -> Hit a wall -> Unlock new system/class/mechanic through grinding quests -> Rerun old dungeons to get RNG enhancements for build -> Tweak build/Rebuild entire team -> Overcome previous wall -> Steamroll enemies -> Repeat. Eventually you beat the "Main Story" and unlock new systems and upgrades to be used in the Mid-Game, with the End-Game even further off in the distance behind even more grinding.

Grinding can be fun for some people ("it's the journey not the destination" type of people), but grind is the name of the game for Siralim Ultimate. If you can endure the "Main Story" a plethora of monsters, spells. passive traits, artifacts, monster fusions, accessories, and more await those who like to theory-craft the ultimate monster team. Recommended if it's discounted or on sale.

Ever played some strange MtG formats like tribal, standard pauper, modern with only common/uncommon ?
Those formats with almost no decklist and you have to theorycraft for hours, then crash and burn because your theory was flawed from the very beginning ?

That's exactly the feeling you will get when playing Siralim. This is a theorycrafter's dream with a ton of achievements, creatures and challenges to try.
Is it ugly ? yes, DLSS and RTX on won't change anything here. 
Is it grindy ? yes if you always play the same class, but if you try to create teams for multiple classes, it's back to the blackboard because they play so differently .

The thing is everything else is so amazing, you won't even notice the weak sides of this game  : 
- you think you have an unkillable team and you get destroyed by a combination of modifiers you didn't even know could work like they do
- everything is streamlined so that you can play really fast : macros, keep the button pressed, no need to press a button when running through ressources, ....
- the soundtrack is great

If you like to sit, think and strategize for hours, there is nothing like it.
Yes, it's not a card game and yet, if you've ever wondered if any game out there could give you the same feeling you get when trying to create a crazy deck with infinite combos from scratch with a limited number of cards, look no further.

Level your new guys right up so they can fight immediately, this roster is a palette and the Dev wants you to PAINT.

Wander the randomly generated maps to gain resources, upgrades, and new monsters to fuel your death machine. Worth full price.

I have a LOT more to say about this game than I feel like typing, so I will put it simply: if I had to remove all but 3 games from my Steam library, and never purchase or download another game again, Siralim Ultimate would make the cut 100% of the time.

Possibly my favorite RPG from a mechanics standpoint. I have another 100+ hours on mobile. Extreme mechanical depth with absurd ability combinations, akin to Path of Exile for creature collectors. Tons of completionist-oriented content, an awesome update years after development concluded, and generally just a fantastic game. You won't play this game for it's lukewarm story, characters, or even general vibe, but it transcends the need for any of these things by excelling in its primary strength--deep progression mechanics--while achieving a satisfactory grade in all other areas. Buy it immediately if you're a numbers pervert who zerks off to ability procs.

Dragon Quest Monsters 2: Cobi's Journey and Tara's Adventure on Gameboy Color has never been topped due to its expansive monster breeding/pedigree system and procedural generated Magic Key worlds. Siralim Ultimate comes close. Highly recommend.
This dev found a way to weaponize autism by giving us ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ unlimited choices. i love it

pokemon + balatro + binding of isaac = ultimate autistic obsession

for real though, if you like any of those games you'll most likely love this game. it features procedurally-generated dungeons with a 6v6 pve pokemon-style combat system that has a ton of depth and basically an infinite number of very satisfying builds. here's a breakdown of what I like and don't like:

gameplay - 9/10
this game really doesn't have very many guardrails when it comes to making a build that's op as ♥♥♥♥ (in fact, you're kind of encouraged to make a really strong build), but that gives it a lot of depth and makes it pretty fun. also, no matter how strong the build, there's almost always a hard counter that leaves you like "never in my 9000 hours". if you're ever at a point where you feel like the game is too easy, the difficulty setting goes from 1-10 and there's a bunch of challenges you can enable for better rewards. 

replayability - 11/10
there is a ridiculous amount of replayability in this game. there are over 30 classes and ~1400 unique creatures, with the ability to fuse creatures to combine their traits in your builds. there's also artifacts and spell gems, which have their own huge customization systems. there are likely millions of possible builds you can go for. additionally, there is a very extensive endgame, going all the way up to realm depth 999. the majority of the game's content (i.e. unique creatures and such) is actually found during the endgame, as when I finished the main storyline I only had ~300 creatures discovered.

* a quick side note about achievements, absolutely positively do [b]NOT[/b] buy this game if you are hoping for an easy 100% (unless you are a masochist). The 300 achievements you see on Steam are only a small fraction of the ~5300 achievements ingame, which are linked to steam though achievements like "get x% of achievement points". out of the thousands of people that have thousands of hours in this game, there's likely only a handful of people in the world who have a true 100%.

story - 4/10
as much as i [i]want[/i] to like the story, it is rather bland for most of the game and has a lot of filler. it's also almost exclusively told through dialogue, with no real cutscenes in the entire game. the story is cohesive enough to follow along, but don't be expecting an indie story masterpiece like undertale or deltarune.

configurability - 10/10
there's a ton of stuff in this game to mess around with, even when excluding all the build customizability. there are tons of settings, including a builtin turbo mode option to make grinding quicker. you can also customize your castle to make it look pretty much however you want, with tons of floors, walls, decorations, expansions, NPCs/utility stations, etc.

tutorial/progression - 5/10
the game's introduction to the mechanics is pretty understandable. however, if you skip the dialogue on accident (as did I), you can't easily get back that information. the menus are pretty easy to understand though, so even if you don't get the explanation, you'll likely figure it out anyway.

the gameplay loop is extremely repetitive, with the pretty much infinite realm-based progression system being used as the basis of the main story's progression. however, even though it's repetitive, it's a pretty satisfying gameplay loop (imo) and usually doesn't get too boring since you're constantly getting new mechanics and things to add to your build such as perk points, projects, creatures, etc. I really would have liked to see at least something dynamic about the realms aside from just the textures, layout (which is only unique because of procedural generation and doesn't mix up the algorithm much), and creatures.

overall - 7/10
i think this game is totally worth the cost. it has its flaws, but makes up for a lot of it by giving you tons of content to play around with. you will definitely get way more playtime out of it than any similarly-priced game.

So far only at Floor 11, but will update as I get deeper, if anything changes up significantly.

It was when I got Fusion at Floor 9, that this game became significantly better, and was ultimately when I reached my selling point.

So part of the selling point of this game is that it's basically like Pokemon, except it has all your units out, along with the enemy. You're trying to get better units, but at level 9 is when you can fuse two units together, to get an averaging of their stats, as well as both of the special traits that each unit has. Having the right 2 traits together can make for a really powerful unit.
In addition, the NPC that offers this, can level up your units. This was critical for me. This was the make it or break it NPC for me. Because a dreadful thought I had leading up to this, was that I would have to level up each unit from Level 1. I was playing the game in an agonized state before floor 9, because I didn't want to do this.
Luckily the Level Up ability he offers will level them up to your highest level for some of your resources that are generally pretty plentiful if you really clear out the maps when you do them.

I'll continue to see what more projects bring to the table, as well as what future floors bring to me. But it's now that I can finally relax and enjoy this game. There's supposed to be like 400 some odd floors, so there's plenty of gameplay to this game, and plenty of units to unlock and farm.

plays a lot like classic asteroids except you scoop up the smallest chunks to sell later!

aside from the two dimensional constraints the game's physics are very realistic, damage will miss align your thrusters and in turn change how the ship will handle (normally for the worse) and as you load up with more and more ore your ship will become more sluggish.

you can buy several different ships most with a few models to choose from, the most expensive won't always be the best, and the best ship for me might not be the best ship for you.

likewise you can retrofit the ship's UI with versions that come with other brands of ships, and again you will find one that you prefer over others.

the one thing that i find really disappointing about the game is you can only take a single ship at a time, no matter how much crew you hire or how many ships you buy.

if you like slow mining runs this game is absolutely for you, there are also other ways to play but i'm less familiar with those.

9 out of 10 for me, would be 10 if i could take several ships on a mission at a time.

It's not Star Wars-style laser battles, you don't fly fighters in this game and don't shoot slow-as-hell lasers. It's also not a game for the impatient. Great joy comes from mastering its systems.

It's like old-school hard Sci-Fi literature.

It's not a big studio. It is created by basically a single passionate person and it's dirt cheap. The dev keeps working on the game, polishing systems, adding new content.

Think of the game more as a sim rather than an action game. It's not for the impatient. It's an immersive, relaxing, meditative combo of drifting, space exploration, money making and occasional space duels.

In DeltaV it takes time to learn to navigate, work with auto-pilot, deal with pirates and optimise your ship through experience and upgrades. It is only after you learn the game better the pirates, from predators, become your prey and their wrecked ships become salvageable bounty.

It's a Newtonian physics-based resource gathering game, rich in atmosphere and science-inspired mechanisms, upgrades and tools at your disposal.

The battles are also real physics-based. Your ship is for mining, not for military purposes. You blast their systems with microwave emmiters (that normally heat iceroids), hit their reactor a few times with mass accelerators and watch their fuel bleed out and their ship shut down in minutes. Your exhaust is a weapon too. Then you salvage it with your arm for good space dollars.

Lots of good Sci-Fi references.

- When you start, it's about learning navigation, proficient mining and exploring. You also learn how to run from pirates.
- Mid game you learn to navigate at high speeds, that more expensive upgrades are not neccesarily better, and how to hunt pirates.
- End game is about fine tuning your setup and getting the most from every run (300k+ earnings each round).

Don't get discouraged. Don't panic and don't push the pedal to the metal unless you know what you are doing. This game is a true gem that hooks you for hours and is less repetitive than it seems.

Congrats to the dev!
At first the controls felt horrific and then i gave it a second chance and this is an amazing game worth anyones playthrough

9/10 Hard Sci Fi Game
I was having a blast mining away in this game even BEFORE the incident. 

I sent my geologist on an EVA to investigate a derelict ship, honestly only because I had a backup geologist back on station. I should have sent my mechanic. Anyway I set her to work investigating the condition of the ship, and upon reports that the reactor appeared to be in good shape, I had her power it up to check basic systems. It looked good for a few seconds, and then a something - a malfunctioning thruster or a sudden atmosphere leak - shoots out the side for a few seconds, sending the derelict ship into an uncontrolled spin at a fast vector away from us. I pursued the ship, dodging asteroids the whole time, to keep it in sight in case the transponder failed. While I was doing that I guess my geologist found her way to a lifepod, and GOOD THING, because moments later the ship made contact an asteroid and the reactor containment breached. I don't know how my ship survived inside the edge of the blast radius, much less the lifepod, but I then chased that lifepod through saturn's rings for FIFTEEN MINUTES. The lifepod only just barely fits inside the cargo bay and only IF you can catch it on one of its narrower ends which are just small enough to fit through the opening. I tried 3 times to get ahead of it to slow it down - But the problem is if you get close and then try a last-second maneuver, the exhaust of your thrusters can send it back into a fast spin away from you, off through the asteroid field again. This chase took me past two spaceships actively having a space battle - the first I witnessed after 17 hours of gameplay, mind you, and the whole time I was most worried that I wouldn't have enough propellant left to make the return journey back to station.... I finally caught the lifepod, rescued my geologist who was still alive (no doubt shaken), and made it back to station where I was able to sell the lifepod for $50,000. 

Can't wait to go out again!

Truly unique game that lets you experience space (in its top down view) in its vastness without being boring, there is always something to do and to look forward to.
It has its quirks especially in the beginning, learning the controls of your spaceship or how the world works takes some time but i'm glad i sticked with it :)

This game really managed to tickle my ship outfitting autism. To my recollection, this might be the only game that lets you tweak the autopilot profiles and behaviours on top of just having different autopilot systems to choose from. The same goes for every other component of your ship, from microwage generator/emitters, lasers, RCS and ore processing units. While combat is not the primary focus, it is still present and can be rewarding.

This game manages to avoid the classic space game upgrade treadmill, where bigger ships are inherently better. At least for the most part. It sports a unique wear and tear system for all components, complete with your HUD glitching out, hard rebooting and throwing alarms until you fix a short.

Delta V goes on sale for decent discounts and is entertaining enough to dive into its hard sci-fi world even at full price. If you ever (re)watch The Expanse, this game makes for a great companion.

If you ever watched The Expanse and wanted to be a Belter mining the rings, this is the game. It respects real physics and orbital mechanics but remains fun and accessible. The atmosphere is simultaneously meditative and terrifying. Highly recommended for anyone who loves grounded space exploration.

On the other hand it's a game where you fly a rust bucket and crack stones for minerals.

I put in about a hundred hours into this game through the full demo, bought it and sunk another hundred hours and I still feel like I've yet to discover all this game has to offer. I truly appreciate how accommodating this game is to different playstyles.

Sink a couple of hours into a long dive, or do a quick one and still make it out with a decent haul if you've got the right loadout for it. Beautiful and fun game. The gameplay loop is not repetive as you might think, and there are so many events and things to explore. 

I love the graphics and art style, there's just a semirealism style that clicks with my brain.

The ship configurations are truly unique, and you can choose to spec into salvage, mining, piracy, or more, all equally viable. 

The game has enough realism to be interesting to me and believable too, without requiring already knowing alot about space to get into. (the opposite of this would be Children of a Dead Earth, also a great game)

A really impressive and engaging game, well worth the price. If you're on the fence you can always try the demo.

Great game with strong ''immersive sim'' aspects to it. Tinkering with your ships is great fun and a core part of the game. The majority of equipment has their uses; you're not buying ''+5% damage'' upgrades in this game, ever. 

I found the 'missions' and crew system underexplained and messy but that only started detracting from the experience once I was many hours in and already kind of had my fill. It's servicable enough. The contact with other ships and mining droids is also clunky, it just felt a bit underutilized and clunky to me. I could've used some guidance and direction 'finishing' those optional sides of the game because I lost patience for it. 

The 'gameplay' trailer does a decent job of showing what's in the game, but it leans into acrade-style gameplay that's not really the focus of the game in my opinion. If you pilot like that all the time you'll smash your ship up for nothing (although that can be fun, it's not overly stingy with money but things still have to be repaired and earned). The game is significantly about asteroid mining and a lot slower and atmospheric than it looks in the trailer.  

I was annoyed with the default camera and very pleased to find out they baked in solid alternatives. 

Besides what I detracted about the UI, it is really satisfying and immersive when it comes to the ship itself. The radar(s) (it's a changeable ship part!) has an excellent utillitarian feel to it for example. Checking for powered up drives (to spot the rare pirate without transponders) in the little macromap is 11/10 and the reactions of your UI when you abuse your ship in varied ways is very immersive. The damage system is also great; thrusters get out of allignment and your board computer will start sending false alerts for example. Combat is brutal and victory quickly decided as is to be expected out in the great vacuum of space. 

The thrusters alone deserve an award. There's a ~dozen options and they all have their own unique character. Some go from 0-100 in output, some are on a gimbal system with varying rotational ability. They have their own proppelant consumption or even run on other sources of energy on your ship. I'm not even touching upon half of the mechanics of them and how they interact with your ship build/traversal, amazing sim stuff! The thrusters take the crown in my opinion but all ship parts you could expect to have interesting interactions with the ship/world have them. 

Anyone remotely interested in the genres it touches upon should definitely try Rings of Saturn.

Amazing title with unlimited potential.  Seems so simple but is really quite deep and is a great way to just blow off some steam and enjoy a few dives into the rings.  I don't write a lot of reviews, and I dont buy a lot of fluff, but I have bought all the DL content to try and support the author and keep this wonderful, fun, engaging game alive and ticking.
what an incredibly immersive, peaceful, chaotic, violent experience. the game is soaked in soulfully detailed worldbuilding. love it so much.

Bro this is so peak, the details of this game is so realistic, though I wish there was some additional gameplay features like multiplayer, buliding your own space stations in the rings, other planets/moons that you can visit, and crashing into Saturn, just because you know?

Going from a small ship zooming around just to make a buck, to a massive freighter that just floats along while your drones and automatic turrets do all the work is one of the most satisfying things you can do in a game.
Interesting story elements but also fun to just chill and collect resources.
This is the best game I've ever played. It's amazingly soothing and exciting in turns, and I love fighting the roids as they bounce around my hold (Dragon Crew Portraits FTW)
exactly what you'd want from a game about mining in space, the rings of Saturn are disappearing and I will be the cause

A hardcore near-scifi space sim that has perfect vibes if you have delved into the books or tv seasons of "The Expanse". Chill but not mindless play. Fairly deep study and analysis needed of the various systems from thrusters through to torch, lasers, mass cannons, manipulator arms... all to get you to the point of taking your simulated crew into the rings of Saturn to mine ore.

Can it get much better? Don't know, but a more attentive and capable developer is hard to find and this gem was made by a dev  who is so obviously invested in making this one of the best niche space sims out there.

Recommend 100%.

Got the key for a review years ago. No idea why but picked it back up and started playing it casually as my slow burner game.

Thoroughly enjoying getting to grips with it and learning the ins and outs.

Would recommend as a casual playthrough/time burner!

While there is no way to make mining not boring, I think the developers did a good job of making as little boring as possible. I wouldn't play this game, but it's a good resource to look at for other games that want to incorporate mining on the side.

The premise is simple, you're an astroid miner in the rings around Saturn. What's interesting is what the dev's have done with that setting. 

The physics are neutonian on a 2D plane, this means that it follows neutonian laws of physics as closely as possible (all velocity is relative, you don't stop or start without some kind of vector force), I'll let a Mass Effect quote speak for that "...Sir Isacc Newton is the deadliest ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ in space!".

All the UI is diagentic. That means if you can see something, there is an in universe explanation for why. Why do you see your ship in motion? There's an unmanned drone above you recording your movements. Why does the mini-map work? You've installed one of a few active sensory systems such as LIDAR. Why do my read outs look the way they do? Your vehicle comes with an exchangeable dashboard package. 

Unreasonably great game. If Delta V: Rings of Saturn was $30 it would still be underpriced for what it is

> pirate wants money

This game really managed to tickle my ship outfitting autism. To my recollection, this might be the only game that lets you tweak the autopilot profiles and behaviours on top of just having different autopilot systems to choose from. The same goes for every other component of your ship, from microwage generator/emitters, lasers, RCS and ore processing units. While combat is not the primary focus, it is still present and can be rewarding.

This game manages to avoid the classic space game upgrade treadmill, where bigger ships are inherently better. At least for the most part. It sports a unique wear and tear system for all components, complete with your HUD glitching out, hard rebooting and throwing alarms until you fix a short.

Delta V goes on sale for decent discounts and is entertaining enough to dive into its hard sci-fi world even at full price. If you ever (re)watch The Expanse, this game makes for a great companion.

If you ever watched The Expanse and wanted to be a Belter mining the rings, this is the game. It respects real physics and orbital mechanics but remains fun and accessible. The atmosphere is simultaneously meditative and terrifying. Highly recommended for anyone who loves grounded space exploration.

This game is so much fun! It's a combination of various liminal spaces while avoiding scp-like monsters with specific abilities. You have to find key items and solve easy puzzles but the stress comes from the entities that are chasing you. 

Even the basic game can be stressful and challenging. When a monster catches you, you have to restart the level but keep all your items which is fair. They might add hard mode where this will most likely be off. A nightmare mode might be that you have to get through all levels without dying.

Every time you die, the game will generate a new layout for the current level and the more you die, the more rooms you will discover. The levels are quite big and there are currently 7 of them. I caught myself dying on purpose to get an easy seed or just find more rooms.

I've only just beat the 5th level, the poolrooms, and it took me 3 hours in story mode so far. You also get endless mode and a custom mode to play against as many entities and their combinations as you wish.

Monster designs and levels are amazing. These things are creepy af and their sounds are the best (worst). The ear guy and the smiler were outright terrifying for me. Can't help but kept lightly freaking out while playing this game. 

It's great, even in early access. I can't wait to see what else the dev has in stock. Also please add co-op. I normally don't enjoy co-op games but this one is a must with friends or random lobbies.

I genuinely love this game. The music, the atmosphere, and the visual identity are outstanding. It feels unique. Building up your empire is incredibly satisfying, and simply looking at what you have created is rewarding on its own. The soundtrack and overall game feel are easily some of the best aspects of the experience.

The economic loop is where the game truly shines. Growing your population increases hunger. Hunger pressures production. Luxury goods generate income but create new resource bottlenecks. You are constantly solving one problem only for another to appear. That tension is challenging and deeply satisfying. That is the magic of this game.

I also really like the recent changes to the card system. Making card decisions more meaningful, choosing whether to buy, dismiss, or spend coins for earlier blueprints, is absolutely the right direction. It adds weight to decisions and improves strategic planning.

That said, I hope future updates focus more on the core gameplay systems. Warfare, combat depth, and diplomacy currently feel underdeveloped compared to the strong foundation the game has elsewhere. These systems should drive long term replayability, especially in multiplayer, but they can become repetitive and lack strategic depth over time.

I also believe the early to midgame is the strongest and most engaging phase. Expanding depth in that stage might benefit more players than adding additional late game complexity, since not everyone consistently reaches those later stages.

Overall, I truly enjoy this game and recommend it. The foundation is excellent. With deeper core mechanics, it could become something truly exceptional.

Building up your empire is incredibly satisfying, and simply looking at what you have created is rewarding on its own. The soundtrack and overall game feel are easily some of the best aspects of the experience.

The economic loop is where the game truly shines. Growing your population increases hunger. Hunger pressures production. Luxury goods generate income but create new resource bottlenecks. You are constantly solving one problem only for another to appear. That tension is challenging and deeply satisfying. That is the magic of this game.

I also really like the recent changes to the card system. Making card decisions more meaningful, choosing whether to buy, dismiss, or spend coins for earlier blueprints, is absolutely the right direction. It adds weight to decisions and improves strategic planning.

That said, I hope future updates focus more on the core gameplay systems. Warfare, combat depth, and diplomacy currently feel underdeveloped compared to the strong foundation the game has elsewhere. These systems should drive long term replayability, especially in multiplayer, but they can become repetitive and lack strategic depth over time.

I also believe the early to midgame is the strongest and most engaging phase. Expanding depth in that stage might benefit more players than adding additional late game complexity, since not everyone consistently reaches those later stages.

Overall, I truly enjoy this game and recommend it. The foundation is excellent. With deeper core mechanics, it could become something truly exceptional.

I had the pleasure of reviewing this game for RPGFan.com, so if you want more in-depth, go check it out.

That said, this games sticks with you, in my opinion. It's so easy to enjoy that "just one more turn" hook.

It feels like you're a DM, sending a party out on adventures to discover new stories and such, but you're also managing a town to support those parties. It has so much going on gameplay-wise to engage you.

The combat and challenges being card-based keeps it really fast, light, and fun, and offers a lot of strategy. The amount of skills is overwhelming, remembering what affects what condition, but with a few playthroughs, you start to pick it up.

What really sells me is the flavour, too. It's been really cool getting steeped in that Nordic/Scandinavian lore and mythos, but presented with some fun tongue-in-cheek by the narrator.

While it feels "simple" graphically, it's still a well-textured and designed game, and any system can handle it. Love the character artwork in this, and item design work.

When I reviewed it, I'd played it on Switch (got it for free there but loved it so much I bought it and the sequal on Steam immediately after), which was good (though the UI isn't as friendly to a controller), but if that's your only option, you won't be disappointed. That said, it's definitely best on a computer with a mouse.

Anyways, take a chance on this one and lose hours of your life to a fun time!

While the game can have a rather steep curve, once you have the basics down and understand that the game WILL KILL YOUR CHARACTERS if you do something you're not prepared for, it becomes much more manageable. I still don't understand the buildings and the story, but as I've learned the combat and developed my strategy's around the different Gods- I've learned that piece by piece you develop with the difficulty of the game.

Currently my favorite game of all time. The story and general feel are just wonderful; you really get into the mindset of a mystical, danger-ridden world that has barely reawakened. If there was any negative I could say its just that there's not enough story events that I can play this every single year (instead I have to take a couple years of a brake so everything feels fresh again). Strong recommend.

Once you get the hang with the unique mechanics of this game, it just hooks you. Still today, after so many years, I keep coming back to Thea: The Awakening for another go, trying to convince myself it will just be.. one more turn..

It's not an easy game, but if you're into survival with crafting, party-management, battle minigames, and just love resource optimization like I do, then this is for you. Love the lore and the dark vibes. Better than its sequel.

Its hard to gauge how good the game is. Id recommend it overall, it does posses many BUTs tho. 

Do not play if you are perfectionist, or ADHD cursed.

Good:
- inovative systems, something new,
- good story, elements, 
- variability
- replayability (its ment for you to die)
- unbalanced skills, which incetivizes you in decision making

Bad:
- owerwhelming, timeconsuming crafting.
- too random. the game heavily relies on your knowledge of the enemies, encounters, when to do what. the skull system (showing how difficult encounter is) is incredibly unreliable. and you can never pull out after iniciating encounter.
- its incredibly complex. if you want to minmax, its a nigtmare. between items, skills, characters, events, resources management.. multiply by number of characters, and you will never make satysfying outcome.
- once you win, you play essentialy the same game. so id recomend starting on hard diffictulty, die often, because once you win there is no reason to play again.
- other challenges are often not worth the hassle. there is too many of them to keep track of, or effectively dealing with all of them. on top of that, if you lose them, your characters might get killed anyway.
- numbers win chalenges easily

What id like to see moded/changed:
- crafting should follow artifact crafting: only first row gives a stat, second amplifies it. 
- automated cooking
- challenges as a means of avoiding conflict (why my dwarf died from talking to orcs?), make some punishment for failing, yes, like all characters lose 20% of HP, but whats the point of avoiding battle, when someone can die anyway.
- ability to "auto battle" but redo it yourself if the computer kills some of your chars.
- see more info, like what percentage of death prevention each medic stat give, how much second medic in party participates in healing, etc.

That said, this games sticks with you, in my opinion. It's so easy to enjoy that "just one more turn" hook.

It feels like you're a DM, sending a party out on adventures to discover new stories and such, but you're also managing a town to support those parties. It has so much going on gameplay-wise to engage you.

The combat and challenges being card-based keeps it really fast, light, and fun, and offers a lot of strategy. The amount of skills is overwhelming, remembering what affects what condition, but with a few playthroughs, you start to pick it up.

What really sells me is the flavour, too. It's been really cool getting steeped in that Nordic/Scandinavian lore and mythos, but presented with some fun tongue-in-cheek by the narrator.

While it feels "simple" graphically, it's still a well-textured and designed game, and any system can handle it. Love the character artwork in this, and item design work.

When I reviewed it, I'd played it on Switch (got it for free there but loved it so much I bought it and the sequal on Steam immediately after), which was good (though the UI isn't as friendly to a controller), but if that's your only option, you won't be disappointed. That said, it's definitely best on a computer with a mouse.

Anyways, take a chance on this one and lose hours of your life to a fun time!

Currently my favorite game of all time. The story and general feel are just wonderful; you really get into the mindset of a mystical, danger-ridden world that has barely reawakened. If there was any negative I could say its just that there's not enough story events that I can play this every single year (instead I have to take a couple years of a brake so everything feels fresh again). Strong recommend.

Once you get the hang with the unique mechanics of this game, it just hooks you. Still today, after so many years, I keep coming back to Thea: The Awakening for another go, trying to convince myself it will just be.. one more turn..

It's not an easy game, but if you're into survival with crafting, party-management, battle minigames, and just love resource optimization like I do, then this is for you. Love the lore and the dark vibes. Better than its sequel.

Its hard to gauge how good the game is. Id recommend it overall, it does posses many BUTs tho. 

Do not play if you are perfectionist, or ADHD cursed.

Good:
- inovative systems, something new,
- good story, elements, 
- variability
- replayability (its ment for you to die)
- unbalanced skills, which incetivizes you in decision making

Bad:
- owerwhelming, timeconsuming crafting.
- too random. the game heavily relies on your knowledge of the enemies, encounters, when to do what. the skull system (showing how difficult encounter is) is incredibly unreliable. and you can never pull out after iniciating encounter.
- its incredibly complex. if you want to minmax, its a nigtmare. between items, skills, characters, events, resources management.. multiply by number of characters, and you will never make satysfying outcome.
- once you win, you play essentialy the same game. so id recomend starting on hard diffictulty, die often, because once you win there is no reason to play again.
- other challenges are often not worth the hassle. there is too many of them to keep track of, or effectively dealing with all of them. on top of that, if you lose them, your characters might get killed anyway.
- numbers win chalenges easily

What id like to see moded/changed:
- crafting should follow artifact crafting: only first row gives a stat, second amplifies it. 
- automated cooking
- challenges as a means of avoiding conflict (why my dwarf died from talking to orcs?), make some punishment for failing, yes, like all characters lose 20% of HP, but whats the point of avoiding battle, when someone can die anyway.
- ability to "auto battle" but redo it yourself if the computer kills some of your chars.
- see more info, like what percentage of death prevention each medic stat give, how much second medic in party participates in healing, etc.

This was my first kinetic novel, no choices to make, just read and think about some of the subjects that are thrown up in the story.  The reader can make their own mind about how deep or how seriously they want to take this. I found it easy to get involved with the characters, to understand the frustrations of trying to earn a living in a city where everyone lives the same boring life-style and where young people and their parents do not understand one another. The protagonist, a reluctant hero, goes for a walk  and meets a young girl one summer's night when his tv breaks down, and thats when it all starts as he has to  grow up and make life decisions that have consequences not just for himself, but other people. I can't say more without giving away the plot.
I enjoyed it. I liked the art style and while the music got a bit monotonous, its not intrusive and suits the story. It can be as deep and philosophical as you want, or the  complete opposite if you just want an interesting tale to entertain you, and there are enough twists to the plot to do that.
For less than the cost of a movie ticket you will get at least a double feature out of this novel, even better if its in a sale! So if you like tales that can make you think, its worth considering.
挺好的故事，UI很棒，美术可能缺点经费...

[b]Note:[/b] I received a Steam key to help with alpha testing although I had no involvement with the development.

[i]Snowdome[/i] is a kinetic visual novel developed by Lunatic Works and published by Lunatic Works and Kikai Digital.  You take on the role of Tan Yan, a young adult who works as a convenience store clerk in the city.  He lives his life day-to-day, feeling unappreciated and that life will never change.  He moved away from the country, as he didn't want to become a farmer like his parents before him.  One day, when Tan Yan decides to take a walk on the beach (because his TV is broken, so he can't watch his favorite show), he encounters a teenage girl.  She makes him an intriguing offer.  [i]Help me save the world.[/i]  This piques Tan Yan's interest and thus, his adventure begins.  As this is a kinetic novel, I'll stop my story synopsis here to avoid spoilers.  ^^

As for the gameplay, there isn't really much in [i]Snowdome[/i].  Since it is a kinetic novel, there are no choices, so you just play through the story from beginning to end.  There are a total of 14 chapters and an epilogue.  Of course, as this is a visual novel, there are plenty of CGs although no Steam achievements at the time of this review.  

Since I've gone over the story and gameplay, I'll move onto my critique.  Upon first playing [i]Snowdome[/i], I found myself drawn into the story.  Like Tan Yan, I was intrigued upon meeting the girl who claims she can help save the world.  At first, I couldn't help wondering what kind of story this would be.  Would it be supernatural, psychological, etc.?  Without trying to spoil, the story is quite complicated.  It will make the reader think and wonder what's real.  Also, what's happening to the people on the island where Tan Yan is?  Although it seemingly appears peaceful, there's definitely way more to it.  I especially loved the plot twists that happen very late in the story, which (again) made me think.  It's really difficult to explain what I loved about this story without spoiling certain parts, but I will say that it'll make you think a lot.  In regards to the writing, I felt that it was well-paced.  At times, I noticed some run-on sentences although the translation itself is more than passable.  It's easy to follow the story.  In regards to art, I liked the backgrounds.  They almost feel real at times and I liked the sprites.  The effects used were really well done, and the CGs were beautifully drawn.  As for the soundtrack, I enjoyed listening to the various tracks.  They give off that mysterious vibe that is prevalent throughout the story.  Oh, and there's also Chinese voice acting (except for Tan Yan).  It's relaxing listening to the voices while reading the story at the same time.

Overall, would I recommend [i]Snowdome[/i]?  If you're looking for a visual novel with an in-depth story, then yes, I would.  As I've mentioned, [i]Snowdome[/i] will make you think a lot, which I personally believe is a good thing.  XD  I wish it had Steam achievements, but this is my only negative (and a minor one at that).  For the asking price, I'd say it's worth getting, as it took me less than 9.5 hours to complete, so there's plenty of story.  However, if you'd rather wait for a bargain, there are sales, of course.  Even at full price, it's a good deal to get this game.  ^^

He moved away from the country, as he didn't want to become a farmer like his parents before him.  One day, when Tan Yan decides to take a walk on the beach (because his TV is broken, so he can't watch his favorite show), he encounters a teenage girl.  She makes him an intriguing offer.  [i]Help me save the world.[/i]  This piques Tan Yan's interest and thus, his adventure begins.  As this is a kinetic novel, I'll stop my story synopsis here to avoid spoilers.  ^^

As for the gameplay, there isn't really much in [i]Snowdome[/i].  Since it is a kinetic novel, there are no choices, so you just play through the story from beginning to end.  There are a total of 14 chapters and an epilogue.  Of course, as this is a visual novel, there are plenty of CGs although no Steam achievements at the time of this review.  

Since I've gone over the story and gameplay, I'll move onto my critique.  Upon first playing [i]Snowdome[/i], I found myself drawn into the story.  Like Tan Yan, I was intrigued upon meeting the girl who claims she can help save the world.  At first, I couldn't help wondering what kind of story this would be.  Would it be supernatural, psychological, etc.?  Without trying to spoil, the story is quite complicated.  It will make the reader think and wonder what's real.  Also, what's happening to the people on the island where Tan Yan is?  Although it seemingly appears peaceful, there's definitely way more to it.  I especially loved the plot twists that happen very late in the story, which (again) made me think.  It's really difficult to explain what I loved about this story without spoiling certain parts, but I will say that it'll make you think a lot.  In regards to the writing, I felt that it was well-paced.  At times, I noticed some run-on sentences although the translation itself is more than passable.  It's easy to follow the story.  In regards to art, I liked the backgrounds.  They almost feel real at times and I liked the sprites.  The effects used were really well done, and the CGs were beautifully drawn.  As for the soundtrack, I enjoyed listening to the various tracks.  They give off that mysterious vibe that is prevalent throughout the story.  Oh, and there's also Chinese voice acting (except for Tan Yan).  It's relaxing listening to the voices while reading the story at the same time.

Overall, would I recommend [i]Snowdome[/i]?  If you're looking for a visual novel with an in-depth story, then yes, I would.  As I've mentioned, [i]Snowdome[/i] will make you think a lot, which I personally believe is a good thing.  XD  I wish it had Steam achievements, but this is my only negative (and a minor one at that).  For the asking price, I'd say it's worth getting, as it took me less than 9.5 hours to complete, so there's plenty of story.  However, if you'd rather wait for a bargain, there are sales, of course.  Even at full price, it's a good deal to get this game.  ^^

This was my first kinetic novel, no choices to make, just read and think about some of the subjects that are thrown up in the story.  The reader can make their own mind about how deep or how seriously they want to take this. I found it easy to get involved with the characters, to understand the frustrations of trying to earn a living in a city where everyone lives the same boring life-style and where young people and their parents do not understand one another. The protagonist, a reluctant hero, goes for a walk  and meets a young girl one summer's night when his tv breaks down, and thats when it all starts as he has to  grow up and make life decisions that have consequences not just for himself, but other people. I can't say more without giving away the plot.
I enjoyed it. I liked the art style and while the music got a bit monotonous, its not intrusive and suits the story. It can be as deep and philosophical as you want, or the  complete opposite if you just want an interesting tale to entertain you, and there are enough twists to the plot to do that.
For less than the cost of a movie ticket you will get at least a double feature out of this novel, even better if its in a sale! So if you like tales that can make you think, its worth considering.
挺好的故事，UI很棒，美术可能缺点经费...

[b]Note:[/b] I received a Steam key to help with alpha testing although I had no involvement with the development.

[i]Snowdome[/i] is a kinetic visual novel developed by Lunatic Works and published by Lunatic Works and Kikai Digital.  You take on the role of Tan Yan, a young adult who works as a convenience store clerk in the city.  He lives his life day-to-day, feeling unappreciated and that life will never change.  He moved away from the country, as he didn't want to become a farmer like his parents before him.  One day, when Tan Yan decides to take a walk on the beach (because his TV is broken, so he can't watch his favorite show), he encounters a teenage girl.  She makes him an intriguing offer.  [i]Help me save the world.[/i]  This piques Tan Yan's interest and thus, his adventure begins.  As this is a kinetic novel, I'll stop my story synopsis here to avoid spoilers.  ^^

As for the gameplay, there isn't really much in [i]Snowdome[/i].  Since it is a kinetic novel, there are no choices, so you just play through the story from beginning to end.  There are a total of 14 chapters and an epilogue.  Of course, as this is a visual novel, there are plenty of CGs although no Steam achievements at the time of this review.  

Since I've gone over the story and gameplay, I'll move onto my critique.  Upon first playing [i]Snowdome[/i], I found myself drawn into the story.  Like Tan Yan, I was intrigued upon meeting the girl who claims she can help save the world.  At first, I couldn't help wondering what kind of story this would be.  Would it be supernatural, psychological, etc.?  Without trying to spoil, the story is quite complicated.  It will make the reader think and wonder what's real.  Also, what's happening to the people on the island where Tan Yan is?  Although it seemingly appears peaceful, there's definitely way more to it.  I especially loved the plot twists that happen very late in the story, which (again) made me think.  It's really difficult to explain what I loved about this story without spoiling certain parts, but I will say that it'll make you think a lot.  In regards to the writing, I felt that it was well-paced.  At times, I noticed some run-on sentences although the translation itself is more than passable.  It's easy to follow the story.  In regards to art, I liked the backgrounds.  They almost feel real at times and I liked the sprites.  The effects used were really well done, and the CGs were beautifully drawn.  As for the soundtrack, I enjoyed listening to the various tracks.  They give off that mysterious vibe that is prevalent throughout the story.  Oh, and there's also Chinese voice acting (except for Tan Yan).  It's relaxing listening to the voices while reading the story at the same time.

Overall, would I recommend [i]Snowdome[/i]?  If you're looking for a visual novel with an in-depth story, then yes, I would.  As I've mentioned, [i]Snowdome[/i] will make you think a lot, which I personally believe is a good thing.  XD  I wish it had Steam achievements, but this is my only negative (and a minor one at that).  For the asking price, I'd say it's worth getting, as it took me less than 9.5 hours to complete, so there's plenty of story.  However, if you'd rather wait for a bargain, there are sales, of course.  Even at full price, it's a good deal to get this game.  ^^

Stellaris is an incredibly fun grand strategy game, but it definitely has its share of frustrations. Like most Paradox titles, Stellaris relies heavily on its DLC to really shine. Expansions like Utopia and Apocalypse don’t feel optional they feel foundational. So many core systems, playstyles, and late game mechanics are tied to them that the base game can feel stripped down without their content. Had some of these ideas been included from the start instead of sold separately, I genuinely think the overall perception of the game would be much higher. Mechanically, it can also be exhausting at times. Dealing with AI empires especially during wars and peace negotiations can be a real test of patience. War exhaustion, claims, and peace deals often feel unintuitive or outright restrictive, turning conflicts into drawn-out chores rather than satisfying strategic victories. There are moments where the friction is high enough that stepping away from the game feels necessary. Despite all that, Stellaris remains a standout experience. The space setting gives it an identity that immediately separates it from more grounded Paradox games like Hearts of Iron or Victoria. Exploring the galaxy, encountering strange anomalies, and shaping an empire through sci fi storytelling makes even familiar 4X mechanics feel fresh.

The presentation is where the game truly excels. The soundtrack is phenomenal arguably Paradox’s best. Tracks like “Faster Than Light” and “Riding the Solar Wind” are permanently burned into my memory and perfectly capture that sense of wonder and scale. Visually, Stellaris is also a step above Paradox’s other titles. The art direction thrives in a space setting, from the stunning main menu backgrounds to the elegant loading screens and beautifully detailed ship models. Fallen Empires in particular stand out with aesthetics that feel ancient, powerful, and distinct.

Nearly a decade since its launch, Stellaris has evolved from a traditional "map-painter" into the most complex, emergent story generator in the 4X genre. In 2026, it stands as a massive, sprawling epic that can be as rewarding as it is overwhelming.

Stellaris is the kind of game where you sit down for an hour and suddenly it’s 3am. There’s just so much going on between exploration, diplomacy, war, and managing your empire. Every playthrough feels different depending on how you build your species and how the galaxy unfolds.

It can be overwhelming at first, but once it clicks, it’s incredibly rewarding. If you enjoy strategy games with depth and tons of replay value, this is an easy recommendatio

All in all, I find the base game is 7/10, and only if on sale.

I didn’t just play Stellaris. I lived a thousand lifetimes in the stars and somehow still had time to misplace a fleet or two. This game is a beautiful, chaotic masterpiece that balances empire management, diplomacy, exploration, existential dread, and a soundtrack that makes you feel like the galaxy itself is cheering you on.

You start small with one planet, a handful of ships, and big dreams. Before long, you are juggling dozens of star systems, negotiating with alien species whose hairstyles make no sense, and wondering why your economy is somehow collapsing while your scientists keep getting eaten by space whales. It is overwhelming in the best way.

After over 200 hours, I still find myself discovering new events, strange anomalies, and ridiculous interactions that make the galaxy feel alive. The depth of Stellaris never stops surprising me.

Exploration is endlessly rewarding. Surveying new systems, discovering anomalies, and encountering strange alien civilizations constantly injects wonder and curiosity into the game. Every jump into the unknown feels like opening a present from the universe.

Science and research are deeply satisfying. You can focus on technologies that improve your economy, your fleets, or your espionage capabilities. Watching your research pay off in clever solutions or occasionally catastrophic failures creates a real sense of growth and accomplishment.

Diplomacy is a riot. You can form alliances, trade deals, federations, or rivalries, and the AI has a knack for surprising you. A friendly neighbor can suddenly declare war over a minor insult, which is both terrifying and hilarious. The events, including random crises and story-driven encounters, give the galaxy a living, unpredictable feel.

Combat is tactical and fun. Fleet composition, weapon types, and strategic positioning all matter. I have spent hours carefully planning an attack only to watch a single space anomaly annihilate my fleet. It is infuriating, but it also makes every victory feel earned.

Empire management is complex without being overwhelming. Balancing planets, resources, pops, and buildings keeps you engaged, and every decision has consequences. Tiny details matter, like which planet gets priority for housing or which system should be defended first.

Customization and DLC elevate the experience further. Creating unique species, governments, and fleets adds personality to your empire. The expansions, from robots to megastructures, bring depth and variety that make each new game feel fresh.

The soundtrack deserves a special mention. Every track perfectly captures the awe, mystery, and occasional absurdity of galactic life. Exploring new systems, managing crises, or watching your empire thrive all feels cinematic with this music playing. Some of my favorite Stellaris moments are just sitting and listening as the galaxy hums beneath my command.

What makes it truly special is how alive the universe feels. Tiny stories emerge from your empire. A diplomat accidentally insults an alien emperor. A scientist invents something horrifying. A planet revolts because you did not stock enough snacks for the population. Somehow, in the middle of all the spreadsheets and menus, you start to care about these little details. You celebrate triumphs, groan at disasters, and occasionally laugh so hard you spill your coffee onto your keyboard.

The humor is subtle but gold. Watching your perfectly balanced fleet get obliterated by a space storm or realizing your friendly alliance was actually a trap hits that perfect combination of oh no and haha yes. Stellaris does not just let you play a game. It lets you live in a galaxy that is weird, beautiful, terrifying, and absurd all at once.

In short, Stellaris is more than a 4X game. It is a love letter to curiosity, creativity, music, and the tiny chaos of trying to be a responsible galactic overlord. It will frustrate you, surprise you, make you laugh, and make you genuinely care about the civilizations you create.

Stellaris is one of the most ambitious and satisfying space 4X strategy games I’ve ever played. From the moment your science ship jumps through the first hyperlane into uncharted systems, you’re exploring a galaxy teeming with mystery, danger, and opportunity. Every game feels unique because the galaxy is procedurally generated with different star layouts, anomalies, resources, and alien empires every time.

What really sets Stellaris apart is how personalized your playthrough can feel. You don’t just pick a faction and go; you build the story of your civilization from the ground up. You choose your species traits, ethics like Militarist or Xenophile, government type like Democracy or Oligarchy, civics that shape your society’s bonuses and quirks, and an Origin that gives your empire a narrative backstory and starting conditions. This means you can roleplay a xenophobic war machine bent on domination, a pacifist federation championing galactic peace, or even a rock-eating Lithoid empire obsessed with mining minerals.

The diplomacy and politics systems are genuinely engaging. You’ll encounter dozens of alien empires and decide whether to trade, negotiate, spy, or go to war. If you prefer cooperation, the Federations system lets you bind empires together into powerful alliances with shared goals and a galactic senate that votes on laws and sanctions, making the political landscape feel alive and consequential.

Exploration and narrative shine too. Science ships uncover anomalies and ancient relics that trigger story events, open archaeological sites, and reveal hidden history scattered across the galaxy, giving your playthrough memorable twists that feel more than random numbers.

Combat and empire management blend macro and micro in a satisfying way. You expand starbases to control chokepoints, design custom fleets, build megastructures like Dyson spheres, and balance your economy of energy, minerals, research, and influence. Strategic wars aren’t just about who has bigger fleets; they’re about timing, alliances, and choosing when to strike or negotiate.

There’s a learning curve and Stellaris gets dense, especially with all the DLC content, but that complexity is part of its charm. Every decision feels meaningful and every game organically crafts its own tale of rise, conflict, and sometimes unexpected peace. If you love grand strategy, emergent storytelling, and sprawling galactic sandbox experiences, Stellaris is a must-play that keeps rewarding you the deeper you dive in.

Stellaris is an imperfect beast that has been in revision based development for years, but if you like grand 4x strategy, this game is absolutely one of the hands down best diverse and comprehensive games you can get into that still offers enough of a GUI that is asthetically pleasing enough to not just be spreadsheets and sprites, something that I am aware many folks are preferential of to help hold attention while processing the cognitive load of learning complex systems. 

It has flaws certainly, the games grand vision is not yet done either, there is still work to be done, but what is on offer for it's pros are:

[b] PROS [/b]


- Diverse ways to play and engage with multiple highly customizable factions with a wide variety of playstyles and objectives 

- A high replayablility thanks to different ways to structure spawning, scenarios, and random encounters 

- Extremely versatile modding capabilities to the point you can play 4 or 5 other games in this game from other universes all built entirely by modders as total conversions 

- Easy to pick up and put down alone or with friends with minimal desync issues and a lot of ways to adjust to accommodate different ways of playing with others

- Solid memes  

The Cons however are: 

[b] CONS [/b]


- No matter how much optimization they put into the game, late game becomes a laggy slog just because of the sheer volume of functional colonized planets and massive militaries, and sometimes makes long term games way too much of a slog to enjoy finishing fully as a result

- Patches are always a gamble on what was improved over what needs fixing due to the sheer volume of things to consider and attend and how they operate in tandem, and any major updates can only ever really be fully tested by the actual player base effectively, just because the developers cannot realistically replicate every potential scenario to test every element of the game, something that is annoying to deal with as a consumer being forced into the job of a QA testing division that realistically is challenging to sustain at a quality with all the variables in something like this without being a MASSIVE Expense 

- The expense of DLC collectively makes it's reach limited, and even though the subscription is a fair price for a certain narrow ratio of investment vs return, adding more subscription management to peoples lives that are already far overburdened by more important priorities also being subscription based makes its incentivization way less effective than alternatives 

Now with these cons of course, I would be just another minimally useful entitled screecher on the internet pouring more water into an already very watered down glass of something that is not water that the reader prefers to drink not watered down, but instead I have here some marginally more useful:

[b] RECOMMENDATIONS [/b]


So, starting with the slowdown, let the player optionally toggle into a less processing intensive generalized mode that cuts down on the games exacts, converts the visuals, all with the click of a button. Let it be the "Tap out" Button that can then be toggled off after a while for anyone who wants to sacrifice some complexity for the sake of actually being able to finish. Some people will see the button and ignore it as long as possible to grit there way through, thus giving an additional motivation to power onwards, while others will appreciate the relief. 

For the QA, make it part of the community and the game. Add a monthly challenge system of specialized scenarios revolving around obscure or uncommon mechanics or bugs, and offer the top scoring players of the challenge either lifetime free DLC for the game and / or special unique titles / cosmetics and / or extra copies of stellars + all the DLC for them to give others. Have them submit recordings / screenshots of problems as they occur and give rewards to the "Bug Hunters" as well as the top scoring challenge winners. This will be wildly unpopular at first and attract more frustration with paradox if it is mishandled thoe, and I only have so much room in a steam review so if anyone at the studio is interested in mitigation strategies and community engagement vectors, do hit me up! 

And lastly, the price. $5.00 a month for the game and all DLC, or $20.00 a month for every paradox game and DLC a month subscription, including the base game with the subs. 

Each un-interrupted year of subscription adds the base game and a handful of DLC permanently to the subscribers account on the $5 sub, or on the $20.00 sub they get to choose what game they get the core game and all DLC permanently on their account for. 

Add monthly subscription rewards such as titles, badges and unique cosmetics as well to each game. 

Add this same $5.00 month to a specific paradox game sub to every paradox game, and let people gift the subscriptions to others over steam for additional rewards for being a gifter. 

There are still plenty of folks out there who want to access and learn these titles with their friends, and this gives a less expensive buy in that will help expand a pool of new blood. 

Again, this has few right ways to fully implement and approach, and a LOT of more harmful wrong ways so, always happy to have a chat with the developers one on one to expand on those concepts! 

I have a laundry list of additional content recommendations and re-tools / optimization suggestions as well but, I think this does a good enough job solidifying i'm someone worth chatting too directly.

As for the final word on if you should play this game?

I'm recommending this to support the project BUT

1. If you expect a polished, complete experience, go somewhere else. This game is still in its very early stages and totally needs another couple of years to cook properly.
2. The foundation is really solid and I liked what I've found so far.
3. Not for solo players.

PROS

- Really good feeling overall. Weapons have a realistic "oomph" and every shot feels meaningful
- The game forces the player to study an approach before starting an operation. Rushing in or forgetting to check a corner usually means you end up back at the lobby real quickly.
- Enemies, for the most part, react well to your presence. Footsteps, suppressed shots, creaking doors opening and (afaik) even VOIP will give out your position. 
- Incredible customization for guns. You can change almost any aspect of your weapons, from the barrel to the stock to the ammo you will use. This is FUNDAMENTAL if you want to succeed. Know your guns.
- Gore and blood are there, and wounded groaning enemies can be finished off with well placed rounds. True grit.
- Extremely deep and funny lobby activities. You can sit on chairs and choose missions from an actual Windows inspired terminal that lets you tweak even the time format in the lower corner. Hysterical.

CONS

- Extremely low map and task variety. Sure we are in Alpha stage, but this is imo something mandatory for any tactical shooter. Major updates do add new elements, but a randomization in objectives should be in order very soon if the title hopes to survive. You play the same map five times, you get bored, you move to other titles. This is not ideal.
- Braindead AI, they won't coordinate to flank or pose a real threat. They will usually wait in corners for you and rush once your position is compromised. They don't aimbot (thankfully), but they end up spraying an insane amount of bullets towards your general direction.
- Multiplayer is pretty much a must here. Soloing a mission is "possible", but not recommended, especially on higher tiers. Also, every aspect of this game is made for multiplayer, so... sorry solo players.
- Maps feel bland and deprived of life and details. 

ROOM FOR IMPROVEMENT (For devs that read reviews)
- Better arm animations, the twisted wrist hurts my heart
- Better details on maps such as the neighborhood, that feels completely artificial.
- AI pathfinding needs improvement, NPCs are too easy to lure and smoke.
- AI teammates for simulation mode. Official operations should stay purely MP for the sake of realism.
- Variety, variety, variety and random objectives!

As I said, if you wish to support a good project, get this. Otherwise, let it cook.

Operator is a tough tactical shooter that punishes every mistake, but that challenge is exactly what makes it so enjoyable. 
Missions demand patience, teamwork, and smart decisions, and even small errors can wipe your squad. The game is undeniably brutal, yet the intensity creates some of the most satisfying moments when a plan finally comes together. 
The visuals are simple but effective, the sound design keeps the tension high, and the gear options give you just enough freedom without overwhelming you. If you like shooters that make you think and keep you on edge, Operator delivers a harsh but rewarding experience that’s as fun as it is unforgiving.

I picked this game up because it was only $20, and it caught my eye with how it was presenting itself as a super realistic tactical shooter. I almost didn't buy it since it looked possibly overwhelming with the sheer depth of the gameplay, but the controls are actually reasonably intuitive and have great flow, and the weapon customization (while being slightly bugged at the moment and janky by not being able to remove attachments) is actually fairly simple for something so in-depth. Overall, I definitely enjoyed this game, and it was worth the $20.

That being said, there are a fair amount of bugs in the game, but that really shouldn't a surprise since the game is still in early access. To anyone interested in this game, but is on the fence, look up some review videos to get a better sense of how this game is, and if it's something you'd actually enjoy. It's definitely fun, but also still in development, and it shows in some places, so keep that in mind if you decide to buy this game.
