[h1] SQUARE BIRD [/h1]

Square is a pretty fun parody of Flappy Bird, where instead of a bird, you control a simple cube in 2D space. The main goal isn’t just to dodge obstacles, but to collect small diamonds. They don’t really serve any purpose besides making your score look good in the stats — there’s nothing to spend them on — but they do add a bit of thrill to the game.

Visually, the game has a neon style and looks pretty decent for an indie project. The main draw here isn’t the graphics, though, but the ability to tweak the level to your liking: even with procedural generation, the developer added sliders to adjust obstacle frequency and cube speed. It’s handy if you want to either relax or go full hardcore. It’s a bit of a bummer, though, that there aren’t many types of obstacles.

Sure, the game lacks a leaderboard and cloud saves, but props for the Steam achievements. The developer didn’t promise the moon on the store page, so you can’t really call them out for lying. As a simple time-killer, the game does its job perfectly. Could it be better? Sure, but for its price, it’s totally worth a few casual gaming sessions.

[h3] Verdict: [/h3] https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3628285134

For no good reason inventory systems are so tiny and items are so obsurdly large. Simply Storing things will be your struggle for majority of the early to mid game. 
There is no reason for this? Ive cooked up many excuses for many games, But I do not understand why it deliberately restricts your ability to "survival craft" in this "survival craft" game. 
It just makes no sense. 
My base is a 3x3x2, 1 of these tiles are for crafting, the rest is STORAGE.
You walk into my base and just see the ugliest Storage container tetris. 

The amount of running early on you will do is insane, The questline sends you from one side of the map to the other over and over again. The quest consist of this. 
Go to X location 
Trade X Item 
Trade X Item
Rinse repeat.

The inventory system and transportation needs alot of work, Many times in my playthrough I was thinking... Why do I put myself through this, I could just go play something that respects my time. 

Once you get your first car, Setup your base. Your good. 
But, Unlike other survival craft games. This... Is multiple sessions.. Im talking Unemployed sessions, Not Dad gamer sessions

But, I come from Ark survival evoloved, I dont care. Ill sit her and headbut a brick wall until it works. 

All in all, A really fun enjoyable game. 
After the moments of early game torture. Its addicting. 

Spoiler/Meta "Tips to get your first car". 
[spoiler]Roam around about 2km from start zone, Find your first Scrapyard settlement. Trade for the electrical compenents(Gold scrap early is hard to find) for the first car, Plop 654583 Storage containers down(Loot about 100 stone to unlock the larger stone storage) Roam again for the rest of the metal parts for the car. [/spoiler]

This game has that "just one more hour" gameplay to it. Graphically it is like an early PS2 game, rather basic but with some good lighting and real time shadows, textures are quite basic but serve their purpose. It is not hideous, just not a visual feast, but I am not really bothered, graphics do not always make a game and this one at such an early stage is pretty darn good. There is a grind and a lot of going forward and backward

While combat is basic and clunky, it is still fun, melee combat is essentially a simple swing of a chosen weapon, no combo's and gun play is pretty basic but functional, just point and shoot. There is a skill stat system in game, you earn XP to level up which in turn gives you points to spend on specific character traits as you wish, this covers accuracy, stealth, resource gathering, combat precision and more. Basic survival stats for food, water, health and contamination (cured by taking crafted pills). The inventory system needs work, resources regularly move around in your storage when you add or remove items making it a pain to keep track of where things are. Complex crafting recipes can not be pinned, so you have to try and remember the recipes resulting in more backwards and forwards in menus to recall requirements. You can craft direct from storage which is good, unless a crafting bench is required, then that does not work.

You do not chop trees for wood which is bizarre, instead interacting with trees and other foliage just gives you bugs, sticks, resin and other basic resources. The world is not surprisingly quite empty due to the setting, but there are settlements around you can interact with, random loot spots with aggressive survivors of varying types depending on the quality of loot. The world is pretty big and despite all the empty space between POI's, there is life such as wanderers, wild animals, raiders, guards and sometimes watching them can be funny as they battle between them. There are various vehicles that fly around, they can be quite dumb though as they crash into rocks or get stuck on structures.

I have yet to build my own base, but I have helped a settlement rebuild theirs, this means I can use their base for storage and resources, shelter when the heatwave hits and also make use of their guards to protect stuff. 
[strike]I am close to completing the repair of a vehicle so have not yet experienced how the vehicles handle, but I look forward to it as running around can be tiresome
[/strike] Edit: I have my first vehicle and it is pretty cool, the vehicles have fully upgradable stats to really tune them up, they handle fine and certainly quicker for getting around, just make sure to refuel with the correct fuel type and get insurance on them. 

It is a grind as I have stated already, but finding and unlocking blueprints for new buildings, vehicles and equipment is satisfying and I have no doubt having a vehicle will greatly help with lugging resources around.

When I first went into this game, I had low expectations, the clunky feeling and seemingly basic presentation left me unsure, but it has clicked with me and I am loving it, particularly since it has a proper singleplayer offline play option which I very much approve of. It does have mutiplayer available, but I prefer singleplayer so I can not comment on that aspect of the game. For the money I have certainly got my value out of it and I look forward to playing more and the future updates.

It's starting to feel a lot more like 3rd person Kenshi which is great!

Give us the ability to join factions, and have roaming faction patrols that end up fighting each other in the open field, not just the outpost assaults, then I'll be so happy! 

Maybe instanced underground vaults that are like mini pve dungeons would be great too. 

But overall, an amazingly unique game. I love it!

Super chill game, keeps surprising me with every play session. Im playing in a solo world.

-Graphics 6/10, nothing to ride home about, but fits the vibe of the game.

-Map 6/10, The only negative I can really find and it's nit picking, theirs no elevation within the geography, cant even recall a hill or incline, just some large rocks and multi story buildings, most of which I couldn't get to the 2nd level. Theirs plenty of things to loot, and little camps/poi's to ransack. Although you do alot of "running" I wouldnt call it a running simulator like some other survivals end up feeling like.

-Combat. Janky, but satisfying when you start to get it down, I like how headshots kill things quickly, makes taking the time to line them up feel worth it.

-Loot. Theirs plenty of it, and it's fun to explore and search for more. Inventory management can be a bit of a pain, but this is most survival games, and they have plenty of storage solutions to utilize early on.

-Building. Haven't fully delved into this aspect yet, but based on the blueprints Ive unlocked, I'm pretty excited. Defenses, walls, gates, decorations, crafting stations, underground bases, vehicles, it seems like quite a bit tbh.

-Performance. Granted im playing solo, IVe had 0 issue. Temps are great, FPS is steady, 0 crashes or bugs. alot of times when events like meteor showers happen in a game like this you'll notice a hitch, or hiccup but nothing of the sort here, while multiple meteors smash into the ground all around you with explosions and dust clouds it runs smooth.

Definitely worth the price if you're looking for a chill, relaxing survival game to scratch that itch.

[edit] with more time spent in the game, it's more expansive than I thought, it has some bugs here an there, mostly vehicle related but nothing game breaking still, and 50 hrs in, still finding surprises and new loot.

So I like where this game is heading so far, I hope it improves. I got it on 50% off sale so that somewhat affects my expectations considering I paid like $12. I'll give you a basic run down on what to expect, and followed by what the developer needs to do to really improve the game.

You start of waking up in the center of the map with nothing, initially your main concern/problem is food and water. Once you get the basics down of how to build the lowest tier water collector and a bit of charcoal to clean the dirty water, water is no longer really an issue for the rest of the game unless you need lots for farming or something. 

The game is somewhat grindy, i found it grindy in mostly a good way, although to build stations and many base pieces takes a ton of resources. All of which are fairly easy to get through the expense of time. There are many settlements on the map, you have the option of putting in your scrap to finish buildings, utilize refining/processing stations, many of these settlements will generate resources over time if you donate food and water to keep their working population high. There are basic quests and objectives to do but nothing in the line of a true story or overall purpose other than survival at least that I have found in 28 hours.

You can build and upgrade many items, many of those blueprints are attained by either doing quests or finding in loot, or buying from vendors. There a many vehicles you can build that can get you across the map fairly quickly.

Now where it needs improvement (by the dev):
[olist]
[*]The two robots, Ron & the other one I cant remember the name of the other robot, anyway you need to tone down the sfx spam of their robot sounds. Your going to spend a lot of time initially at this outpost until you get a base setup that will take a ♥♥♥♥ metric ton of resources and when you are at the starter POI these two robots spam the ♥♥♥♥ out of their sfx constantly and follow you doing it. Tone it back to like 1/10 of what it is now.
[*]Dev you need an artistic vision to how you design these Places of interest/Settlements, you probably need to invest in more assets for the game to facilitate this. At no point was I like wow thats interesting looking, or this tells are story, or makes the world feel lived in. Probably the closest thing I saw that looked interesting and part of a living world was the One Guard Dynasty Dome city. So yeah if you can in an editor design like 50 unique looking pois that the random world generator could use to plop into the game that would go a long way to feeling like anywhere I went I had something cool and interesting to look at.
[*] I actually never built myself an actual base, seemed pointless really. It takes so many resources to build the simplest ♥♥♥♥, so whats the point. Id have to spent another 20 hours ingame farming resources at the various settlements that slowly produce over time to be able to build anything bigger than a shack with few stations around it. And if you're going to keep things the way it is, needing metric ♥♥♥♥ tons of resources to build a decent looking base, you need to come up with some more efficient mid to late game ways to collect resources other than settlements and banging on wrecked cars for parts with the stone hammer.
[*] flesh out some lore or something about the factions I can collect and read because after 28 hours I knew zero One Guard Dynasty (green faction), the bandits, or syndicate. 
[/olist]

So yeah I recommend the game. Id like to see it develop further with more depth. Some of the animations are janky but somehow in a way that adds to the games charm. If you plan on building a base, being a good citizen and building up settlements as what seems like you're meant to do its going to be a grindfest. As an indie game its updated regularly, will keep you busy for a weekend or two but it needs some more character and staying power, because once you've done every action in the game to collect resources 300 times, killed the same enemies over and over, eventually it will burn you out and you'll put it away waiting for something new to sink your teeth into.
Pretty amazing game, 200+ hours and I still enjoy the heck out of it. However if Dynamic Trading 0.8 is introduced to the game with no way to opt out, I will no longer play the game.
Passion project that I love seeing evolve. Worth keeping on your radar or getting it!
Fun game and can be addictive.

The gameplay loop is pretty addictive and the wasteland is pretty brutal. One of the only games I've ever played that feels like a real apocalyptic landscape. It's a little janky but definitely worth a try if you like survival crafting games at all.

I would recommend this to others, the gameplay loop is solid and has a unique feel.  There is a bit of jank here and there and some of the control schemes are a poor choice but nothing too bad other than the issue with vehicles falling through the world map, which IS a serious issue that has to be resolved sooner than later.  People WANT it to be fixed because they enjoy playing the game.  

Pick the game up on a sale, this has the potential to be something special with a bit more time under the sun.

spent 6 hours grinding for materials to build a vehicle, I sit back and finally admire my work when a meteor aimbots the ♥♥♥♥ out of my dustglider and obliterates all my progress and effort. Please add manual saving for single player. Just put me off so bad can't recommend this

edit: I think I enjoy suffering

I have 200 hours in this game so far and It's been a blast !! The Devs have been consistently adding new content and  actively fixing issues . i highly recommend this game . the building/crafting mechanics are awesome .
The game still being worked on but it is arguably cool seeing the world develop around you. Its ascendingly fun, and the developers are hella active so NEW THINGS!! WHOO! Enjoy the wasteland
this game is very good and very fun also its hard but worth it. One of the best games most have never played!
I enjoyed it, not for a Solo player though. Also wished it had more admin controls for my world. Still in EA though so that could be coming soon.

This one is under the radar early access worth to try.
It offers multiple types of gameplay in an amusing mad max style game with some surprises.

Still in development, so some unwanted interactions can happen, but getting up and recovering is not hard.
It' a struggling start (gets hot in the dessert) until you figure out some tips and tricks on storage, how to save your car from drunk flying NPC-s and dodging the hyenas.

I play games to spend some time in the day and chill. As most currently can be achieved early on you need to choose your end goal in building, rushing POI and making faction enemies to get your blood flowing.
The fun for me is helping new people and developing settlements, finding the best gear and scavenging for parts that you can produce if you want.

Game has potential and hours fun. Playing in groups on private servers or just singleplayer for mammoth builds and world development are options.
I recommend joining servers to test and consider game directions with game discord comments/suggestions.

There are ofc flying cars. If this is how cars will drive in the future, it's good to learn now what awaits. 
Just build one, or shoot one of the npc ones down and fix it, but the owner might have some say in that move.
Fun wasteland survival, had a lot of laughs with friends as we crashed and burned more vehicles than I can count. Can't wait to see where they take this game.

This game is rough, it's still EA and has some serious jank in certain UI/gameplay elements. That being said, I highly recommend it, the core gameplay loop is developing quite nicely. They're not trying to make this another hand-holding experience, they want it to be difficult, think DayZ (hopefully they can get to that level of detail eventually). I would like to see the melee combat tweaked more, but overall combat is pretty satisfying. I kinda look at this game like DayZ lite, and I mean that as a compliment.

Good game. It's frequently updated. Although there are some bugs right now and the UI is a bit inconvenient, these can be fixed in the long run.

The highlight is the game concept. It’s somewhat like Minecraft’s random maps, combined with Kenshi-style character survival, plus elements of faction missions like in S.T.A.L.K.E.R. And if you get tired of the singleplayer world, you can take your upgraded character to the official multiplayer server, or invite friends to join your world.

I’m very optimistic about this game in the long term. Keep up the good work.

Absolutely love the concept, the guys behind this have been carefully curating the experience us as players want from this game while still maintaining a healthy dose of pain as the creators intended. You gotta be somewhat of a masochist to enjoy this game and the learning curve is hard but that's what makes this game a gem, it's a breath of fresh air compared to these soulless AAA slop games we've been fed these past few years. 

All in all, owner of the game personally stated in his discord today "I will never stop working on this game for you guys!" to me that speaks for itself.

Really fun game with a dedicated team working on it. Don't get me wrong--it's outlandishly difficult most of the time, but here's hoping that some balance and more QoL improvements come with time. I look forward to seeing what comes of this passion project and check back weekly to see what the devs have gotten up to and added or tweaked. If you liked "Fallen Earth" back in the day, then you'll probably like the vibe of "Plains of Pain."

Game has some rough edges, but the devs are quite active and have been making great progress towards 1.0. I highly recommend checking out some of the steam guides for the game; You get stat reset points, and there's definitely some stats that you NEED early, but will want to reset out of later.

This is one of those hidden early access gems, that while not complete, has so much potential and hours and hours of fun to guarantee a great bang for your buck. What's more, Frost and the other Dev's of the tiny team are more actively communicative and involved in answering feedback, playing alongside, and updating the game truely listening to players wishes concerns and criticisms. If you like Mad Max/ wasteland survival crafting, this ones a definite must to pick up in EA, or at least keep on your wishlist to keep up to date on.

There's a recent review downvoting it due to losing your vehicle under the map. It's apparent to me they don't know that you can and should insure your vehicle, so if it gets stuck you can literally /Recover and it'll pop back up. Something Dev's put in as an answer to the clipping while they continue diligently working on many facests of the game.
A good mix of Mad Max & Kenshi for open world survival. Highly active Devs who actually care about the game and also the community that supports them. Gets better with every update!

OMG what an awesome game!! 44 hours in so far and it is such a blast already in early access. Atmosphere is great, love all the factions plus the lowlifes. Vehicles. Overall it's been a great experience for me. Can't wait to see future updates.

To the Devs:

Great job on this so far, I am really enjoying this game. I know you have heard it a bunch of times but yeah sure, it does give me that Kenshi vibe but is it a copycat? No. Plains of Pain already has it's own identity with features that seperate it from Kenshi like the Meteor/Heat events, the Crafting system, the survival mechanics. As long as it keeps getting love and attention I think this has the potential to be a really good one.

The only bug I encounted so far was when I was inside a building at (Bullet Ace I think his name is?) and during a meteor shower I was hit and killed but I wouldn't say it is a major bug priority as it isn't too bad to get back to where you last died to retrieve your belongings.

Quality of life I would say is more important. For example; when looting a corpse I can see the inventory of the corpse and choose what I want to take. Looting a container however I cannot see what it holds and I pick something up one by one each time I press the "F" key and most of the time I have to drop a bunch of stuff I do not want. It's just little things like that I would improve; being able to see container inventory, having to actually sleep 8 or so hours instead of a minute, when enemies are killed and turned into skeletons it is a little hard to see and remember where they died, especially when you are fighting multiple enemies, etc.

For anyone thinking about playing; if you enjoy apocalyptic survival then this may be for you. It is EA so you need to keep in mind it is not entirely fleshed out yet and the beginning can be hard but once you understand how to play it gets easier and is fun. And maybe when you see that giant gorilla thing in the distance don't run up to screw around with it otherwise you'll get crushed like I did. Enjoy.

- There are a few quirks that are not well-received, particularly regarding the locking system during combat.
- The game would benefit from a locking mechanism similar to the one used in Conan Exiles, allowing players to lock onto characters or enemies more effectively.
- Despite these minor issues, the overall gameplay experience is highly enjoyable and the game is considered very good.

A niche game for a niche audience. Interesting mix of hardcore survival +RPG. Reminds me of DayZ with a setting of classic Fallout. Various factions exist with a reputation system to work through, shaping your experience within the world. Crafting is fun starting with basic stuff and learning blueprints as you progress. Combat is a bit watered down. I suggest starting on an official server to get the hang of it.

Solid indie base building survival IP, the AI thing might push some people away, but honestly I think it was done in a much better way here than bigger IP, of course in the end games are about inspiring by creating and AI creation is gay so it can't possibly carry the power but it can be useful in dev progress and I'd say here it was a positive thing.

Game lacks awareness and number of players but, solo or online, its engaging, its simple but interesting an world, somewhere between post-apocalyptic/Cyberpunk/Fallout its quite immersive world!

Solid 8/10
Woah, awesome... awesome. It's like a beautiful Kenshi RPG, in it's own beautiful way. I can only imagine how much better it will get with time. So much fun so far

Really cool wasteland survival game, bit clunky around the edges but darn ya can get hooked up pretty easy! World feels alive compared to other same titles, building is allright, mad max/dune vibes are there especially with everything on ultra sand in your eyes and sun particle are a treat. You can easely get sidetracked with quests or oh look that new POI in the distance or the trading/delivery system! Item's have passives and it feels like a semi mmo. Beside a difficulty spike after 20 lvls (but who dosen't welcome a challenge) and the fact it dosen't have that many online players atm it's really a blast to play!
Fun game. Unique in it's execution.
Nice!
Is it janky? Yes. But you get to play as a Mad Max character with a full crafting system and rusted hover cars, and the weapon progression genuinely feels earned.

As of right now the game is fairly minimalistic.  That's okay as it's still in beta.  It's on the right path though.

At 3 hours what have I done or discovered?
-Weird not zombie half naked guys charge you till you whack them a few times then they try to run away.  This is nice as it show the "oh sh**" factor of the npc's
-Restricted zone means don't go there noob.  I went, that's obviously for other people.  Noop.  I boldly ran away.
-Clear defined paths to crafting stuff.  Sure you can make floating hover cars that will probably give you cancer, but f it.  Back to the future is finally here.  I don't have one yet but I see them and am working on my own.  I know what I need to put it together and it shouldn't be too hard, but yet hard enough as it IS the apocalypse.  
-It warns you not to loot hoard.  It meant it.  The loot gremlin in me is sad that I can't hold half a walmart's worth of stuff before I get weighed down, but it also forces the need to make a camp and storage.
-Interactable NPC's are kind of bland.  I'm sure the team will make the game more populated eventually.  It's the end of the world, but that doesn't mean it has to be so dry.

I can recommend this game at this time.  It's not done, by a lot, but I have no doubt that this will get better and better as the years tick on.  So long as we don't find ourselves in a real world Fallout situation, I think this team will get there.

It's great, but has quite a ways to go. I will say the single player experience, as it exists now, feels pretty limited. 

Not quite as bad as playing RUST alone, but I think in time, this will continue to improve.

It's fun to explore, get cool gear, new weapons, tons and tons of crafting components, but the quest system is also sorely lacking. 

"Go get 2 tomatoes" was a quest I received about 5 times in a row. The solo experience is.. lacking as I said, BUT-

My biggest complaint is vehicles. You work SO HARD just to get a functioning one, and the glitches cause them to either instantly explode for no reason, or get completely lost under the environment. It's so discouraging, and feels so so so, so so so, so bad every time.

Hello... I love this game, I play it offline in PVE and it reminds me of Kenshi the desert biome and just trying to survive the brutal landscape , I have spent the first 20 hours building up my Charater to reasonable efficency and just built my first vehicle, now I have to build my base and have it decked out with NPC's who will run my oil and petrolium production, my metal production whilst I go  and explore this vast map to scavange the matrerial I need to be greater. So many weapons, armour and quests to get. I recommend Plains of pain.

There are plenty of reviews comparing it to kenshi and city 20 and also a number of reviews calling out legitimately janky issues with the game.

All that stuff is surface level. What really matters is that since launch I've seen the devs clearly state an ambitious goal in their dev logs and then achieve it. CONSISTENTLY!

This game has improved greatly since it's early days and based on the way they have been handling it, I think its fair to say they are going to hit 1.0 and likely beyond with an incredible experience.

If you are sensitive to jank, keep this on the wishlist and check back in every now and then. If you can handle some rough edges though, you'll find a really fun time that is also really impressive to see it grow.

I can't wait to see what new stuff they cook up next.

this game is like a 3rd person kenshi. Kenshi is still by far more complex but this game is smth special. Its not like the typical survival games. The world is against you. Learn from it and become better. Rlly good game for people who want it hard and different. Combat feels nice

Super chill game, keeps surprising me with every play session. Im playing in a solo world.

-Combat. Janky, but satisfying when you start to get it down, I like how headshots kill things quickly, makes taking the time to line them up feel worth it.

-Loot. Theirs plenty of it, and it's fun to explore and search for more. Inventory management can be a bit of a pain, but this is most survival games, and they have plenty of storage solutions to utilize early on.

-Building. Haven't fully delved into this aspect yet, but based on the blueprints Ive unlocked, I'm pretty excited. Defenses, walls, gates, decorations, crafting stations, underground bases, vehicles, it seems like quite a bit tbh.

-Performance. Granted im playing solo, IVe had 0 issue. Temps are great, FPS is steady, 0 crashes or bugs. alot of times when events like meteor showers happen in a game like this you'll notice a hitch, or hiccup but nothing of the sort here, while multiple meteors smash into the ground all around you with explosions and dust clouds it runs smooth.

Definitely worth the price if you're looking for a chill, relaxing survival game to scratch that itch.

[edit] with more time spent in the game, it's more expansive than I thought, it has some bugs here an there, mostly vehicle related but nothing game breaking still, and 50 hrs in, still finding surprises and new loot.

So I like where this game is heading so far, I hope it improves. I got it on 50% off sale so that somewhat affects my expectations considering I paid like $12. I'll give you a basic run down on what to expect, and followed by what the developer needs to do to really improve the game.

You start of waking up in the center of the map with nothing, initially your main concern/problem is food and water. Once you get the basics down of how to build the lowest tier water collector and a bit of charcoal to clean the dirty water, water is no longer really an issue for the rest of the game unless you need lots for farming or something. 

The game is somewhat grindy, i found it grindy in mostly a good way, although to build stations and many base pieces takes a ton of resources. All of which are fairly easy to get through the expense of time. There are many settlements on the map, you have the option of putting in your scrap to finish buildings, utilize refining/processing stations, many of these settlements will generate resources over time if you donate food and water to keep their working population high. There are basic quests and objectives to do but nothing in the line of a true story or overall purpose other than survival at least that I have found in 28 hours.

You can build and upgrade many items, many of those blueprints are attained by either doing quests or finding in loot, or buying from vendors. There a many vehicles you can build that can get you across the map fairly quickly.

Now where it needs improvement (by the dev):
[olist]
[*]The two robots, Ron & the other one I cant remember the name of the other robot, anyway you need to tone down the sfx spam of their robot sounds. Your going to spend a lot of time initially at this outpost until you get a base setup that will take a ♥♥♥♥ metric ton of resources and when you are at the starter POI these two robots spam the ♥♥♥♥ out of their sfx constantly and follow you doing it. Tone it back to like 1/10 of what it is now.
[*]Dev you need an artistic vision to how you design these Places of interest/Settlements, you probably need to invest in more assets for the game to facilitate this. At no point was I like wow thats interesting looking, or this tells are story, or makes the world feel lived in. Probably the closest thing I saw that looked interesting and part of a living world was the One Guard Dynasty Dome city. So yeah if you can in an editor design like 50 unique looking pois that the random world generator could use to plop into the game that would go a long way to feeling like anywhere I went I had something cool and interesting to look at.
[*] I actually never built myself an actual base, seemed pointless really. It takes so many resources to build the simplest ♥♥♥♥, so whats the point. Id have to spent another 20 hours ingame farming resources at the various settlements that slowly produce over time to be able to build anything bigger than a shack with few stations around it. And if you're going to keep things the way it is, needing metric ♥♥♥♥ tons of resources to build a decent looking base, you need to come up with some more efficient mid to late game ways to collect resources other than settlements and banging on wrecked cars for parts with the stone hammer.
[*] flesh out some lore or something about the factions I can collect and read because after 28 hours I knew zero One Guard Dynasty (green faction), the bandits, or syndicate. 
[/olist]

So yeah I recommend the game. Id like to see it develop further with more depth. Some of the animations are janky but somehow in a way that adds to the games charm. If you plan on building a base, being a good citizen and building up settlements as what seems like you're meant to do its going to be a grindfest. As an indie game its updated regularly, will keep you busy for a weekend or two but it needs some more character and staying power, because once you've done every action in the game to collect resources 300 times, killed the same enemies over and over, eventually it will burn you out and you'll put it away waiting for something new to sink your teeth into.

It's has it's issues but I'm really enjoying it so far, though I only have 15+ hours.  Hopefully the hit boxes will have some improvments and ability to adjust admin server settings.  Well worth giving it a try.

To the Devs:

Great job on this so far, I am really enjoying this game. I know you have heard it a bunch of times but yeah sure, it does give me that Kenshi vibe but is it a copycat? No. Plains of Pain already has it's own identity with features that seperate it from Kenshi like the Meteor/Heat events, the Crafting system, the survival mechanics. As long as it keeps getting love and attention I think this has the potential to be a really good one.

The only bug I encounted so far was when I was inside a building at (Bullet Ace I think his name is?) and during a meteor shower I was hit and killed but I wouldn't say it is a major bug priority as it isn't too bad to get back to where you last died to retrieve your belongings.

Quality of life I would say is more important. For example; when looting a corpse I can see the inventory of the corpse and choose what I want to take. Looting a container however I cannot see what it holds and I pick something up one by one each time I press the "F" key and most of the time I have to drop a bunch of stuff I do not want. It's just little things like that I would improve; being able to see container inventory, having to actually sleep 8 or so hours instead of a minute, when enemies are killed and turned into skeletons it is a little hard to see and remember where they died, especially when you are fighting multiple enemies, etc.

For anyone thinking about playing; if you enjoy apocalyptic survival then this may be for you. It is EA so you need to keep in mind it is not entirely fleshed out yet and the beginning can be hard but once you understand how to play it gets easier and is fun. And maybe when you see that giant gorilla thing in the distance don't run up to screw around with it otherwise you'll get crushed like I did. Enjoy.

- There are a few quirks that are not well-received, particularly regarding the locking system during combat.

Inside the Backrooms constitutes a harrowing descent into a non-Euclidean labyrinth of liminal desolation, where the player is subjected to a relentless assault on both their spatial orientation and psychological fortitude. This digital odyssey begins in the monochromatic ochre expanse of Level 0, characterized by its pervasive, damp carpets and the auditory cacophony of fluorescent hum-buzz, before transitioning into the industrial miasma of Level 1 and the claustrophobic, subterranean conduits of Level 2. Each subsequent stratum, from the mechanical complexity of the Electrical Station to the deceptively festive yet perfidious Level Fun, serves to amplify the existential dread of being "clipped" from reality. The most macabre elements arise not merely from the environment, but from the malevolent denizens—the skeletal Bacteria, the canine-like Hounds, and the luminescent, disembodied grins of the Smilers—that turn these vacant corridors into a theater of visceral survival.

Original review:

RECOMMEND:NO

Unfortunate possible end for a early access game. 

Seems abandoned for some time as it has not been updated in a year. 

It had a different feel to other backroom games, as it looked grittier/nastier and honestly felt a bit scarier than the others competitors in the genre that I have played. 

I don't know why, but the game has gotten worse since 2022 and 2023 when I played it. Developer seems to have only added new content, but has failed to bugfix along the way.

I tried it with a friend now in 2025, and I could not hear the squigly monsters walking sound but my friend the host could hear it. 

All the skins(are they store bought assets?) are not interesting to me, and everything just feels way worse than the other games it competes with. The vibes seem cheaper, and the mood of the game felt way different now.

There is a potential for a decent game here, but the developer has not been able to realize it.
it's fun , and scary sometimes , play it with friends

The grass room was insane and took me and my partner 3 1/2 hours to complete (my least favorite room so far due to the enemy there and lack of lockers) but the rest of the levels I've played were challenging and really fun (usually about 2 hours to finish a level).  I've currently completed 6 out of 10 rooms.  Bottom line, this game is totally worth the money and playing co-op with someone.  I don't think I could do this solo, lol

Although buggy, each level has a good amount of puzzles that keep it fun! The newest Caves level introduces a handful of new entities that I haven't seen before in any of the other backrooms games, which was really nice. 
Some of the major grievance I have are mainly the audio issues; 
1) At some point, everyone is always in one ear, and no matter how far away you are from each other, you can always hear them. We ended up just using discord to communicate.
2) Some of the audio used for the entities/music aren't mixed as well as they could be, so you'll have to turn everything down in the settings. 
When it comes to gameplay;
1) The most recent thing I can comment on is that when I did eventually run out of lives in the caves, I was stuck at the bridges and unable to actually spectate anyone else. So for the rest of the game I was stuck just listening to everyone. 
2) When figuring out some of the puzzles in each level, there are some steps that aren't clear and had me looking for guides to try and figure everything out. I think having a "journal" aspect added would help organize all the steps you need to do and hint at what you should be looking for.
3) I think the spray-paint can is one of the newer items you can use, except you can't actually use it. You can rotate the image, change the image, and color, but there's no button to actually spray the image onto anything.
Overall, I love the amount of stuff you need to do in each level, and I love the addition of the newest level. I think it just needs a few adjustments for a more smoother gameplay, and I hope the devs keep adding new things!

Used to first person shooters? Here's one to throw you for a loop. Remember The Ocean Hunter from ye olde arcade days? Here's your chance to get off the rails.

First things first, it's going to take some getting used to turning and aiming the diver's weapon, which is a little floaty if you're not used to sensitivity, but thankfully is much less sensitive while aiming. Also, no enemy (sharks, and others...) dies in one shot (except one, but it's hard)- unless you get really good at targeting weak-spots. For sharks, through the gills (maybe?), and up through the roofs of their mouths into their head. And some sharks are big and powerful enough that you just really need the most powerful weapons you can get.

So, the gameplay loop. In every level, there are three-four treasure chests to find, and at least some time before a certain something happens. Before that point, sharks won't bother you if you don't bother them. There's no HUD indicator, so you've got to follow the icon on your compass and carefully track toward the heading. Expect to find chests to hide in the terrain and vegetation, and listen for the jingling tone- it means you're close enough and can check vertically. Finding chests and collecting treasure is where you get the currency to buy and upgrade your equipment.

Either you're (done) finding treasures or you pickup the spawned air tank, that's when DEATH starts sending the marine life after you. Whether you "run and gun" or find good cover, either kill enough enemies or wait long enough and the onslaught will end, and so will the level.

Between levels, you get to use your gold to buy weapons and upgrades- my tip, FOCUS ON BUYING OUT ALL WEAPONS. No upgrade is worth it if you don't have a better loadout. Apart from speargun, get the grenades, shotgun, pistol, sniper, and gatling (roughly in that order). Fighting sharks is not like fighting "cooperative" enemies in other FPS. The sharks like to come at you from blind spots and odd angles, so keep moving or keep a wall at your back, because they always break away before trying to pathfind behind you.

Also, use chum grenades to lure sharks into the open, especially if they're not "cooperating" by swimming into your kill zone. Also KILL any lionfish you find. Not only are they disgusting vermin, the sharks will ALWAYS go chomp on their floating carcasses first BEFORE coming back for you- this saved me from a Great White the first time. If you know the paths sharks can take to get at you, you can choose a good spot to thin out their numbers, bit by bit, at least until you can reach airtanks or ammo boxes.

If you struggle to advance, here's a good trick. Pick a new game, choose to load your current save-file, you can then choose any or latest dive you've tried, and when I did it, I got ALL WEAPONS AND UPGRADES, so I could focus on the fighting and ambiance. I really hope the developers don't patch this, it made such a difference!

A really cool mode included is Free Roam, and you can navigate the whole map, where all the levels meld together, and even find a secret area if you follow the campaign and know where to look...

After about 5 dives, 3 attempts at survival and 3 hours in, I gotta say the game is actually pretty decent. 
The story is kinda out there (so to speak) but im enjoying myself so far. 
The different sharks you encounter is terrifying (♥♥♥♥ those Makos really) and the jumpscares i encounters I have seen so far almost made me quit the game. 
I dont have Thalassophobia but good lord I think I might after this game. 
Not a HUGE fan of the AI usage, but im use to it at this point. 
It gets the job done to an extent and it pays off. 

One thing I can honestly appreciate and absolutely LOVE is how fluent the sharks movement is! Holy cow its BEAUTIFUL. They look so life like and the reactions to damage and darting away and then slowly zoning in on you is amazing. 
Honestly well done on that part!
I'll never go diving again after this one. Nice little snack of a game. Took me like around 3 hours for 100%.

I reviewed the predecessor of this game back in 2024. The sequel was actually released 4 years after the original. A lot has changed since - shooting is now more interesting thanks to the different types of weapons we have and there are also more enemy types. Let's look at everything in more detail!

[b]Story[/b]

The first game mode (the one you'll probably play the longest) is labelled as campaign and it includes 15 different chapters (labelled as "dives") with the last being a boss battle. There is a lady at the beginning of each level asking us to visit certain areas of interest. These places can be old ruins, a cave system or the wreckage of a crashed plane.

Given how detailed the underwater world is orienting ourselves is a bit difficult at first. The waypoint for more oxygen is always visible, though, and this is supposed to tell us where to go. Extra oxygen always spawns regularly in the same place which can add to the difficulty level as sometimes these objects appear in the open so you have to come up with a strategy against sharks.

[b]Gameplay[/b]

Identical to the first game in the sense that the primary objective is to eliminate sharks. However, there are more versions of said animals, plus snakes and even spear-throwing sirens appear regularly. There is always a somewhat casual roaming section to our objective which is followed by a loud noise asking us to eliminate all "possessed" creatures. Every "dive" applies this 2-stage approach.

The enemies come in waves. Once a wave is completed you can see light moving around fast - probably the evil entity looking for more underwater creatures to possess. An icon is fully red in the upper-right corner at the beginning of the fight - and this red part keeps decreasing as we eliminate more and more Hammerheads, Makos, Great Whites and other predators of the deep.

To make things more interesting, roaming allows us to looks for treasure chests. The extra money is good for purchasing new weapons, gear and upgrading those we already have between dives. The compass shows the direction and a small dot even lets us know of the elevation relative to our position. You have to be fast, though! Once the alarm sounds there is no point in exploring and your best bet is to get ready and look for a good cover! When we are done there is another alarm and the stage is complete.

[b]Variety[/b]

The underwater world definitely has its atmosphere. Locations seem to be varied, but a new level (dive) often continues the previous one. Weapons are also interesting - we can upgrade our default harpoon or use the money to buy something with rapid fire instead. Grenades do not do damage but they can act as bait (chum grenade) or they can simply shoo away all sharks (flashbang grenades).

[b]Difficulty[/b]

Later levels include more enemy waves. Looking for chests with extra ammo will be a must and medkits are also important to heal ourselves after receiving a couple of bites. My issue was that hitboxes are somewhat glitchy in this game - you can hit sharks on the head from the side but your best bet is to wait until they turn toward you and charge. A headshot is guaranteed from the front which causes the victim to turn away and probably does the most damage.

Still, waves tend to be way too long sometimes and leaving the ruins (an example for a safe haven) to look for ammo can be your end. I also noticed that I missed the trigger on a specific level so I kept killing enemies but they did not add to my progress. You know you are on the right track when fallen enemies leave shiny items behind that you can use as a score multiplier. 

[b]Added content[/b]

The campaign can be a chore (especially the last couple of levels) and you only have a save at the beginning of each dive. A checkpoint-based system could have been great or by simply allowing the player to choose from different difficulty settings.

And if the campaign is not enough, you can free roam or start up the survival mode. In the latter you keep moving to new locations while collecting ammo and each elimination adds XP which means purchasing new gear is a possibility when levelling up. You always have 3 choices. I thought this was procedurally generated but I found the same shipwreck when starting a new game in the same place so I assume it just uses a relatively big map.

[b]Summary[/b]

This is a surprisingly great sequel and hats off to the developer studio if indeed there were 2 people achieving all this. The underwater atmosphere is superb - hearing the sirens approaching or a great white suddenly closing in on you all add to the already terrifying atmosphere. 

I wish the difficulty was adjustable and a weapon for stabbing (such as a melee harpoon or a knife) was available so that we could conserve some ammo. Better hitboxes can also help with the ammo issue. Overall, I was really satisfied with the game and I can recommend it.

So, the gameplay loop. In every level, there are three-four treasure chests to find, and at least some time before a certain something happens. Before that point, sharks won't bother you if you don't bother them. There's no HUD indicator, so you've got to follow the icon on your compass and carefully track toward the heading. Expect to find chests to hide in the terrain and vegetation, and listen for the jingling tone- it means you're close enough and can check vertically. Finding chests and collecting treasure is where you get the currency to buy and upgrade your equipment.

Either you're (done) finding treasures or you pickup the spawned air tank, that's when DEATH starts sending the marine life after you. Whether you "run and gun" or find good cover, either kill enough enemies or wait long enough and the onslaught will end, and so will the level.

Between levels, you get to use your gold to buy weapons and upgrades- my tip, FOCUS ON BUYING OUT ALL WEAPONS. No upgrade is worth it if you don't have a better loadout. Apart from speargun, get the grenades, shotgun, pistol, sniper, and gatling (roughly in that order). Fighting sharks is not like fighting "cooperative" enemies in other FPS. The sharks like to come at you from blind spots and odd angles, so keep moving or keep a wall at your back, because they always break away before trying to pathfind behind you.

Also, use chum grenades to lure sharks into the open, especially if they're not "cooperating" by swimming into your kill zone. Also KILL any lionfish you find. Not only are they disgusting vermin, the sharks will ALWAYS go chomp on their floating carcasses first BEFORE coming back for you- this saved me from a Great White the first time. If you know the paths sharks can take to get at you, you can choose a good spot to thin out their numbers, bit by bit, at least until you can reach airtanks or ammo boxes.

If you struggle to advance, here's a good trick. Pick a new game, choose to load your current save-file, you can then choose any or latest dive you've tried, and when I did it, I got ALL WEAPONS AND UPGRADES, so I could focus on the fighting and ambiance. I really hope the developers don't patch this, it made such a difference!

A really cool mode included is Free Roam, and you can navigate the whole map, where all the levels meld together, and even find a secret area if you follow the campaign and know where to look...

After about 5 dives, 3 attempts at survival and 3 hours in, I gotta say the game is actually pretty decent. 
The story is kinda out there (so to speak) but im enjoying myself so far. 
The different sharks you encounter is terrifying (♥♥♥♥ those Makos really) and the jumpscares i encounters I have seen so far almost made me quit the game. 
I dont have Thalassophobia but good lord I think I might after this game. 
Not a HUGE fan of the AI usage, but im use to it at this point. 
It gets the job done to an extent and it pays off. 

One thing I can honestly appreciate and absolutely LOVE is how fluent the sharks movement is! Holy cow its BEAUTIFUL. They look so life like and the reactions to damage and darting away and then slowly zoning in on you is amazing. 
Honestly well done on that part!

Death in the Water 2 is a massive step up from the original. This game nails the underwater atmosphere, and the briney horror that lurks within it, perfectly. The open sea world and procedural marine life make the environment genuinely compelling and tense. If you enjoy survival shooters with horror vibes and deep-sea exploration, this game delivers some intense moments. Seriously, witnessing a Great White slow approach from murky depths is something primal. The sharks and marine life move and behave very life-like, and are highly detailed, even down to the battle damage and grazing of their skin.

In essence, this is a Arena Shooter, with Score Attack elements. There are three game modes offered: Campaign, Survival, and Free Roam. Campaign can be a bit of a slog, and repetitive, but holds most of the horror atmosphere. Survival Mode is where the action is, if you want faster gameplay. Free Roam is for checking out the beautiful map that you are never really given time to explore otherwise.

It shines brightest as an indie experience, and I would like to see missions, marine life, and mechanics expanded in a future title or DLC. At its price point, many players will feel it’s a worthwhile plunge into the depths. Pick this one up when you can.

As someone who DESPISES the ocean and everything that exists within it, GOD this game is fun.

15 Dives + a Tutorial in which you are just a simple Treasure Diver... until the wildlife gets a bit too quirky and decide that your flesh looks better off of you. The level after he just started to body slam me, which was... weird? I wasn't in "Open water" or explicitly in any zone deemed unallowed IMO? The level with the drowned sailors and very little cover, before he started the Fight he was also just going after me I really... don't know if that's intended but I took it in stride. It was a very interesting experience, being jumpscared by the thing that lurks just in the cloudy waters on what seemed like an innocent mission, and going toe to toe with it for no reason partway through the game added some depth for me. I dunno, it feels like it's just a Full Auto Speargun with how much damage each bolt deals but not alot likes to flinch from it. Stun and Chum Grenades, though I never really used them much beyond a VERY RISKY low O2 run out of cover. I will say they are VERY useful.

I had alot of fun and only reset once rather early when I just had a brainfart and hugged a poisonous snake for far longer than I should have. This reminds me of an indie game that I cannot recall the title of. There was a Megalodon secret level if you like, collected all the hidden goodies and stuff? If anyone knows of it PLEASE let me know.

Death in the Water 2 is fascinating from the first second to the very last. The atmosphere, visuals and sound are amazing, the animations of the sea creatures are life-like and the controls are simple and intuitive. My only advice is to turn off the music. Not that it is bad, but it takes you out of the experience a bit and gives away if hostile creatures are nearby. It’s much more unsettling if there‘s nothing but the sounds of the ocean floor.

On the downside the game is pretty short and somewhat repetitive. Every level is basically the same. You rush and try to find the three treasure chests per level as fast as possible, before all the sea creatures turn hostile and you have to kill all of them. The first game is much scarier because you have nowhere to hide and death is inevitable. Here, as soon as you get your hands on the good guns with a few upgrades and stocked up on medkits, the animals don‘t stand a chance. It‘s very easy to just camp near an oxygen supply with your back against a wall and just let them come to you. I only died a handful of times during the campaign (two or three times during the final boss fight), so some options for higher difficulties would‘ve been nice.

Overall this game is a strong 6, maybe even a 7 out of 8 for indie standards. I strongly recommend it to everybody, not only diving enthusiasts.

Death in the Water 2 is an atmospheric survival-horror shooter that leans heavily into the primal fear of the deep, transforming the ocean into a vast, hostile wilderness where beauty and terror coexist. Developed and published by Lighthouse Games Studio, the sequel expands significantly on its predecessor by emphasizing open-ended exploration, a stronger sense of place, and a constant psychological tension that stems from vulnerability rather than scripted scares. From the moment you descend beneath the surface, the game establishes a mood of isolation and unease, using the open sea itself as its most powerful antagonist.

The core experience revolves around diving into expansive underwater zones filled with wrecks, reefs, caves, and ruins, searching for treasure and resources that fuel progression. These early moments often feel deceptively calm, encouraging slow movement and careful observation. Light filters down through the water in soft shafts, debris drifts lazily with the currents, and marine life initially behaves in a neutral or curious manner. This tranquility is deliberate, lulling the player into a false sense of safety before the game abruptly reminds you that nothing in this environment is truly under your control.

Combat enters the picture through the looming presence of an ancient kraken entity known only as Death, whose influence corrupts the surrounding sea life and triggers waves of aggression. When Death’s power surges, the ocean transforms from a quiet expanse into a feeding ground, with sharks and other creatures swarming relentlessly. These moments force a sharp shift in playstyle, demanding quick reactions and aggressive defense as you rely on an arsenal that includes spearguns, firearms, and specialized tools to keep the creatures at bay. The contrast between peaceful exploration and sudden chaos is one of the game’s defining strengths, keeping tension consistently high even during quieter stretches.

Progression is tied closely to scavenging and upgrades. Treasure collected from shipwrecks and hidden caches allows you to improve weapons, survivability, and efficiency underwater, giving a tangible sense of growth across multiple dives. Each upgrade feels meaningful, not only because it increases damage or resilience, but because it slightly reduces the overwhelming vulnerability that defines the early hours. Still, the game is careful never to let the player feel completely safe, ensuring that even fully upgraded loadouts require awareness and positioning to survive intense encounters.

Visually, Death in the Water 2 punches well above its weight. The underwater environments are rich with detail, capturing both the majesty and claustrophobia of the ocean depths. Visibility varies depending on depth and lighting, sometimes granting sweeping views of coral fields and at other times plunging you into murky darkness where threats can emerge without warning. Creature animations are convincing and unsettling, particularly during attack sequences, where sharks circle, dart, and strike with unnerving speed. These visuals reinforce the game’s horror elements without relying on artificial jump scares.

Sound design plays an equally critical role in shaping the experience. The muffled ambiance of being underwater, punctuated by the hiss of breathing equipment and distant, distorted noises, creates a constant sense of unease. The soundtrack is sparse, allowing environmental sounds to dominate and making moments of silence feel just as threatening as full-scale assaults. Audio cues often provide the only warning of incoming danger, encouraging players to listen closely and stay alert even when nothing is immediately visible.

Mechanically, the game follows a loop that blends exploration, preparation, and wave-based survival. While this structure is effective at maintaining tension, it can begin to feel repetitive over long sessions, as encounters rely on familiar patterns of escalation. Enemy variety is somewhat limited, and although the intensity of attacks increases, the fundamental rhythm remains consistent. For players drawn to atmosphere and immersion, this repetition is easier to forgive, but those seeking evolving mechanics may find the experience less dynamic over time.

Narratively, Death in the Water 2 takes a minimalist approach. Story elements are delivered subtly through environmental details and brief contextual clues rather than explicit exposition. This restraint suits the setting well, allowing the mystery of the ocean and the unknowable nature of Death itself to remain central. The lack of a heavily guided narrative reinforces the feeling of isolation, making each dive feel like a personal struggle rather than part of a grand scripted arc.

Ultimately, Death in the Water 2 succeeds as a slow-burn survival horror experience that thrives on atmosphere, tension, and the inherent fear of what lies beneath the surface. It may not offer extensive narrative depth or mechanical variety, but it compensates with a powerful sense of immersion and a hauntingly beautiful world that constantly reminds you of your fragility. For players who appreciate methodical exploration, environmental storytelling, and horror rooted in vulnerability rather than spectacle, it delivers a memorable and unsettling journey into the depths where danger is always just out of sight.

I reviewed the predecessor of this game back in 2024. The sequel was actually released 4 years after the original. A lot has changed since - shooting is now more interesting thanks to the different types of weapons we have and there are also more enemy types. Let's look at everything in more detail!

[b]Story[/b]

The first game mode (the one you'll probably play the longest) is labelled as campaign and it includes 15 different chapters (labelled as "dives") with the last being a boss battle. There is a lady at the beginning of each level asking us to visit certain areas of interest. These places can be old ruins, a cave system or the wreckage of a crashed plane.

Given how detailed the underwater world is orienting ourselves is a bit difficult at first. The waypoint for more oxygen is always visible, though, and this is supposed to tell us where to go. Extra oxygen always spawns regularly in the same place which can add to the difficulty level as sometimes these objects appear in the open so you have to come up with a strategy against sharks.

[b]Gameplay[/b]

Identical to the first game in the sense that the primary objective is to eliminate sharks. However, there are more versions of said animals, plus snakes and even spear-throwing sirens appear regularly. There is always a somewhat casual roaming section to our objective which is followed by a loud noise asking us to eliminate all "possessed" creatures. Every "dive" applies this 2-stage approach.

The enemies come in waves. Once a wave is completed you can see light moving around fast - probably the evil entity looking for more underwater creatures to possess. An icon is fully red in the upper-right corner at the beginning of the fight - and this red part keeps decreasing as we eliminate more and more Hammerheads, Makos, Great Whites and other predators of the deep.

To make things more interesting, roaming allows us to looks for treasure chests. The extra money is good for purchasing new weapons, gear and upgrading those we already have between dives. The compass shows the direction and a small dot even lets us know of the elevation relative to our position. You have to be fast, though! Once the alarm sounds there is no point in exploring and your best bet is to get ready and look for a good cover! When we are done there is another alarm and the stage is complete.

[b]Variety[/b]

The underwater world definitely has its atmosphere. Locations seem to be varied, but a new level (dive) often continues the previous one. Weapons are also interesting - we can upgrade our default harpoon or use the money to buy something with rapid fire instead. Grenades do not do damage but they can act as bait (chum grenade) or they can simply shoo away all sharks (flashbang grenades).

[b]Difficulty[/b]

Later levels include more enemy waves. Looking for chests with extra ammo will be a must and medkits are also important to heal ourselves after receiving a couple of bites. My issue was that hitboxes are somewhat glitchy in this game - you can hit sharks on the head from the side but your best bet is to wait until they turn toward you and charge. A headshot is guaranteed from the front which causes the victim to turn away and probably does the most damage.

Still, waves tend to be way too long sometimes and leaving the ruins (an example for a safe haven) to look for ammo can be your end. I also noticed that I missed the trigger on a specific level so I kept killing enemies but they did not add to my progress. You know you are on the right track when fallen enemies leave shiny items behind that you can use as a score multiplier. 

[b]Added content[/b]

The campaign can be a chore (especially the last couple of levels) and you only have a save at the beginning of each dive. A checkpoint-based system could have been great or by simply allowing the player to choose from different difficulty settings.

And if the campaign is not enough, you can free roam or start up the survival mode. In the latter you keep moving to new locations while collecting ammo and each elimination adds XP which means purchasing new gear is a possibility when levelling up. You always have 3 choices. I thought this was procedurally generated but I found the same shipwreck when starting a new game in the same place so I assume it just uses a relatively big map.

[b]Summary[/b]

This is a surprisingly great sequel and hats off to the developer studio if indeed there were 2 people achieving all this. The underwater atmosphere is superb - hearing the sirens approaching or a great white suddenly closing in on you all add to the already terrifying atmosphere. 

I wish the difficulty was adjustable and a weapon for stabbing (such as a melee harpoon or a knife) was available so that we could conserve some ammo. Better hitboxes can also help with the ammo issue. Overall, I was really satisfied with the game and I can recommend it.

Death in the Water 2 is an atmospheric survival-horror shooter that leans heavily into the primal fear of the deep, transforming the ocean into a vast, hostile wilderness where beauty and terror coexist. Developed and published by Lighthouse Games Studio, the sequel expands significantly on its predecessor by emphasizing open-ended exploration, a stronger sense of place, and a constant psychological tension that stems from vulnerability rather than scripted scares. From the moment you descend beneath the surface, the game establishes a mood of isolation and unease, using the open sea itself as its most powerful antagonist.

The core experience revolves around diving into expansive underwater zones filled with wrecks, reefs, caves, and ruins, searching for treasure and resources that fuel progression. These early moments often feel deceptively calm, encouraging slow movement and careful observation. Light filters down through the water in soft shafts, debris drifts lazily with the currents, and marine life initially behaves in a neutral or curious manner. This tranquility is deliberate, lulling the player into a false sense of safety before the game abruptly reminds you that nothing in this environment is truly under your control.

Combat enters the picture through the looming presence of an ancient kraken entity known only as Death, whose influence corrupts the surrounding sea life and triggers waves of aggression. When Death’s power surges, the ocean transforms from a quiet expanse into a feeding ground, with sharks and other creatures swarming relentlessly. These moments force a sharp shift in playstyle, demanding quick reactions and aggressive defense as you rely on an arsenal that includes spearguns, firearms, and specialized tools to keep the creatures at bay. The contrast between peaceful exploration and sudden chaos is one of the game’s defining strengths, keeping tension consistently high even during quieter stretches.

Progression is tied closely to scavenging and upgrades. Treasure collected from shipwrecks and hidden caches allows you to improve weapons, survivability, and efficiency underwater, giving a tangible sense of growth across multiple dives. Each upgrade feels meaningful, not only because it increases damage or resilience, but because it slightly reduces the overwhelming vulnerability that defines the early hours. Still, the game is careful never to let the player feel completely safe, ensuring that even fully upgraded loadouts require awareness and positioning to survive intense encounters.

Visually, Death in the Water 2 punches well above its weight. The underwater environments are rich with detail, capturing both the majesty and claustrophobia of the ocean depths. Visibility varies depending on depth and lighting, sometimes granting sweeping views of coral fields and at other times plunging you into murky darkness where threats can emerge without warning. Creature animations are convincing and unsettling, particularly during attack sequences, where sharks circle, dart, and strike with unnerving speed. These visuals reinforce the game’s horror elements without relying on artificial jump scares.

Sound design plays an equally critical role in shaping the experience. The muffled ambiance of being underwater, punctuated by the hiss of breathing equipment and distant, distorted noises, creates a constant sense of unease. The soundtrack is sparse, allowing environmental sounds to dominate and making moments of silence feel just as threatening as full-scale assaults. Audio cues often provide the only warning of incoming danger, encouraging players to listen closely and stay alert even when nothing is immediately visible.

Mechanically, the game follows a loop that blends exploration, preparation, and wave-based survival. While this structure is effective at maintaining tension, it can begin to feel repetitive over long sessions, as encounters rely on familiar patterns of escalation. Enemy variety is somewhat limited, and although the intensity of attacks increases, the fundamental rhythm remains consistent. For players drawn to atmosphere and immersion, this repetition is easier to forgive, but those seeking evolving mechanics may find the experience less dynamic over time.

Narratively, Death in the Water 2 takes a minimalist approach. Story elements are delivered subtly through environmental details and brief contextual clues rather than explicit exposition. This restraint suits the setting well, allowing the mystery of the ocean and the unknowable nature of Death itself to remain central. The lack of a heavily guided narrative reinforces the feeling of isolation, making each dive feel like a personal struggle rather than part of a grand scripted arc.

Ultimately, Death in the Water 2 succeeds as a slow-burn survival horror experience that thrives on atmosphere, tension, and the inherent fear of what lies beneath the surface. It may not offer extensive narrative depth or mechanical variety, but it compensates with a powerful sense of immersion and a hauntingly beautiful world that constantly reminds you of your fragility. For players who appreciate methodical exploration, environmental storytelling, and horror rooted in vulnerability rather than spectacle, it delivers a memorable and unsettling journey into the depths where danger is always just out of sight.

I reviewed the predecessor of this game back in 2024. The sequel was actually released 4 years after the original. A lot has changed since - shooting is now more interesting thanks to the different types of weapons we have and there are also more enemy types. Let's look at everything in more detail!

[b]Story[/b]

The first game mode (the one you'll probably play the longest) is labelled as campaign and it includes 15 different chapters (labelled as "dives") with the last being a boss battle. There is a lady at the beginning of each level asking us to visit certain areas of interest. These places can be old ruins, a cave system or the wreckage of a crashed plane.

Given how detailed the underwater world is orienting ourselves is a bit difficult at first. The waypoint for more oxygen is always visible, though, and this is supposed to tell us where to go. Extra oxygen always spawns regularly in the same place which can add to the difficulty level as sometimes these objects appear in the open so you have to come up with a strategy against sharks.

[b]Gameplay[/b]

Identical to the first game in the sense that the primary objective is to eliminate sharks. However, there are more versions of said animals, plus snakes and even spear-throwing sirens appear regularly. There is always a somewhat casual roaming section to our objective which is followed by a loud noise asking us to eliminate all "possessed" creatures. Every "dive" applies this 2-stage approach.

The enemies come in waves. Once a wave is completed you can see light moving around fast - probably the evil entity looking for more underwater creatures to possess. An icon is fully red in the upper-right corner at the beginning of the fight - and this red part keeps decreasing as we eliminate more and more Hammerheads, Makos, Great Whites and other predators of the deep.

To make things more interesting, roaming allows us to looks for treasure chests. The extra money is good for purchasing new weapons, gear and upgrading those we already have between dives. The compass shows the direction and a small dot even lets us know of the elevation relative to our position. You have to be fast, though! Once the alarm sounds there is no point in exploring and your best bet is to get ready and look for a good cover! When we are done there is another alarm and the stage is complete.

[b]Variety[/b]

The underwater world definitely has its atmosphere. Locations seem to be varied, but a new level (dive) often continues the previous one. Weapons are also interesting - we can upgrade our default harpoon or use the money to buy something with rapid fire instead. Grenades do not do damage but they can act as bait (chum grenade) or they can simply shoo away all sharks (flashbang grenades).

[b]Difficulty[/b]

Later levels include more enemy waves. Looking for chests with extra ammo will be a must and medkits are also important to heal ourselves after receiving a couple of bites. My issue was that hitboxes are somewhat glitchy in this game - you can hit sharks on the head from the side but your best bet is to wait until they turn toward you and charge. A headshot is guaranteed from the front which causes the victim to turn away and probably does the most damage.

Still, waves tend to be way too long sometimes and leaving the ruins (an example for a safe haven) to look for ammo can be your end. I also noticed that I missed the trigger on a specific level so I kept killing enemies but they did not add to my progress. You know you are on the right track when fallen enemies leave shiny items behind that you can use as a score multiplier. 

[b]Added content[/b]

The campaign can be a chore (especially the last couple of levels) and you only have a save at the beginning of each dive. A checkpoint-based system could have been great or by simply allowing the player to choose from different difficulty settings.

And if the campaign is not enough, you can free roam or start up the survival mode. In the latter you keep moving to new locations while collecting ammo and each elimination adds XP which means purchasing new gear is a possibility when levelling up. You always have 3 choices. I thought this was procedurally generated but I found the same shipwreck when starting a new game in the same place so I assume it just uses a relatively big map.

[b]Summary[/b]

This is a surprisingly great sequel and hats off to the developer studio if indeed there were 2 people achieving all this. The underwater atmosphere is superb - hearing the sirens approaching or a great white suddenly closing in on you all add to the already terrifying atmosphere. 

I wish the difficulty was adjustable and a weapon for stabbing (such as a melee harpoon or a knife) was available so that we could conserve some ammo. Better hitboxes can also help with the ammo issue. Overall, I was really satisfied with the game and I can recommend it.
