A great scuba game fighting sharks and the denizens of the deep.  Main campaign has 15 levels that end with fighting the Kraken of the deep!  Once completed you can redo any level and can play endless which has challenges as well.

Used to first person shooters? Here's one to throw you for a loop. Remember The Ocean Hunter from ye olde arcade days? Here's your chance to get off the rails.

First things first, it's going to take some getting used to turning and aiming the diver's weapon, which is a little floaty if you're not used to sensitivity, but thankfully is much less sensitive while aiming. Also, no enemy (sharks, and others...) dies in one shot (except one, but it's hard)- unless you get really good at targeting weak-spots. For sharks, through the gills (maybe?), and up through the roofs of their mouths into their head. And some sharks are big and powerful enough that you just really need the most powerful weapons you can get.

So, the gameplay loop. In every level, there are three-four treasure chests to find, and at least some time before a certain something happens. Before that point, sharks won't bother you if you don't bother them. There's no HUD indicator, so you've got to follow the icon on your compass and carefully track toward the heading. Expect to find chests to hide in the terrain and vegetation, and listen for the jingling tone- it means you're close enough and can check vertically. Finding chests and collecting treasure is where you get the currency to buy and upgrade your equipment.

Either you're (done) finding treasures or you pickup the spawned air tank, that's when DEATH starts sending the marine life after you. Whether you "run and gun" or find good cover, either kill enough enemies or wait long enough and the onslaught will end, and so will the level.

Between levels, you get to use your gold to buy weapons and upgrades- my tip, FOCUS ON BUYING OUT ALL WEAPONS. No upgrade is worth it if you don't have a better loadout. Apart from speargun, get the grenades, shotgun, pistol, sniper, and gatling (roughly in that order). Fighting sharks is not like fighting "cooperative" enemies in other FPS. The sharks like to come at you from blind spots and odd angles, so keep moving or keep a wall at your back, because they always break away before trying to pathfind behind you.

Also, use chum grenades to lure sharks into the open, especially if they're not "cooperating" by swimming into your kill zone. Also KILL any lionfish you find. Not only are they disgusting vermin, the sharks will ALWAYS go chomp on their floating carcasses first BEFORE coming back for you- this saved me from a Great White the first time. If you know the paths sharks can take to get at you, you can choose a good spot to thin out their numbers, bit by bit, at least until you can reach airtanks or ammo boxes.

If you struggle to advance, here's a good trick. Pick a new game, choose to load your current save-file, you can then choose any or latest dive you've tried, and when I did it, I got ALL WEAPONS AND UPGRADES, so I could focus on the fighting and ambiance. I really hope the developers don't patch this, it made such a difference!

A really cool mode included is Free Roam, and you can navigate the whole map, where all the levels meld together, and even find a secret area if you follow the campaign and know where to look...

After about 5 dives, 3 attempts at survival and 3 hours in, I gotta say the game is actually pretty decent. 
The story is kinda out there (so to speak) but im enjoying myself so far. 
The different sharks you encounter is terrifying (♥♥♥♥ those Makos really) and the jumpscares i encounters I have seen so far almost made me quit the game. 
I dont have Thalassophobia but good lord I think I might after this game. 
Not a HUGE fan of the AI usage, but im use to it at this point. 
It gets the job done to an extent and it pays off. 

One thing I can honestly appreciate and absolutely LOVE is how fluent the sharks movement is! Holy cow its BEAUTIFUL. They look so life like and the reactions to damage and darting away and then slowly zoning in on you is amazing. 
Honestly well done on that part!

This title took me by surprise. Graphics are good, sound design is solid and the gameplay is smooth. The core gameplay loop is quite repetitive, but its a short game and very effective at what it does. Which is scare the thalassophobic pants off of me.

Death in the Water 2 is a massive step up from the original. This game nails the underwater atmosphere, and the briney horror that lurks within it, perfectly. The open sea world and procedural marine life make the environment genuinely compelling and tense. If you enjoy survival shooters with horror vibes and deep-sea exploration, this game delivers some intense moments. Seriously, witnessing a Great White slow approach from murky depths is something primal. The sharks and marine life move and behave very life-like, and are highly detailed, even down to the battle damage and grazing of their skin.

In essence, this is a Arena Shooter, with Score Attack elements. There are three game modes offered: Campaign, Survival, and Free Roam. Campaign can be a bit of a slog, and repetitive, but holds most of the horror atmosphere. Survival Mode is where the action is, if you want faster gameplay. Free Roam is for checking out the beautiful map that you are never really given time to explore otherwise.

It shines brightest as an indie experience, and I would like to see missions, marine life, and mechanics expanded in a future title or DLC. At its price point, many players will feel it’s a worthwhile plunge into the depths. Pick this one up when you can.

As someone who DESPISES the ocean and everything that exists within it, GOD this game is fun.
You get over your fear of sharks real quick when you learn how to sink a spear into their gullet. 
15 Dives + a Tutorial in which you are just a simple Treasure Diver... until the wildlife gets a bit too quirky and decide that your flesh looks better off of you. Learning how to deal with certain sharks and overall play the game was a really enjoyable experience. I had 2 separate instances where the Big Boy shows up and just decides to assault me, dunno what that's about. It was sometime near the Shipping Containers Dive... he pulled up out of nowhere, and when I actually realized "wait that's a THING?!" it bounced and THEN started the fight for that level. The level after he just started to body slam me, which was... weird? I wasn't in "Open water" or explicitly in any zone deemed unallowed IMO? The level with the drowned sailors and very little cover, before he started the Fight he was also just going after me I really... don't know if that's intended but I took it in stride. It was a very interesting experience, being jumpscared by the thing that lurks just in the cloudy waters on what seemed like an innocent mission, and going toe to toe with it for no reason partway through the game added some depth for me. Made it feel like I'm being Tested by It, or something. Of course, when the Fights truly started he dipped. Sometimes it did feel REALLY QUICK how early the fights would start, leaving me no time to loot in relative peace (Looting while in combat is so damn tense)

You got 5 Guns, plus 2 types of non-lethal grenades, plus some gear that needs upgrading which you do over the course of your dives with the Gold you do find. Your standard issue Speargun, a Sidearm that shoots Explosive Bolts (I'm not entirely sure how effective they were but it does help get things that are charging off their course and for cleaning up lesser Snakes and whatnot), a Shotgun (which was just the greatest upgrade in kit I needed during my playthrough considering Makos started spawning regularly), a "Precision" high power 1-Bolt Speargun called the Posideon which has enough recoil to knock you backward and is the only thing I found aside from the Shotgun to really "Deter" something like the bigger Sharks. Even knocks back the Big Boy from what I saw. And a Minigun that... is one of the best feeling weapons. I dunno, it feels like it's just a Full Auto Speargun with how much damage each bolt deals but not alot likes to flinch from it. Stun and Chum Grenades, though I never really used them much beyond a VERY RISKY low O2 run out of cover. I will say they are VERY useful.

I had alot of fun and only reset once rather early when I just had a brainfart and hugged a poisonous snake for far longer than I should have. The Multiplier and Score system is icing on the cake I think, overall makes the game feel more arcade-y and replayable. I would absolutely recommend this game, especially to those with fear of the deep. Trust me, I was jumping and screaming and ♥♥♥♥-talking everything in the deep the whole damn time, but by god I had alot of fun.

This reminds me of an indie game that I cannot recall the title of. I remember the start of the game having you hunt a measly Lemon Shark that bit a poor swimmer by accident, and it scales in diffiulty as you have to navigate more and more terrifying depths and conditions (Pitch Black Oil Rig was just the worst haha). There was a Megalodon secret level if you like, collected all the hidden goodies and stuff? If anyone knows of it PLEASE let me know.

Incredible game made by 2 developers based on Cthulu Mythology. Deep Diving, Treasure Hunting, Harpooning Sirens. 

It's also got massive tentacles for all you Hentai Lovers out there. IYKYK

The game is pretty linear but still pretty fun and intense. The graphics, soundtrack and gameplay is great. Although there is not much to do, the game is still pretty fun and scary especially if you fear the open waters like me. The devs did a fantastic job of creating the game, the sharks are terrifying and the animations are really slick. Pick it up!

[h1]EA, Activision Blizzard, Ubisoft, whoever: Give those two guys a job.[/h1]

Death in the Water 2 is fascinating from the first second to the very last. The atmosphere, visuals and sound are amazing, the animations of the sea creatures are life-like and the controls are simple and intuitive. My only advice is to turn off the music. Not that it is bad, but it takes you out of the experience a bit and gives away if hostile creatures are nearby. It’s much more unsettling if there‘s nothing but the sounds of the ocean floor.

On the downside the game is pretty short and somewhat repetitive. Every level is basically the same. You rush and try to find the three treasure chests per level as fast as possible, before all the sea creatures turn hostile and you have to kill all of them. The first game is much scarier because you have nowhere to hide and death is inevitable. Here, as soon as you get your hands on the good guns with a few upgrades and stocked up on medkits, the animals don‘t stand a chance. It‘s very easy to just camp near an oxygen supply with your back against a wall and just let them come to you. I only died a handful of times during the campaign (two or three times during the final boss fight), so some options for higher difficulties would‘ve been nice.

Overall this game is a strong 6, maybe even a 7 out of 8 for indie standards. I strongly recommend it to everybody, not only diving enthusiasts.

Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy is an indie action title that strives to deliver a blend of mythological grandeur, fast-paced sword combat, and stylized environments. Developed by Quacky Games and published by Whale Rock Games, it presents itself as a journey through Scandinavian-inspired realms filled with supernatural foes and elemental power. At first glance, the game’s visual tone and combat-driven premise suggest a compact but energetic hack-and-slash experience. It promises a series of battles across varied landscapes, offering players a taste of mythic fantasy without demanding the complexity of a full RPG.

The combat system is at the heart of the game, and it is here where Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy tries to establish its identity. Encounters unfold in straightforward but punchy fashion: enemies approach in waves, the player reacts with melee strikes or ranged abilities, and timing becomes a crucial factor in managing each skirmish. Early encounters can feel engaging, especially when the rhythm of dodging, swinging, and spacing begins to click. There is a sense of satisfaction in carving through opponents with decisive blows, and the fantasy aesthetic of glowing weapons and elemental effects adds flair to these moments. While not deep in terms of variety or combo systems, the core movements and impacts do offer occasional bursts of fun that hint at what the game might have grown into with more development time.

Visually, the game leans into stylization rather than realism. Its environments—vast fields, shadowy valleys, and mythic battlegrounds—possess a certain atmospheric charm. The lighting effects and color palettes contribute to the sense of scale the developers aimed for, and while not lavishly detailed, the world does manage to convey the feeling of a lone warrior navigating a divine conflict. Character and enemy designs follow this same approach: recognizable silhouettes and dramatic proportions over intricate detail. For an indie title with modest system requirements, the visual direction succeeds in establishing mood even when technical limitations are apparent.

However, the cracks in the game’s foundation become clear as the experience progresses. One of the most persistent criticisms is the repetitive nature of the level design. Although the maps themselves vary in theme, the structure of the gameplay rarely evolves. Most stages follow the same loop: advance, fight a group of enemies, move forward again, and repeat. Without meaningful environmental interaction or evolving challenges, the repetition becomes increasingly noticeable. Enemy AI is also simplistic, often relying on predictable patterns that reduce the sense of tension or tactical decision-making. Once the player adapts to enemy behavior, the combat risks feeling mechanical rather than dynamic.

Technical and structural polish are also areas where the game struggles. Animations can appear stiff, hit detection uneven, and transitions between actions occasionally abrupt. These issues undermine the otherwise promising feel of the core combat. Interface elements, health indicators, and feedback cues lack refinement, which contributes to the impression that the game is still in an early developmental state despite being a full release. The lack of narrative framing or character progression further compounds this impression. With no developing story arc, upgrades, or evolving abilities, the motivation to continue rests entirely on the combat—and the repetition makes that a difficult foundation to sustain.

Despite these shortcomings, Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy is not without its audience. Players who enjoy lightweight hack-and-slash games and do not mind a repetitive structure may still find pockets of enjoyment in its fantasy presentation and straightforward combat loop. The game does not pretend to be a sprawling action RPG or a genre-defining adventure; it offers brief bursts of stylized action that may appeal to those looking for a simple, low-pressure experience. For some, the opportunity to swing a glowing sword in a myth-inspired landscape may be enough to justify the journey.

Ultimately, Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy feels like a concept with potential rather than a fully realized vision. It carries the ambition of a mythological action game but lacks the mechanical depth, polish, and variety needed to deliver a lasting or memorable experience. Its strengths—atmosphere, stylization, and occasional satisfying clashes—are consistently overshadowed by repetitive design, shallow encounters, and technical roughness. For players curious about the premise or those with a soft spot for experimental indie action titles, it may offer fleeting enjoyment. But for anyone seeking a polished, dynamic, or richly structured fantasy combat experience, Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy remains more of a rough sketch than a completed work.

Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy is an indie action title that strives to deliver a blend of mythological grandeur, fast-paced sword combat, and stylized environments. Developed by Quacky Games and published by Whale Rock Games, it presents itself as a journey through Scandinavian-inspired realms filled with supernatural foes and elemental power. At first glance, the game’s visual tone and combat-driven premise suggest a compact but energetic hack-and-slash experience. It promises a series of battles across varied landscapes, offering players a taste of mythic fantasy without demanding the complexity of a full RPG.

The combat system is at the heart of the game, and it is here where Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy tries to establish its identity. Encounters unfold in straightforward but punchy fashion: enemies approach in waves, the player reacts with melee strikes or ranged abilities, and timing becomes a crucial factor in managing each skirmish. Early encounters can feel engaging, especially when the rhythm of dodging, swinging, and spacing begins to click. There is a sense of satisfaction in carving through opponents with decisive blows, and the fantasy aesthetic of glowing weapons and elemental effects adds flair to these moments. While not deep in terms of variety or combo systems, the core movements and impacts do offer occasional bursts of fun that hint at what the game might have grown into with more development time.

Visually, the game leans into stylization rather than realism. Its environments—vast fields, shadowy valleys, and mythic battlegrounds—possess a certain atmospheric charm. The lighting effects and color palettes contribute to the sense of scale the developers aimed for, and while not lavishly detailed, the world does manage to convey the feeling of a lone warrior navigating a divine conflict. Character and enemy designs follow this same approach: recognizable silhouettes and dramatic proportions over intricate detail. For an indie title with modest system requirements, the visual direction succeeds in establishing mood even when technical limitations are apparent.

However, the cracks in the game’s foundation become clear as the experience progresses. One of the most persistent criticisms is the repetitive nature of the level design. Although the maps themselves vary in theme, the structure of the gameplay rarely evolves. Most stages follow the same loop: advance, fight a group of enemies, move forward again, and repeat. Without meaningful environmental interaction or evolving challenges, the repetition becomes increasingly noticeable. Enemy AI is also simplistic, often relying on predictable patterns that reduce the sense of tension or tactical decision-making. Once the player adapts to enemy behavior, the combat risks feeling mechanical rather than dynamic.

Technical and structural polish are also areas where the game struggles. Animations can appear stiff, hit detection uneven, and transitions between actions occasionally abrupt. These issues undermine the otherwise promising feel of the core combat. Interface elements, health indicators, and feedback cues lack refinement, which contributes to the impression that the game is still in an early developmental state despite being a full release. The lack of narrative framing or character progression further compounds this impression. With no developing story arc, upgrades, or evolving abilities, the motivation to continue rests entirely on the combat—and the repetition makes that a difficult foundation to sustain.

Despite these shortcomings, Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy is not without its audience. Players who enjoy lightweight hack-and-slash games and do not mind a repetitive structure may still find pockets of enjoyment in its fantasy presentation and straightforward combat loop. Press "Enter" to skip the intro.
We use "Alt" to somersault free from enemies when they pinch the hero in narrow passages.

You think you know what time is. You think it’s numbers on a screen, a sun setting, a deadline approaching. You are wrong. Time is a predator. It is a silent, invisible beast that stalks you from the moment you take your first breath, counting down the beats of your heart with cold, indifferent precision. I was running from this beast, hiding in the noise of a world that wouldn't shut up, until I downloaded this game.

I thought it was a joke. A rhythm game where you play as the second hand? It sounded like a distraction. But as the screen faded to that stark, minimalist void, the horror set in.
The Prison of the Second

The game demands perfection. You click. Tick. You wait. Tick. If you are too fast, time rushes and you stumble. If you are too slow, the universe drags. I played for hours. My room grew dark, the shadows stretching out like fingers trying to drag me into the abyss. I became the clock. I felt the agonizing weight of every single second passing.

I realized then that my life was not my own. I was just a mechanism, clicking away in a loop, terrified of missing a beat. The thriller wasn't on the screen; it was in my chest. The anxiety was suffocating. I was trapped in the circle, marching toward an end I couldn't see, chased by the phantom of wasted potential.
The Pig That Broke the World

And then, it happened.

I was deep in a trance, my finger tapping in sync with the heartbeat of a dying god, when the Pig appeared.

It wasn't just a sprite. It was an anomaly. A glitch in the matrix of my sorrow. In this world of rigid lines and unforgiving circles, this creature existed without permission. And then... it jumped.

It didn't just jump over the hands. It jumped over the concept of the Clock itself.

I stopped breathing. The controller slipped from my sweat-slicked hands. In that split second, watching that pixelated swine arc over the mechanism of time, something inside me—some vital, calcified wall—shattered.

The pig didn't care about the rhythm. It didn't care about the rules. It didn't care that the time was running out. It leaped with a reckless, beautiful abandon that I had never known in my entire twenty-something years of existence. It mocked the circle. It told me that the loop only exists if you refuse to jump.
A Permanent Scar

I sat in the darkness of my room, the blue light of the monitor washing over my face, and I wept.

I cried not because I was happy, but because I was mourning the person I used to be. The person who thought he had to tick. The person who was afraid to jump. That pig destroyed me. It tore open my chest and showed me the hollow space where my courage should have been.

My life is different now. I walk down the street, and I don't hear the ticking anymore. But there is a sadness that lingers, a beautiful, haunting ache. I realized that to be free, you have to be willing to look ridiculous. You have to be the pig in a world of clocks.
Why You Must Play

You need to play Clock Simulator. Not for the high score. Not for the rhythm. You need to play it to feel the suffocating grip of order, just so you can witness the miracle of the chaos.

I am damaged, yes. I am haunted by a digital animal. But I have never been more in love with the tragic, fleeting mess of being alive.

Come meet me in the void. Wait for the pig. And when it jumps, I pray that you, too, find the strength to break your own heart, just so you can finally see what beats inside it.
final score 3/10 meh

Clock Simulator isn't just a game, its an experience. The skill to click at the very right time as if you are ticking your own tock, turns you into something of no other. The ability to theoretically become your own clock is a lot easier said than done, this game has taught me in the hours I've put in, that clicking at the right time of a clock doesn't only make you a better coordinated person, but will also turn your life around.

To be more in depth, I clicked at the wrong time, when I clicked at the wrong time, I got decimated for it. This game helped me realize, that time is money, and money is time. When you click at the wrong time, you waisted said time. However, the moment you click in the right time, you are blessed with the potential ability, to keep going. At some point, when you've failed so many times, the game sends you to the main menu, why is it like this? Well, the game likes to treat you as a pig. Pigs like to do the same thing all day, they oink, and in this game, its no other. The pig oinks every time you click, but you also need to click perfectively. Now back to why the game sends you to the main menu. It loves to give you a second chance, typically games that show trial and error, will give you a failure screen. This game, on the other hand, not only says you didn't fail, but its also indicating that you tried your best. This is the most important part, when a game gives you the chance to try again, you give into that chance. Now the most important part is here for why they send you back to the main menu.

When you're sent back to the main menu, its abrupt. Abrupt meaning that there isn't a sound, isn't a text, but just you staring at the starting screen once again, heavily implying that you should try again. Back to what I was saying about the pigs. In this game you are put onto a clock, you are the pig, you must jump every second, for eternity. This is the most emotional part of the game. Time is on your hands in this mode. if you fail, the pig fails with you, this shows that time being money, you wouldn't want to waste money, every time you fail, you waste money in some way.

Why would I ever recommend this you may ask? Reasons being, it will forever change the way you percept time. It shows that time, isn't your best friend, time goes by, this game helps you realize that. You could spend hours on the pig rhythm or hours on the clock (probably not) but each time you succeed it shows you that, time keeps going, no matter what. SO if you are looking to perfectively not only spend your time wisely, evolving your immerse with time itself, but also, it will make you realize, that time is money, and money is time.

I cried not because I was happy, but because I was mourning the person I used to be. The person who thought he had to tick. The person who was afraid to jump. That pig destroyed me. It tore open my chest and showed me the hollow space where my courage should have been.

My life is different now. I walk down the street, and I don't hear the ticking anymore. But there is a sadness that lingers, a beautiful, haunting ache. I realized that to be free, you have to be willing to look ridiculous. You have to be the pig in a world of clocks.
Why You Must Play

You need to play Clock Simulator. Not for the high score. Not for the rhythm. You need to play it to feel the suffocating grip of order, just so you can witness the miracle of the chaos.

I am damaged, yes. I am haunted by a digital animal. But I have never been more in love with the tragic, fleeting mess of being alive.

Come meet me in the void. Wait for the pig. And when it jumps, I pray that you, too, find the strength to break your own heart, just so you can finally see what beats inside it.
final score 3/10 meh

Clock Simulator isn't just a game, its an experience. The skill to click at the very right time as if you are ticking your own tock, turns you into something of no other. The ability to theoretically become your own clock is a lot easier said than done, this game has taught me in the hours I've put in, that clicking at the right time of a clock doesn't only make you a better coordinated person, but will also turn your life around.

To be more in depth, I clicked at the wrong time, when I clicked at the wrong time, I got decimated for it. This game helped me realize, that time is money, and money is time. When you click at the wrong time, you waisted said time. However, the moment you click in the right time, you are blessed with the potential ability, to keep going. At some point, when you've failed so many times, the game sends you to the main menu, why is it like this? Well, the game likes to treat you as a pig. Pigs like to do the same thing all day, they oink, and in this game, its no other. The pig oinks every time you click, but you also need to click perfectively. Now back to why the game sends you to the main menu. It loves to give you a second chance, typically games that show trial and error, will give you a failure screen. This game, on the other hand, not only says you didn't fail, but its also indicating that you tried your best. This is the most important part, when a game gives you the chance to try again, you give into that chance. Now the most important part is here for why they send you back to the main menu.

When you're sent back to the main menu, its abrupt. Abrupt meaning that there isn't a sound, isn't a text, but just you staring at the starting screen once again, heavily implying that you should try again. Back to what I was saying about the pigs. In this game you are put onto a clock, you are the pig, you must jump every second, for eternity. This is the most emotional part of the game. Time is on your hands in this mode. if you fail, the pig fails with you, this shows that time being money, you wouldn't want to waste money, every time you fail, you waste money in some way.

Why would I ever recommend this you may ask? Reasons being, it will forever change the way you percept time. It shows that time, isn't your best friend, time goes by, this game helps you realize that. You could spend hours on the pig rhythm or hours on the clock (probably not) but each time you succeed it shows you that, time keeps going, no matter what. SO if you are looking to perfectively not only spend your time wisely, evolving your immerse with time itself, but also, it will make you realize, that time is money, and money is time.

You think you know what time is. You think it’s numbers on a screen, a sun setting, a deadline approaching. You are wrong. Time is a predator. It is a silent, invisible beast that stalks you from the moment you take your first breath, counting down the beats of your heart with cold, indifferent precision. I was running from this beast, hiding in the noise of a world that wouldn't shut up, until I downloaded this game.

I thought it was a joke. A rhythm game where you play as the second hand? It sounded like a distraction. But as the screen faded to that stark, minimalist void, the horror set in.
The Prison of the Second

The game demands perfection. You click. Tick. You wait. Tick. If you are too fast, time rushes and you stumble. If you are too slow, the universe drags. I played for hours. My room grew dark, the shadows stretching out like fingers trying to drag me into the abyss. I became the clock. I felt the agonizing weight of every single second passing.

I realized then that my life was not my own. I was just a mechanism, clicking away in a loop, terrified of missing a beat. The thriller wasn't on the screen; it was in my chest. The anxiety was suffocating. I was trapped in the circle, marching toward an end I couldn't see, chased by the phantom of wasted potential.
The Pig That Broke the World

And then, it happened.

I was deep in a trance, my finger tapping in sync with the heartbeat of a dying god, when the Pig appeared.

It wasn't just a sprite. It was an anomaly. A glitch in the matrix of my sorrow. In this world of rigid lines and unforgiving circles, this creature existed without permission. And then... it jumped.

It didn't just jump over the hands. It jumped over the concept of the Clock itself.

I stopped breathing. The controller slipped from my sweat-slicked hands. In that split second, watching that pixelated swine arc over the mechanism of time, something inside me—some vital, calcified wall—shattered.

The pig didn't care about the rhythm. It didn't care about the rules. It didn't care that the time was running out. It leaped with a reckless, beautiful abandon that I had never known in my entire twenty-something years of existence. It mocked the circle. It told me that the loop only exists if you refuse to jump.
A Permanent Scar

I sat in the darkness of my room, the blue light of the monitor washing over my face, and I wept.

I cried not because I was happy, but because I was mourning the person I used to be. The person who thought he had to tick. The person who was afraid to jump. That pig destroyed me. It tore open my chest and showed me the hollow space where my courage should have been.

My life is different now. I walk down the street, and I don't hear the ticking anymore. But there is a sadness that lingers, a beautiful, haunting ache. I realized that to be free, you have to be willing to look ridiculous. You have to be the pig in a world of clocks.
Why You Must Play

You need to play Clock Simulator. Not for the high score. Not for the rhythm. You need to play it to feel the suffocating grip of order, just so you can witness the miracle of the chaos.

I am damaged, yes. I am haunted by a digital animal. But I have never been more in love with the tragic, fleeting mess of being alive.

Come meet me in the void. Wait for the pig. And when it jumps, I pray that you, too, find the strength to break your own heart, just so you can finally see what beats inside it.

review_text: This series needs polishing but never disappoints. It's improved game after game and always comes with new features. Kudos to the dev for bringing me some good nuts.

game_name: Love n War: Hero by Chance II
review_text:
This is a decent line fighter and a step up from the first game. The sexy bits are just pics that move a little but you do not really interact with them outside picking a thing and a place. If the sexy stuff if all you want there are 3 difficulties where easy unlocks it all.
Most levels have 3 lanes you have to defend though a few only have 1. There are also levels where you pick a few units to clear a path through a preset of enemies. if you have the dlc when you get to those battles you'll be able to build obstacles in the lanes. Some of your units will have special gear you can make you change the way they look and make them stronger. There is also an arena mode (with the dlc) that let's you do the fights without the story and you'll see what units they enemy can spawn before hand.
The story is ok not great but more than just bleh, bleh though you could skip it all and not miss anything.
5/10

game_name: Love n War: Hero by Chance II
review_text:
This is a decent line fighter and a step up from the first game. The sexy bits are just pics that move a little but you do not really interact with them outside picking a thing and a place. If the sexy stuff if all you want there are 3 difficulties where easy unlocks it all.
Most levels have 3 lanes you have to defend though a few only have 1. There are also levels where you pick a few units to clear a path through a preset of enemies. if you have the dlc when you get to those battles you'll be able to build obstacles in the lanes. Some of your units will have special gear you can make you change the way they look and make them stronger. There is also an arena mode (with the dlc) that let's you do the fights without the story and you'll see what units they enemy can spawn before hand.
The story is ok not great but more than just bleh, bleh though you could skip it all and not miss anything.
5/10

I Recommend it, but Only Just.

Be warned, this game features heavy use of AI generated Images. To its credit, however, it does give you an option in the settings to hide AI. Unfortunately, there are still quite a few images that appear to be AI that are not labelled as such, and therefore still appear after selecting that filter. 

Gameplay wise, it's solid. Yan do puzzles alone or with friends, you can favorite images while you're browsing and come back to them later, and you can customize the table color and puzzle mat that covers the ghost image. The UI does leave a lot to be desired. I would very much like to see a filter to only show free puzzles rather than having to scroll through countless images I have zero intention of paying for in a free game. 

That being said, a paid DLC to use your own images is fine, but needing to purchase credits for each custom image is a big turnoff. If the dev(s) absolutely HAVE to have that paid DLC, I suggest making it a one-time purchase for unlimited images, and perhaps just increasing the price of the DLC a little. I don't plan on wasting my money on it though when there are websites that let me do that for free anyway.

Overall, I would give this game a 3/5, docking those 2 points for the lax AI filtering, the complete lack of filter for paid vs free puzzles, and and the need to make multiple in-game purchases to use your own images. If those drawbacks don't dissuade you, then by all means, this free game is a great deal.
Very relaxing game. I like that you can play it with your friends and that you can create puzzle with whatever picture you want.

If you like jigsaw puzzles but either don't have space or money enough to do them as often as you'd like then this is a great alternative. 
I dislike how most are locked behind a paywall but the cost is quite low and I can understand why you'd need to pay for certain things.
Great for introverts who want to spend time together too! You can turn off voice chat even if you want, or you can play solo, it's really nice.

We both enjoy this game and the fact that there's so many packs to choose from that are interesting is even more better! So many options to choose from to work on, solo or with others.

Play this with my roommate when we just wanna chill and not have to think too hard about whatever game we are playing we will both get on our Steamdecks and work a puzzle together. Very well done. Very relaxing. I love working puzzles.

Easy to navigate and use.  Good choice of puzzles whether paid or free.  Can play alone or with others.  Can play on your own puzzles or someone elses.  Can meet some interesting people from around the word on there too, or puzzle in silence.

The only down sides are that some players have zero etiquet, they will join a game, won't determine where people are playing and just come and start messing with your setup, and add all the partial bits of the jigsaw to the main table completely messing things up... no converstion or anything.   We share the points! It's not like they get the points just because they put it on the table, everyone in the room shares them.

The other is lazy people.   You can be the only one in a game, spend ages sorting a section in colours and shapes just to have someone enter the game and, instead of getting their own section. they start working on the ones you have spent ages on and spreading pieces all over the place.  Pure laziness.  Both of these  types of people will generall act in childish behaviour if you politely ask them to stop.  More people should report those players because it ruins the atmosphere.

Ok so imma finally gonna leave a review. I'm a returning player that has played since alpha. 

This is what I think of the state of the game:

For a new player that's never played a Korean MMO, its grindy and has RNG systems for progressing. It has p2w leaned mechanics for end game. Not necessary but will get u there faster.

If that's a pass in your book, try the game. You're given power passes (every release of a new class) which boost your level up to (currently tier 4) skipping all old content. 
If you start fresh, you'll most likely have to do all old raids for progression solo and imo can be a rough experience especially the current T3 honing currency.
 Its still required gold instead of silver. We are supposed to get an update soon.
 (As of 2/4 this change has been implemented.)  

With Guardian Knight (the newest class) being released in Feb 2026 there will be a new power pass.

With that being said, its a pretty game with unique classes. Designed with Unreal engine 3.
Director said they will not upgrade and keep this engine.

Amazon Game Studio is the publisher and I can tell you from experience that the customer service is horrid.
Don't expect them to help you recover anything over a month ago. Customer Service Representative told me they only keep purchasing records for a month. (on a long break i lost most skins, including my founders skin and mount)

I'm an MMO veteran and can say the true action combat system and raids so far have been the best experience I've had so far within the genre. 

Feel free to ask any questions and/or if your looking for learners raids (for current content) on NA.

Hope this review helps you make your decision. We'd love to have you!
damn as soon as i start actually playing this game, everyone says it's going to ♥♥♥♥, it is a good game though
played  a bit and forgot evrything about game so now i can t play it cause im LOST now.
In all honesty? I haven't played this game much. I'm not deep into it. this is why i'm actually recommending it since i don't feel a need to be there all the time.

Overall a really good MMO.
Let break down what the people complain about:
- "It's a job after the job" : well of course it needs a decent amount of grind to reach the full endgame content, by reading the guides, learning the patterns, die-and-retrying the raids.... but what do you expect from a game like this. It would be like buying a moto and complain about being wet under the rain!
Each fight has its unique mechanics and visuals, it's really fun to learn and satisfying to succeed.

-"It's a p2w" : Ngl I guess it would help a lot for fast progress, but it's still a real f2p. I reached all the endgame content without spending any real money, just time and dedication. It gives a real sense of achievement and great memories when you finally get the drop you expected for a few days.
Also "Mokoko Express" or similar events from times to times help to reach higher ilevel easily.

- "It's impossible to get a lobby to do older raids" : I must agree with this one. Most online communities are focused on latest content, and lobbies for older content are either bussing or fast reclean. It's not really welcoming for learning newcomer/returning players. BUT, to me, the best point in this game is that there is solo content that is actually doable, and fun to play, with simplified/adapted boss patterns. So it's not a problem learning older content solo, to catch up to the latest raids.

- "It's overloaded with things to do and menus" : It gives multiple ways to continue to enjoy the game if you are not interested in the grind/raids, or just want to take a break from it, while still helping the progress of your character.

So to me, there are no big red flags in the game, (of course it's not perfect either), but people need to know what they are getting into. If you don't like challenging yourself, your focus and your patience, maybe enjoy another game.

* from a returning/pausing/returning again/pausing again/re-returning again player, since the release of the game in EU. Objective mokoko 100%

Pros: As a free to play game Lost Ark is good. If you like a Diablo-like birds-eye view hack and slash game it is also top tier. The main draw of Lost Ark comes from its boss fights. The boss fights are unlike any other MMO I've played. The story is good or can be skipped with the crtl+g mechanic. Recently Lost Ark has made steps in allowing people to play raids solo as of this review it is 1680 item level and below. That is every raid in the game except the last 2. Lost Ark provides plenty of oppurtunities to power level characters to help get newer players into the end game. As of writing they are currently giving a powerpass, which automatically catches you're character up into the story and gives them appropriate gear ie 1640 for tier 4. Lost Ark is an unusual beast: a Korean-developed MMOARPG that blends the frenetic, loot-driven combat of Diablo with the persistent world and social systems of traditional MMOs. The result is overwhelmingly dense, occasionally frustrating, and surprisingly compelling once you surrender to its rhythm.

The Currency Conundrum
Let's address the elephant in the room immediately: Lost Ark has a currency problem. Silver, gold (two types), pirate coins, crystals in multiple hues, and more tokens than a county fair—all exchangeable for other currencies or goods in ways that feel deliberately obfuscated. It's indicative of the game's primary issue: Lost Ark is as dense as a block of lead and roughly as good at explaining itself. Nearly 60 hours in, my storage box remains full of items I don't understand and will probably never use.

Combat That Sings
Thankfully, everything else sings. The combat is immediately cathartic—frenetic, impactful, and designed to make you feel overpowered in the best way. With 15 classes spread across five main archetypes (Warrior, Martial Artist, Gunner, Mage, Assassin), each plays distinctly enough to warrant multiple playthroughs. Playing as a Deathblade Assassin, every skill found purpose; modifiers unlock as abilities level, creating meaningful customization without the paralysis of getting it wrong.

The game thoughtfully starts you at level 10 with a full skill bar—a change from earlier builds that streamlines the experience. While veteran players might mourn the lost introductory levels, the adjustment demonstrates developer Smilegate's willingness to learn and improve.

Structure and Story
Quests follow familiar paths: kill quests, fetch quests, more fetch quests. The menial tasks can feel beneath your station as the Champion of Luterra, and the lack of quest stacking means you're rarely leaving hubs with a full log. But Lost Ark cleverly structures each new continent as a self-contained story arc. One region channels Final Fantasy VII's Midgar with steampunk aesthetics; another requires shrinking potions to fight monstrous insects. This creativity keeps the busywork tolerable.

The overarching plot—hunting seven pieces of the titular Ark to banish darkness—takes a backseat to these regional tales. Characters are likeable, writing cheesy in that JRPG way but sprinkled with genuine pathos. The first major arc involving Prince Thirain's kingdom is genuinely engaging.

World and Systems
This is where Lost Ark reveals its true ambition. Beyond the main quest lies staggering depth:

Chaos Dungeons offer procedural horde-mode fights

Guardian Raids and Abyssal Dungeons provide challenging group content

Your Stronghold functions as a customizable base for crafting and research

Trade skills cover foraging, hunting, fishing, mining, and treasure hunting

The Adventurer's Tome tracks collectibles across continents, rewarding completionists with mounts, skins, and stat boosts

Mokoko Seeds number in the thousands for those who must find everything

Each new continent (with three added for Western release) contains hidden stories, secret bosses, and rare discoveries. It's designed for hundreds of hours.

Solo vs. Multiplayer
Playing pre-release on an under-populated server meant experiencing much of Lost Ark solo—not ideal for an MMO. The 20-hour journey to level 50 works fine alone, but the endgame content demands cooperation. Once you reach Vern and unlock the good stuff, the game transforms. A free level 50 boost for a second character after your first max-level run is a welcome quality-of-life touch.

Presentation and Customization
Character creation offers surprising depth—easily 15 minutes vanished before my first adventure. The zones remain consistent rather than procedurally generated, making subsequent playthroughs feel familiar and efficient. Visuals are vibrant, and the world feels alive in ways procedurally generated ARPGs often don't.

Shortcomings
Beyond the currency confusion and fetch quest fatigue, a few issues persist:

Generic side quests rarely advance story or interest

Limited quest stacking means constant back-and-forth travel

Traditional MMO roles (tank/healer/DPS) don't really exist—adjust expectations accordingly

The endgame mountain requires months to properly evaluate

Verdict
Lost Ark is a lot. A lot of currencies, a lot of systems, a lot of content, a lot of hours. Approach it for what it is—an Action-RPG with MMO trappings, not the reverse—and there's a sprawling world of adventure to discover. The combat is excellent, the class variety genuine, and the density of things to do borders on overwhelming.

It's the kind of game that connects people across distances, the way MMOs have always done best. I can't wait to play it with friends.

Lost Ark has the best combat system in any MMO I've ever played.[b] Unfortunately[/b], it's wrapped in so many layers of predatory systems, FOMO mechanics, and terrible new player experience that I can't recommend it unless you hate yourself or have unlimited free time.

[h1]The Good (Yes, There Is Some)[/h1]

[h2]Combat: 10/10[/h2] - No joke, the raid fights and combat mechanics are chef's kiss. This is what keeps people playing despite everything else. The boss encounters are challenging, rewarding, and genuinely fun. If you could extract just the combat and raids, this would be a masterpiece.

[h2]Visuals: 8/10[/h2] - Game looks gorgeous. The particle effects, character designs, and animations are top-tier. You'll look cool while suffering.

[h2]Content Volume: 9/10[/h2] - There's an insane amount of stuff to do. Whether you should do it all is another question entirely.

[h1]The Bad (Oh Boy, Here We Go)[/h1]

[h2]New Player Experience: 2/10[/h2]

Imagine walking into a library where every book is written in a different language, the Dewey Decimal System was replaced with hieroglyphics, and the librarian just screams FIGURE IT OUT before running away. That's Lost Ark for new players.

You'll be bombarded with:
47 different currencies (I counted)
Systems within systems within systems
Pop-ups that make early 2000s malware look subtle
Events you can't do
Guides that are already outdated
That nagging feeling you're doing everything wrong (you probably are)

[h2]The Grind: 3/10[/h2]
Korean MMO grind is expected, but Lost Ark takes it to another level. Everything is time-gated, ticket-gated, RNG-gated, and paywall-gated. Miss your daily/weekly stuff? Congrats, you're now permanently behind forever.

[h2]P2W Elements: 4/10[/h2]
"It's not P2W, it's pay-to-progress-faster!"... Sure BRO, while you can technically do everything as F2P, whales will reach endgame content months before you. The game constantly reminds you that your life would be easier if you just opened your wallet

[h2]Story: 1/10 [/h2]
The story is so bad it's almost impressive. You're supposedly the chosen one, but you feel like an NPC in your own adventure


[h1]The Ugly[/h1]

Returning Player Experience: 0/10

Took a break? Cool, the game changed completely, your guides are worthless, and there's zero help to catch you up. Hope you enjoy feeling lost in a game called Lost Ark (maybe that's the joke?).

FOMO Mechanics: 0/10

This game wants you to feel bad about having a life. Limited events, daily lockouts, weekly resets, seasonal content - it's all designed to keep you anxious and logging in. It's exhausting.

Customer Service: 1/10

Amazon Games customer service is about as useful as a chocolate teapot. Lost items? Bugs? Good luck.


[h1]Who Is This Game For?[/h1]

[h2]Yes if[/h2]
You enjoy Korean MMO grind culture and know what you're signing up for 
You have 4-6 hours daily to commit 
You love spreadsheets, wikis, and external guides 
You either started at launch or you're a masochist 
You have a guild/friends to carry you through the learning curve
You don't mind P2W elements

[h2]No if[/h2]
You have a job, family, or literally any other hobby 
You want to play casually 
You value your mental health 
You expect games to teach you how to play them 
You took a break and want to return 
You're new to MMOs


[h1]Final Thoughts[/h1]

I have almost 1,2k hours in this game. That should tell you something about how good the core combat is. But it should also tell you something that despite loving the raids, I still can't recommend this to anyone. The combat is a 10/10 trapped inside a 4/10 game.

It is one of the best MMORPGs I have ever played. Its vast world, the diverse storylines in the quests, the well-designed character skill sets, and the cosmetic options are all excellent. The fact that there are so many different things to do in the universe definitely keeps you from getting bored and makes you lose track of time. The reason I stopped playing was that I am a solo player, and since raids require coordinated teamwork with multiple people, I could no longer make progress. Otherwise, I would definitely never have quit.

Overall a really good MMO.
Let break down what the people complain about:
- "It's a job after the job" : well of course it needs a decent amount of grind to reach the full endgame content, by reading the guides, learning the patterns, die-and-retrying the raids.... but what do you expect from a game like this. It would be like buying a moto and complain about being wet under the rain!
Each fight has its unique mechanics and visuals, it's really fun to learn and satisfying to succeed.

-"It's a p2w" : Ngl I guess it would help a lot for fast progress, but it's still a real f2p. I reached all the endgame content without spending any real money, just time and dedication. It gives a real sense of achievement and great memories when you finally get the drop you expected for a few days.
Also "Mokoko Express" or similar events from times to times help to reach higher ilevel easily.

- "It's impossible to get a lobby to do older raids" : I must agree with this one. Most online communities are focused on latest content, and lobbies for older content are either bussing or fast reclean. It's not really welcoming for learning newcomer/returning players. BUT, to me, the best point in this game is that there is solo content that is actually doable, and fun to play, with simplified/adapted boss patterns. So it's not a problem learning older content solo, to catch up to the latest raids.

- "It's overloaded with things to do and menus" : It gives multiple ways to continue to enjoy the game if you are not interested in the grind/raids, or just want to take a break from it, while still helping the progress of your character.

So to me, there are no big red flags in the game, (of course it's not perfect either), but people need to know what they are getting into. If you don't like challenging yourself, your focus and your patience, maybe enjoy another game.

* from a returning/pausing/returning again/pausing again/re-returning again player, since the release of the game in EU. Objective mokoko 100%

Pros: As a free to play game Lost Ark is good. If you like a Diablo-like birds-eye view hack and slash game it is also top tier. The main draw of Lost Ark comes from its boss fights. The boss fights are unlike any other MMO I've played. The story is good or can be skipped with the crtl+g mechanic. Recently Lost Ark has made steps in allowing people to play raids solo as of this review it is 1680 item level and below. That is every raid in the game except the last 2. Lost Ark provides plenty of oppurtunities to power level characters to help get newer players into the end game. As of writing they are currently giving a powerpass, which automatically catches you're character up into the story and gives them appropriate gear ie 1640 for tier 4. Next they give you a power pass which boosts honing success rates, decreases honing costs, and gives free items to help the honing processes.

Cons: As a free to play game Lost Ark needs to make money somehow. First things first Lost Ark is pay to win. The top players can buy materials to hone and upgrade their characters using the in-game item shops and gold. Next is the RNG factor that comes with upgrading characters and honing. Every possible upgrade is made through an RNG system. Honing is not as bad as BDO's was but it is still miserable. Ex. you get 28,000 gold for doing the 1680 raid solo. Each chance of honing has to upgrade your armor from 1680 to 1685 has a 5% chance to succeed and costs around 2,000 gold per attempt. This is one of the largest downsides of Lost Ark as more senior players will tell you more details about how their journey went. BY FAR THE WORST PART IS THE RAID GATEKEEPING. For the last couple of raids you'd need to find a raid group. You'll see terms like prog, homework, and very occasionally learning. The only chance you'd have to get in one of these raids groups is to find or create you're own learning raid and do this 10 times, for 10 weeks or so to get the title which will allow you to participate in the hw raids and sometimes prog raids. 

My verdict as a free to play player; I would suggest playing lost ark if you like playing cool boss fights and number go up style of satisfaction. The first time you'd hit 1,000,000 is a great feeling. However, playing during events is heavily recommended.

[h1]A Final Farewell to the Land of Aeternum[/h1]

[i]Writing this brings tears to my eyes. After a lifetime spent in World of Warcraft, I didn't think I could find another world that felt like home. I was wrong.[/i]

[b]The Beauty in the Quiet Moments[/b] I will never forget the peace of gathering alone in those beautiful landscapes. The sound of the pickaxe, the light through the trees—it was my escape. But as much as I loved the solitude, I loved the community more. In this game, you constantly needed help, and people always gave it. Returning those favors was one of the greatest pleasures of the journey.

[b]When the World Stood Together[/b] I was there from the beginning. I saw the "fall" when the initial hype faded and the crowds left. But those of us who stayed did something amazing. To survive the game's own attacks (The Corrupted), we stopped fighting each other. We made a pact: each faction took one city. We teamed up as an entire server to protect those three cities and upgrade the crafting stations together. We chose cooperation over war to keep our world alive.

[b]The End of an Era[/b] I loved that this game required us to work together to build something. It wasn't just about my level; it was about the city's level. It was about [i]us[/i].

Thank you, New World, for the memories, the friendships, and the sense of belonging. I’m heartbroken to see you go, but I’m so grateful I was there to see the sun rise and set on Aeternum.

[b]Farewell, Traveler. I’ll see you in the next world.[/b]

New World’s launch mechanics delivered a bold and ambitious MMO experience built around territory control, persistent combat systems, and survival-flavored progression, even if they weren’t fully polished at first.

⚔️ Combat & Action Mechanics

At release, New World’s combat was real-time, responsive, and stamina-driven — closer to action RPGs than many tab-target MMOs. Dodging, blocking, and directional attacks mattered. Each weapon had a distinct rhythm: rapiers rewarded spacing and counters, great axes leaned into heavy hits and crowd control, and muskets/warbows emphasized positioning and aim. Blocking wasn’t just a numeric reduction — it mechanically stopped momentum and opened counter windows.

Weapon mastery wasn’t surface-level either. Abilities on each weapon had meaningful cooldowns and distinct mechanics (e.g., area denial with fire staves, knockdowns with void gauntlets), forcing you to pick loadouts based on tactic, not just DPS.

🛡️ Stamina & Resource Pulse

Stamina was a core mechanical meter, not a cosmetic bar. Sprinting, dodging, blocking, and heavy attacks all drained stamina, and running out in a fight wasn’t just an inconvenience — it mechanically prevented actions, forcing you to retreat or reposition. This made movement and timing as crucial as gear score in fights.

🌍 Open-World Systems & Progression

New World’s open world wasn’t a backdrop — it was a persistent mechanical ecology. Resources respawned predictably, nodes regenerated, and resource gathering (logging, mining, harvesting) unlocked crafting tiers that mechanically affected gear strength. Crafting wasn’t icing — it was a core progression loop: refine → craft → equip → repeat.

Leveling was equipment-integrated. Gear score mattered mechanically, not just numerically. Better gear unlocked higher thresholds in elite zones and defended territory bonuses more effectively.

🏙️ Territory & Faction Control

The territory control system was a standout mechanic at launch. Factions vied for control of settlements, and war sieges were large-scale mechanical events. Controlling a territory provided bonuses — tax cuts, resource yields, crafting perks — that weren’t cosmetic. These mechanics tied social strategy directly into gameplay systems, making guild organization, zoning, and contribution meaningful.

Outpost Rush, the flagship PvP mode, was built around objective mechanics rather than pure deathmatching. Control points, scoring loops, reinforcement summoning, and push mechanics made it feel like a dynamic battlefield machine rather than repetitive skirmishing.

🧍 Survival & Lifestyle Loops

At launch, food, consumables, and gear degradation had mechanical impact. Eating food provided stat buffs that mattered in combat and crafting loops; failing to repair gear meant mechanically weaker performance in fights. These systems reinforced engagement rather than being fluff.

⚙️ Rough Edges (At Launch)

While many systems were mechanically rich, they weren’t fully polished on day one. Combat could feel floaty at times, encounter pacing uneven, and some resource loops weren’t fully balanced. But mechanically, the design intent was clear: systems should interlock, not exist in isolation.

9/10 — robust action combat, territory mechanics, and progression loops that made the early MMO experience feel fresh and meaningful.

I’ve put many, many hours into New World, and I can honestly say it’s one of the most meaningful gaming experiences I’ve had. From the moment I stepped into Aeternum, the world pulled me in not just with its visuals and combat, but with the people.

This game gave me more than quests and gear. It gave me friends, late night adventures, spontaneous PvP chaos, territory wars that got my heart racing, and quiet moments just wandering the world with familiar names in chat. The social side of New World was special in a way that’s hard to explain unless you lived it. Companies felt like real communities. 

Was it perfect? No. Like any MMO, it had its ups and downs, balance changes, bugs, and growing pains. But none of that outweighs what it did right. The world was beautiful and immersive. And most importantly, it created memories that stuck.

What makes me saddest is that nothing has quite compared since. I’ve tried other games, but they haven’t captured the same mix of atmosphere, camaraderie, and emotional investment. New World was lightning in a bottle for me, and I’m genuinely heartbroken to see that era come to an end.

I loved this game. I’ll always remember it, not just as something I played, but as a place where I spent time with people who mattered. And that’s not something you forget.

Do you know that feeling when you stumble upon a game that you just give a shot to and then after a multiple hour session you realise, that you have just opened a gate to infinite addiction? 
Yup thats what I got from this immediately.
I have been a TEW enjoyer for a long time but I always feel like I need to plan everything into such detail that it doesnt leave space for the thing I love most about wrestling. And that is telling stories and creating fantasy storylines.
PWS is a more enjoyable wrestling simulator, where you can get into easily and have a time of your life.
The mods are what really make this pop. Recreating or rebooking certain time periods or storylines is exactly what this type of games are made for. Personally I love rebooking TNA or WCW and trying to beat WWE. That underdog story is just lovely to play.
I would love to see more mods in the future but that ofc depends on the community.
Very much a work in progress but with each update and the community's work on making saves to play and various add-ons, PWS's potential is sky high!
Honestly if the features and updates keep going even after full 1.0 release this might be a great budget alternative to the more expensive booker simulator games out there.

Pro Wrestling Sim is a game that feels like it was made just for me. This is a management, text-based game where you take control of a Wrestling Promotion and manage such things like signing talent, be that wrestlers, staff, announcers, or personalities, the drug policy of the promotion, titles, teams, stables, storylines, network deals, and events. Be that weekly, monthly, annual, or tours, the events is where you schedule matches, the match type, who is on commentary, even the winner and what titles are up for grabs. You can set up a variety of angles, such as Attacks, Promos, Talking, and more. You can even add your own details to matches and angles. All while managing your workers moral, momentum, your promotions cash flow, production quality, and fans. One tip is that workers stats and your own fanbase grows in points, like 0.2 or 0.7, so the game is built for a marathon style play. Your rookies won't suddenly become experts over night.

The game comes with a built in editor and you can change nearly everything. Want to give yourself more money, up a workers stats, create your own match types and angles and maybe even add your own workers? All possible with the in-game editor but made even easier with the SQLiteStudio software. There are a chunk of mods, such as Modern Day ones, Classic Era ones, fictional and what if ones and they can all be merged together.

You will not like this game if you are looking for a graphic showcase like WWE2k or you aren't into text-only gameplay. There is AI usage in the game, but it's used for Worker pictures and has an entirely optional AI Assistant that can help you plan stories, keep up with your roster, and be excited for you. The AI Assistant's interactions are saved per save file so it's not recording them across your entire playtime.

As a fan of management games, imaginative fill-in-the-gaps-yourself storytelling and wrestling, this game is an extremely addictive and fun time.
A very visually appealing game to play for pro wrestling fans who either don't like or want to buy TEW
Amazing game, happy that it is constantly getting updates.
A great fun sandbox, a little basic which is good making it easy to get into and not too much scarring. Hopefully with more updates and mods it will always continue being more and more fun.
Really stunk up the place with negative five star review. On top of that, B. Brian Blair has a bad attitude. He will be buried. Great game.
Great game! Developer actively listens and updates the game to make it better!

Huge potential for this to be the best wrestling simulation ever produced.
If you loved GM mode, and have always wished there was a more in-depth version - this game is for you.

Be that weekly, monthly, annual, or tours, the events is where you schedule matches, the match type, who is on commentary, even the winner and what titles are up for grabs. You can set up a variety of angles, such as Attacks, Promos, Talking, and more. You can even add your own details to matches and angles. All while managing your workers moral, momentum, your promotions cash flow, production quality, and fans. One tip is that workers stats and your own fanbase grows in points, like 0.2 or 0.7, so the game is built for a marathon style play. Your rookies won't suddenly become experts over night.

The game comes with a built in editor and you can change nearly everything. Want to give yourself more money, up a workers stats, create your own match types and angles and maybe even add your own workers? All possible with the in-game editor but made even easier with the SQLiteStudio software. There are a chunk of mods, such as Modern Day ones, Classic Era ones, fictional and what if ones and they can all be merged together.

You will not like this game if you are looking for a graphic showcase like WWE2k or you aren't into text-only gameplay. There is AI usage in the game, but it's used for Worker pictures and has an entirely optional AI Assistant that can help you plan stories, keep up with your roster, and be excited for you. The AI Assistant's interactions are saved per save file so it's not recording them across your entire playtime.

As a fan of management games, imaginative fill-in-the-gaps-yourself storytelling and wrestling, this game is an extremely addictive and fun time.
This is one of the most replayable fun games I've ever played.  It's not easy, but it's deep and enjoyable.
Suprisingly addictive.
It's so so good. Devs, please make a few DLCs, I beg you

Let my hour count speak for itself when I say this game is extremely replayable and detailed. I can not recommend this game more, especially if you enjoy long, single-player, strategy games. There is so much customization that each run feels completely different, and you can very easily tailor the difficulty to what suits you best. Also, getting the secret ending in this game was one of the most unique and thought-provoking experiences in any game I've ever played. And who knows, maybe you'll actually find yourself stranded on an island one day, and having played this game will save your life! ;)

An interesting game, and you can see how many hours people have sunk into this by the looks of the reviews, which is actually what led to my purchase.

I had a little fun, but be warned you will be fighting the interface every step of the way, and some decisions that seem simple are just impossible.

One example is if you cut down a palm frond you receive one long stick and somewhere between 10-16 palm fronds placed into your "hand". You must then drag and drop all 10-16 of them onto your pile of stuff on the beach.

There's some minor built-in workarounds for certain actions, such as laying out coconut halves to collect water during a rainstorm, you can "do 12x" with the tap of a button, but not being able to do that with "stacks" is a pain. Similarly, palm fronds add 2% charge to any fire you have going. Want to add 30 percent charge to the fire? Guess what you've got to do.

The thing that ticked me off the most though was roasting meat in the fire. It tells you exactly how long it will take to cook, but there are only certain multiples of "waiting time" that can be used. You can't "Rest 30 minutes) for a piece of meat that takes 27 minutes to cook.

Crafting takes a certain amount of time too, and whether you can eat your roast meat is binary- it's pass/fail cooked or burnt.

So, say you want to sit down at the fire and work on crafting a new item- you get an "oops! That item took 45 minutes to craft and you sat there without putting it down while the delicious sizzling meat smell turns into a thick smoke then burns to a crisp.

That was the final straw for me for my 2 hours of play, which I mostly enjoyed a lot.

Just the thought of sitting there fiddling with some tools trying to make something while next to the fire and letting your food burn to a crisp because you couldn't stop partway for 2 minutes to take the meat off the heat is just ridiculous and led to more "number finicking" with "Can i craft this entire item or drill an entire hole in a coconut in time to not watch my meat burn to cinders" was too much.

Comments welcome if I'm wrong or missed something, I'd be happy to go back to this.

Edit: steam has asked me to revise my review as I have continued to play, I'd like to say that the cooking issue is still frustrating and that I still don't see a way to move stacks of items.

Edit 2, done on my own with no prompting from steam:

You can dive or spear fish while meat is cooking for almost the exact amount of right time, and also you can quickly right click to move stacks or else drag one and hold and it vacuums up others.

This game is pretty dope, even though I think I might be done for a bit. Maybe.
This is one of the most replayable fun games I've ever played.  It's not easy, but it's deep and enjoyable.

This game is surprisingly very immersive, like it feels like other survival games where you are dealing with a 3D space even though this is 2D. And it's so replayable! Time doesn't move in it until you do an action, so I've been playing while dealing with bronchitis and coughing over and over without it hindering the gameplay. It's been incredibly fun diving in and learning while playing, even when dying over and over. Each fresh start is exciting! And the ambient sounds are really soothing (except for the heartbeat while dying of course). A friend got me this game, but I still think with how much you can get out of it, this game is worth getting

Very good survival game that really draws me in the situations. I'm not fan of some mystic dimensions of game, but I thibk of them like mental ilness or something like that. There are just couple of those things and they are temporary anyway. Randomness and different challenges give it plenty of replayability.

I wanted to give an review to this game. Nov/25
I barely ever wrote an feedback to a game but i think this game tropical island from them is really right up my alley of replayable worthy and ive put so many hours into each of them. I have over 234 hours in fantasy forest and another 200 hours in tropical island. 

And it replayable worthy and lots to discover ingame.
The only downside is the Mermaid Character is really hard to play because even with my knowledge and hours its just really easy for me every other game play character also costum ofc are all great but after all so much love and eye for details in these kinda unique indepth card games. 

Aswell its lovely how these two games play so well on the steamdeck i never had issues with them on my Decky. I love how the Developers are actually paying attention to the gamers and their report system and actively working on these games. A thank you to all of them. I just love playing these games and trying to have a little alone time to play in peace and quiet thanks and cheers.
I haven't played this game in years, but I still regularly come back to it in my thoughts – so unique and deep its gameplay is. Very fun, with just the right learning curve.
Suprisingly addictive.
It's so so good. Devs, please make a few DLCs, I beg you

Let my hour count speak for itself when I say this game is extremely replayable and detailed. I can not recommend this game more, especially if you enjoy long, single-player, strategy games. There is so much customization that each run feels completely different, and you can very easily tailor the difficulty to what suits you best. Also, getting the secret ending in this game was one of the most unique and thought-provoking experiences in any game I've ever played. And who knows, maybe you'll actually find yourself stranded on an island one day, and having played this game will save your life! ;)

An interesting game, and you can see how many hours people have sunk into this by the looks of the reviews, which is actually what led to my purchase.

I had a little fun, but be warned you will be fighting the interface every step of the way, and some decisions that seem simple are just impossible.

One example is if you cut down a palm frond you receive one long stick and somewhere between 10-16 palm fronds placed into your "hand". You must then drag and drop all 10-16 of them onto your pile of stuff on the beach.

There's some minor built-in workarounds for certain actions, such as laying out coconut halves to collect water during a rainstorm, you can "do 12x" with the tap of a button, but not being able to do that with "stacks" is a pain. Similarly, palm fronds add 2% charge to any fire you have going. Want to add 30 percent charge to the fire? Guess what you've got to do.

The thing that ticked me off the most though was roasting meat in the fire. It tells you exactly how long it will take to cook, but there are only certain multiples of "waiting time" that can be used. You can't "Rest 30 minutes) for a piece of meat that takes 27 minutes to cook.

Crafting takes a certain amount of time too, and whether you can eat your roast meat is binary- it's pass/fail cooked or burnt.

So, say you want to sit down at the fire and work on crafting a new item- you get an "oops! That item took 45 minutes to craft and you sat there without putting it down while the delicious sizzling meat smell turns into a thick smoke then burns to a crisp.

That was the final straw for me for my 2 hours of play, which I mostly enjoyed a lot.

Just the thought of sitting there fiddling with some tools trying to make something while next to the fire and letting your food burn to a crisp because you couldn't stop partway for 2 minutes to take the meat off the heat is just ridiculous and led to more "number finicking" with "Can i craft this entire item or drill an entire hole in a coconut in time to not watch my meat burn to cinders" was too much.

Comments welcome if I'm wrong or missed something, I'd be happy to go back to this.

Edit: steam has asked me to revise my review as I have continued to play, I'd like to say that the cooking issue is still frustrating and that I still don't see a way to move stacks of items.

Edit 2, done on my own with no prompting from steam:

You can dive or spear fish while meat is cooking for almost the exact amount of right time, and also you can quickly right click to move stacks or else drag one and hold and it vacuums up others.
