Ancient Russian Life Simulator, developed and published by GosDev Production, is a deliberately absurd and meme-driven action-simulation hybrid that reimagines ancient Rus through the lens of exaggerated folklore, internet humor, and over-the-top fantasy warfare. Despite its title suggesting a grounded historical life simulator, the game quickly reveals itself as something entirely different—a parody experience that blends clicker mechanics, light combat progression, and satirical storytelling into a short but energetic package. Rather than striving for historical accuracy, it embraces outlandish scenarios and comedic reinterpretations of cultural mythology.

The central premise revolves around an imagined ancient war against invading reptilian enemies. Players step into the role of a heroic defender of Rus, battling hordes of lizard-like adversaries across various stylized locations. The narrative framing is intentionally tongue-in-cheek, presenting its events with exaggerated claims of historical authenticity while clearly leaning into parody. This comedic tone defines the entire experience, with dialogue and descriptions often designed to entertain through absurdity rather than deliver a serious historical account.

Gameplay focuses primarily on combat and incremental progression. Players engage in repeated battles against waves of enemies, earning experience and unlocking increasingly powerful abilities. The combat system is relatively simple, emphasizing rapid attacks and exaggerated special techniques inspired by playful reinterpretations of Slavic warrior culture. Abilities carry flamboyant names and dramatic animations, reinforcing the humorous tone. As players progress, they unlock new skills that enhance damage output and efficiency, creating a satisfying sense of escalation even if the core mechanics remain straightforward.

Structurally, the game incorporates light clicker-style elements. Combat encounters can feel rhythm-based and repetitive in design, encouraging players to optimize their abilities and maximize resource gain. This loop—fight, upgrade, repeat—forms the backbone of the experience. While it lacks the depth of traditional simulation titles or complex action games, the steady progression system provides enough motivation to continue unlocking new techniques and seeing increasingly chaotic battles unfold on screen.

Visually, Ancient Russian Life Simulator embraces a bold and colorful aesthetic that complements its satirical tone. Character designs, enemy models, and environmental backdrops are stylized rather than realistic. The art direction does not aim for high-fidelity historical detail but instead supports the exaggerated fantasy premise. Visual effects during combat amplify the sense of spectacle, especially when using special abilities that fill the screen with dramatic flourishes. The presentation aligns closely with the game’s comedic identity, prioritizing personality over realism.

The humor is central to its appeal. The game frequently breaks the fourth wall through playful descriptions and ironic statements about its supposed realism. It mocks traditional simulation tropes by presenting outlandish mechanics as historically accurate while clearly winking at the player. This self-aware approach distinguishes it from straightforward parody titles, as it leans fully into its absurd premise without attempting to disguise it as a serious product.

In terms of longevity, the game offers a relatively compact experience. Most players will likely complete its progression within a few hours, as the gameplay loop does not dramatically evolve over time. However, achievements and score-chasing elements provide additional incentives for completionists. The brevity works in its favor for players seeking a quick, entertaining diversion rather than a long-term investment.

Ultimately, Ancient Russian Life Simulator succeeds as a comedic action experience built around parody and accessible mechanics. It does not attempt to deliver deep historical simulation or intricate gameplay systems. Instead, it offers an energetic and humorous journey that thrives on exaggerated mythology, meme-inspired storytelling, and light progression-based combat. For players willing to embrace its absurd premise and focus on casual entertainment, it provides a short but memorable romp through a wildly reimagined version of ancient Rus.

Rating: 8/10

Ancient Russian Life Simulator, developed and published by GosDev Production, is a deliberately absurd and meme-driven action-simulation hybrid that reimagines ancient Rus through the lens of exaggerated folklore, internet humor, and over-the-top fantasy warfare. Despite its title suggesting a grounded historical life simulator, the game quickly reveals itself as something entirely different—a parody experience that blends clicker mechanics, light combat progression, and satirical storytelling into a short but energetic package. Rather than striving for historical accuracy, it embraces outlandish scenarios and comedic reinterpretations of cultural mythology.

The central premise revolves around an imagined ancient war against invading reptilian enemies. Players step into the role of a heroic defender of Rus, battling hordes of lizard-like adversaries across various stylized locations. The narrative framing is intentionally tongue-in-cheek, presenting its events with exaggerated claims of historical authenticity while clearly leaning into parody. This comedic tone defines the entire experience, with dialogue and descriptions often designed to entertain through absurdity rather than deliver a serious historical account.

Gameplay focuses primarily on combat and incremental progression. Players engage in repeated battles against waves of enemies, earning experience and unlocking increasingly powerful abilities. The combat system is relatively simple, emphasizing rapid attacks and exaggerated special techniques inspired by playful reinterpretations of Slavic warrior culture. Abilities carry flamboyant names and dramatic animations, reinforcing the humorous tone. As players progress, they unlock new skills that enhance damage output and efficiency, creating a satisfying sense of escalation even if the core mechanics remain straightforward.

Structurally, the game incorporates light clicker-style elements. Combat encounters can feel rhythm-based and repetitive in design, encouraging players to optimize their abilities and maximize resource gain. This loop—fight, upgrade, repeat—forms the backbone of the experience. While it lacks the depth of traditional simulation titles or complex action games, the steady progression system provides enough motivation to continue unlocking new techniques and seeing increasingly chaotic battles unfold on screen.

Visually, Ancient Russian Life Simulator embraces a bold and colorful aesthetic that complements its satirical tone. Character designs, enemy models, and environmental backdrops are stylized rather than realistic. The art direction does not aim for high-fidelity historical detail but instead supports the exaggerated fantasy premise. Visual effects during combat amplify the sense of spectacle, especially when using special abilities that fill the screen with dramatic flourishes. The presentation aligns closely with the game’s comedic identity, prioritizing personality over realism.

The humor is central to its appeal. The game frequently breaks the fourth wall through playful descriptions and ironic statements about its supposed realism. It mocks traditional simulation tropes by presenting outlandish mechanics as historically accurate while clearly winking at the player. This self-aware approach distinguishes it from straightforward parody titles, as it leans fully into its absurd premise without attempting to disguise it as a serious product.

In terms of longevity, the game offers a relatively compact experience. Most players will likely complete its progression within a few hours, as the gameplay loop does not dramatically evolve over time. However, achievements and score-chasing elements provide additional incentives for completionists. The brevity works in its favor for players seeking a quick, entertaining diversion rather than a long-term investment.

Ultimately, Ancient Russian Life Simulator succeeds as a comedic action experience built around parody and accessible mechanics. It does not attempt to deliver deep historical simulation or intricate gameplay systems. Instead, it offers an energetic and humorous journey that thrives on exaggerated mythology, meme-inspired storytelling, and light progression-based combat. For players willing to embrace its absurd premise and focus on casual entertainment, it provides a short but memorable romp through a wildly reimagined version of ancient Rus.

Rating: 8/10

I fired up Saelig expecting a quiet medieval life simulator.

What I found instead was one of the most honest economic and logistics simulations I’ve played in years.

On the surface, it’s simple: build a family, run businesses, manage workers, survive. But underneath, Saelig models something most games never even attempt—sustainment. Overhead matters. Property taxes matter. Wages matter. Supply chains matter. You cannot brute-force your way to success forever. If you expand too fast, the system will quietly eat you alive.

That’s rare. And it’s brilliant.

The game rewards specialization, logistics discipline, and long-term thinking. I eventually built a multi-town trade network anchored on mining, manufacturing, and export control. Flooding markets, watching prices collapse, hoarding strategic reserves, and pivoting production based on resource availability felt less like “playing a game” and more like managing a living economic organism.

What makes Saelig exceptional is that it doesn’t fake difficulty. It lets you succeed—and then forces you to sustain that success. The real enemy isn’t competition. It’s mismanagement.

If you enjoy systems, strategy, economics, or logistics, this game is outstanding.

I wrote a full essay on how Saelig captures real-world systems thinking here:
https://open.substack.com/pub/frankslaviniii/p/the-dude-in-the-toc?r=3hhs9t&utm_campaign=post&utm_medium=web&showWelcomeOnShare=true

The developer built something special. This isn’t just a medieval simulator.

It’s a systems simulator.

This game is a pearl and clearly meant for the "Labor of Love" award. The dev is tireless and the updates, content and depth keep growing. 
I have high expectations and would not mind paying for a DLC if the content were to exponentially grow.

i enjoy city builders, colony survival style games and this hits every single mark i have. i rarely give reviews, even when i like games but when i recently found out that this game is independently developed by not just a small team, but mostly (if not all) done by a single person, i'm floored. i love this game, the mechanics, the story you can build along the way. The continuous development and updates. the fact you can turn raids off if you want a more chill playthrough letting seasons and survival be the enemy. running your own business, playing as a vagrant stealing from others until you become an outlaw, just to turn your life around and eventually become a noble. is it perfect: no. is it in my top 5 games of the genre: yes.. is it number 1: yes (in my opinion). it might not have the same perspective and graphics of Bellwright or Medieval Dynasty, but it is much more flexible and immersive for my style play. oh and when my first character got robbed by some Jack-A and i turned around and stole his wife, became a guard and harassed him until he mysteriously died in a fire... this game will forever live in my heart

This game has been in early access for a long time now. And I've looked into it a few times over the years, because I'm a huge fan of The Guild 1 and 2. And with The Guild 3 being a huge let down, I was looking at Saelig for a long time, wishing it to become a game at the scale of the Guild, but without all the bugs. 

And boy, the wait was worth it. It still needs some time to cook, but the core gameplay loop already works. You can decide if you want to start a whole town from scrath or be a peasant in an already build one. So many possibilites. 

Also the dev is very very very very active on the forum and puts out updates on a regular basis. I really hope he brings it to the finish line.

The developer, Atorcoppe, enjoys continually working on his game and responding to his player base. I play on an ultra wide monitor and from my initial purchase of the game 2 weeks ago to now, he responded to some display issues and enhanced the functionality for people like me.

Medieval Sims / Medieval Kenshi? This is a feel-good game about medieval life - Almost like the 'Guilds' games, but with some added flare. Every NPC is trying to get ahead, some will die, some will get pregnant and have to take leave if they work for you, raiders might burn your business to the ground or kill a family member. -- Live life as a vagabond hunter in the woods? Business owner? Town watch? Get a job anywhere, and do basically anything you want. 

I will say that the gameplay loop isn't yet vast, but it's captivating and enjoyable. This game can be as hardcore or as relaxing as you want through the settings - Turn off raiders and just enjoy climbing the social ladder, expanding your family and growing the towns and villages surrounding you. 

I have been enjoying this game and hope that more people make the purchase to motivate the developer to continue to forge ahead and expand the gameplay and deepen the existing mechanics.

This is a game I could definitely get at least 50-100 hours out of, no problem. Very fun game that has more depth than it initially appears. The life simulation aspect is fun by itself, but the real joy comes in the micromanagement of businesses and optimization of production chains. Before you know it, you'll be a medieval monopolist. It's a lot of fun working your way up, the gameplay loop is very rewarding, and there's a fair amount of different options to pursue. There's several maps to try, and the developer is quite active and communicative even after working on it as long as they have.

Would definitely recommend if you enjoy games such as Kenshi, the Guild, Big Ambitions, Mount & Blade, etc. Easily worth the $15 price tag and especially if it is on sale.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Being an avid fan of the Gabriel Knight series and knowing where the Markus Ritter series has drawn its biggest inspiration from, Ghosts of the Past honours the GK series well with a good storyline and backstories which helps give the GK series a universe-like feel. The most impressive thing about this game is the screens where dialogue between characters are involved. The subtle movements adds another dimesion and quality of FMV games today. The gameplay is good for moderate adventure gamers and there's some well-inserted sometimes dry humour, which makes it fun, especially when stuck on something. Much care and consideration of locations, set decorations and camera work can be seen in this game as well. I love learning new languages, so it's great that this game allows you to play the game with audio in one language and subtitles in another language. Overall, this game is wunderbar and I can't wait to see what happens next!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
I played the game after the June 2024 update so my comments are based on this version.  I really enjoyed the game and would love to see another adventure for our two Shadow Hunters.  I love FMV games in general so I am forgiving of the genre.  However, I do think this is a good game and it is easy to see the love and care put into it.  

The story is an homage to the Gabriel Knight lore of the Shadow Hunters and has many nods to other classic adventure games scattered throughout.  There is a real game here with inventory, some puzzles and exploration / investigation besides the dialogue.  It was nice to have a task list to help keep on track.  The story kept my interest and the acting was good for the genre.  It was both serious and funny at times and the love story between Markus and Ludwig was sweet.  

I had 8 hours of play time which is also very solid for a FMV game.  There are both auto and manual saves.  It was quite the ambitious endeavor and I think the team succeeded in making a fun adventure game that should appeal to adventure fans as well as FMV ones.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Just finished it, took me about 6 hours. So the game is rather short, but a well-rounded FMV gaming experience. Designed with great attention to detail. The makers call it "queer cheesy trash" - so "queer" is true, "cheesy" is true, "trash" rather less so. Of course, the story is full of clichés, but it still manages to create believable characters whose dialogues are well-recorded by the speakers. Occasional overacting included. The puzzles are mostly simple, but not simplistic. Here and there I found the solution a little "random", but perhaps that changes if you read the text snippets more carefully... All in all, the game clearly plays in the premier league of FMV adventure games alongside modern classics such as "Late Shift" and "Contradiction", although it is much more of a traditional point & click than a quick-time click orgy, fortunately there are no QTEs in the gameplay. A clear recommendation!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
As a massive Gabriel Knight fan, I at first had a chuckle when I heard of this game. It's so obvious that it's a fan game. But I've also been missing those games for decades, so I knew I had to play it anyway. 

Well, turns out it's good on its own. Though not without its bugs and navigation issues, the game features  interesting characters, locations, and mechanics. It also distances itself from GK in a lot of ways, particularly with the antagonists. While in GK the 'shadows' can be any sort of supernatural beast, in Markus Ritter they are more like a species of body snatchers.

Overall, an enjoyable experience. Hopefully we see more.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Just finished playing Markus Ritter - Ghosts of the Past. What can I say? I binged this game. Absolutely love it and am actually a little sad it is over now. The story is capturing - with some tiny and very delightful elements of cringe and some great humour. If you have played the demo of this game, the full version is even better - the demo ended before the proper story really started, so I recommend, definitely give the game a go. I hope there'll be a third part to the Markus Ritter games. Couldn't recommend this game highly enough. It deserves a lot more attention.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Wow wow wow! That was absolutely amazing. I can honestly say that this was the best game I've played in years. The drama is non-stop, there is just the right amount of challenge and no puzzle outstays its welcome - as there is a task list on the phone. The musical score is again really strong and always apt, though no big standout this time like the main song in the first installment as well as that beautiful piano piece. The places to visit and explore are interesting and a couple of the houses are amazing. It's refreshing to have a bisexual main character with a strong romantic and emotional focus on his current partner. Ludwig looks like a cute pixie / elf. There is a lot of fun in this game too - I loved reading the quirky astrology section in the daily newspaper and there are some sudden, unexpected laugh out loud moments too. There are also sprinklings of 90's point and click game references throughout, not least an article in the newspaper about holidaying on Monkey Island. In terms of tech requirements, I did get a little lag in places, most notably the club/cafe (just a short section)  but it was no game-breaker and I played it on a very old desktop computer with an i3 processor. The FMV is really crisp and high resolution. The huge effort and work that has clearly gone into the production of this game is very evident. Anyone who loves 90's style FMV games (often regarded as the golden age of point and click) will also love this game as it's up there with the best of those while being for a modern audience with modern values. Just brilliant!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: This game was made with a lot of love and attention to detail. Lots of hidden nods and eastereggs that point to various point & click classics. It's amazing to see a game set in my home city. Just  started out and hope to dive deeper into the adventures of a new generation of Schattenjäger! :) We will catch the killer!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
After a very promising demo/prologue (The Lost Family), the Markus Ritter storyline now continues with the main game, Ghosts of the Past. A lovingly conceived, beautifully designed and executed tribute to Jane Jensen's iconic Gabriel Knight universe, the Markus Ritter games feature the eponymous character and his estranged cousin, Roberta - another honorable allusion to a full motion video (FMV) game pioneer and Jensen's early collaborator, Roberta Williams.

Playing along some of the themes and construction of the second instalment in the Gabriel Knight trilogy, The Beast Within (the only one in the saga using the FMV technology), Ghosts of the Past throws Markus and Roberta into the eternal battle between the forces of light and darkness, as Schattenjaegers (Shadow Hunters) with the hereditary superpower - and duty - to recognize and fight evil which appears in deceptively ordinary human forms. Like his legendary ancestor and predecessor, Markus has to face a fatal dilemma when he finally finds true love, but the Shadows have ominous plans for him and the future of his new relationship.

Despite the obvious and oblique references to the Gabriel Knight universe (with many clever and enjoyable Easter eggs), Markus Ritter: Ghosts of the Past is, in its own right, an exceptionally well-rounded Indie game with original elements, interesting quirky characters and a captivating story. The controls are the same as in The Lost Family demo, but have been considerably smoothed out. The scenery and soundtrack are gorgeous, and in most instances act as organic ingredients of the plot.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: What an improvement over the demo!  I enjoyed the demo thoroughly, but every part of Ghosts of the Past is improved, video quality, game mechanics, pacing, even the acting is better.  It's not a long game, but it's a solid edition to the Shattenjager legacy from the Gabriel Knight games.  If you like FMV games, this tour of dark occult Vienna is worth your time.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
I like this game, good old FMV stuff.
It was fun to play, even when it was not always clear what to do next, I had some unintentionally funny moments with Markus and Ro alonge the road.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
Ein wundervolles, liebevoll gemachtes indie Spiel, dass mir und meinen Freunden sehr viel Spaß bereitet hat. Natürlich sind die schauspielerischen Leistungen nicht die Kirsche auf der Sahne aber ich würde es auch gar nicht anders wollen. An manchen Stellen ist das Spiel doch recht unübersichtlich und man weiß nicht genau was man machen soll ohne in sein Questlog zu gucken. Der Murder Mystery Part in dem Ferienhaus hat richtig Spaß gemacht, davon gerne mehr! Freuen uns schon sehr auf den nächsten Teil!

PS: Ist halt kein Dark Souls.

PPS: Müsst ihr wissen.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
I very much enjoyed both demo's and looked forward to the full game. I am not far advanced into the game as yet but, having enjoyed Gabriel Knight, I of course like the story line , characters, humour, and atmosphere.  
It is also visually appealing.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
As a huge fan of FMV games, I jumped at the chance to become a Kickstarter backer for Ghosts of the Past after really enjoying The Lost Family. I wasn’t disappointed – this release has become one of my favourite results from a crowdfunding project.

While the storyline is quite serious, Ghosts of the Past has a dry sense of humour running throughout it which had us laughing at times. Adventure gamers will notice the Gabriel Knight influences and happily recognise the references to the classics. Unlike recent FMV games which seem to focus on choice and consequence, this plays more like a point-and-click. The puzzles aren’t particularly challenging but they’re enjoyable and don’t outstay their welcome.

Seeing how Markus’s character has grown between the first and second game was a treat. He starts off as someone who’s drifting along without direction, and he now has it after delving into his family’s history. I won’t say too much for fear of spoiling it, but the end of Ghosts of the Past sets him up to develop further and show a stronger side to his personality.

On that note, well done to the cast of actors! FMV games tend to be over-the-top and, although there are some appropriately campy moments here, everyone does an excellent job of making their characters believable. I wasn’t fond of Ro at the beginning, but after she drops her guard, you can see why she’s a great counterpart for Markus.

If there’s ever a Kickstarter campaign for a third Markus Ritter instalment, sign me up already.

Our playthrough can be watched here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3uD5pqZX-WI&list=PLUZJQ9yOnp3k8mDWZbB7MQb-apVggpn_v

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
Hat ein bisschen für mich gedauert, bis ich es an einem weiteren Summer-Sale schnappen konnte und ganz ehrlich.. besser spät als nie! Ich hatte schon vorher die kostenlose The Lost Family-Demo im letzten Jahr zu Ende gespielt und hab für dieses Spiel meine Erwartungen anticipated.

Trotz der vielen kleinen Macken, vorallem in Texten sowohl auf Deutsch und Englisch, hatte ich ein wahres Erlebnis mit der vollen Ausstattung der Markus Ritter-Geschichte mit Ghosts of The Past.

Wenn ein dritter Teil jemals in Planung kommen würde, werde ich es definitiv nachverfolgen!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
Markus Ritter - Ghosts of the Past is a captivating continuation of Markus's journey, delivering a compelling narrative filled with twists, turns, and moments of redemption. As a fan of the original game, I was thrilled to dive back into the world of Markus Ritter, and I'm pleased to say that this sequel did not disappoint.

One of the standout features of Ghosts of the Past is its gripping storyline. Set against the backdrop of Markus's quest to confront his demons and seek redemption for past mistakes, the narrative is filled with suspense, intrigue, and emotional depth. As Markus grapples with the ghosts of his past and struggles to forge a new path forward, I found myself deeply invested in his journey, rooting for him every step of the way.

But what truly sets Ghosts of the Past apart is its richly detailed world and immersive atmosphere. The game's hand-crafted environments are simply stunning, with lush landscapes, intricate architecture, and atmospheric lighting that bring the world to life in vivid detail. Whether I was exploring ancient ruins, navigating treacherous terrain, or encountering mysterious creatures, every moment felt like a journey into the unknown.

In addition to its captivating storyline and stunning visuals, Ghosts of the Past also features innovative gameplay mechanics that keep players engaged and entertained throughout. From challenging puzzles to intense combat sequences, the game offers a satisfying blend of gameplay elements that keep the experience feeling fresh and exciting.

But perhaps what I love most about Ghosts of the Past is its message of redemption and second chances. Through Markus's journey, I was reminded of the power of forgiveness, perseverance, and self-discovery in overcoming life's greatest challenges. It's a story that resonated with me on a deeply personal level, and one that I won't soon forget.

In conclusion, Markus Ritter - Ghosts of the Past is a must-play for fans of the original game and anyone looking for a gripping tale of redemption. With its compelling storyline, stunning visuals, and innovative gameplay, it's a journey that will stay with you long after the credits roll. Trust me, you won't want to miss out on this unforgettable adventure.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: I already had "Lost" but didn't quite get into it.  When I started "Ghosts" I understood and returned to finish "Lost" first.  FMV was actually a new approach for me.  Now I want more if the quality is as good as the Markus Ritter stories.  Conclusion:  More Markus Ritter, please.  Also I hope Markus will find a way to retrieve ... (I won't say.  Spoilers, you know.)

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
Für wen ist es:
- Für Fans von Gabriel Knight
- Für FMV-Fans
- Für Menschen, die mit einer queeren Beziehung im Spiel klarkommen oder dies klar wünschen

Für wen ist es nicht:
- Für homophobe Menschen
- Für jeden, der generell keine Beziehungen in Spielen möchte
- Für FMV-Hasser (offensichtlich)

Was ich mir gewünscht hätte...
...wäre, ohne den ganzen Beziehungskram spielen oder mir aussuchen zu können, wen ich daten möchte. Bei so einem Setting wäre ich Single geblieben. (Die Story hätte sich dennoch auf die gleiche Art entfalten können und wäre trotzdem glaubwürdig gewesen.)

Mein Problem ist nicht die queere Beziehungssituation, sondern dass ich mich mit Markus nicht so recht identifizieren kann. Es ist für mich schwer genug, mich in einen Menschen zu versetzen, der so unsicher ist. Das letzte, was ich da noch brauche, ist Unentschlossenheit in Herzensangelegenheiten (sowas hasse ich im realen Leben)!

Hat es mir gefallen? 
Ja, ich mochte das Spiel, auch wenn ich lieber als der Freund des Protagonisten gespielt hätte. 😉


Next part Please !

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Good FMV adventure, decent length, fun characters except the women, they all come across as man hating feminazis for some reason, why they gotta be so angry, the way they treat Markus is friggin' disgusting.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
You can feel the passion put into the game. Love the game and the cast!

Really hoping for a sequel!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: It is essentially Gabriel Knight fan-fiction, with loads of LGBTQ+ representation. What's not to like?

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: A point and click adventure that has a good story line. The puzzles are not too hard, and it's made for fans of Gabriel Knight and the old Sierra adventure games. An earnest tribute to the classics.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
Little budget, a lot of heart. Tribute to the classics FMV, pleasant and fun which, aware of its limitations, manages to bring the player back to the atmosphere of the classics that inspires it.
You will love it and want more

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: A fun FMV game with beautiful locations. Very interested to see where the story goes next.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
Aside from this one and Flimmersoft's first title "Markus Ritter: The Lost Family", I actually have not played other games of this genre - yet. With their first game they caught my attention and with this one they got me on boat. They've sucessfully managed to foster interest for this type of games in me. Although I can't say - for my own lack of experience with these kinda games - where this one truly ranks when directly compared to other names on the playing field, I still very much enjoyed spending time with it. I'd still give them only 0/100 points possible, for they dared teasing me with a food recipe while I was hungry. :(

I'm definitely gonna check out the games that inspired the people over at Flimmersoft to create this, if only to make the wait for their next game feel shorter.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
Edit: ok FINE. I keep on thinking about this game and it obviously made me feel something so I am going to change my review to a thumbs up. The ending still sucks, though. :(

I wish I could give this a thumbs up. I really enjoyed it; it's just the kind of thing that I'm into - FMV, campy, doesn't take itself too seriously, unexpected things that make you laugh... 

Unfortunately, the ending was just too INFURIATING for me to give it a good review. It's not the gameplay or the characters - although I don't know if I've ever seen a game with a bigger couple of bumblebunnies for heroes - both of which were fun and disarming. The ending was just a complete leveler, even though I did finally start to see it coming at one point. 

If an alternate ending was added, I could give it a thumbs up. I swore there was going to be one even - but look as I might, I couldn't find any evidence of one.  :(((((((((((

Like his legendary ancestor and predecessor, Markus has to face a fatal dilemma when he finally finds true love, but the Shadows have ominous plans for him and the future of his new relationship.

Despite the obvious and oblique references to the Gabriel Knight universe (with many clever and enjoyable Easter eggs), Markus Ritter: Ghosts of the Past is, in its own right, an exceptionally well-rounded Indie game with original elements, interesting quirky characters and a captivating story. The controls are the same as in The Lost Family demo, but have been considerably smoothed out. The scenery and soundtrack are gorgeous, and in most instances act as organic ingredients of the plot.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
As a huge fan of FMV games, I jumped at the chance to become a Kickstarter backer for Ghosts of the Past after really enjoying The Lost Family. I wasn’t disappointed – this release has become one of my favourite results from a crowdfunding project.

While the storyline is quite serious, Ghosts of the Past has a dry sense of humour running throughout it which had us laughing at times. Adventure gamers will notice the Gabriel Knight influences and happily recognise the references to the classics. Unlike recent FMV games which seem to focus on choice and consequence, this plays more like a point-and-click. The puzzles aren’t particularly challenging but they’re enjoyable and don’t outstay their welcome.

Seeing how Markus’s character has grown between the first and second game was a treat. He starts off as someone who’s drifting along without direction, and he now has it after delving into his family’s history. I won’t say too much for fear of spoiling it, but the end of Ghosts of the Past sets him up to develop further and show a stronger side to his personality.

On that note, well done to the cast of actors! FMV games tend to be over-the-top and, although there are some appropriately campy moments here, everyone does an excellent job of making their characters believable. I wasn’t fond of Ro at the beginning, but after she drops her guard, you can see why she’s a great counterpart for Markus.

If there’s ever a Kickstarter campaign for a third Markus Ritter instalment, sign me up already.

Our playthrough can be watched here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3uD5pqZX-WI&list=PLUZJQ9yOnp3k8mDWZbB7MQb-apVggpn_v

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
Markus Ritter - Ghosts of the Past is a captivating continuation of Markus's journey, delivering a compelling narrative filled with twists, turns, and moments of redemption. As a fan of the original game, I was thrilled to dive back into the world of Markus Ritter, and I'm pleased to say that this sequel did not disappoint.

One of the standout features of Ghosts of the Past is its gripping storyline. Set against the backdrop of Markus's quest to confront his demons and seek redemption for past mistakes, the narrative is filled with suspense, intrigue, and emotional depth. As Markus grapples with the ghosts of his past and struggles to forge a new path forward, I found myself deeply invested in his journey, rooting for him every step of the way.

But what truly sets Ghosts of the Past apart is its richly detailed world and immersive atmosphere. The game's hand-crafted environments are simply stunning, with lush landscapes, intricate architecture, and atmospheric lighting that bring the world to life in vivid detail. Whether I was exploring ancient ruins, navigating treacherous terrain, or encountering mysterious creatures, every moment felt like a journey into the unknown.

In addition to its captivating storyline and stunning visuals, Ghosts of the Past also features innovative gameplay mechanics that keep players engaged and entertained throughout. From challenging puzzles to intense combat sequences, the game offers a satisfying blend of gameplay elements that keep the experience feeling fresh and exciting.

But perhaps what I love most about Ghosts of the Past is its message of redemption and second chances. Through Markus's journey, I was reminded of the power of forgiveness, perseverance, and self-discovery in overcoming life's greatest challenges. It's a story that resonated with me on a deeply personal level, and one that I won't soon forget.

In conclusion, Markus Ritter - Ghosts of the Past is a must-play for fans of the original game and anyone looking for a gripping tale of redemption. With its compelling storyline, stunning visuals, and innovative gameplay, it's a journey that will stay with you long after the credits roll. Trust me, you won't want to miss out on this unforgettable adventure.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Being an avid fan of the Gabriel Knight series and knowing where the Markus Ritter series has drawn its biggest inspiration from, Ghosts of the Past honours the GK series well with a good storyline and backstories which helps give the GK series a universe-like feel. The most impressive thing about this game is the screens where dialogue between characters are involved. The subtle movements adds another dimesion and quality of FMV games today. The gameplay is good for moderate adventure gamers and there's some well-inserted sometimes dry humour, which makes it fun, especially when stuck on something. Much care and consideration of locations, set decorations and camera work can be seen in this game as well. I love learning new languages, so it's great that this game allows you to play the game with audio in one language and subtitles in another language. Overall, this game is wunderbar and I can't wait to see what happens next!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
I played the game after the June 2024 update so my comments are based on this version.  I really enjoyed the game and would love to see another adventure for our two Shadow Hunters.  I love FMV games in general so I am forgiving of the genre.  However, I do think this is a good game and it is easy to see the love and care put into it.  

The story is an homage to the Gabriel Knight lore of the Shadow Hunters and has many nods to other classic adventure games scattered throughout.  There is a real game here with inventory, some puzzles and exploration / investigation besides the dialogue.  It was nice to have a task list to help keep on track.  The story kept my interest and the acting was good for the genre.  It was both serious and funny at times and the love story between Markus and Ludwig was sweet.  

I had 8 hours of play time which is also very solid for a FMV game.  There are both auto and manual saves.  It was quite the ambitious endeavor and I think the team succeeded in making a fun adventure game that should appeal to adventure fans as well as FMV ones.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Just finished it, took me about 6 hours. So the game is rather short, but a well-rounded FMV gaming experience. Designed with great attention to detail. The makers call it "queer cheesy trash" - so "queer" is true, "cheesy" is true, "trash" rather less so. Of course, the story is full of clichés, but it still manages to create believable characters whose dialogues are well-recorded by the speakers. Occasional overacting included. The puzzles are mostly simple, but not simplistic. Here and there I found the solution a little "random", but perhaps that changes if you read the text snippets more carefully... All in all, the game clearly plays in the premier league of FMV adventure games alongside modern classics such as "Late Shift" and "Contradiction", although it is much more of a traditional point & click than a quick-time click orgy, fortunately there are no QTEs in the gameplay. A clear recommendation!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
As a massive Gabriel Knight fan, I at first had a chuckle when I heard of this game. It's so obvious that it's a fan game. But I've also been missing those games for decades, so I knew I had to play it anyway. 

Well, turns out it's good on its own. Though not without its bugs and navigation issues, the game features  interesting characters, locations, and mechanics. It also distances itself from GK in a lot of ways, particularly with the antagonists. While in GK the 'shadows' can be any sort of supernatural beast, in Markus Ritter they are more like a species of body snatchers.

Overall, an enjoyable experience. Hopefully we see more.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Just finished playing Markus Ritter - Ghosts of the Past. What can I say? I binged this game. Absolutely love it and am actually a little sad it is over now. The story is capturing - with some tiny and very delightful elements of cringe and some great humour. If you have played the demo of this game, the full version is even better - the demo ended before the proper story really started, so I recommend, definitely give the game a go. I hope there'll be a third part to the Markus Ritter games. Couldn't recommend this game highly enough. It deserves a lot more attention.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Wow wow wow! That was absolutely amazing. I can honestly say that this was the best game I've played in years. The drama is non-stop, there is just the right amount of challenge and no puzzle outstays its welcome - as there is a task list on the phone. The musical score is again really strong and always apt, though no big standout this time like the main song in the first installment as well as that beautiful piano piece. The places to visit and explore are interesting and a couple of the houses are amazing. It's refreshing to have a bisexual main character with a strong romantic and emotional focus on his current partner. Ludwig looks like a cute pixie / elf. There is a lot of fun in this game too - I loved reading the quirky astrology section in the daily newspaper and there are some sudden, unexpected laugh out loud moments too. There are also sprinklings of 90's point and click game references throughout, not least an article in the newspaper about holidaying on Monkey Island. In terms of tech requirements, I did get a little lag in places, most notably the club/cafe (just a short section)  but it was no game-breaker and I played it on a very old desktop computer with an i3 processor. The FMV is really crisp and high resolution. The huge effort and work that has clearly gone into the production of this game is very evident. Anyone who loves 90's style FMV games (often regarded as the golden age of point and click) will also love this game as it's up there with the best of those while being for a modern audience with modern values. Just brilliant!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: This game was made with a lot of love and attention to detail. Lots of hidden nods and eastereggs that point to various point & click classics. It's amazing to see a game set in my home city. Just  started out and hope to dive deeper into the adventures of a new generation of Schattenjäger! :) We will catch the killer!

review_text:
As a huge fan of FMV games, I jumped at the chance to become a Kickstarter backer for Ghosts of the Past after really enjoying The Lost Family. I wasn’t disappointed – this release has become one of my favourite results from a crowdfunding project.

While the storyline is quite serious, Ghosts of the Past has a dry sense of humour running throughout it which had us laughing at times. Adventure gamers will notice the Gabriel Knight influences and happily recognise the references to the classics. Unlike recent FMV games which seem to focus on choice and consequence, this plays more like a point-and-click. The puzzles aren’t particularly challenging but they’re enjoyable and don’t outstay their welcome.

Seeing how Markus’s character has grown between the first and second game was a treat. He starts off as someone who’s drifting along without direction, and he now has it after delving into his family’s history. I won’t say too much for fear of spoiling it, but the end of Ghosts of the Past sets him up to develop further and show a stronger side to his personality.

On that note, well done to the cast of actors! FMV games tend to be over-the-top and, although there are some appropriately campy moments here, everyone does an excellent job of making their characters believable. I wasn’t fond of Ro at the beginning, but after she drops her guard, you can see why she’s a great counterpart for Markus.

A really fun little horror game, I quite enjoyed it.
I do think the school could have been decorated better, specifically upstairs, as it felt a bit bland.

I really enjoyed this little horror game, it doesn't rely on jumpscares every few seconds that you become numb too. It has a few here and there but instead uses eerie ambiance to put you out of your element. There is a decent storyline and who could forget Officer Jeff? Sometimes the tasks are a little hard to achieve, you have to scrub tables and every pixel has to be clean. I don't think I ever found all of the trash cans either. while not technically abandoned, the game hasn't received any meaningful content updates in 3 years other than an unfinished beta that overhauls landscape generation and makes multiplayer more playable, and the developer doesn't communicate ever so we don't really know what to expect.

despite this i find myself coming back to the game all the time to just play around with the mechanics for a few hours. although it's mostly based around driving and modifying cars (which itself is quite good), there are just a lot of mechanics to play around with and i keep finding myself coming back to it just to screw around for an hour or two and i keep discovering fun quirks that make me laugh.

above anything else i wish the multiplayer was more playable, even in the beta it's still quite buggy but much closer to being playable, and even if its current state just being able to screw around in the game with a car full of people is really fun, even if trying to smoke a cigarette might cause the physics to glitch out and crash the car.

i also wish the game as more difficult, possibly even with an AI director of sorts to force creativity, because the funnest moments of the game are when your car runs out of gas or gets destroyed and you have to scrounge and put together a half-baked ghetto solution, really makes you feel like that guy who escaped the motorcycle by turning his citroën 2cv into a motorcycle.

It’s hard to say who has given up more on this potential gem, the developer or the community. Both seem stuck in a shared feedback loop where frustration, silence, and oddly passionate arguments orbit a game that is very close to being great and has been for a long time.

What makes this sting is how near the finish line it feels. If the main branch and the beta branch were ever properly merged, taking the best ideas from both, the game would probably feel nearly complete. Instead, development has the energy of someone rearranging furniture instead of finishing the house.

This really should have been built more modularly. Sparse and minimal can sell desolation, but repetition without variation just sells “ran out of time.” You can’t rely on empty space alone to do worldbuilding. Desolate still needs texture.

At this point, The Long Drive feels like it wants to be a physics sandbox first and a “game” second, closer to Garry’s Mod than survival. The character controller is genuinely clever and object interaction feels great once you learn it. It also very clearly wants to be VR, which makes sense because half the time you’re already role-playing as a confused human forklift. There is a very basic VR alpha player controller but it's not really useable.

Unfortunately, it lacks the modding support that would let the community carry it the rest of the way, which is a shame because that’s exactly the kind of game this is.

The physics are the real highlight. They’re just lax enough. Many physics games fall apart because emergent failure states are too punishing or too stupid. This one quietly avoids that. The no-inventory slots, hands-on interaction system works, feels precise, and rewards learning it instead of fighting it.

If you find this on sale, it’s absolutely worth it. If you enjoy lovable, janky games with unrealized potential, this one qualifies. It’s easy to lose time in once you find a loop that gets you through the mileage goal. There are maps and modes, but what it really needs isn’t quests. It needs more mechanics and emergent goals that exploit the physics systems already here. Goat Simulator figured this out years ago.

I still keep this installed. My Summer Car is hardcore in a way that feels personal, it and Mon Bazou doesn’t let you shoot anything. The Long Drive occupies a very specific niche that nothing else quite fills (unless it attempts to fully rip the formula which I cannot afford to try right now), even while actively refusing to become more than it is.

Buy it if you like honestly crap games with real potential.
Pass if you want regular updates.
Pass if you like clear communication.
Pass if you expect the developer and the community to exist in the same reality.

I recommend it but cannot write a more positive review than that at this time.

Great game it has some technical problems and some gameplay elements i wish would be inside without 3rd party program need. 

RPG and Visual novel combined and that combo is just good. 
My 1st playthrough was on easy (Story mode) I somehow gotten up to french campaign where i had to swim naked and that was a point where i died (don't remember which part directly). 
I was an idiot who wasted a lot of time because i didn't know what to do. Biggest problem i didn't know you could train your stats by clicking on them so instead i played on a freaking lute just to pass time. 

I was tilted by it, why the f can't i train my stats. What did i do? Delete the game? Nope, let's go with HWR difficulty where you can manually create your character and with each ending or DEATHS increase your odds the next time. 
It took me around 30-35 hours i think to finally to get to a point where i had max stats and could steamroll HWR difficulty (Axe is a good choice btw).

Visuals look good, some bugs with models (shields). 

Story branching is interesting and the story itself? Not perfect but damn good if i may say so. 
There is even an option to say f that all i'm gonna be a craftsman. (Though developer made an annoying achievement i.e. Nobody (you are a nobody). I tend to think when you have an option and refuse to play along with others and just choose the ordinary life it something akin to Diogenes refusing Alexanders offer. 
You are not a nobody you are an content and independent person who is by far more amazing than a lot of prominent figures. If by not playing games other people set up for you forces you to become a nobody, then so be it but it was disappointing.

As for cons: 
The massive one HWR difficulty has autosave option ONLY WHEN YOU LEAVE NOT WHEN YOU CRASH. 
Imagine you are near the end of the game (can take roughly 1 or 2 hours depending) and bam crash. You come back and it didn't save, you have to start a new game so 1-2 hours wasted. 

Next point, some if not all stats cannot be trained beyond a certain point (i think it's 60 for stats and 35 for weapon mastery). And manual creation is capped at 25 for stats and 10 for weapons. 
The worst? Game still allows you to click on weapons mastery beyond 35 but you will likely (in my case 100%) waste time and get tired (your character). The same cannot be said for stats, they are coded to stop you from clicking and even have a message. 

Last con is just my preference, now that i have an uber profile i want to go back to story mode with my profile and dominate. Or at least have an option to have more time. HWR forces you to be economic on time and presses you for hard decisions, now i want to enjoy since i achieved that but only can do so in HWR difficulty which once again forces you into situation.

My 1st playthrough was on easy (Story mode) I somehow gotten up to french campaign where i had to swim naked and that was a point where i died (don't remember which part directly). 
I was an idiot who wasted a lot of time because i didn't know what to do. Biggest problem i didn't know you could train your stats by clicking on them so instead i played on a freaking lute just to pass time. 

I was tilted by it, why the f can't i train my stats. What did i do? Delete the game? Nope, let's go with HWR difficulty where you can manually create your character and with each ending or DEATHS increase your odds the next time. 
It took me around 30-35 hours i think to finally to get to a point where i had max stats and could steamroll HWR difficulty (Axe is a good choice btw).

Visuals look good, some bugs with models (shields). 

Story branching is interesting and the story itself? Not perfect but damn good if i may say so. 
There is even an option to say f that all i'm gonna be a craftsman. (Though developer made an annoying achievement i.e. Nobody (you are a nobody). I tend to think when you have an option and refuse to play along with others and just choose the ordinary life it something akin to Diogenes refusing Alexanders offer. 
You are not a nobody you are an content and independent person who is by far more amazing than a lot of prominent figures. If by not playing games other people set up for you forces you to become a nobody, then so be it but it was disappointing.

As for cons: 
The massive one HWR difficulty has autosave option ONLY WHEN YOU LEAVE NOT WHEN YOU CRASH. 
Imagine you are near the end of the game (can take roughly 1 or 2 hours depending) and bam crash. You come back and it didn't save, you have to start a new game so 1-2 hours wasted. 

Next point, some if not all stats cannot be trained beyond a certain point (i think it's 60 for stats and 35 for weapon mastery). And manual creation is capped at 25 for stats and 10 for weapons. 
The worst? Game still allows you to click on weapons mastery beyond 35 but you will likely (in my case 100%) waste time and get tired (your character). The same cannot be said for stats, they are coded to stop you from clicking and even have a message. 

Last con is just my preference, now that i have an uber profile i want to go back to story mode with my profile and dominate. Or at least have an option to have more time. HWR forces you to be economic on time and presses you for hard decisions, now i want to enjoy since i achieved that but only can do so in HWR difficulty which once again forces you into situation.

Overall, the core content is pretty good, but everything else could be substantially improved. I recommend to anyone who appreaciated A Legionnary's Life. I had been waiting for a similar game for years and just never heard of Heads Will Roll.

game_name: Crowalt: Traces of the Lost Colony
review_text:
Crowalt: Traces of the Lost Colony, developed by Madcraft Studios and published by GrabTheGames, is a point-and-click adventure game that invites players into a richly detailed world filled with mystery, history, and intrigue. Released on January 6, 2022, the game has garnered a "Very Positive" rating on Steam, reflecting its appeal to fans of narrative-driven adventures.

Set in 1737, the story follows Hugh Radcliff, a young adventurer determined to uncover the fate of the Lost Colony of Roanoke, which vanished in 1587, leaving behind only the word "Crowalt" carved into a tree. The game's narrative weaves historical elements with fictional twists, creating a compelling backdrop for the player's journey. As Hugh explores the island of Crowalt, he encounters a diverse cast of characters, including pirates, natives, and settlers, each with their own stories and secrets.

The gameplay centers around traditional point-and-click mechanics, where players collect, combine, and use items to solve puzzles and advance the story. Hugh's journal serves as a crucial tool, updating with sketches and notes that provide hints and context for the unfolding events. The game also introduces moral choices, allowing players to decide whether Hugh speaks the truth or resorts to deception, adding depth to character interactions and influencing the narrative's progression.

Visually, Crowalt employs a modern pixel art style that captures the essence of classic adventure games while offering a fresh aesthetic. The environments are richly detailed, from the lush forests to the rustic settlements, immersing players in the game's world. Character animations are fluid, and the overall design reflects a careful attention to detail that enhances the storytelling experience.

The game's soundtrack complements its setting, featuring atmospheric music that underscores the mystery and adventure at the heart of the story. The sound design effectively immerses players, with ambient sounds that bring the island's environment to life.

Critically, Crowalt has been praised for its engaging narrative, well-crafted puzzles, and charming art style. However, some players have noted that the pacing can be slow at times, and certain puzzles may feel repetitive. Despite these minor critiques, the game's overall design and storytelling have been well-received, making it a worthwhile experience for fans of the genre.

In conclusion, Crowalt: Traces of the Lost Colony offers a captivating adventure that blends historical intrigue with classic gameplay mechanics. Its engaging story, immersive world, and thoughtful design make it a standout title for those seeking a rich, narrative-driven experience. Whether you're a long-time fan of point-and-click adventures or new to the genre, Crowalt provides a memorable journey into the heart of one of history's greatest mysteries.

Rating: 7/10

As Hugh explores the island of Crowalt, he encounters a diverse cast of characters, including pirates, natives, and settlers, each with their own stories and secrets.

The gameplay centers around traditional point-and-click mechanics, where players collect, combine, and use items to solve puzzles and advance the story. Hugh's journal serves as a crucial tool, updating with sketches and notes that provide hints and context for the unfolding events. The game also introduces moral choices, allowing players to decide whether Hugh speaks the truth or resorts to deception, adding depth to character interactions and influencing the narrative's progression.

Visually, Crowalt employs a modern pixel art style that captures the essence of classic adventure games while offering a fresh aesthetic. The environments are richly detailed, from the lush forests to the rustic settlements, immersing players in the game's world. Character animations are fluid, and the overall design reflects a careful attention to detail that enhances the storytelling experience.

The game's soundtrack complements its setting, featuring atmospheric music that underscores the mystery and adventure at the heart of the story. The sound design effectively immerses players, with ambient sounds that bring the island's environment to life.

Critically, Crowalt has been praised for its engaging narrative, well-crafted puzzles, and charming art style. However, some players have noted that the pacing can be slow at times, and certain puzzles may feel repetitive. Despite these minor critiques, the game's overall design and storytelling have been well-received, making it a worthwhile experience for fans of the genre.

In conclusion, Crowalt: Traces of the Lost Colony offers a captivating adventure that blends historical intrigue with classic gameplay mechanics. Its engaging story, immersive world, and thoughtful design make it a standout title for those seeking a rich, narrative-driven experience. Whether you're a long-time fan of point-and-click adventures or new to the genre, Crowalt provides a memorable journey into the heart of one of history's greatest mysteries.

Hell Division is a gritty third-person action game developed by Quacky Games and published by Whale Rock Games that centers its entire experience around the fantasy of piloting a high-tech combat drone in a dystopian, corporate-controlled future. Rather than casting the player as a traditional soldier or hero, the game places you inside a machine—an autonomous weapon designed to operate where humans cannot—immediately setting a colder, more mechanical tone that aligns well with its cyberpunk influences. From the outset, Hell Division positions itself as a focused combat experience, prioritizing tension, positioning, and survival over cinematic storytelling or spectacle.

The setting frames a near-future world dominated by ruthless megacorporations locked in a shadow war for technological supremacy. You are deployed as a single advanced drone unit sent into hostile zones to disrupt enemy operations and eliminate rival machines. The narrative is intentionally minimal, serving more as contextual flavor than a driving force, but it succeeds in establishing a bleak atmosphere where corporations treat warfare as a calculated business expense. This stripped-back storytelling places emphasis on the player’s role as a disposable asset, reinforcing the idea that you are just one unit in a much larger, uncaring system.

Gameplay revolves around third-person drone combat across a series of contained mission areas. You maneuver freely through urban environments, industrial complexes, and neon-lit cyberpunk zones while engaging waves of enemy drones that vary in size, behavior, and threat level. Combat demands constant movement; hovering in one place for too long is often a death sentence. Dodging incoming fire, managing distance, and prioritizing targets are critical, as enemy swarms can overwhelm you quickly if approached recklessly. The game leans more toward tactical survival than pure arcade shooting, rewarding cautious positioning and situational awareness.

Enemy encounters escalate steadily as missions progress, introducing tougher units and denser formations that test both reflexes and patience. Some adversaries pressure you with aggressive pursuit, while others attack from range, forcing you to adapt on the fly. This layered enemy design gives combat a sense of tension, as engagements often feel like controlled chaos rather than straightforward shootouts. Success usually comes from reading the battlefield and exploiting brief openings rather than overpowering enemies outright.

Visually, Hell Division commits fully to its cyberpunk aesthetic. Neon highlights, dark cityscapes, and metallic industrial structures dominate the environments, creating a mood that feels hostile and oppressive. While the game is clearly built on a modest budget, its art direction compensates by maintaining a consistent visual identity. The environments may not be highly detailed, but they effectively communicate the world’s tone: cold, corporate, and mechanically brutal. Lighting plays a key role, often guiding the player’s eye while adding atmosphere to otherwise stark locations.

Sound design supports this mood with mechanical hums, weapon fire, and electronic effects that reinforce the feeling of piloting a machine rather than inhabiting a human body. Audio cues are functional, helping players track threats during intense encounters, though the overall soundscape is more utilitarian than cinematic. Music, when present, underscores tension rather than driving emotion, aligning with the game’s restrained approach to presentation.

One of the more divisive aspects of Hell Division is its control and interface design. Piloting a drone feels intentionally weighty and sometimes awkward, which can add to immersion but may also frustrate players expecting fluid, arcade-style responsiveness. The learning curve is noticeable, particularly early on, as players acclimate to movement, aiming, and spatial awareness in three-dimensional combat spaces. Once mastered, the controls can feel deliberate and precise, but reaching that point requires patience and tolerance for initial friction.

Progression in Hell Division is straightforward, focusing on completing missions rather than extensive customization or RPG-style upgrades. This simplicity keeps the experience focused but also limits long-term variety. Without deep progression systems or branching paths, replayability depends largely on enjoyment of the core combat loop. For some players, this focus is a strength, stripping away distractions and letting the gameplay stand on its own. For others, it may feel like missed potential in a genre that often thrives on customization and growth.

Ultimately, Hell Division is a game defined by its concept and atmosphere more than its polish. It delivers a distinct take on third-person combat by placing players inside a drone rather than a human avatar, emphasizing isolation, vulnerability, and mechanical precision. Its cyberpunk world and tactical combat can be genuinely engaging for players willing to invest time into mastering its systems, even if its rough edges and limited scope hold it back from broader appeal. For those drawn to niche action experiences, dystopian sci-fi settings, and methodical combat over spectacle, Hell Division offers a tense and memorable, if imperfect, journey into corporate warfare from the cold perspective of a machine.

Been following I-Novae Studios for well over almost 20 years years now since 2006 and 2007 when their Combat Prototype trailer was released and playable. Infinity Battlescape is seriously refined and much larger scope and must say it has come a seriously long way in 20 years. Game is really fun, not like your typical space space games today. Not on a mass scale as Elite Dangerous, or Star Citizen. But that of a single massive star system with proper massive battle mechanics and MASSIVE fleet brawls. 

Gameplay is still being refined year by year, though I am somewhat saddened at how long it is taking to develop and the lack of players. I was part of the initial EA release on steam which before that was a Beta and Alpha tester to some extent. The game has ok initial player numbers, but seriously wish it was more. This game has so much massive fleet battle armada vibes, I'm saddened to see that not many people have actually come across this and stuck with it. On average we are looking at a weekly high of 8 people. This is not very impressive. But what is impressive is the massive fights still to be had with such a low number of people playing. I just wish there was a bigger community, to organize fleet actions together and make a bigger difference in the overall battles. 

Overall graphics are top notch, not something you would find in your typical EA space game. They continue to be refined as well as the years go by. I do wish the community was much larger, and less worried about the price I see constant complaints about. Any true space sim fan see's that $34.99 for an endless war in massive space fleet battles is totally worth it. I mean, I see absolutely no other games, to this scale of combat, not even Star Citizen where you can seamlessly go from space, to atmosphere and have clear objectives to do for all size ships with hundreds of ships nearby all shooting at each other and still maintain a decent frame rate. 

Hoping for more ship designs, and types in the distant future as all the ship designs are shared across both factions outside of livery. But this game is a serious mistake to not have in your library and continue to monitory it as it develops.

Do I recommend this game, hell yeah! Hits that right niche of starting off as a small interceptor all the way to carrier command being escorted by dozens of ships providing as a spawn location for those interceptors, all of which works pretty seamlessly with absolutely no loading screens. Has a little bit of a learning curve, but once you understand the mechanics of the flight systems and game mechanics. You will easily lose your self in the endless war that is Infinity Battlescape.

the game is surprisingly good for how small its size is (less then 4 gigabytes) although it has a small player population the bots make that a lot less noticeable not to mention the more players there is online the harder it would be for new people who like to play alone (unless u play smart and try to stick closer to allied AIs and support them and use them as meat shields). though on the days there is more then 7 players i noticed they don't tend to target other players to heavily so long as they aren't a major threat/target to the whole battlefield (playing with a spinal cruiser or carrier) or you didn't kill them once, so i find it pretty fun and i don't regret buying it
Really solid game and it sucks the community is so small and multiplayer seems dead but even in singleplayer I had a really good time playing this game
The way a space fight should be.

Very epic. I would still higly recommend to do the tutorials first because some important HUD elements and controls will be explained that i would not have been aware about otherwise. If done, prepare for very intense cinematic battles. Multiplayer lacks people unfortunately, but it is very very playable and awesome in singleplayer mode as well.

Awesome space combat game, probably one of the best out there for very large fleet battles. Ignore the people saying it's a dead multiplayer game, because it is completely viable in single player as well. 

Very fun game, recommend supporting this small dev team.

Been following I-Novae Studios for well over almost 20 years years now since 2006 and 2007 when their Combat Prototype trailer was released and playable. Infinity Battlescape is seriously refined and much larger scope and must say it has come a seriously long way in 20 years. Game is really fun, not like your typical space space games today. Not on a mass scale as Elite Dangerous, or Star Citizen. But that of a single massive star system with proper massive battle mechanics and MASSIVE fleet brawls. 

Gameplay is still being refined year by year, though I am somewhat saddened at how long it is taking to develop and the lack of players. I was part of the initial EA release on steam which before that was a Beta and Alpha tester to some extent. The game has ok initial player numbers, but seriously wish it was more. This game has so much massive fleet battle armada vibes, I'm saddened to see that not many people have actually come across this and stuck with it. On average we are looking at a weekly high of 8 people. This is not very impressive. But what is impressive is the massive fights still to be had with such a low number of people playing. I just wish there was a bigger community, to organize fleet actions together and make a bigger difference in the overall battles. 

Overall graphics are top notch, not something you would find in your typical EA space game. They continue to be refined as well as the years go by. I do wish the community was much larger, and less worried about the price I see constant complaints about. Any true space sim fan see's that $34.99 for an endless war in massive space fleet battles is totally worth it. I mean, I see absolutely no other games, to this scale of combat, not even Star Citizen where you can seamlessly go from space, to atmosphere and have clear objectives to do for all size ships with hundreds of ships nearby all shooting at each other and still maintain a decent frame rate. 

Hoping for more ship designs, and types in the distant future as all the ship designs are shared across both factions outside of livery. But this game is a serious mistake to not have in your library and continue to monitory it as it develops.

Do I recommend this game, hell yeah! Hits that right niche of starting off as a small interceptor all the way to carrier command being escorted by dozens of ships providing as a spawn location for those interceptors, all of which works pretty seamlessly with absolutely no loading screens. Has a little bit of a learning curve, but once you understand the mechanics of the flight systems and game mechanics. You will easily lose your self in the endless war that is Infinity Battlescape.

I bought it for the new planet classes and to support the dev. The MTH system is pretty opaque, but the player CAN exert influence on the various trade houses if they are willing to invest a steady amount of cards/political power. Said influence can also allow the player to eventually destroy, diminish, build up and split those shipping companies, allowing you to achieve nominally indirect control over the seas. But it's hard to get into that system because it's very much unlike the base game. Still, I don't regret buying it, and I recommend it for everyone who enjoys the base game.
