Final Upgrade, developed and published by TaosX, is a sprawling automation and space-strategy sandbox that imagines the galaxy not as a battlefield or a frontier of exploration, but as a massive industrial canvas waiting to be systematized. It takes the core appeal of factory-building games—the joy of turning raw matter into complex, self-sustaining production webs—and expands it outward until production chains span star systems, planets, and entire fleets of player-designed ships. Instead of embodying a conquering empire or a wandering explorer, the player becomes an architect of interconnected processes, building a mechanized civilization whose strength comes not from firepower, but from logistics, efficiency, and relentless technological momentum. It is a game that asks players to think like an engineer rather than a commander, designing not just individual machines, but the invisible flow between them.

The early game introduces this vision through modest beginnings. A handful of vessels mine asteroid fields, basic crafting modules produce simple components, and the player learns how to automate fundamental resource loops—metal refining, energy distribution, storage allocation, and material transport. What differentiates Final Upgrade from genre peers is how quickly even these early systems reveal hidden layers of complexity. Ships are not static vessels purchased from a menu—they are modular constructions whose internal layout, propulsion arrangement, cargo space, and computing capacity directly influence their performance. A transport freighter with a poorly optimized interior may bottleneck an entire supply chain, and a miner lacking structural support may operate inefficiently. This design philosophy—where every detail matters—establishes a satisfying sense of authorship over the entire industrial ecosystem.

As the player expands into new star systems, the scale increases dramatically. Individual planets become specialized industrial centers, contributing unique resources or manufacturing capabilities. Trade routes must be automated and safeguarded. Energy production, once a simple concern, becomes a galaxy-wide balancing act. Supply shortages in one sector cascade into distant manufacturing delays, forcing players to monitor and redesign logistics networks rather than merely placing more machines. The game thrives on these emergent problems—never scripted, always systemic—giving progression a natural, self-generated rhythm. Instead of waiting for a quest or objective, players feel pushed onward by the consequences of their own decisions, and victory comes from taming the chaos they created.

The technological progression system reinforces this expanding scope. Research is not just a checklist of upgrades—it unlocks new building blocks for creative engineering, new transportation models, new hull designs, new automation tools, and ultimately, the ability to construct staggering megastructures that reshape entire star systems. Mastery comes not from grinding resources, but from learning how to integrate these technologies into existing infrastructure without destabilizing what already works. Late-game empires become dizzyingly complex organisms—ships ferrying advanced components between orbital foundries, refineries feeding terraforming operations, computational stations optimizing traffic, and automated fleets maintaining the entire industrial lattice with minimal player oversight. Watching the galaxy run itself becomes one of Final Upgrade’s most rewarding endgame experiences.

Visually, the game embraces clarity over spectacle. Its interface-driven presentation favors crisp icons, readable galaxy maps, and clean ship schematics, ensuring that even enormous networks remain understandable at a glance. Planets, starfields, and nebulae provide atmosphere, but never overshadow the informational backbone that drives decision-making. The soundscape reinforces this functional beauty—soft ambient hums, mechanical pulses, and understated musical themes that support long sessions without fatigue. It’s a game built for focus rather than adrenaline, and its aesthetic choices reflect that purpose.

However, the game’s ambition also creates barriers. The learning curve can feel imposing, especially for players unfamiliar with automation or logistical simulations. Tutorials help, but they cannot fully prepare players for the complexity that unfolds hours later, when intersystem production chains require troubleshooting and strategic redesign. The interface, while powerful, demands patience and experimentation. Some players may crave stronger guidance, clearer goals, or narrative framing to contextualize their actions. Performance may dip in massive late-game networks, and occasional rough edges remind players that Final Upgrade is the product of a small, evolving indie effort rather than a polished corporate release. But for players who enjoy wrestling with complexity, these challenges become part of the appeal rather than deterrents.

Final Upgrade ultimately succeeds because it respects the intelligence and curiosity of its audience. It does not simplify or shrink its systems to broaden its reach—it trusts players to rise to the challenge, to experiment, fail, redesign, and gradually transform a handful of miners into a galaxy-spanning industrial civilization. It offers the fantasy of engineering mastery rather than conquest, and derives excitement not from explosions or scripted drama, but from witnessing a perfectly optimized supply chain operate across light-years. Few games capture the thrill of creation on such a grand, systemic scale.

For fans of automation sandboxes, complex management simulations, modular ship design, and open-ended strategy, Final Upgrade offers a deep, demanding, and immensely satisfying experience—one that rewards long-term thinking, analytical problem-solving, and a fascination with interconnected systems. It asks for time, patience, and commitment, but it gives players a galaxy that becomes unmistakably theirs—built not through brute force, but through elegant efficiency.

Visually, the game embraces clarity over spectacle. Its interface-driven presentation favors crisp icons, readable galaxy maps, and clean ship schematics, ensuring that even enormous networks remain understandable at a glance. Planets, starfields, and nebulae provide atmosphere, but never overshadow the informational backbone that drives decision-making. The soundscape reinforces this functional beauty—soft ambient hums, mechanical pulses, and understated musical themes that support long sessions without fatigue. It’s a game built for focus rather than adrenaline, and its aesthetic choices reflect that purpose.

However, the game’s ambition also creates barriers. The learning curve can feel imposing, especially for players unfamiliar with automation or logistical simulations. Tutorials help, but they cannot fully prepare players for the complexity that unfolds hours later, when intersystem production chains require troubleshooting and strategic redesign. The interface, while powerful, demands patience and experimentation. Some players may crave stronger guidance, clearer goals, or narrative framing to contextualize their actions. Performance may dip in massive late-game networks, and occasional rough edges remind players that Final Upgrade is the product of a small, evolving indie effort rather than a polished corporate release. But for players who enjoy wrestling with complexity, these challenges become part of the appeal rather than deterrents.

Final Upgrade ultimately succeeds because it respects the intelligence and curiosity of its audience. It does not simplify or shrink its systems to broaden its reach—it trusts players to rise to the challenge, to experiment, fail, redesign, and gradually transform a handful of miners into a galaxy-spanning industrial civilization. It offers the fantasy of engineering mastery rather than conquest, and derives excitement not from explosions or scripted drama, but from witnessing a perfectly optimized supply chain operate across light-years. Few games capture the thrill of creation on such a grand, systemic scale.

For fans of automation sandboxes, complex management simulations, modular ship design, and open-ended strategy, Final Upgrade offers a deep, demanding, and immensely satisfying experience—one that rewards long-term thinking, analytical problem-solving, and a fascination with interconnected systems. It asks for time, patience, and commitment, but it gives players a galaxy that becomes unmistakably theirs—built not through brute force, but through elegant efficiency.

Final Upgrade, developed and published by TaosX, is a sprawling automation and space-strategy sandbox that imagines the galaxy not as a battlefield or a frontier of exploration, but as a massive industrial canvas waiting to be systematized. It takes the core appeal of factory-building games—the joy of turning raw matter into complex, self-sustaining production webs—and expands it outward until production chains span star systems, planets, and entire fleets of player-designed ships. Instead of embodying a conquering empire or a wandering explorer, the player becomes an architect of interconnected processes, building a mechanized civilization whose strength comes not from firepower, but from logistics, efficiency, and relentless technological momentum. It is a game that asks players to think like an engineer rather than a commander, designing not just individual machines, but the invisible flow between them.

The early game introduces this vision through modest beginnings. A handful of vessels mine asteroid fields, basic crafting modules produce simple components, and the player learns how to automate fundamental resource loops—metal refining, energy distribution, storage allocation, and material transport. What differentiates Final Upgrade from genre peers is how quickly even these early systems reveal hidden layers of complexity. Ships are not static vessels purchased from a menu—they are modular constructions whose internal layout, propulsion arrangement, cargo space, and computing capacity directly influence their performance. A transport freighter with a poorly optimized interior may bottleneck an entire supply chain, and a miner lacking structural support may operate inefficiently. This design philosophy—where every detail matters—establishes a satisfying sense of authorship over the entire industrial ecosystem.

As the player expands into new star systems, the scale increases dramatically. Individual planets become specialized industrial centers, contributing unique resources or manufacturing capabilities. Trade routes must be automated and safeguarded. Energy production, once a simple concern, becomes a galaxy-wide balancing act. Supply shortages in one sector cascade into distant manufacturing delays, forcing players to monitor and redesign logistics networks rather than merely placing more machines. The game thrives on these emergent problems—never scripted, always systemic—giving progression a natural, self-generated rhythm. Instead of waiting for a quest or objective, players feel pushed onward by the consequences of their own decisions, and victory comes from taming the chaos they created.

The technological progression system reinforces this expanding scope. needs work, but has the potential to be a great 2D platformer.

Game needs a "restart" option for each levels in case you mess up and just want to try again. maybe even implement a checkpoint system with a "restart at checkpoint" kind of feature rather than force the player to play through the entire stage just to come back and play it all over again.

KB+M controls could benefit from having a crosshair on screen. While the controller works just fine, sometimes I just want to play on a KB+M without having to plug in my controller (I have to unplug it most times to prevent games from autodetecting it and either defaulting to it or activating its rumble feature, causing it to rattle off of the desk).

All in all, fun game. Reminds me of Lemmings Mixed with another retro game I used to play but can't remember the name of.

There is a lot to like about this game.  First, the price is right!!  Also it is a game where you are the general manager of your favorite team.  This simulation uses fictitious players and team names.  Not a problem for me.  You are responsible for developing your stadium and selecting minor league teams.

The biggest responsibility is to decide who to keep, who to let go and how much to pay everyone.  You have a limited amount of money so bad decisions will cost you money.

A great game for a great price!

World of Fate, developed and published by NIK’s Games, is an ambitious strategy sandbox that aims to merge grand-scale empire building with real-world geography. Rather than placing players in a fictional map or a procedurally generated world, the game’s defining feature is its use of real street and building data, allowing players to establish their empire in actual cities across the globe. This concept immediately sets World of Fate apart from conventional strategy titles, promising a sense of scale and personal connection that comes from conquering and managing locations that exist in reality. It is a bold idea, and one that shapes every aspect of the experience, for better and for worse.

Gameplay begins by selecting any real-world building as the foundation of your faction. From there, expansion is carried out by capturing nearby structures and assigning them roles within your growing domain. Buildings can become housing, production centers, storage facilities, or military outposts, forming the backbone of your economy and territorial control. As your holdings expand, you organize them into sectors, rename areas, and gradually transform scattered buildings into something resembling a coherent empire. This process feels deliberately methodical, emphasizing planning and long-term organization rather than rapid conquest or constant conflict.

Resource management and development sit at the core of progression. Captured buildings generate income and population, and growth depends on how effectively you balance expansion with infrastructure. There is a strong administrative flavor to the gameplay, as much of your time is spent managing sectors, assigning functions, and optimizing layouts. Military units and squads can be trained to defend territory or push outward, but in its current form, combat and conflict feel secondary to expansion and management. The strategic challenge lies more in maintaining a sustainable empire than in outmaneuvering enemies on a battlefield.

The pace of World of Fate is slow and deliberate, particularly in the early stages. Progress can feel incremental, as claiming buildings and watching numbers rise lacks the immediate feedback found in more action-oriented strategy games. This can be appealing to players who enjoy patient, systems-driven gameplay, but it may feel tedious to those expecting dramatic events or frequent turning points. Tutorials and onboarding are minimal, which means new players often need to experiment extensively to understand how systems interact. This learning curve reinforces the game’s sandbox nature but also contributes to a sense of opacity that can be discouraging.

Visually, the game prioritizes function over flair. Maps are clear and readable, but largely utilitarian, relying on icons, overlays, and interface elements rather than detailed environments. The real-world map data gives cities recognizable layouts, which is intriguing from a conceptual standpoint, but the presentation itself is sparse. The interface communicates information effectively once understood, yet it lacks the polish and clarity that could make complex systems feel more approachable. As a result, immersion comes more from the imagination and the novelty of real locations than from audiovisual presentation.

A major aspect of the game’s identity is its Early Access status, which strongly influences how it should be approached. Many of the most enticing features—such as deeper political systems, large-scale wars, alliances, advanced AI factions, and more dynamic multiplayer interaction—are either partially implemented or planned for the future. In its current state, the game feels more like a foundation than a complete strategic experience. The core loop of expansion and management is present, but depth and variety are limited, leading to repetition once the initial novelty wears off.

Community reception reflects this tension between potential and execution. Players intrigued by the concept often praise the originality of using real-world geography and the freedom to start anywhere on the planet. At the same time, criticism frequently centers on a lack of content density, unclear progression, technical issues, and concerns about long-term support. For some, the experience feels closer to a proof of concept than a fully realized game, making it difficult to recommend without reservations.

Ultimately, World of Fate is a game defined by its ambition. The idea of building and managing a global empire rooted in real locations is genuinely compelling and opens the door to strategic storytelling that few games attempt. However, the current execution struggles to fully capitalize on that promise. It is best suited for players who enjoy slow, administrative strategy sandboxes and are willing to tolerate rough edges in exchange for experimental ideas. For those seeking a polished, content-rich strategy game with immediate depth and strong presentation, World of Fate may feel incomplete. As a long-term project with significant unrealized potential, it remains intriguing, but its value depends heavily on how much patience a player has for systems still in development.

For some, the experience feels closer to a proof of concept than a fully realized game, making it difficult to recommend without reservations.

Ultimately, World of Fate is a game defined by its ambition. The idea of building and managing a global empire rooted in real locations is genuinely compelling and opens the door to strategic storytelling that few games attempt. It is best suited for players who enjoy slow, administrative strategy sandboxes and are willing to tolerate rough edges in exchange for experimental ideas. For those seeking a polished, content-rich strategy game with immediate depth and strong presentation, World of Fate may feel incomplete. Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy is an indie action title that strives to deliver a blend of mythological grandeur, fast-paced sword combat, and stylized environments. Developed by Quacky Games and published by Whale Rock Games, it presents itself as a journey through Scandinavian-inspired realms filled with supernatural foes and elemental power. At first glance, the game’s visual tone and combat-driven premise suggest a compact but energetic hack-and-slash experience. It promises a series of battles across varied landscapes, offering players a taste of mythic fantasy without demanding the complexity of a full RPG.

The combat system is at the heart of the game, and it is here where Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy tries to establish its identity. Encounters unfold in straightforward but punchy fashion: enemies approach in waves, the player reacts with melee strikes or ranged abilities, and timing becomes a crucial factor in managing each skirmish. Early encounters can feel engaging, especially when the rhythm of dodging, swinging, and spacing begins to click. There is a sense of satisfaction in carving through opponents with decisive blows, and the fantasy aesthetic of glowing weapons and elemental effects adds flair to these moments. While not deep in terms of variety or combo systems, the core movements and impacts do offer occasional bursts of fun that hint at what the game might have grown into with more development time.

Visually, the game leans into stylization rather than realism. Its environments—vast fields, shadowy valleys, and mythic battlegrounds—possess a certain atmospheric charm. The lighting effects and color palettes contribute to the sense of scale the developers aimed for, and while not lavishly detailed, the world does manage to convey the feeling of a lone warrior navigating a divine conflict. Character and enemy designs follow this same approach: recognizable silhouettes and dramatic proportions over intricate detail. For an indie title with modest system requirements, the visual direction succeeds in establishing mood even when technical limitations are apparent.

However, the cracks in the game’s foundation become clear as the experience progresses. One of the most persistent criticisms is the repetitive nature of the level design. Although the maps themselves vary in theme, the structure of the gameplay rarely evolves. Most stages follow the same loop: advance, fight a group of enemies, move forward again, and repeat. Without meaningful environmental interaction or evolving challenges, the repetition becomes increasingly noticeable. Enemy AI is also simplistic, often relying on predictable patterns that reduce the sense of tension or tactical decision-making. Once the player adapts to enemy behavior, the combat risks feeling mechanical rather than dynamic.

Technical and structural polish are also areas where the game struggles. Animations can appear stiff, hit detection uneven, and transitions between actions occasionally abrupt. These issues undermine the otherwise promising feel of the core combat. Interface elements, health indicators, and feedback cues lack refinement, which contributes to the impression that the game is still in an early developmental state despite being a full release. The lack of narrative framing or character progression further compounds this impression. With no developing story arc, upgrades, or evolving abilities, the motivation to continue rests entirely on the combat—and the repetition makes that a difficult foundation to sustain.

Despite these shortcomings, Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy is not without its audience. Players who enjoy lightweight hack-and-slash games and do not mind a repetitive structure may still find pockets of enjoyment in its fantasy presentation and straightforward combat loop. The game does not pretend to be a sprawling action RPG or a genre-defining adventure; it offers brief bursts of stylized action that may appeal to those looking for a simple, low-pressure experience. For some, the opportunity to swing a glowing sword in a myth-inspired landscape may be enough to justify the journey.

Ultimately, Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy feels like a concept with potential rather than a fully realized vision. It carries the ambition of a mythological action game but lacks the mechanical depth, polish, and variety needed to deliver a lasting or memorable experience. Its strengths—atmosphere, stylization, and occasional satisfying clashes—are consistently overshadowed by repetitive design, shallow encounters, and technical roughness. For players curious about the premise or those with a soft spot for experimental indie action titles, it may offer fleeting enjoyment. But for anyone seeking a polished, dynamic, or richly structured fantasy combat experience, Firelight Fantasy: Force Energy remains more of a rough sketch than a completed work.

A fun puzzle game with easy achievements. All three achievements can be obtained in 10-15 minutes.
We interact with energy modules with special weapons, drag a couple of cubes and memorize the sequence to get past the lasers.
I liked the game, but it's too short. I hope more content will be added to the release.

made me slow down and actually think, not just push buttons. Some puzzles had me staring at the room for a good minute before it clicked, and that moment felt great. The gravity gun is fun to mess with, and the game doesn’t rush you, which I appreciated. Quiet, focused, and oddly satisfying to get through.
Well, it's a very visually beautiful puzzle.
It's difficult, but it will work

Moons of Ardan has quietly abducted a large chunk of my weekend, and I regret nothing.
You’re in charge of a small civilisation of adorable Axonauts, spreading across multiple planets and moons, setting up production chains, research, and interplanetary logistics. No pressure, just casual galaxy management.

The real joy comes from juggling planets that all need different resources, then calmly shipping things across space like a very relaxed cosmic supply manager. It scratches that “everything must connect” itch in the most satisfying way possible.

It’s still in Early Access, but in my experience it’s been surprisingly stable and already feels like there’s plenty to get stuck into. If you enjoy space, city builders, and watching a tiny civilisation thrive without everything immediately exploding, this one is well worth boarding.

Warning: you may look up and realise several hours have passed. Time behaves strangely in space.

After the collapse takes gameplay style notes from games like "Rimworld" and "Prison Architect". You build a colony in the ruins of a nuclear war-ravaged civilization. It's different enough from Rimworld in tone and in gameplay that it stands apart. It's worth investing some time in this different flavor of colony sim - it's a good one.

So, review time. IDK what all the drama is about, I won't pretend that I do. This is just a review of the game in its current state in January of 2026. It's (almost) everything I wanted in a VR Rhythm Game. Most of the games that people talk about using for exercise involve heavy arm use--but if you want to burn calories, you're best off using the bigger muscles: the legs (this is true for both men and women). This isn't DDR--it's all horizontal and vertical movement, there's no forward and back, but it will make you sweat. Plus, for the small library, there's a HUGE community contributed library of which I have barely even scratched the surface.

Constructive Criticisms:
(1) Using HTC Base Stations and Trackers for feet, the shoe calibration is wonky. It's almost mirrored, and I know that transformation matrices and quaternions can be hard, but this is just hilariously bad. So my quick workaround: strap the trackers to your ankles, compensate to the best of your ability by doing opposite of what it looks like you should do, then after "calibration" bend down and physically rotate the tracker around your ankle until the shoe is in the right spot. Devs, please fix this. 

(2) Game library is too small. "Okay but you said there's community content." Yes, I did, and there is, but of the tracks I sampled, most ONLY have a "Hard" mode with a rare "Normal" (which is also very hard for me), and "Expert" (that I don't even want to open for fear of shock). Now, I do hope to get to a point where I can play harder content, but bottom line is that there's not enough "Easy" content (that I've found) for people who are starting, who have a disability, or who are overweight. Devs, please add new content, I'll pay a fair fee for DLC to cover licensing costs--and Rebuff already knows how to tap the VRC scene for good artists.  

(3) Community Content that uses YT, the one community song I tried that connected to YT experienced a latency issue that was not-insignificant. The lag between the game and the song was more than half a beat. This is a tricky problem to solve, assuming it's not the fault of the community creator who provided the one mapping I tried. That having been said, I feel like there must be some way to fish out a fix or solution--but it just adds more fuel to the fire in Criticism (2), please add more content--I will happily pay for DLCs to cover song licensing and dance mapping. Maybe contract/hire some of your community creators if you need extra hands!  

Overall: If you have VR with FBT capacity and you want to sweat, this game is worth it. However, it also needs more content, and a little more fine-tuning: the last update was in June of last year, so someone needs to light a fire under the devs' butts, because the game is almost perfect.

That having been said, I feel like there must be some way to fish out a fix or solution--but it just adds more fuel to the fire in Criticism (2), please add more content--I will happily pay for DLCs to cover song licensing and dance mapping. Maybe contract/hire some of your community creators if you need extra hands!  

Overall: If you have VR with FBT capacity and you want to sweat, this game is worth it. However, it also needs more content, and a little more fine-tuning: the last update was in June of last year, so someone needs to light a fire under the devs' butts, because the game is almost perfect.

So, review time. IDK what all the drama is about, I won't pretend that I do. This is just a review of the game in its current state in January of 2026. It's (almost) everything I wanted in a VR Rhythm Game. Most of the games that people talk about using for exercise involve heavy arm use--but if you want to burn calories, you're best off using the bigger muscles: the legs (this is true for both men and women). This isn't DDR--it's all horizontal and vertical movement, there's no forward and back, but it will make you sweat. Plus, for the small library, there's a HUGE community contributed library of which I have barely even scratched the surface.

Constructive Criticisms:
(1) Using HTC Base Stations and Trackers for feet, the shoe calibration is wonky. It's almost mirrored, and I know that transformation matrices and quaternions can be hard, but this is just hilariously bad. So my quick workaround: strap the trackers to your ankles, compensate to the best of your ability by doing opposite of what it looks like you should do, then after "calibration" bend down and physically rotate the tracker around your ankle until the shoe is in the right spot. Devs, please fix this. 

(2) Game library is too small. "Okay but you said there's community content." Yes, I did, and there is, but of the tracks I sampled, most ONLY have a "Hard" mode with a rare "Normal" (which is also very hard for me), and "Expert" (that I don't even want to open for fear of shock). Now, I do hope to get to a point where I can play harder content, but bottom line is that there's not enough "Easy" content (that I've found) for people who are starting, who have a disability, or who are overweight. Devs, please add new content, I'll pay a fair fee for DLC to cover licensing costs--and Rebuff already knows how to tap the VRC scene for good artists.  

(3) Community Content that uses YT, the one community song I tried that connected to YT experienced a latency issue that was not-insignificant. The lag between the game and the song was more than half a beat. This is a tricky problem to solve, assuming it's not the fault of the community creator who provided the one mapping I tried. That having been said, I feel like there must be some way to fish out a fix or solution--but it just adds more fuel to the fire in Criticism (2), please add more content--I will happily pay for DLCs to cover song licensing and dance mapping. Maybe contract/hire some of your community creators if you need extra hands!  

Overall: If you have VR with FBT capacity and you want to sweat, this game is worth it. However, it also needs more content, and a little more fine-tuning: the last update was in June of last year, so someone needs to light a fire under the devs' butts, because the game is almost perfect.

[h1] SQUARE BIRD [/h1]

Square is a pretty fun parody of Flappy Bird, where instead of a bird, you control a simple cube in 2D space. The main goal isn’t just to dodge obstacles, but to collect small diamonds. They don’t really serve any purpose besides making your score look good in the stats — there’s nothing to spend them on — but they do add a bit of thrill to the game.

Visually, the game has a neon style and looks pretty decent for an indie project. The main draw here isn’t the graphics, though, but the ability to tweak the level to your liking: even with procedural generation, the developer added sliders to adjust obstacle frequency and cube speed. It’s handy if you want to either relax or go full hardcore. It’s a bit of a bummer, though, that there aren’t many types of obstacles.

Sure, the game lacks a leaderboard and cloud saves, but props for the Steam achievements. The developer didn’t promise the moon on the store page, so you can’t really call them out for lying. As a simple time-killer, the game does its job perfectly. Could it be better? Sure, but for its price, it’s totally worth a few casual gaming sessions.

[h3] Verdict: [/h3] https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3628285134

A small action slasher with six levels and a hero armed with a sword and shield.

At each level, you need to complete the tasks. It's mostly about killing enemies. At the very end of the game, you need to cut down a huge boss.

Press "Enter" to skip the intro.
We use "Alt" to somersault free from enemies when they pinch the hero in narrow passages.

game_name: Love n War: Hero by Chance II
review_text: This series needs polishing but never disappoints. It's improved game after game and always comes with new features. Kudos to the dev for bringing me some good nuts.

game_name: Love n War: Hero by Chance II
review_text:
This is a decent line fighter and a step up from the first game. The sexy bits are just pics that move a little but you do not really interact with them outside picking a thing and a place. If the sexy stuff if all you want there are 3 difficulties where easy unlocks it all.
Most levels have 3 lanes you have to defend though a few only have 1. There are also levels where you pick a few units to clear a path through a preset of enemies. if you have the dlc when you get to those battles you'll be able to build obstacles in the lanes. Some of your units will have special gear you can make you change the way they look and make them stronger. There is also an arena mode (with the dlc) that let's you do the fights without the story and you'll see what units they enemy can spawn before hand.
The story is ok not great but more than just bleh, bleh though you could skip it all and not miss anything.
5/10

[h1]OK as long as you are careful NOT to spend any money[/h1]

If you spend money on this game, it will quickly become a life-ruining money-sink because that is what it is designed to do.

However, if you are careful not to spend anything, it's an OK game that is actually much more generous in what you can get for free than most live-service trash.

Think of this like a casino that gives away free breakfast in the hopes that you will start gambling once you are there. If you are careful only to eat the free breakfast and never buy a drink or gamble, you can get something out of it but if you start gambling, it will ruin your life.

Don't spend money on this game and ESPECIALLY don't buy the gamble-boxes which are so manipulative and life-ruining that they ought to be illegal. 

Don't try to recruit your friends or engage in any of that other life-destroying MLM-style behavior the scum running this game try to promote either.

Just play the game, have fun, and don't let the scumbags running it turn you into a bad person.

Started my journey in 2014 and haven't looked back.

Pros: Incredible ship models (historical and fiction), deep strategic layers, and satisfying progression.

Cons: The "power creep" is real, and the learning curve for new players in the current version can be brutal. But don't worry, this game is tier-wise.

Is it perfect? No. Is it the best naval game on the market? Absolutely. It’s a marathon, not a sprint. Take your time at the mid-tiers (V-VII) to learn the ropes before rushing to Tier X (which is costly if you're unable to sink min. 2 ships).

This is genuinely one of my favorite games of all time. I only have 14 hours here because I play over on the WGC launcher but I will probably switch my account over to steam now that you can. Yes, WG has made some bad decisions with certain things but the core of the game is still strong. World of Warships is a free-to-play naval combat MMO where you command ships from destroyers to battleships, cruisers, and carriers. It’s all about positioning, strategy, and timing your shots — not just running and gunning. (store.steampowered.com
)

Strengths:
✔️ Variety of ships and nations — each vessel feels unique with distinct playstyles.
✔️ Tactical depth — positioning, angling armor, and teamwork matter more than raw aim.
✔️ Epic naval battles — large maps and strategic objectives make each match feel cinematic.
✔♥♥♥♥♥♥♥to-play friendly — accessible progression without mandatory paywalls.
✔️ Regular updates and events — new ships, missions, and seasonal content keep the game fresh.

Weaknesses:
✖️ Steep learning curve — new players can be overwhelmed by ship mechanics and maps.
✖️ Matchmaking imbalance — sometimes players face opponents with vastly better ships or experienced teams.
✖️ Slow-paced gameplay — battles can take 15–25 minutes, which may feel long for casual players.
✖️ Pay-to-progress temptation — some players feel pressured to spend for faster upgrades or rare ships.

This is one of the best Free to Play games out there, play at your own pace, go slow and snipe from the back with a battleship if you want to relax or go hard & fast with the destroyer firing torps at your enemies or do a sneaky with a submarine. Plenty of ships to choose from, from multiple different nations, I am pleased some Aussie ships even made the list under Commonwealth nations. 20 minutes a match, play as little or as much as you want, great for those with a busy life or with plenty of spare time on their hands.

This is a good game, way better than war thunder, however if you have a favorite boat, go somewhere else. Unless it is the most famous boat on earth, you have no hopes of getting it. My favorite boat is BB-63 Battleship Missouri, they added the boat to the game, but for some reason, I can't get it, it was a limited and because I wasn't playing the game then, and even if I was I wouldn't fork over 100k dollars to buy it, so now I can't get it. This same problem also applies with World of Warplanes.

Edit: I wish to clarify, this is a problem with Wargaming and the gaming industry in general. The industry has moved to making content that is good, only for them to remove it at random.

Edit 2: I have changed this to be recommended, this game is fun, I just want my favorite boat. Very sad.

I installed World of Warships thinking I’d casually sail around and relax.
Instead, I now hold a PhD in “Getting Deleted by Something I Never Saw.”

The tutorial: “Here is a ship.”
The first real match: “Here is a satellite-guided nuclear meteor fired by a cruiser 18km away.”

Destroyers are invisible.
Cruisers are fragile.
Battleships are slow.
Aircraft carriers are legally allowed to ruin your day from another timezone.

Every match starts with confidence and ends with me reverse-sailing into an island while apologizing to my team in 7 languages.

I absolutely loved this game and its DLC. The artwork takes center stage and pulls you into its mysteriously dark and macabre world. It really makes you think about humans from a "seemingly" innocent monster's point of view. The story telling and pacing was right up my alley, you weren't forced to push through too fast and were rewarded for exploring. It made going back and 100% alluring instead of daunting. If you like visual novels, unique art and story telling with a more explorative pace this is the game for you!

Call of Duty to marka, która od lat wyznacza tempo w świecie strzelanek pierwszoosobowych i dla wielu graczy stanowi punkt odniesienia dla całego gatunku. Niezależnie od kolejnych odsłon, rdzeń doświadczenia pozostaje rozpoznawalny: szybka, responsywna rozgrywka, kinowa oprawa i silny nacisk na rywalizację sieciową. To właśnie konsekwencja w dopracowaniu podstaw sprawia, że seria wciąż utrzymuje ogromną popularność.

Największą siłą Call of Duty jest płynność strzelania i dynamika starć. System poruszania się, czas reakcji broni oraz czytelność trafień zostały przez lata doprowadzone do bardzo wysokiego poziomu, dzięki czemu każda potyczka daje natychmiastowe poczucie kontroli. Rozgrywka nagradza refleks i znajomość map, ale pozostaje przystępna także dla mniej doświadczonych graczy.

Tryb wieloosobowy niezmiennie stanowi serce serii. Różnorodność map, broni i konfiguracji wyposażenia pozwala dopasować styl gry do własnych preferencji, a szybkie tempo meczów sprzyja krótkim, intensywnym sesjom. Dodatkowe tryby, takie jak kooperacyjne warianty PvE czy battle royale, rozszerzają doświadczenie bez odchodzenia od charakterystycznej tożsamości marki.

Na uwagę zasługuje również warstwa audiowizualna. Call of Duty konsekwentnie stawia na filmowy rozmach — szczegółowe animacje, mocne efekty dźwiękowe i dynamiczną reżyserię scen akcji. Dzięki temu nawet krótkie starcia potrafią sprawiać wrażenie fragmentów dużej produkcji kinowej.

Mimo silnej konkurencji na rynku shooterów, Call of Duty wciąż pozostaje jedną z najbardziej dopracowanych i dostępnych serii w swoim gatunku. To propozycja dla graczy szukających szybkiej, satysfakcjonującej akcji i sprawdzonej formuły, która przez lata była konsekwentnie rozwijana i udoskonalana.

Call of Duty is one of the most famous first-person shooter franchises in gaming history. The series is known for fast, cinematic action, realistic military themes, and competitive online multiplayer. Whether you play for the story, the intense PvP matches, or cooperative modes, the main goal is always the same — quick reactions, smart positioning, and teamwork.

From the moment you start playing, the game feels polished and high-energy. Explosions, sound design, and smooth gunplay create a strong “action movie” atmosphere that few shooters match.

🎮 Gameplay – Fast and competitive
4

The core gameplay is simple but addictive:

Fast-paced matches with quick respawns

Large variety of weapons and loadouts

Leveling system that unlocks new gear

Different modes like Team Deathmatch, Domination, and Search & Destroy

The movement is smooth and responsive, which makes firefights feel exciting and skill-based. Players who enjoy competitive games will appreciate how much practice and map knowledge matter.

One big strength is variety — every match feels slightly different depending on maps, weapons, and team strategy.

However, one major downside is the presence of cheaters. Unfortunately, hackers can make matches frustrating and sometimes unfair, which takes away from the competitive fun. When you lose to skill, it feels motivating — but losing to cheats can really ruin the experience.

**PUBG: Battlegrounds** is the kind of game that doesn’t try to be flashy. It’s raw, sometimes clunky, sometimes brutal, but when it works, nothing else feels quite the same.

The tension is what makes it special. Dropping into a match knowing you have one life changes everything. Early game is chaos, mid game is paranoia, late game is pure stress. Sometimes you spend 15 minutes looting and moving carefully, and then it all ends in two seconds because you checked the wrong angle.

Gunplay feels heavy and unforgiving. Recoil matters. Positioning matters. Sound matters. It rewards patience more than aggression, and that slower, tactical pace is what separates it from other battle royale games.

The maps each have their own personality. Some favor long-range fights, others force close-quarters chaos. Learning rotations and understanding terrain becomes just as important as aiming.

It’s not perfect. There are still rough edges, and sometimes the pacing can feel slow if you don’t find action early. But that unpredictability is part of the experience. Every match tells a slightly different story.

When you win in PUBG, it doesn’t feel casual. It feels earned.

Still one of the most intense battle royale experiences out there.

100% classic Battle royal, I've played since release or maybe it was open beta. This battle royal is the roots to many BR's, but it came after H1Z1 and this is how it plays: there are lean mechanics, you can choose games of third person or first, the movements are blocky, but pretty fair and  skill based. The player base is the only downside to the game as the match making isn't fun due to cheaters, robots, or people who've had a lot of hours consistently. There are many game modes and not every game will you face constantly max level players so for the times you don't face cheaters or insane players you have great games that are extremely complex with different ways the fights go down

Example: I've ran over people with vehicles, done drive-by in the vehicles of the game, been killed by people shooting out of the plane vehicles, had enemies and teammates crushed by the "Deployable cover flair" when it drops 2 large cement structures very fast, killed 10 players with crossbows (hard difficulty)(achievement), grenades  of many different varieties (including blue zone grenades - copied from fortnite, but good move), deaths from the many different special weapons that drop from the red crates, and so much more.

One map had a gulag, but i think it's out of rotation now, but they instead also took the concept of being revived by teammates just as other BR's did and that is a good  change.

The game mode of 88 bots and 12 players is the most enjoyable to get warm and ready to face harder opponents. I hope for any of you reading this that you get to play versus people your own level. Whatever you do, dont let your first game be with that lvl 100+  friend... ENJOY IT WHILE YOU CAN.

There is a team deathmatch game mode, it takes place in small circles of random spots on the actual maps so that's awesome for getting warmed up

The games are exhilarating, Balanced well to where all guns are useful, you can choose to play safe, slow, fast, like a rat, or  however you like. Ghillie suits are a thing and they're fun. 

I've only seen 3 bad changes and they were map changes, but the new items added, new vehicles, skins, gameplay (Except the Match Making), emotes, etc... is all pretty great.


Tell me your thoughts?

I remember fondly that the first game I ever played was a racing game. Hashiriya Drifter brought back those memories with its flow-y controls and straight-to-the-point gameplay. The unique aspect of this racing game is its drifting mechanics. Now, I might be a driver, but I am no expert in the art of drifting; HOWEVER, the drift controls of this game feel good.

[list]
[*] It is intimidating at the start but when you get the trick to drifting, you can go at it endlessly. As in, complete a whole race drifting away without colliding with the barriers. My highest drift score that I got in one go is 2.2k beat that lmao;
[*] The car customization is actually pretty cool! I am quite partial to the GT86, kitted with Wheel27 (you can also remove car parts if that's what you're into);
[*] BEAT DROP BGM
[/list]

All in all, I say give this game a go. You might find your new past-time in this game.

I joined the fray and at once beheld a man hurled into another plane of existence by a cannonball. Moments later, I myself was dispatched by a fellow wielding a hatchet. All I wished, in my final breath, was to glimpse my wife’s calves one last time.

A most harrowing affair.
10 out of 10.

If you’ve ever wanted to experience the tactical brilliance of the 18th century combined with the decision-making skills of a sleep-deprived potato, Holdfast: Nations At War has you covered. 
Where else can you watch 50 impeccably dressed soldiers form a perfect firing line, miss every shot, and then get obliterated by one guy with suspiciously good aim and a flute? 
The proximity voice chat turns every battle into a live theatre performance featuring opera singers, drill sergeants, and at least one person aggressively role-playing as Napoleon. 
It’s historically immersive right up until someone yells “fix bayonets” and the entire formation sprints directly into cannon fire. 
10/10, would politely reload for 20 seconds while chaos unfolds around me again.

Really fun to play in big servers. Reloading takes a long time across all weapons and artillery. So it really forces you to appreciate delayed gratification. Some of the physics/mechanics are really janky. For example, running into a stump with a horse pretty much spells your death. But you can just trample and run over people without a care in the world. Jumping with it is also weird, and the melee system in this game takes a long time to get used to. Since it's about big team battles, you'll occasionally get into scenarios where your team is losing, and you're just getting torn a new one with almost nothing you can do about it since you're just one guy against a whole faction. Some maps are heavily sided to one side, and you'll see that when you join most players will attempt to join one team as fast as they can. In a way, it's nice to have something like this since it's polarizing. Spicing things up, but it also just gets really frustrating sometimes. 
It's hilariously fun to goof around, and even rewarding to learn how to use artillery or learn how to effectively melee. It does however, greatly rely on player to player experience, so if you have bad luck playing with randoms and their just no fun you're likely not to have any fun either. It's not likely tho.

Dear Dennis,

I am writing this to you not as a fan asking for an update, but as a 17-year-old who has been deeply shaped by your work.

In your games—especially Granny—you didn't just create a horror experience; you created a masterpiece of 'Atmosphere.' The feeling of being trapped, of every floorboard creak being a risk, and the sheer logic required to escape has always fascinated me. It actually made me want to start learning C# and Unity so I can understand the 'Soul' of game design.

I often wonder: when you created the concept of being 'trapped,' was it inspired by a time in your own life where you felt stuck or had to solve complex tasks to find your way out? Your games feel like they come from a place of true understanding of what it means to seek freedom.

Thank you for being a legend in the solo-dev community. Whether you ever make another game or not, please know that your 'Code' lives on in the inspiration you give to the next generation of developers like me.

Stay strong and thank you for everything.

I usually don't leave reviews but felt like I should for this one after 35 hours. First and foremost, it is very repetitive with its gameplay loop and a lot of the ships in the base game (haven't gotten to the dlc yet) have similar layouts. 

Also you can just skip using the saw a lot when you're suppose to, as in the case of when you tear down part of a wall and expose the struts under it, you're suppose to use a saw to cut the supports to break the struts off but you can just use a hammer to destroy all the connection points and break down the wall, bypassing it completely. So I wish they would have made it more required to do that part.

Now that is the negatives for me, but I do enjoy the game. It is very mindless, but  I can just throw on a youtube video or music on the other monitor and bash away on a ship for hours at a time. If you want something deep and meaningful with an involved game loop, this isn't going to be for you. But if you just want to beat the hell out of an old ship while vibing this is a fantastic game.

Just a tip for the future, don’t waste time on proper punctuation when you’re writing a bad review that’s meant to show people what a headache it was to play a broken game. People should have the patience to wait and let the developers cook.
Other than that, it can absolutely be recommended to friends. Go buy the game and play it together—it’s worth it now!

Like the first time I destroyed a crane-able by accidentally removing the floor from under it, it told me AFTER I'd destroyed it that that is what would happen. Again, maybe I didn't read it well, but I also had issues with not being informed that I had to finish my contracts before sorting my recyling; maybe that's my bad for assuming having the materials in my "inventory" would still allow me to give them to people? My last main gripe would be picking up the materials. Please, please, PLEASE, for the love of all the gods GET RID OF THE ANIMATIONS!! I do not want to be stalled several seconds between picking up each piece of the ship, let me rapid click to pick them up or a high-level upgrade that auto-boxes up the dropped materials; something! Get rid of that pick-up animation!!!

EDIT: The ladders and lights left around the boat being useless is.... Infuriating.

EDIT2: Note, don't bother using the saw or torch... Removing all the rivets causes the whole ship to fall apart regardless. Yay physics!

Ship Graveyard Simulator 2 is a very relaxing and satisfying game. Slowly dismantling massive ships piece by piece, with no time limits, is incredibly calming and gives you total freedom to scrap things your own way.

The mechanics are easy to understand, tool upgrades don’t require heavy grinding, and progression feels smooth. As you advance, more depth is added with systems like power lines, gas pipes, and radioactive materials, which makes planning each wreck fun and rewarding. Walking around a ship, figuring out the safest and most efficient way to dismantle it, and making space for crane access adds a nice layer of strategy.

The visuals are solid, and everything feels straightforward and well-paced. I don’t own all DLCs yet, but I’ll definitely be picking them up for more ships and variety.

So after 100~ hours...
Do I recommend it:  Yes but... it is frustrating (but not because of player skill issue) read below why.

The good:
Maps have nice areas to explore, missions, boss NPCs, so much to loot and there is a day/night cycles on some maps, sometimes it rains, some times very dark nights (great for night vision), it is very, very nice.

Game has PVE mode for single player but if you want to play PVE with friends somebody needs to own a DLC named "Team Tactics" DLC and then that person can invite others to play PVE in a squad of 3.

The bad:
The implementation for physical interactions with your gun, magazines, picking up things and backpack placement it is very frustrating, game seems does not a tolerance in the hotspots, the minimum extra movement from your controller when placing something in the wall will result in a fail placement and your weapon will fall to the floor, the same happens when changing magazines, picking up objects from the floor, putting your gun on your belly, etc. 

This will frustrate players a lot, sadly they may just return the game and buy something else. The Game Studio should take a look into this if they want to sell even more. 

So you then can decide if want to give it a try.

I have bought a copy from meta store but there is no option to adjust gramma and brightness, it is too dark even in the daytime to see the enemy.

This game is also extremely unfriendly to players using physical gunstocks, as foregrip can change handling logic.  I suggest to remove at least the stock part if you want to have a better experience with foregrip.

I've played a decent amount of time to finally make a review and share my thoughts. I'll go over the following things.

.Quests
The quests in the game are simple. Right now they're divided into your mission (main quest) and tasks,
which are simple mission. There's 7 traders which offer those missions. Completing said mission grants you Trader Reputation (Basically trader xp), Korunas (money) and sometimes other rewards based on what the trader sells. There's five levels of trader reputation you can gain. Basically the higher the level, the better stuff you can purchase. 

.Progression
The progression is fairly simple. Levelling the traders and finding good loot in raid to use in future raids. As of right now, there isn't really a story to uncover.

.Gameplay loop
The gameplay loop is very fun and not as repetitive. Every raid has a different experienced. This is based on the things and people you encounter and what you bring into the raid. Bring high-end stuff and you'll find yourself either afraid to lose it, or steam rolling everyone. Bring close to nothing and you'll have more fun scavenging loot and being carefull with what fights you pick.

.Gun play
The gun play is satisfying. Each weapon is very detailed and reloading is very fleshed out. Shooting weapons doesn't feel clunky and it feels like how a weapon would act irl.

.My thoughts
I really love Ghosts of Tabor. I like how it plays, the intense moments, and overall everything this game has to offer. There are a few things that stop me from playing time to time. The developers keep pushing out updates whilst the game still need LOADS of polishing. To name a few issues: Your gear not loading correctly, when filling a magazine with the ammo loader it vacuums up ammo for no reason, Armor wanting to float next to you instead of wearing it, laggy areas with the newer maps. So with everything quickly summerized. The game is good. It just needs fixing with bugs.

Executive Assault 2 is an ambitious genre-blending strategy title from Hesketh Studios Ltd that attempts to fuse large-scale real-time strategy with direct first-person combat inside a persistent sci-fi sandbox. Set in a fractured galaxy where rival corporate CEOs battle for dominance, the game casts you not merely as a battlefield commander but as the executive head of a militarized corporation. From the outset, it is clear that the design philosophy revolves around freedom: freedom to expand across sectors, freedom to shape your industrial network, and freedom to personally step onto the battlefield when strategic oversight alone is not enough. Rather than confining players to a distant top-down perspective, Executive Assault 2 encourages constant shifts between macro-level management and boots-on-the-ground action, creating a hybrid experience that feels both expansive and immediate.

The strategic layer forms the backbone of the game. Players establish bases on asteroids and space stations, carefully placing production facilities, power generators, research labs, and defensive structures. Resource management plays a central role, with mining operations and logistical planning driving expansion. Energy distribution, production efficiency, and territorial control are constant considerations, especially as rival factions compete for limited space and strategic positions. The tech progression system allows players to unlock increasingly advanced units and structures, from heavily armed mechs to starships capable of dominating orbital space. Because the battlefield spans multiple vertical layers—ground installations, asteroid surfaces, and open space—strategic awareness must extend beyond a single plane, reinforcing the sense that you are managing a living, interconnected war machine.

What truly distinguishes Executive Assault 2 from conventional RTS titles is the seamless transition into first-person mode. At any moment, you can inhabit one of your units and directly participate in combat, whether piloting a starfighter through asteroid fields, operating a mech in close-quarters assaults, or fighting as infantry within contested corridors. This shift fundamentally changes the rhythm of play. Strategic decisions are no longer abstract; they become tangible as you personally experience the effectiveness—or shortcomings—of your base design and unit composition. Leading a charge into an enemy stronghold or defending your infrastructure from boarding parties provides a visceral counterbalance to the methodical pacing of economic management. It transforms victories into personal triumphs and defeats into immediate, felt consequences.

Faction diversity and replayability further enhance the experience. Different corporate entities bring variations in technological emphasis and combat doctrine, encouraging experimentation across multiple campaigns. Some approaches favor heavy mechanized forces and brute strength, while others rely on agile starfighters or economic superiority. Multiplayer adds another dimension, allowing cooperative or competitive engagements between multiple CEOs. These matches can become sprawling conflicts defined by shifting alliances, economic warfare, and direct skirmishes that erupt unexpectedly when a rival executive decides to personally intervene on the frontlines.

Visually, Executive Assault 2 reflects both its ambition and its independent origins. The scale of space environments and the density of constructed bases convey a strong sense of industrial sprawl, though textures and animations can vary in polish. The user interface prioritizes functionality over flashiness, offering clear access to production queues and sector management tools, but it may lack the refinement found in larger-budget strategy games. Sound design effectively reinforces the metallic, mechanized tone of the universe, with industrial hums, weapons fire, and atmospheric effects contributing to immersion during both strategic oversight and FPS encounters.

Pacing is one of the game’s defining characteristics. Periods of careful base expansion and logistical planning can quickly erupt into intense firefights when enemy fleets arrive or sabotage operations begin. This constant oscillation between calm strategy and chaotic combat keeps sessions dynamic and unpredictable. However, it also means that success requires adaptability. Players must be comfortable juggling multiple responsibilities—overseeing production lines while simultaneously repelling attacks or personally leading counteroffensives. For some, this multitasking will feel exhilarating; for others, it may be overwhelming.

Ultimately, Executive Assault 2 succeeds because it fully commits to its hybrid identity. It does not attempt to simplify either half of its design; instead, it embraces the complexity of merging RTS depth with FPS immediacy. While certain aspects of presentation and polish may reveal its indie scale, the core concept remains compelling and unusually well-realized. For players who enjoy strategy titles but crave a more direct connection to the action, or for FPS fans curious about commanding entire war economies, Executive Assault 2 offers a distinctive and memorable experience. It stands as a testament to the creative potential of blending genres, delivering a spacefaring corporate war where strategic vision and personal combat skill are equally vital to achieving supremacy among the stars.

Rating: 8/10

Ok, so RTS hybrid with FPS. There's a lot this game offers compared to your typical RTS game. When you toss in friends or others on other teams as the Executrons then it becomes a very interesting and fun experience that you can tell them to capture a system or defend/attack a base. At first glance (as with many games) I will admit the UI and the objective can be overwhelming. Especially if your first time you don't play Industrial. However, on my 3rd game playing as the Science faction, I pretty much understood the gist of things and it was a very simple routine. I like the variety of play styles you can do and the fact that you could either brute force your way into their base with ground forces like soldiers, tanks, or mechs, OR you can just build a space fleet with a special ship to destroy their entire system. Of course you could go the cinematic route too and plan for both. If you love FPS large scale fights and RTS controls, this is definitely worth giving it a try. If you are overwhelmed from the gameplay, you can join their discord and ask someone to help you play a round against AI or something.

This is one of those games I come back to from time to time, play for a while and enjoy it, then put it back on the shelf for another day. I really enjoy its blend of space and ground combat (even if it stretches my brain to track both) and the RTS/FPS combo. Being able to jump into a fight and have direct fun and control without becoming seriously overpowered keeps things grounded.

I enjoy that units can be upgraded over time with different tech, and that the tech tree isn't super fast like in some games. It takes a while to get through to the better units, and if you turtle too much, you'll be overwhelmed by superior strength enemies.

It's still being polished, yes. It's not a AAA title (which let's be honest, is a good thing these days). The passion and effort going into this game is incredible. I enjoyed the first game and this one improves in just about every way. The three unique factions with different aspects to them allows for different style gameplay, as well. I haven't tried the Executron mode, but I imagine it makes for another interesting twist. And being able to actually interact with all the RTS controls from the FPS perspective via in-game consoles is pretty awesome.

Give it a try, a really solid try, and be fair. Don't expect the end-all-be-all of games, but do come expecting hard work, solid gameplay, regular updates and tweaking, and you'll enjoy your time with this game. So worth it.

For those simply skimming the reviews - I liked the game and enjoyed it thoroughly

For a more deep dive: The reason i like Executive Assault is because of its RTS - FPS features. Essentially, its a real time strategy game where you control the economy, fleet, and army from the 300 foot view (as you would in most other strategy games) with the additional option to, at any moment, choose to control an individual unit directly in first person mode. Furthermore, you can give simple orders to the units around you while in first person mode. This makes the game unique, and is one of the main reasons I enjoy playing it. I also like how there is a full FPS mode where you play as an "executron" while the AI does the economy and strategy, and this fits well with multiplayer as well, where one player can play the CEO and another the executron

One thing i have noticed about the game that i disliked. First, there wasnt as much ground combat as i would have liked, its focus is more on space. I do not dislike the ship combat, its not boring to me, its just i wish there were more ground battles. While this is the case, its also sounds like the ground will be getting some attention during the next big update. another thing i noticed after playing for awhile, is that it seems a bit "flat". What I mean specifically by that is there isnt much variety to the hight to the map, you dont see yourself moving up or down very often. The ground asteroids currently have very little terrain, and the ships all battle on more or less the same plain. This is a relatively small complaint, but I do wish that the terrain was more dynamic. Ultimately I would still highly recommend the game.

If you are thinking of playing this game, you can expect games to take around 1-2 hours generally (though there is variation outside of that) with a focus on building up a strong economy and navy, followed by battles in the sky and an occasional ground raid, with a rare ground battle taking place. Though as mentioned earlier, the next big update will likely bring some prominence to ground assaults and battles, with a specific gamemode emphasizing that. There is definitely a good amount of strategy and thinking involved. Overall, as already stated, I would recommend this game, especially if you are interested in Strategy games. I would also say this game is good to play alongside friends.

Fun game with a bit of jank here or there but the concept is too interesting, three factions that play different enough from each other to matter and the ability to take over units to play an FPS or Third Person perspective depending on what you take control of is another level of fun.

All of that with the fact you can play with AI or players as a dedicated FPS trooper. Yeah I'd say its a pretty fun time.

Its pretty good overall for a single dev project that's been going on for a while, said dev still updates the game regularly, and the most recent updates have added a lot of stuff from new ships, to game ending weapons and tools. I will say though, that despite being at least worth its price (Especially if you have a friend or friend group to play with so they can join you as the games executrons) It does have its issues.

The dev however does listen to feedback, has a feedback option on the game, listens to reviews, and also has a Discord that you can also report issues to if you feel its a better option. Either way, if you have issues then its best to send them. The main noticeable issues is a limitation on Corridors that you can place, unit caps can sometimes also be a bit of an issue, but that's more of a pet peeve of mine, and the AI's aggressiveness and cheats are utterly insane, so I do not recommend playing on anything other than Easy or lower till its possibly addressed. I haven't tried Normal or higher yet, but it is NOT for those who are new to the game or haven't tried every faction to see how it works. Even on Easy it can be unbearable sometimes. I can just say, AI will immediately rush, rush, rush. they'll send massive waves of their cheapest units to kill you or slow you down, so you MUST prepare with that in mind, they also, unlike you, do not need line of sight and do not care for fog of war, so they will know where you are almost immediately, but will take time to prepare themselves.

With all that in mind however, the game is amazing and has insane potential and has an active dev who does care about their projects, as much as they did in the first one. But also, having friends helps to manage certain aspects of the game, as they can be an executron which can take control of units, ships, and even help to build or manage research, they do not get the RTS aspect and must do a lot of it in the FPS aspect, but it isn't as hard as one would think. I would recommend maybe shifting around some Hotkeys though as a lot of them are set to the Fkeys.

All together, this game is heavily enjoyed with friends, but can be played alone on both fronts, don't like working with people and want to help the AI against people, assuming the players that are hosting allow it, you can do just that as the executron as well.

As a longtime RTS fan, Executive Assault 2 really hits a sweet spot. The mix of classic base-building, economic management, and large-scale strategic combat feels familiar, but the ability to jump directly into first-person control makes it stand out. Issuing high-level commands one moment and personally piloting a unit the next gives a great sense of scale and agency. It’s ambitious, systems-driven, and scratches that “commander brain” itch in a way few modern RTS games do. Definitely worth checking out if you enjoy strategy with depth and experimentation.

EA2 is a good RTS and an alright shooter, but when you put them together you've got something really special.

One player on each side (the CEO) is playing Capitalist Sins of a Solar Empire.  They have to setup resource mining, produce goods, sell those goods to traders, build more buildings, design and produce ships, and crush the competition.  The other players are Executrons, who can do quite a few things.  As an Executron, you can engage in ground or space combat, but you can also engage with many of the same functions as the CEO.  You can manage unit production, upgrades, research, base defenses, power, and other functions by accessing terminals located throughout the base.

So if the CEO is getting overwhelmed by **all the things** happening in the RTS, you can easily hop in and assist by taking some of those tasks off his back through terminals.  Or you can transfer to a ship and take command of the fleet.  Or join a ground assault and seize enemy stations or bring back some good loot.  Good Executrons make all the difference, and really elevate the experience.

All the buildings have fully-realized interiors so there's quite an emphasis on ground combat, which goes MUCH better when you have player Executrons on the ground leading assaults or performing covert operations for you.  Getting hammered by Science fleets and their shields?  Send a dude in to steal them.  Industrial dropping too many missiles and pods on your base?  Send a player in to retarget those systems onto their own facilities.  The possibilities for shenanigans are endless.

The game is not perfect, and it does have some limitations.  This is truly the work of a single developer, so set your expectations accordingly.  RTS mode is very polished and the AI can be very challenging.  Fighter controls are touchy, but capital ships are easy to handle.  As an Executron, you will crash troop transports many times before you get the hang of docking them.  FPS mode is alright if you're not expecting AAA quality, but the battles get very big and very fun.  Tanks and mechs are a hoot.

It's a great game for the right kind of player.

What I think it needs:

Cool vr Rythm game,
there is alot of great stuff in the game and the developers keep improving things.
Here a gameplay video: https://youtu.be/BdpejRJXJLY

Good things:

they have a music pool of licensed music that the community can access for making custom maps that are available from within the game without any mods being required. also no dmca ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ with theses custom songs, good for streaming.

Ingame Twitch chat, Emote Rain, and a customizeable third Person camera. Good for streaming.

Very well performance optimized.

Multiplayer (sadly 4players only at the moment) and if the host selects an "official" custom song people from within the lobby can download it.

Notejumpspeed and offset is the same on all maps, and can be set by the player, which makes the notes always comming the same way at you. this is a huge problem in Beat Saber with every mapper using different values and you basicly have to restart alot of maps and changing settings to make it feel right which is very bad for streaming.

There are alot of different envirnments to choose from and also alot of unlockable skins for the cat and the drumsticks.

The developers actually listening and improving things that sucks. Currently there is a new, more userfriendly map editor in the making.

It has a built in fpfc mode which let you control the game with mouse and keyboard, which is extremly usefull for setting up things and trying technical stuff out.

It has LIV support, even though we are not using LIV for mixed reality.

Looks like everyone loves the cat.


Bad Stuff:

Most of the music in the game so far is very similar genre stuff and it is not that great to dance to. Hopefully there will be some edm or k-pop stuff in the future.
If you like camellia they got you covered for sure.

No friends score board so far.


All in all it's a very cool game, hopefully the new Map Editor will bring more people into mapping for the game and more dance maps are comming.

EVEN MORE IMPORTANTLY this game has a good built in level editor and LICENSED CUSTOM SONG SUPPORT which is in my opinion the foundation of a good rhythm game. This is such a fun game and I really like the devs and community!  It's one of the first times I've joined a game's discord and immediately felt like part of the community from day 1 (even before getting a chance to try the game first!).  The devs are super open to suggestions and are very responsive to feedback, and I really like how transparent they are about what they're working on and what's coming down the pipe (e.g. they made their trello board viewable to the public and are constantly updating us on the status of things).

A lot of people compare it to beat saber, but the gameplay feels very different to me.  It feels much lighter and not as hard on my wrists, since I only need to bounce off the tops of the drums instead of swinging all the way through.  The game runs a lot smoother than BS for me as well, and I really like that they made performance-oriented environments too. It makes streaming so much easier on my pc! Also there's a cat named Pupa that runs with you while you play and you can pet her in the lobby and she purrs and gives you hearts! c: Oh and also the ost is actually good, which is something else that separates it from BS lol.

One of the absolute coolest things to me is that there's custom song support and they licensed 250 songs (100 FiXT and 150 Camellia) for community mapping, so they can officially support their mapping community and host and advertise the maps for those songs!  I think it's a great idea and I really hope they can continue with this model, since it also means that content creators can monetize their videos of those maps and don't have to worry about DMCA.  Being able to play customs on stream without having to worry about DMCA is SO nice.  You can also suggest additional songs/artists for them to license, and there's a donation bucket specifically for custom song licensing that the community can contribute to for helping out with that!

I'm really looking forward to seeing where this game goes, and I definitely recommend trying it out and joining the discord, even if you aren't sure you'll like it.  There's a bunch of different ways you can play (dance, acc, challenge, etc) and the devs are always looking for suggestions/feedback on what they can improve to make it more enjoyable for people.

From a fellow VR rhythm game dev to you BoomBox devs -- I recognize the passion to spread the fun for your players -- and you've done it!! There is something special about what you've created to share the joy of the music and the environments in BoomBox. You can tell there is passion for fun behind all of it. And the feedback of the drums is great -- It makes you want to smash them all to enjoy every single bit of each song!

Overall a good game that could be great with some quality of life improvements. I do enjoy beat saber better, but that game has had a longer time in development and has a dedicated modding community. But Boombox is a lot easier on the shoulders. And even though it looks and feels more simplistic the game feels a lot more challenging than beat saber.

Here's some improvements that I would love to see in Boombox:

1. I would like an in game clock so I don't have to take my headset off to see the time.
2. An option after completing a song to just pick a random song without going to the song selection.
3. An option to randomly select the environments instead of having to change the environments manually.
3. The game should automatically default to your last selected difficulty setting instead of automatically defaulting to the hardest difficulty setting.
4. Yo where's the puppers???

*if any of these options are already in the game then the game should do better at making those options clear*
